experiments.title: How gift array options in a high urgency sticky bar impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the HUC sticky bar for IMB website visitors will increase engagement and donor conversion by minimizing the number of steps on the donation page, reducing friction and decision-making, resulting in more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/15/2026 - 01/19/2026
During IMB’s 2026 Winter Olympics campaign, we re-tested the sticky bar’s gift array buttons to understand their impact on donor behavior.
In reviewing the original execution, we identified opportunities to improve how the sticky bar guided visitors toward giving—particularly around campaign value proposition and giving array amounts. We re-tested this approach to determine whether modifying the sticky bar could more effectively encourage gifts and to gather data needed to inform future fundraising tactics.
For this test, 50% of site traffic was shown the standard sticky bar experience featuring a single call-to-action button. The remaining 50% received a treatment version with three buttons mirroring the suggested gift amounts on the donation page, allowing visitors to engage with a giving option earlier in their journey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment shows that adding a gift array to the IMB website’s sticky bar significantly increased both visitor engagement and donor conversion. The treatment version—featuring three suggested gift amount buttons—outperformed the standard sticky bar by 448.4% in Clicks for All Traffic, with 99.9% statistical confidence.
These results suggest that introducing giving options earlier in the donor journey reduces friction and simplifies decision-making on the donation page—leading to more completed gifts.
Downstream Impact on Donations:
To understand how increased engagement translated into giving behavior, we analyzed downstream donation metrics. During the test period, the control version generated 1 gift, while the treatment generated 16 gifts, representing a 1,495% increase in transactions (100% LOC).
This confirms that the gift array not only drives more clicks but also meaningfully motivates visitors to complete a donation.
Gift Amount Performance:
We also evaluated which gift array buttons led to the most donations:
$25 Button: 6 gifts, all at $25
 	$50 Button: 3 gifts, all at $50
 	$100 Button: 3 gifts (2 at $100, 1 at $150)
 	More Options: 4 gifts, resulting in varied amounts ($20, $50, $50, $200)
Notably, most donors retained the amount they selected in the sticky bar through to the completion of the donation, reinforcing the value of early commitment.
Conclusion & Recommendation:
Overall, the gift array on the sticky bar effectively removes a key decision point from the donation page, leading to higher engagement and more completed gifts.
For future testing, IMB should continue exploring gift arrays and similar friction-reducing elements in high-visibility areas of the site. Ongoing iteration will help further optimize the giving experience and maximize donor conversion and revenue.

experiments.title: Does Increasing the Number of Appeal Links Boost Email Clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the # of donation appeal links for a campaign appeal email will achieve a higher number of clicks to the donation page because the additional links will reduce friction and capture reader attention at different moments of engagement within the email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2026 - 02/28/2026
Bible League Canada (BLC) is a ministry organization dedicated to placing Bibles and biblical resources into the hands of waiting believers in Canada and around the world. During one of their fundraising campaigns, we wanted to test whether increasing the number of appeal links in an email would help drive more visitors to the donation page.
We decided to test this in Email #2 of this campaign, which served as a key follow-up touchpoint. While the first email introduced the need, Email #2 was designed to reinforce urgency and convert engaged readers into donors. Because it targeted an already-warmed audience, it represented a meaningful opportunity to optimize click-through behavior and drive additional gifts.
This experiment asked: Would increasing the number of donation links within the body of the email increase clicks to the donation page? Or is a single plain-text link sufficient once donor motivation is established?
The Control version included one plain-text donation link placed within the email.
The Treatment version included the same plain-text donation link, but also hyperlinked two additional portions of body copy higher up in the email. These additional links directed to the same donation page and provided readers with more opportunities to click as they engaged with the message.
The language, story, and overall appeal copy did not change between versions. The only variable tested was the number and placement of donation links within the email.
We randomly split the Email #2 audience between the two versions and measured clicks to the donation page as the primary outcome. Clicks on non-donation links (such as social media icons or the organization logo) were excluded from our analysis. Click results were pulled from February 11 – 28.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test confirmed our hypothesis that increasing the number of appeal links within the email would cause a lift in clicks to the donation page.
The Control email generated 47 clicks, resulting in a 1.8% click-through rate. The Treatment generated 90 clicks, resulting in a 3.5% click-through rate. This means that adding additional appeal links in the email drove a 90.9% increase in clicks to the donation page, with a statistically significant 90.9% level of confidence.
When readers feel motivated to act, even briefly, having an immediate opportunity to click increases the likelihood of follow-through. The additional links likely reduced friction by allowing readers to click at the moment conviction or urgency peaked — instead of requiring them to scroll down to find the link.
Due to the donation sample size from this test, we were not able to validate any specific downstream impacts, such as differences in total gifts or revenue between versions. As a result, this test confirms increased giving intent (clicks), but does not yet confirm increased giving outcomes.
Further testing is needed to determine if this lift in clicks consistently translates to increased gifts and revenue, and also to confirm whether this effect holds across other campaign emails like a launch email or final day ask.
Our recommended next step is to test this hypothesis again across multiple emails within a fundraising campaign and evaluate not only clicks, but downstream conversion and revenue impact. Aggregating results across several sends will help determine whether this tactic should become a standard structural best practice for Bible League Canada’s email program.

experiments.title: How quantifying the time investment in a high urgency email appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe quantifying the time investment for donors will increase the click-through rate because more people will see it as within reach.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2025 - 12/18/2025
The IMB was running a donor survey as part of their year end campaign. They knew that people generally enjoyed giving their opinions, but wondered whether many thought it might take too long and therefore wouldn’t click.
The survey was short, but that couldn’t be seen in the email. They decided to run an experiment with a treatment that explicitly stated the survey would take “two minutes” to help donors answer the mental question: “Do I have time for this?” and see if it impacted click-through rate from the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that quantifying the time investment for donors by stating that the survey would only take "two minutes" led to a 6.2% increase in clickthrough rate from the email. This indicates that explicitly mentioning the survey's short duration positively affected recipients' decision to engage with it.
In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing the effectiveness of providing specific details, such as time investment, to reduce donors' perceived effort. This approach can increase engagement and ultimately improve the success of campaigns or initiatives designed to elicit responses from the target audience. Additionally, it is important to incorporate similar strategies across other communication channels to further enhance donor participation and interaction.

experiments.title: How including an image of the premium in a web asset donation ask impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the premium image for Today in the Word website visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because it visually reinforces the offer and increasing its perceived value to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Today in the Word’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding a premium image to the website Dear Reader.
Both the control and treatment included identical premium language; however, only the treatment featured the premium image.
The test ran for one and a half weeks, with traffic evenly split between both variants.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learning
The primary takeaway from this experiment is that adding a premium image to the Today in the Word website did not increase engagement or downstream donor metrics. Instead, the treatment featuring the premium image experienced a statistically valid 47% decrease in clicks compared to the control.
This decline extended to giving behavior. The control generated six gifts through the Dear Reader, while the treatment generated none—resulting in a 100% decrease in gifts at a 98% level of confidence.
Importantly, this outcome was consistent across devices. Clicks declined on mobile by 43% (valid), on desktop by 81% (valid), and on tablet by 21% (27% LOC). While desktop users responded most negatively to the image, the pattern indicates that users on all devices were less likely to click the CTA when the image was present.
Implications
These results suggest that the premium image may have distracted from the core message or failed to reinforce the value of the offer for this audience. Rather than increasing motivation, the visual element appears to have introduced friction at a critical decision point.
Next Steps
Future experimentation should approach visual elements with caution and intentionality. Continued testing of alternative imagery—or the removal of images altogether—will help identify what best supports clarity, engagement, and conversion. Ongoing analysis of engagement and donation data will remain essential to guiding optimizations that drive meaningful donor response.

Ended On: 01/27/2025
For Time of Grace, we send out emails to the housefile for TOG’s premium offers. For this email, we wanted to test how presenting a more direct email with shorter copy would affect click-through rate for their email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting a more direct email with shorter copy resulted in a significant increase in click-through rate for Time of Grace Ministry's premium offers. The treatment group saw a 153.9% increase in clicks compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 100.0%.
During this experiment, we also saw a valid 111% increase in donations.
This outcome indicates that users responded positively to the shorter, more direct messaging by being able to grasp the email's message quicker and focus on the call-to-action. In future experimentation, Time of Grace Ministry should consider using shorter and more direct copy in their emails to the housefile to potentially increase click-through rates. This strategy could lead to more effective communication with their audience and ultimately drive more conversions or engagement with their premium offers.

experiments.title: How removing an image in a fundraising direct appeal email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the image for a direct appeal email will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because it will simplify the email, reduce distractions, and focus the reader’s attention on the call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2024 - 11/25/2024
America Needs Fatima’s Fatima Statue Custodians travel across the country, bringing the statue of Our Lady of Fatima into homes to foster prayer, healing, and spiritual renewal. To sustain this mission, they launched a fundraising campaign to cover rising fuel costs and tested how an email image might impact engagement.
In a previous test, we found that removing an image of a Custodian visit significantly decreased clicks, suggesting that the image played a key role in engagement. However, we wanted to further explore whether this finding applied across different types of images.
For this follow-up test, we examined whether an image of the sender, Mr. Ferrera, an active Fatima Statue Custodian, standing with the statue of Our Lady, would help or hinder email engagement. The Control email included this image, while the Treatment removed it.
We hypothesized that removing the image would increase clicks by simplifying the email, reducing distractions, and keeping the reader focused on the call to action.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control email (with the image) and the other half receiving the Treatment email (without the image).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Surprisingly, removing the image of the sender led to a 59.6% increase in email clicks, with a 97% statistical level of confidence. This result contradicts the first test, where removing an image of a Custodian visit led to a significant decrease in clicks.

This suggests that not all images contribute equally to engagement—context and content matter. Several psychological principles may help explain this outcome:
Personal Connection vs. Mission Focus: While personal images can build trust, they can also shift focus away from the larger mission. The first test’s image reinforced the mission (showing a Custodian in action), whereas this test’s image may have felt less necessary.
 	Reader Expectations: Donors might be more compelled by an image that directly reinforces the impact of their gift (e.g., a Custodian at work) rather than one featuring the sender.
testing different image types
how different images—such as those of the people served or of Custodians in action—affect engagement and donor response

experiments.title: How shortening email copy for a content offer impacts non-donor signups and donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening email copy for non donors will achieve a change in content offer KPIs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2025 -
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and Non-Donor audiences. For this specific recirculation, we tested whether shorter, more concise email copy would impact clicks, email signups, and donation conversions.
This experiment result is for non-donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that shortening email copy for non-donors resulted in a significant increase in clicks across all traffic. The treatment group saw a 14.2% increase in clicks with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the change was not due to random chance.

Moreover, when looking at the downstream impacts of this test:
The treatment version had 6% more email signups, with an 80% level of confidence.
 	The treatment version showed a 50% increase in donations after email signup, when considering total emails deployed, with a 56% level of confidence (note that the donation sample size was small, however). When comparing this number for those who signed up and THEN donated, the treatment version had a 49% increase in donations with a 56% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a softer ask on a sticky bar impacts click rate and downstream donor metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the softer language on the sticky bar for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and downstream metrics because it leads to a larger pool of warm prospects who are more likely to donate once on the page to sign his card.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/26/2025
For Dr. Carson’s birthday this year, we tested the language on their website sticky bars to see which message resonated more with the audience: a direct ask like “Make my gift” or a softer invitation like “Sign the card.”
The test ran for about three weeks, with traffic split evenly between the two versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using softer language on the sticky bar for homepage visitors, such as "Sign the card", resulted in a significant increase in clicks compared to a direct ask like "Make my gift". The treatment version saw a 425.7% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the softer approach resonated better with the audience.
More importantly, though, we wanted to see the downstream metrics on this test to measure the true impact of it.
Control:
21 gifts, $1,594 $75 Average gift
Treatment:
31 gifts, $876$28 Average gift
These results indicated a 46.7% lift in transactions (82.8% LOC), which is encouraging to see, but they also indicate a substantial drop in average gift.
In future experimentation, we should continue to test the use of softer language and invitations in our calls to action. By creating a more inviting and inclusive message, we can attract a larger pool of warm prospects and potentially increase the likelihood of donations. It is important to remember that the tone and language used in our communications can have a significant impact on audience engagement and conversion rates. By leveraging these learnings, we can optimize our messaging to better connect with our audience and drive meaningful actions.

experiments.title: How using ease language impacts clicks in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using ease language for people who receive the email will achieve more clicks because they will be more encouraged to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
When asking donors to consider monthly giving, the way you frame the opportunity can make all the difference. In this test, the team focused on how to talk about ease. Rather than only highlighting the benefits of monthly giving, the copy emphasized how simple and straightforward it is to sign up as a recurring donor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language about the ease of becoming a monthly donor significantly increased clicks in the email. The treatment, which focused on the ease of becoming a monthly donor, saw a 67.4% increase in clicks for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 96.5%. This means the hypothesis was supported, showing that people were more encouraged to become a monthly donor when presented with language highlighting the ease of doing so.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of language on donor behavior. Donations like Dallas Theological Seminary can increase donor engagement and acquisition by incorporating ease-of-donation language in future appeals and communications. It is also crucial to analyze the impact of different messaging strategies on other metrics such as donation amount, donor retention, and overall campaign success. Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of using language that resonates with donors and simplifies the donation process to inspire action.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for donors can significantly increase the click-through rate. In this case, the treatment with the button resulted in a 59.2% increase in clicks compared to the control group with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

This result confirms the hypothesis that a clear, visually distinct call-to-action, such as a button, can reduce friction, draw the reader’s eye, and make it easier for people to take the intended next step.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of different call-to-action elements, such as buttons, in emails targeted to donors. By consistently analyzing and optimizing our email designs, we can maximize engagement and ultimately drive more conversions for The Heritage Foundation’s campaigns.

experiments.title: How including a button in an appeal email affects email click-through rates for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button that says “Double My Gift” will increase email click-through rate because it draws more attention than a plain-text hyperlink, creates a clearer and more compelling call to action, and reduces friction by making the next step more obvious and easier to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/01/2025
The Heritage Foundation conducted this experiment as part of their President’s Challenge matching gift campaign. This campaign was designed to drive donor engagement by offering a limited-time opportunity to double the impact of a donor’s gift through a matching challenge. The email in question was the first send in the campaign sequence, making it a high-leverage moment for influencing donor behavior and maximizing initial response rates.
Given the high stakes of the campaign’s early momentum and the importance of clearly communicating the match opportunity, the Heritage Foundation sought to test whether different call-to-action (CTA) formats would affect click-through and conversion rates.
The test was deployed via email, targeting the Heritage Foundation’s non-member list.
Both versions of the email—control and treatment—were linked to the same donation landing page but differed in their CTA presentation.
The experiment was driven by a hypothesis rooted in behavioral and conversion rate optimization principles: a visually prominent, action-oriented button might outperform a plain-text hyperlink in driving user action.
Key motivations included:
Improving clarity and visibility of the CTA: While the raw URL in the control version is technically functional, it lacks the immediate visual weight and action language of a button. The team suspected that this might cause the CTA to be overlooked or feel less urgent.
Reducing cognitive friction: A clear, concise button like “Double My Gift” directly mirrors the donor’s intent and may simplify decision-making.
Encouraging mobile-friendly design: Buttons often perform better on mobile devices where touch interaction is key, and plain-text links can be harder to tap.
This test is a good example of using micro-optimizations in email fundraising design to explore how seemingly small changes can have an outsized effect on user behavior and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button with clear and compelling language can significantly increase click-through rates in email campaigns. In this case, the treatment with the button saying “Double My Gift” outperformed the control with a plain-text hyperlink, resulting in a 42.0% increase in clicks for all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different elements of email design, such as CTAs, to optimize for user engagement and conversion rates. By focusing on creating clear, visually engaging CTAs that reduce cognitive friction and make the next steps obvious and easy to complete, organizations can increase the effectiveness of their email fundraising efforts. Additionally, considering the importance of mobile-friendly design in email campaigns, using buttons rather than plain-text links can further improve user experience and drive better results.

experiments.title: What is the impact of personalizing Agenda articles based on website history on Clicks and Opens?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing Immigration content in The Agenda for subscribers with Heritage website history related to crime, immigration, and national security will achieve a significant increase in the click/open rate because we personalizing content based of their previous engagement with heritage.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/22/2025
The membership team conducted a series of tests to create a personalized Agenda Email Newsletter based on the subscribers website history using Marketo data.
Two personalization groups were created based on keywords from subscribers Heritage website history. Group A – focused on subscribers with website history associated with the economy and Group B – focused on subscribers with website history associated with National Security, Crime, and Immigration.
Testing segments breakdown:
Group A – Control (25%)- received the “national security version” with articles related to the national security being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter
Group A (75%)- Treatment – received the “economy version” with articles related to the economy being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter
Group B (25%)- Control – received the “economy version” with articles related to the economy being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter,
Group B (75%)- Treatment – received the “national security version” with articles related to the national security being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter
In every test, the “National Security” personalization group outperformed the “Economy” group and so the metrics reported in this test only include results from Group B.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that prioritizing Immigration content in The Agenda for subscribers with Heritage website history related to crime, immigration, and national security significantly increases the click/open rate. The treatment group, which received the "national security version" with articles related to national security being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter, saw a 46.5% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group. This result was achieved with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating a strong significance in the impact of the personalized content.

In future experimentation, we should continue to personalize content based on subscribers' previous engagement with the Heritage website. By analyzing subscribers' website history and tailoring the content to their specific interests, we can enhance engagement and drive higher click-through rates. It is essential to continue testing different personalization strategies to understand what resonates best with our audience and refine our approach based on the results. This experiment demonstrates the effectiveness of personalized content in driving engagement and highlights the importance of catering to subscribers' individual interests to optimize performance.

experiments.title: How using a content offer instead of a direct ask in a dear reader affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free ebook in a dear reader for website visitors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides immediate value to the audience, aligns with their interests, and incentivizes engagement by presenting a tangible benefit in exchange for their attention, making the offer more appealing and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2025 - 04/08/2025
For MPR, we decided to optimize their article pages through the addition of a dear reader. But before rolling this out to all traffic, we wanted to test how a content offer vs a direct ask would impact the click-through rate.
The content offer used in this test was their Talking Sense e-Book.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a free ebook in a dear reader for website visitors significantly increased the click-through rate by 733.3% with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This suggests that providing immediate value to the audience and aligning with their interests can incentivize engagement and make the offer more appealing and actionable than a direct ask.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test different content offers and calls to action to optimize click-through rates on our website. It is essential to understand the preferences and behaviors of our audience to tailor our offers effectively. Additionally, we should consider implementing similar strategies across different channels to maximize engagement and drive conversions.

experiments.title: How copy and checkbox design drive GDPR consent opt-ins during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the GDPR consent process by using a single checkbox with clear privacy language will reduce friction for users and increase the opt-in rate for receiving future communications.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2025 - 04/24/2025
CitizenGO is a global community that uses online petitions and action alerts to defend life, family, and liberty. Their platform enables individuals to influence decision-makers worldwide by leveraging the collective support of like-minded citizens.
This experiment stems from a review of GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) requirements, which mandate that organizations obtain explicit consent from users before collecting or processing their personal data, including email addresses. As part of this compliance, it’s important to design petition sign-up forms in a way that ensures users are properly informed and opt in to receive future communications.
For this test, we are evaluating different designs for the GDPR consent checkbox on petition landing pages. The current design includes the following two options:
I want to know if this petition wins and how I can help other citizen petitions.
I don’t want to know how this petition or other important petitions are progressing.
The proposed test will compare three variations of the GDPR consent checkbox:
Control – The current checkbox design to track opt-ins.
Treatment 1 – A simplified design with a single checkbox and long privacy copy above it, providing detailed information in accordance with GDPR requirements.
Treatment 2 – A “Christian Lawyer” style design featuring a concise statement next to the checkbox, with a link to additional privacy policy details placed below the petition submission button.
The test will be run on the German petition pages (launching inline with their Stop Digital Euro Campaign) with traffic split evenly across all three variations. This experiment will help determine which design leads to higher consent opt-in rates while maintaining GDPR compliance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test ran for 1 week and we observed significant differences in opt-in clicks across the different treatments. This result is parallel to what we saw when we ran this test on Hazteoir petition pages.

Results:
Control: 14,099 samples | 535 opt-in clicks | 6,707 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 1: 14,274 samples | 1,272 opt-in clicks | 6,667 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 2: 14,155 samples | 1,680 opt-in clicks | 6,808 email acquisitions
137.7%
213.7% increase
100% Level of Confidence (LoC)
Control conversion rate: ~47.6%
 	Treatment 1 conversion rate: ~46.7%
 	Treatment 2 conversion rate: ~48.1%
Recommendation:
Treatment 2

experiments.title: How adding a sticky navigation bar on mobile affects clicks on Donate button
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a stacked navigation bar for mobile users will achieve an increase in clicks to the donate button because adding that stacked bar would increase clarity among the buttons and look cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2025 - 05/20/2025
For CaringBridge, their mobile navigation bar consisted of multiple buttons where there was minimal space and all the buttons looked very small and stuck together. For this test, we wanted to change the layout of the navigation bar. We added a space above the original navigation bar and added the donate and search button on the extra space above the nav bar. The stacked part with the search button and donate button was sticky, so users could see it while scrolling through. We wanted to test if doing this would affect clicks to the donate button for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

 	The navigation search button also saw a substantial increase in clicks (676% increase). This suggests that improving the visibility and usability of buttons in the navigation bar can drive higher interaction rates across multiple features.

In future experimentation, we should consider the following:

 	Continue to prioritize the visibility and accessibility of key features, such as the donate button, by strategically placing them in the navigation bar where they are easily noticeable and clickable.
 	Use A/B testing and other experimentation methods to iteratively optimize the layout and design of the mobile navigation bar to enhance user experience and drive engagement with important features.
 	Consider applying similar strategies to other areas of the website or app to improve overall user interaction and achieve organizational goals, such as increasing donations or promoting specific actions.

experiments.title: How copy and checkbox design drive GDPR consent opt-ins
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the GDPR consent process by using a single checkbox with clear privacy language will reduce friction for users and increase the opt-in rate for receiving future communications.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2025 - 03/12/2025
This experiment stems from a review of GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) requirements, which mandate that organizations obtain explicit consent from users before collecting or processing their personal data, including email addresses. As part of this compliance, it’s important to design petition sign-up forms in a way that ensures users are properly informed and opt in to receive future communications.
For this test, we are evaluating different designs for the GDPR consent checkbox on petition landing pages. The current design includes the following two options:
I want to know if this petition wins and how I can help other citizen petitions.
I don’t want to know how this petition or other important petitions are progressing.
The proposed test will compare three variations of the GDPR consent checkbox:
Control – The current checkbox design to track opt-ins.
Treatment 1 – A simplified design with a single checkbox and long privacy copy above it, providing detailed information in accordance with GDPR requirements.
Treatment 2 – A design featuring a concise statement next to the checkbox, with a link to additional privacy policy details placed below the petition submission button.
The test will be run with traffic split evenly across all three variations. This experiment will help determine which design leads to higher consent opt-in rates while maintaining GDPR compliance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test ran for 1 week, and we observed significant differences in opt-in clicks across the different treatments.

 	Control: 10,980 samples, 481 opt-in clicks, 5,599 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 1 (Long): 11,262 samples, 1,094 opt-in clicks, 5,718 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 2 (Christian): 11,195 samples, 1,387 opt-in clicks, 5,762 email acquisitions

The Treatment 1 (Long) increased opt-in clicks by 121.7% at a 100% Level of Confidence (LoC), while Treatment 2 (Christian) showed an even stronger increase of 182.8% at a 100% LoC. Both treatments outperformed the control, where the opt-in clicks were much lower.

Despite these significant increases in opt-in clicks, there was no impact on email acquisitions. This is fine, as the goal of the test was to increase consent to the terms (via checking the box) rather than directly driving email sign-ups. The conversion rates for email acquisitions were as follows:

 	Control: ~4.38%
 	Treatment 1 (Long): ~4.13%
 	Treatment 2 (Christian): ~4.17%

This indicates that while the treatments successfully increased opt-in clicks, they did not lead to a notable change in email sign-ups. Given the strong results from Treatment 2 (Christian), we recommend implementing this treatment across all petition pages. This approach had the highest increase in opt-in clicks and aligns with the goal of improving user engagement with the petition.

experiments.summary:

experiments.keyLearnings:

2. The treatment group experienced a 43.6% increase in clicks compared to the control group, showing the effectiveness of mentioning the premium as an incentive for donations.

3. The statistical significance level of 98.6% provides high confidence in the results of the experiment meaning that the treatment should be used in future campaigns.

Implications for Future Experimentation:

1. Future experiments should continue to explore the impact of premiums or incentives on donor behavior.

2. It is recommended to consistently test different types of premiums and messaging strategies to optimize the effectiveness of incentives in driving donations.

3. Consider implementing similar tactics in other fundraising campaigns or donation drives to leverage the success observed in this experiment.

4. Continuously monitor and analyze the results of experiments to refine strategies and improve performance in fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How should we layout the episode pages?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the featured resource CTA higher on the page for the broadcast episode pages will achieve more traffic getting to the donation page because the CTA is more visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2025 - 05/02/2025
Each day tons of people go to their broadcast pages to pick through the episodes that they would like to listen to. On the episode pages you can watch the show, read more about the guests, and then towards the bottom of the page you have the opportunity to get the resources that they talked about on the show.
The resources offer leds the user to a donation page where they can get the resource with a gift. We thought, if we rearrange the page to make the resource CTA more at the top we could drive more traffic to the donation page and get more transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the featured resource CTA higher on the broadcast episode pages significantly increased the traffic to the donation page. The treatment group, which had the CTA placed higher on the page, saw an impressive 18.9% increase in CTA button clicks for all traffic compared to the control group. This difference was statistically significant at a confidence level of 100.0%, indicating that the results are very reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the placement of CTAs on the page to maximize engagement and conversion rates. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further analyze user behavior to understand why the higher placement of the CTA led to more clicks. This insight can help in optimizing the design and placement of CTAs across other pages on the website as well.

Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of CTA placement in driving user action. By utilizing this learning and continuing to test and optimize CTAs, Focus on the Family can further improve user engagement and increase donations on their broadcast episode pages.

experiments.title: How comparing a curiosity-driven subject line to an exclusivity-based subject line to determine impact on opens and engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a vague, curiosity-driven subject line (“Here’s your first look”) instead of an exclusivity-based subject line (“Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide”) for people viewing a content offer email in their inbox will achieve an increase in email opens because the curiosity gap encourages recipients to open the email to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/17/2025
Luther Seminary develops useful ebooks and other digital resources that help their audience deepen their faith and knowledge of the Bible. They recently created a new ebook about the book of Exodus and wanted to send it to their housefile email audience.
We wanted to know what kind of subject line would drive more opens and clicks for this content offer email, so we decided to run an A/B test.
The Control subject line used exclusivity messaging: “Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide.” The Treatment subject line was shorter and designed to spark curiosity: “Here’s your first look.”
We hypothesized that the curiosity-driven subject line would generate more opens by enticing recipients to learn more.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control subject line and the other half receiving the Treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment confirmed that a curiosity-driven subject line can significantly increase email opens. The Treatment subject line ("Here's your first look") outperformed the Control ("Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide") by increasing opens 9.4%, with a 100% statistical level of confidence.

The success of the curiosity-based subject line aligns with the Curiosity Gap Theory, which suggests that people feel compelled to seek out missing information when presented with an incomplete or intriguing statement. Unlike the Control, which relied on exclusivity messaging, the Treatment sparked interest without revealing too much—prompting recipients to open the email to learn more.

However, while opens increased, click rates remained the same (~2.2% for both versions). This suggests that while curiosity successfully drove more people to open the email, it did not necessarily increase engagement with the content inside. A possible explanation is that the Treatment subject line created intrigue but did not set clear expectations about the offer, leading some recipients to open the email without a strong intention to engage further.

To build on this learning, future tests should explore:
Refining curiosity-driven subject lines – Testing variations that maintain intrigue while hinting more explicitly at the content's value.
 	Combining curiosity with faith-based language – Testing curiosity-driven phrasing with biblical references to see if it enhances both opens and clicks. Update: We ran a follow-up experiment to test curiosity-based language against a subject line featuring a specific biblical reference to see which approach is more effective at engaging faith-driven audiences.
 	Aligning email content with subject line expectations – Ensuring that the email itself delivers on the curiosity generated in the inbox, potentially increasing clicks.

Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/25/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
One area is the section on the homepage that directs people to consume PTV content by watching, listening or reading. For this area we decided to test against the carousel version versus a static 3 column layout. Our hypothesis is that the static layout will increase engagement because the effort of waiting or scrolling is not needed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted to compare a carousel layout versus a static 3 column layout on the homepage of Pathway to Victory's website showed that the static layout did not perform as well as the carousel in terms of engagement, with a 2.4% decrease in clicks for all traffic.

We also observed a 9% decrease in time on site for the treatment version.

experiments.title: How does an additional Share to Socials prompt impact share clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a Share to Socials prompt for authors after they’ve just made a post will achieve an increase in Share Clicks because it presents this option in an intentional way, directly after an action that is related and relevant.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2025 - 03/03/2025
Currently, CaringBridge authors have a “Share” prompt in their flywheel/manage my page options. We hypothesized that presenting this pop-up prompt at the end of another relevant experience, creating post, would increase overall Share Clicks. Posting to their feed is an action that can then relevantly followed up with a share to socials—improving overall metrics by introducing another way to make this action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Timing is crucial: Presenting prompts or options to users immediately after they have completed a related action can significantly increase engagement. In this case, asking authors to share their post after they have just made one led to a significant increase in Share Clicks.

2. Relevance is key: Making the prompt or option relevant to the user's current experience can make them more likely to engage. By offering a "Share to Socials" prompt after a user has just made a post, the prompt felt intentional and related, resulting in a higher engagement rate.

3. Testing and iteration are essential: Running A/B experiments can help determine the effectiveness of new features or prompts. By continuously testing and iterating based on the results, we can optimize user engagement and overall metrics.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the impact of presenting prompts or options at strategic times, such as immediately after a related action. Additionally, we should always consider the relevance of the prompt to the user's current experience to increase engagement. By following these key learnings, we can continue to improve user interactions and drive desired outcomes for Caringbridge.

experiments.title: How a story-based faux-forward email performed against a standard faux-forward email on Giving Tuesday

rows.name: Control - Faux Forward with Standard Direct Appeal
Treatment - Faux Forward with a Story

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a donor testimonial in a faux-forward for a direct ask email will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because personal stories from donors can build trust, create emotional connection, and motivate action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/03/2024
During the critical Giving Tuesday fundraising period, Human Coalition wanted to test the effectiveness of their Giving Tuesday PM email communication strategy. Our traditional approach has been to use a faux-forward email, where the PM message included a “forwarded” copy of the AM email, with a brief added note from the person forwarding it.
For this experiment, we wanted to test the faux-forward against a treatment email that still kept the forwarded AM content but instead focused on a testimonial from two Human Coalition donors sharing their personal connection to the mission.
The goal of this test was to determine if focusing on a donor testimonial rather than reiterating the AM email’s message would lead to higher engagement and more conversions. Our theory was that using donor stories would drive more trust and emotional connection to help inspire the reader to take action, while the repeated messaging of the faux-forward strategy could be leading to fatigue or disengagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that personal donor testimonials were far more effective in driving engagement than the traditional faux-forward approach. The Treatment email, which focused on a heartfelt testimonial from two Human Coalition donors, saw a 41.2% increase in clicks to the donation page, suggesting that emotional connection and social proof are powerful motivators for potential donors.
The results indicate that people are more likely to take action when they hear directly from fellow supporters who share their values and experiences. Testimonies not only provide social proof but also humanize the organization, fostering a deeper sense of community and purpose. This highlights the psychological principle of social proof, where individuals are influenced by the actions and opinions of others, especially those they can relate to.
On the other hand, the Control email, which repeated the morning’s message in a faux-forward format, did not resonate as strongly. This suggests that while reminders can be helpful, they may not always be as compelling as personalized, story-driven content, which has a stronger emotional pull.
Moving forward, it will be beneficial to incorporate more donor testimonials and personal stories into email campaigns, especially in critical fundraising periods like Giving Tuesday, to build trust and drive higher engagement.

experiments.title: How a testimonial email from a donor performed against a faux-forward email during a fundraising appeal

rows.name: Control - Faux Forward Email from Luther Staff
Treatment - Testimonial Email from a Donor

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donor testimonial email from a real donor for people receiving a campaign appeal email will achieve an increase in email clicks to the donation page because personal stories can increase social proof and encourage readers to join them by becoming a donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/03/2024
During the critical calendar year-end fundraising period, Luther Seminary wanted to test the effectiveness of their Giving Tuesday PM email communication strategy. The traditional approach has been to use a faux-forward email, where the PM message includes a “forwarded” copy of the AM email, with a brief added note from the person forwarding it.
For this experiment, we tested the faux-forward strategy against a Treatment email that replaced the forwarded content with a testimonial from a real donor, Greg, sharing his personal reasons for supporting Luther Seminary. The goal of this test was to determine whether focusing on a donor testimonial would lead to higher engagement and more conversions compared to the repetitive messaging of the faux-forward email.
Our hypothesis was that donor stories would create stronger emotional connections and trust, motivating the reader to take action and contribute to Luther Seminary’s mission. We believed that a personal endorsement from a peer would resonate more deeply with recipients, leading to increased clicks to the donation page, while the faux-forward approach might contribute to disengagement or message fatigue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we found that the Treatment email, featuring a testimonial from a donor (Greg), led to a -39% decrease in clicks to the donation page, with a 97.9% statistical level of confidence. This result was unexpected, as we hypothesized that the personal donor story would increase trust and emotional connection, ultimately driving more clicks to the donation page.

One major factor contributing to this decrease could be the -21.8% decrease in email opens for the Treatment email, with a 100% statistical level of confidence. This drop in opens suggests that the unfamiliar sender, Greg Myhr, may have impacted the email's ability to capture the recipient’s attention. People are more likely to open emails from familiar senders, a concept known as sender recognition or trust transfer. In this case, emails from Luther Seminary staff may have felt more familiar and trustworthy, prompting more recipients to open the email.

Psychologically, cognitive fluency plays a role here: people are more likely to engage with content that feels easy, familiar, and comfortable. When the email is from a known and trusted source (like Luther Seminary staff), recipients may feel more at ease engaging with the content. When the email comes from an unfamiliar source (like a donor), it may subconsciously signal uncertainty or a potential risk, causing people to hesitate or overlook the email altogether.

Additionally, while social proof can be a powerful motivator, it needs to be framed in a way that maximizes its impact. Although Greg’s testimonial aimed to leverage the principle of social proof by showing how a fellow donor supports Luther Seminary, the email’s unfamiliar sender might have diminished its credibility. In-group bias suggests that people tend to trust and engage more with others who belong to their perceived social group or network. Because Greg was an outsider to the recipients (compared to the staff sender), the emotional pull of his testimonial may have been weakened.

Another contributing factor could be cognitive overload. The Treatment email included a personal story from Greg, which while emotionally powerful, might have added a layer of complexity or unfamiliarity that decreased its effectiveness compared to the more straightforward faux-forward email (the Control). The faux-forward email, which came from a familiar source, was simpler and less cognitively demanding for the reader, making it easier to process and act upon.

This suggests that while donor testimonials are effective in fostering connection and trust, the familiarity of the sender is crucial in determining how much attention and engagement the email receives. If the recipient doesn't recognize or trust the sender immediately, they may be less likely to open the email and, as a result, less likely to engage with the content, including the donation link.

experiments.title: How a personalized sender impacts open and click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personalized sender for Franciscan University’s emails will achieve an increase in opens and click-thru rate because it humanizes the entire interaction—addressing the reader by name and writing from the perspective of a real person. because it humanizes the entire interaction—addressing the reader by name and writing from the perspective of a real person.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2025
Historically, the majority of Franciscan University’s email have come from the organization itself. They do not address the reader by name with a salutation, are written in a third-person tone, and do not come from a specific sender at the University. We hypothesized that personalizing this experience for the reader: addressing them by name in the opening, sending from a real person, and humanizing the email copy so it read like an email rather than a newsletter would increase opens and click-thru rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that personalizing the sender for Franciscan University's emails had a significant impact on the open rates, resulting in a 33.5% increase in opens for all traffic with a 100.0% level of statistical confidence. This outcome suggests that humanizing the email interaction by addressing the reader by name and sending from a real person resonated positively with the recipients and encouraged them to engage more with the emails.

Additionally, our second hypothesis is that this would increase click-thru rate as well as opens. While just below statistical validity, Franciscan also saw a 12% increase in click-thru rate with a 93.9% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is advisable to continue personalizing the sender for Franciscan University's emails to maintain or even further improve the open and click-thru rates. However, it may be worthwhile to determine whether this approach results in the same effect on Faith and Reason emails—which users are more accustomed to receiving from the organization itself. Regularly testing and analyzing different tactics will provide valuable insights for optimizing email communications and achieving the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How underlining post titles increases clicks to single-post page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that underlining post titles for users on an author profile will achieve an increase in clicks to the single-post page and by extension Tribute Gifts because it directs more traffic to a widget that, otherwise, users are not aware how to get to.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2025 - 02/10/2025
We hypothesized that in the current experience, users are not aware that they can click the titles of an Author’s Post—leading them to the single-post page view. To test this hypothesis, we wanted to observe the impact of underlining this title in the treatment, more clearly denoting that it was clickable. This would have two potential impacts. First, it could help improve the user experience by allowing them to read longer posts in a designated window versus in-feed. Second, it would increase traffic to the single-page view. On this page, they are presented with a Tribute Widget (16003). We hypothesized that directing more traffic to this widget would also increase donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that underlining post titles on an author profile significantly increased the number of clicks to the single-post page. This successfully led to a substantial increase in traffic to the single-post page. Confirming that in the current experience, users are not aware that the titles can be clicked. However, this did not by extension lead to an increase in Tribute widget gifts as hypothesized.

When considering the user journey, these results can be understood better. While conversion rate on this widget is typically high for JEN traffic (those clicking from an email and going to the post) it may not convert as highly for traffic who is coming to the single-post page from the user's profile. In the latter, they are already shown tribute widgets and asks on the author's profile, seeing another on the single-post page doesn't necessarily inspire them to give on its own. Unlike JEN traffic, which is clicking from an email where no ask is present and arriving on a page with an ask.

However, what these results underscore is the potential importance of increasing traffic while also optimizing the widget. If more users arrived on this page AND were presented with a unique ask, it could potentially convert that traffic into donors.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a CTA button instead of hyperlinked text for email subscribers will achieve an increase in click-through rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2022 - 06/03/2022
Established in 1996, Pathway to Victory serves as the broadcast ministry of Dr. Robert Jeffress and the First Baptist Church of Dallas, Texas. Part of the ministry’s digital offerings includes a podcast hosted by Dr. Jeffress. This experiment involved driving traffic to the podcast through an email that promoted the availability of the podcast on Apple’s platform. experiments.keyLearnings: Using a logo and CTA button, instead of hyperlinked text, drove a 133% higher click-through rate. Since this email was more promotional in nature, we hypothesize this was because people were able to easily subscribe to the podcast through a one-click button (since most people subscribe to a podcast through their mobile device.

experiments.title: How a tooltip on Ways to Help impacts clicks and Gift Card setups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tooltip describing what step one should take for authors will achieve an increase in Gift Card setups because adding that tooltip will give clarity on what the user should do and help reduce confusion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2025 - 01/31/2025
CaringBridge has the option for authors to set up a gift card so that people visiting their page can send the author a gift card as a way to help. For this test, we tried adding a tooltip that focused on what the user should do and specifying what the option is used for.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a tooltip describing what step the user should take for setting up a gift card for authors led to a significant increase in the Save button clicked for gift card setups on the website. The treatment group experienced a 363.9% increase in Save button clicked for gift cards on the What They Heard page for all traffic, with a statistical significance level of 100.0%.
Furthermore, the Tooltip CTA button clicked also saw a significant increase of 1,383.8% with 100% statistical confidence. This indicates that the tooltip providing clarity on what the user should do helped reduce confusion and encourage more users to set up gift cards.
In future experiments, it is important to continue emphasizing the importance of providing clear instructions and guidance for users to navigate the website effectively. This includes using tooltips or other visual cues to assist users in understanding the steps they need to take. Additionally, the success of this experiment highlights the value of testing different strategies for improving user experience and conversion rates. By continuing to experiment and iterate on website design and functionalities, we can further optimize the user experience and achieve better results.

experiments.title: How using a content offer instead of a direct ask in an article interstitial affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free ebook for website visitors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides immediate value to the audience, aligns with their interests, and incentivizes engagement by presenting a tangible benefit in exchange for their attention, making the offer more appealing and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/08/2025 - 01/08/2025
MPR has direct ask interstitials on its article pages throughout its website. They wanted to see how a content offer would affect click-through rate on the interstitial so they tested advertising their Talking Sense ebook for 50% of traffic to see.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment:

1. Offering a free ebook as a content offer can significantly increase the click-through rate on interstitials.

2. The treatment group, which received the ebook offer, experienced a 93.7% increase in clicks compared to the control group.

3. The results were statistically significant with a 100.0% confidence level, indicating that the ebook offer had a clear impact on user behavior.

**It's important to note that the test did not track email acquisition or donor conversion so we do not know the impact on those two metrics.

How to use these learnings in future experimentation:

1. Consider using content offers, such as ebooks, as incentives to encourage user engagement and click-throughs.

2. Continue to test different types of content offers to see which ones resonate best with the audience.

3. Use A/B testing and statistical significance to measure the effectiveness of different strategies and make data-driven decisions for future campaigns.

4. Incorporate learnings from this experiment into overall marketing and engagement strategies to optimize user interactions and drive desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How using a more provocative title for the subject of a survey impacts Google ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an more provocative name for the Administrative State for survey Google ad viewers will achieve an increase in clicks on the ad because the term “Deep State” invokes stronger emotions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
The Heritage Foundation was running a “Stop the Administrative State” survey and promoting through Google ads. In an attempt to increase click rates on the ads, they wanted to test whether using the more provocative term “Deep State” in place of the more straightforward “Administrative State” survey title would achieve this effect because people would be more provoked to take action against the subject when referred to as a more negative term.
The control’s Google ad image and copy options, including 8 headline and 5 description options, referred to the Administrative State, while the treatment’s ad image and copy referred to the Deep State, and both received 50% of traffic. In addition, the control led to survey landing page with “Administrative State” language, and the treatment led to a survey page with “Deep State” language for continuity.
This test ran for 3 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using the term "Deep State" in Google ads instead of the more straightforward "Administrative State" led to a significant decrease in clicks. Contrary to the hypothesis, the more provocative term did not invoke stronger emotions or prompt more action from viewers.

In addition to click rate, the treatment also saw a 42.3% decrease in email acquisitions on the survey at 100% level of confidence, and an invalid 2.7% decrease in donor acquisition rate (6.82% level of confidence). The email acquisition results also support the same thought that the more provocative term did not invoke stronger emotions and action from viewers.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the language and terminology used in promotional materials. While provocative terms may sometimes generate interest, they can also backfire and deter potential participants. It is essential to test different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with the target audience before implementing them widely. Additionally, it is crucial to always track and analyze the results of experiments to draw meaningful conclusions and inform future decision-making.

experiments.title: How beginning a high urgency appeal email with a story impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that beginning an email appeal with a story for CYE Email 4 recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because the reader will be immediately engaged and drawn in by the narrative therefore increasing the reader retention.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For Email 4 of The Navigators CYE campaign, we opted to test into removing a personal reflection from the beginning of an appeal email.
We believe that starting the email with the story directly will immediately engage the reader by drawing them into a compelling narrative, leading to increased reader retention and higher click-through rates.
This approach bypasses the personal reflection, which may delay the email’s emotional hook, ensuring the audience connects more quickly with the mission and impact of the Navigator staff.
The treatment was sent to 50% of the Email 4 recipients.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Beginning an email appeal with a story for CYE Email 4 recipients resulted in a 44.0% increase in clicks compared to starting with a personal reflection.

2. The treatment showed a high level of statistical confidence at 97.4%, indicating that the results are reliable and significant.

3. By immediately engaging the reader with a compelling narrative, the treatment increased reader retention and click-through rates.

Future Experimentation:

1. Consider incorporating storytelling at the beginning of email appeals for other campaigns to enhance reader engagement and drive desired actions.

2. Test different types of stories, such as success stories, impact stories, or personal narratives, to determine which resonates best with the audience.

3. Continue to prioritize testing and data analysis to identify effective strategies for engaging donors and supporters in email communication.

By leveraging the success of this experiment and applying the key learnings to future campaigns, The Navigators can continue to optimize their email appeals for increased impact and engagement.

experiments.title: How testing an organic single image ad creative against a carousel ad impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the utilization of carousel ads for prospective donors will achieve an increase in click-though rates because the multi-imagery and story-telling aspects of the carousel ads will will provide a more comprehensive and compelling story, allowing donors to see the tangible impact of their support.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2025
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit serving children and families for over one hundred years. Their top donor acquisition campaign has been their Shoes for Orphan Souls (SOS) campaign.
For this experiment, we tested an organic-style direct appeal ad against a carousel ad telling a personal story of a child receiving shoes, from the child’s perspective. The experiment aims to determine which ad format is more effective in driving donor action. The carousel ad utilized the image of a child on the first card, and used organic-style ads for the remaining cards, telling the child’s personal story.
Our hypothesis is that the carousel direct appeal ad featuring a narration of a child’s personal story receiving their shoes will outperform the organic-style direct appeal ad in engagement because showcasing multiple images will provide a more comprehensive and compelling story, allowing prospective engagers to see the tangible impact of their support.
In addition, by presenting a child benefiting from donations with the utilization of their personal story, the carousel ad may create a stronger emotional connection and drive more conversions by demonstrating the broader impact of giving.
We set this test up on Facebook as an A/B test so that 50% of our audience would see the control ads and the other 50% would see the treatment ads. The control campaign had the organic style single-image ad, and the treatment campaign had the carousel multi-image ads. Both campaigns had the same budget and audience targeting, and the test ran for 15 days on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the organic-style direct appeal ad outperformed the carousel ad featuring a child's personal story in driving donor action, as evidenced by a 31.1% decrease in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This means that the single-image ad was more effective in engaging the audience and motivating them to take action compared to the multi-image carousel ad.

In addition, it is important to note that the organic-style direct appeal ad significantly outperformed the carousel ad in both donor response and cost efficiency. The organic ad generated 18 gifts with an ROI of 0.61 and a $63 gross cost per donor, whereas the carousel ad resulted in only 3 gifts, an ROI of 0.16, and a $341 cost per donor. These results suggest that a singular, emotionally compelling message with a focused visual may more effectively drive conversions than a multi-image format that disperses attention.

While the data does suggest that prioritizing organic-style direct appeals will maximize donor engagement and cost efficiency, in future campaigns, it would be beneficial to continue testing different ad formats and messaging to identify the most effective strategies for driving donor engagement and action.

experiments.title: How using ease language impacts clicks in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using ease language for people who receive the email will achieve more clicks because they will be more encouraged to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
DTS was sending an appeal during their calendar year end campaign. The goal of this appeal was to acquire new monthly donors. We wanted to test how using language about the ease of becoming a monthly donor impacts clicks in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language about the ease of becoming a monthly donor significantly increased clicks in the email. The treatment, which focused on the ease of becoming a monthly donor, saw a 67.4% increase in clicks for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 96.5%. This means that the hypothesis was supported, showing that people were more encouraged to become a monthly donor when presented with language highlighting the ease of doing so.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of language on donor behavior. By incorporating ease of donation language in future appeals and communications, nonprofits like Dallas Theological Seminary can potentially increase donor engagement and acquisition. It is also crucial to analyze the impact of different messaging strategies on other metrics such as donation amount, donor retention, and overall campaign success. Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of using language that resonates with donors and simplifies the donation process to inspire action.

experiments.title: How rotating a Ways to Help card in the Tribute Widget Impacts Clicks and Tribute Gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that rotating Tribute Widget that displays a Ways to Help card 50% of the time for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in Send a Gift card traffic while having minimal impact on the tribute widget because the variety of calls to action will encourage users to explore other ways to give while also not presenting them with the same Tribute ask 100% of the time.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2024 - 12/02/2024
On CaringBrige’s Tribute Widget, we hypothesized that having this card alternate (showing a Ways to Help gift card opportunity) 50% of the time would result in an increase of Send a Gift card traffic, while also having minimal impact on the Tribute Gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:

1. **Increased Engagement:** The variation that displayed the Ways to Help gift card opportunity 50% of the time resulted in a significant increase in Send a Gift Card clicked for All Traffic by 41.8%. This indicates that offering a variety of calls to action can encourage users to explore different ways of giving.

2. **Positive Impact on Ways to Help Clicks:** The experiment also led to a 153% increase in the Ways to Help button in the menu clicked, a 3,457% increase in the Ways to Help widget CTA button clicked, and a 418% increase in Send a Gift Card clicked (src=widget). This demonstrates that featuring different giving options can drive higher engagement.

3. **Decrease in Tribute Donations:** The experiment resulted in a 398% decrease in Donations - Tribute clicked. While this may seem like a negative outcome, it indicates that users were more inclined to explore other giving opportunities beyond the Tribute widget.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the impact of rotating calls to action on user engagement. By offering a variety of giving options and monitoring their performance, we can optimize the user experience and maximize the impact of our fundraising efforts. Additionally, we should explore ways to further personalize the calls to action based on user behavior and preferences to drive even higher engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How shortening email copy for a content offer impacts non-donor signups and donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening email copy for non donors will achieve a change in content offer KPIs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2024 - 09/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation, we wanted to test whether shorter, more-to-the-point email copy impacted clicks and email signups, as well as donation conversions.
This experiment result is for non-donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that shortening email copy for non-donors led to a significant increase in clicks for all traffic. The treatment group saw a 14.2% increase in clicks with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the change was not due to random chance.
Moreover, when looking at downstream impacts of this test:
The treatment version had 6% more email signups, with an 80% level of confidenceThe treatment version had a 50% increase in donations post email-signup when looking at total emails deployed, with a 56% level of confidence (donation sample size was small, however). When comparing this number for those who signed up and THEN donated, the treatment version had a 49% increase in donations with a 56% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a clickable button to a high-urgency sticky bar impacts donor conversions and clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a clickable button CTA for users on the CaringBridge site will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it makes the “next step” clear to the reader, rather than relying on the user to figure out that the underlined text is a hyperlink.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/13/2024
During CaringBridge’s high-urgency appeal, we hypothesized that adding a clickable button with a clear CTA to the donation page would improve clicks and downstream donor conversion as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a clickable button with a clear call-to-action (CTA) to the donation page resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which received the clickable button CTA, saw a 287.6% increase in donations compared to the control group. Furthermore, the results were statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating that the improvement was not due to chance.

In future experimentation, we should continue to prioritize clear CTAs and user-friendly design elements to guide our audience towards taking the desired action. This experiment highlights the importance of making the "next step" clear to the reader, as it can have a substantial impact on donor conversion rates. Going forward, we should consider testing different variations of CTAs, buttons, and other design elements to continuously optimize the donation page and enhance the user experience.
