experiments.title: How a personal ask to match a gift amount affects overall conversions in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal ask to match a gift for donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because  it creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, especially when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/04/2024
The Navigators wanted to test how a more personal approach to asking to match a gift on Giving Tuesday would affect overall conversion rates including clickthrough rate from the email to donation page and donation conversion rate.
They tested this concept within the four emails they sent on Giving Tuesday. The control group received the same four email and the treatment group received the same treatment emails.
The control emails presented the matching gift by stating a generous donor has offered to match all gifts up the gift amount like below –
This is vital and exciting work! And we have a generous donor who agrees and who has committed $100,000 as a matching challenge to encourage others of like heart to show their generosity today on Giving Tuesday.
Whereas the treatment emails presented the matching gift by stating there was a generous donor who wanted to personally match their gift like below –
This is vital and exciting work! And we have a generous donor who would like to encourage you to give personally, [First name] through a matching challenge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a personal ask to match a gift for donors significantly increases the clickthrough rate from the email to the donation page. The treatment group, which received the personalized matching gift ask, saw a 57.0% increase in clicks compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, demonstrating that the treatment was more effective in driving donor engagement.
Additionally, when comparing clicks from the email to donations on the donation page, we saw a 30.6% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 99% level of confidence. But we believe these numbers to be skewed since the treatment sent so much more traffic to the donation page. Therefore we calculated the email delivered to donation conversion rate and saw a 9% increase in donor conversion rate with a 67.7% level of confidence.
Although those results did not validate, we believe this test to have an overall positive impact as it drove so much more traffic to the donation page.
In future experimentation, The Navigators should consider implementing personalized asks for matching gifts in their communications to donors. This approach creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact. By offering donors the opportunity to double their support through matching, organizations can potentially increase donor engagement and achieve higher conversion rates. It is important to continue testing and optimizing personalized approaches to matching gift asks to further enhance donor response and maximize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How focusing copy in a non-donor email on a specific course being promoted impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing copy on a course for a non donor audience will achieve a change in content offer performance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/19/2024 - 09/19/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of the Blueprint for America course, we wanted to see whether a variant of an email that focused on the course specifically would have an impact on sign ups and donations. This is the experiment for the non-donor version of this email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that focusing the copy on a course for a non-donor audience did not lead to an increase in performance for the Blueprint for America course offer. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in clicks compared to the control group.

Therefore, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and focus of content offers when targeting different audience segments. In this case, it may be more effective to highlight different aspects of the course or offer to non-donor audiences in order to drive engagement and conversion.

For future experimentation, it is crucial to test different messaging strategies and closely monitor the performance metrics to identify what resonates best with the target audience. By continuously testing and analyzing results, we can optimize our approach to content offers and maximize the impact on sign-ups and donations.

experiments.title: How focusing copy in a donor email on a specific course being promoted impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on a course for donors will achieve a change in offer performance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/19/2024 - 09/19/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of the Blueprint for America course, we wanted to see whether a variant of an email that focused on the course specifically would have an impact on sign ups and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that focusing on a course specifically for donors did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing sign-ups and donations. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence.

What's even more interesting is looking at this experiment from a full funnel perspective.

In terms of email signups, the treatment version had 19% LESS signups than the control, with an 86% level of confidence.

The treatment version also had a 6% decline in instant donations after people signed up, albeit with just a 14% level of confidence.

In this experiment, the control, which had a much more conversational tone to it, clearly outperformed the email that focused on the course itself.

experiments.title: How adding a popularity number impacted email clicks and donations on a NonDonor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that refreshing the email copy for Hoover’s non-donor audience will achieve an increase in clicks and conversions because they see that other people wanted this with the “30,000 people” callout.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2024 - 11/13/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Victory and the Savior Generals. This experiment was for their non-donor audience.
We wanted to update the copy to have these updates:
Shorter
Mentions well-known and respected Hoover fellow, Victor Davis Hanson, at the beginning of email
And have the callout “Already downloaded by more than 30,000 people in the last 6 months, you don’t want to miss your chance to learn from history’s most influential leaders from one of our country’s most esteemed historians.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Updating the email copy to be shorter and mentioning Hoover fellow Victor Davis Hanson at the beginning proved to be effective in driving engagement. This indicates that concise and personalized content resonates well with the non-donor audience.

2. The callout highlighting the popularity of the offer with "Already downloaded by more than 30,000 people in the last 6 months" likely created a sense of urgency and social proof, contributing to the increase in clicks and conversions.

3. The treatment showed a significant 11.2% increase in Clicks for All Traffic, a 19% increase in Opens, and a 117% decrease in Clicks to Opens, all with high levels of statistical confidence.

 

However, with the donations Version B showed a 30% decrease in number of donations with a 79% level of confidence.

With total revenue being:

Version A: $1415 with 30 gifts

Version B: $890 with 21 gifts

 

 

To leverage these learnings in future experimentation, Hoover Institution should consider:

1. Continuing to focus on crafting concise and personalized content that resonates with the audience.

2. Utilizing social proof and urgency in callouts to drive engagement and conversions.

By applying these learnings, Hoover Institution can optimize their email marketing strategy to better engage their non-donor audience and drive conversions effectively.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button CTA for email subscribers will achieve a higher engagement because it is a clear and obvious place to click and take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/05/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a featured resource. For November 2024, this is the book “A Place Called Heaven”
The main CTA in PTV featured resource emails is to make a generous gift of any amount and receive the resource as a thank you. Periodically we will do a PS where the subscriber can make a larger donation to receive a bundle that include the monthly resource.
For this email, we decided to test the PS vs a button CTA to request the book.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button CTA for email subscribers resulted in a significant increase in engagement compared to the PS (post-script) CTA. The treatment group, which included the button CTA, saw a 74.0% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% level of statistical confidence, indicating that the button CTA was more effective in encouraging subscribers to take action.
While the control offers a PS to make a larger gift, we interestingly saw a slightly higher average gift for the treatment version $28 vs $30.
For donations we saw no change between the control or treatment.
This test is intriguing, not only that the button received more clicks, but that we did not see a decrease in revenue or gifts given.

experiments.title: How changing the copy to focus on a problem/solution impacted email click rates for a non-donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a problem/solution approach for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in click through rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 10/24/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. This specific test was on their Donor audience. For this test on one of their most popular offers, we wanted to see whether email copy focused on a problem/solution approach outperformed the control copy, which has been used multiple times.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the control email copy, which has been used multiple times and does not focus on a problem/solution approach, outperformed the treatment email copy that did focus on a problem/solution approach. The treatment saw a 14.2% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic with a 97.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control performed better.

Moreover, the result was even more pronounced when we looked at both email acquisition and donation statistics. In terms of email signups, the treatment saw a 27% DECREASE with a 100% level of confidence.

Donations also experienced a decline, with a 19% decrease in donation conversions on the treatment version, with a 69% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the effectiveness of the current approach before making changes. It's also crucial to test new strategies on a smaller scale before implementing them widely. Additionally, analyzing the results of A/B tests and using statistical significance to determine the impact of changes is crucial in making informed decisions about email copy and content strategies.

experiments.title: How Facebook copy focused on the felt need met by an offer impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that explaining the felt need of Pastors Pen for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher engagement because there is a personal connection of what I could get out of Pastors Pen.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/28/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Via direct mail, Pathway to Victory offers a regular mailing of “Pastor’s Pen” a monthly mailout of encouragement from Dr. Robert Jeffress. This piece via direct mail has performed well in being a favorite piece to the audience but also as a channel for revenue.
In an effort to increase channel crossover between digital and direct mail, we decided to try out offering Pastors Pen through Facebook ads where the viewer can sign up to receive Pastors Pen in the mail.
For this offer, we decided to test copy. The control version focuses on the letter being a personal letter from Dr. Jeffress. Whereas the treatment presents Pastors Pen as an encouraging offer when life is difficult.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the perceived benefit or felt need of a product or service can significantly impact engagement and conversion rates. In this case, highlighting how Pastors Pen can be an encouraging resource during difficult times led to a 16.8% increase in clicks through Facebook ads.

While the treatment saw more clicks we actually did not experience an increase in form fills with a 2% increase with 12% loc.

However with donations we saw a 60% decrease with 89% loc.

So while this treatment got more clicks on the ad, the downstream metrics would tell us to keep the control.

experiments.title: How a slide-up with gift array vs sticky bar impacts clicks to the donation page during a high-urgency direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a slide-up with a gift array for users on Luther’s Working Preacher site will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2024 - 10/17/2024
Luther Seminary was running a high-urgency campaign to support their Working Preacher site, and one of the web elements on the site was a standard direct appeal sticky bar. We wanted to test whether using a slide-up element instead of a sticky bar would drive more user engagement through clicks to the donation page.
The sticky bar and slide-up both used the same language, but the slide-up presented users with 3 gift options. Clicking a gift option would direct users to the donation page and the form would be pre-filled with their selected gift amount.
Our hypothesis was that the slide-up would drive an increase in clicks to the donation page because it lets users begin the process of giving before they even land on the donation page. We also hoped that this would result in a downstream lift in donations from the slide-up.
We split the website traffic 50/50 between the two variants, and the test ran for ~3 days before we ran out of testing credits and had to end it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a slide-up with a gift array on Luther Seminary's Working Preacher site led to a significant increase in clicks to the donation page compared to a standard sticky bar. The treatment resulted in a 43.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a high statistical level of confidence (99.5%)!
We also found that the slide-up led to a 33.4% increase in donor conversion, but this was only with a 68.3% level of confidence.
From this test we learned that it is important to continue exploring different ways to engage Working Preacher users and streamline the donation process for them. It is clear that providing users with the option to select a gift amount before landing on the donation page can be effective in driving more clicks and potentially increasing donations. For future testing, we want to experiment with the language in the slide-up, as well as the psychology of price anchoring with our gift array amounts.

Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV’s book Are We Living in the End Times has historically struggled to perform well on Facebook, however it has been on of the best offer via Email.
In an attempt to increase engagement with the ad and therefore donations, we decided to test a notes/check list approach with creative. By featuring the 7 questions answered by the book, we hypothesis that more people will be drawn to the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:

In future experimentation, we should continue to explore this strategy of framing the book as a solution rather than just another end times book. This could involve incorporating the 7 questions into other marketing materials such as emails, website banners, and social media posts to see if it consistently boosts engagement and drives conversions.

Additionally, we should strive to reach the minimum sample size of 99,999,999 in future experiments to ensure that the results are statistically significant. This will provide more confidence in the findings and allow us to make informed decisions based on the data collected.

Overall, the success of this experiment underscores the importance of testing different messaging strategies and continuously optimizing our approach to maximize engagement and achieve our goals.

experiments.title: How a blog-style cultivation email impacts email engagement and content consumption
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a longer-form blog-style cultivation email for email subscribers will achieve more of our content being consumed and better email engagement because it gives the value away without requiring any further action . because  it gives the value away without requiring any further action. because  it gives the value away without requiring any further action. because  it gives the value away without requiring any further action . because  it gives the value away without requiring any further action. because  it gives the value away without requiring any further action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2024 - 08/27/2024
In previous experimentation, we’ve learned that shorter emails win higher click rates, but that is for appeal-style emails. We were interested in testing this same concept in a cultivation email to see if the added value would lead to better results.
Since this is a cultivation email, the concept is to see if giving away the value in the email gets more people to consume our content vs. making them take an additional step to click through to the resource. The resource that we did link in both emails was not gated — it was a PDF.
The real objective here isn’t so easily measured, so the results of this test can’t rely totally on quantitative data. Because it’s not a metric-based a/b test, but an operational test we will be tracking email replies to help inform our learning.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The longer treatment email resulted in a 69.8% decrease in clicks. However, the goal of the test wasn't to get more email clicks. What we are looking to for success in this test is the qualitative data and the email reply rate. Version A had just 1 email reply (.01% reply rate), while Version B had 7 email replies (.07% reply rate). The email replies to Version B all provided positive feedback about the blog-style format. Things to note include:
They appreciated the tips being given away right in the emailThey liked the plain-text design, formatting, and length of the contentThe advice was timelyThey appreciated not having to complete a form or take another step
Other than that, there were no significant metric differences between the two email versions, so we can conclude that the treatment version did not negatively impact anything.
Because of the positive feedback received regarding the blog-style email, we will begin to implement this email format into the mix for our weekly cultivation emails.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity in copy for a sticky bar impacts click through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the messaging and call-to-action in the sticky bar from a generic request for feedback to a more specific and personalized invitation to take a survey about the user’s learning experience will result in a higher click through rate because it is a more tangible and manageable ask.

Based on these results, it is recommended to use the learnings from this experiment in future experimentation. When designing sticky bars or any other call-to-action messaging, it would be beneficial to use specific and personalized language that clearly communicates the purpose of the action and the value it provides to the user. This approach can increase engagement and participation rates. Additionally, it is important to continue testing and iterating on messaging to optimize click-through rates and other desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How using an iPhone Notes App style of Facebook ad creative impact clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis is that the Notes App ads, with their authentic and eye-catching design, will significantly boost clicks and email signups. This is due to their unique appearance, which we believe will resonate more with our target audience on Facebook.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2024 - 07/12/2024
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit committed to serving children and families, with their Shoes for Orphan Souls (SOS) campaign being a significant donor acquisition effort.
In this experiment, we compared the effectiveness of an iPhone notes app style ad to an organic-style text ad. The iPhone notes app style ad mimics the look of a personal notes application, featuring a familiar and engaging format. The organic-style text ad has a straightforward design with just text in a plain background. Our hypothesis is that the Notes App ads, with their authentic and eye-catching design, will significantly boost clicks and email signups. This is due to their unique appearance, which we believe will resonate more with our target audience on Facebook.
We set up two Advantage+ campaigns on Facebook with equal budgets for each ad style, and the test ran for 18 days. Both ad creatives have the same call-to-action: donate $15 to provide shoes but with slight differences in language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we anticipated that the iPhone notes app style ad would outperform the organic-style text ad, leveraging its familiar and visually engaging format. However, the results showed the opposite, with the Control ads (organic-style text) achieving better performance.
The Treatment ads led to a -17.9% decrease in clicks compared to the Control ads, with a 96.5% level of confidence (LOC). The Control ads had a higher click rate (0.50%) compared to the Treatment ads (0.41%).
This test did reach statistical validity, indicating that the iPhone notes app style ad was less effective at driving clicks. Despite our hypothesis, the Treatment ads may not have resonated as strongly with the audience.
We theorized it could be due to visual engagement. The Treatment ads, designed to mimic the iPhone notes app, may have lacked the necessary visual contrast to stand out in users’ Facebook feeds. The minimalistic design, while intended to appear authentic, could have been perceived as bland or too subtle, leading users to overlook the ad. The Control, while also very minimal in design was mostly in a deep blue color that can stand out more clearly.

experiments.title: How targeting a specific audience effected click-through rates on a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe targeting a specific audience on Facebook ads leads to higher click-through rates due to increased ad relevance, reduced ad fatigue, and enhanced personalization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2024 - 08/30/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, utilized a paid Facebook media campaign to test a new approach. Our goal was to determine whether tailoring our creative and ad copy to target Facebook users with an interest in health and wellness would lead to higher click-through rates and an increased number of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. **Targeting a specific audience leads to higher click-through rates**: The experiment showed that personalizing the Facebook ad creative and copy to target an audience interested in health resulted in a 153.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. This demonstrates that targeting a specific audience can significantly improve the effectiveness of ad campaigns.
2. **Significant improvements in key metrics**: The treatment group not only saw a substantial increase in Clicks for All Traffic but also experienced a 453.9% increase in Complete Registrations. This indicates that by targeting a specific audience, not only can you drive more traffic but also increase the likelihood of conversions.
3. **Segment-specific improvements**: The experiment also revealed segment-specific improvements, such as a 342.4% increase in clicks for the 55-64 age group, a 71.4% increase for visitors aged 65 and above, a 194.6% increase for Mobile visitors, and a 137.4% increase for Female visitors. This underscores the importance of audience segmentation in ad targeting.

experiments.title: How more hyperlinks within a high-urgency campaign email appeal impact email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a higher number of links  for Eagle Lake email appeal recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because it gives the reader more opportunities to navigate to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 between June and July to raise money for the camps they host.
We have seen success using more hyperlinks within an email appeals in terms of both unique clicks and transactions, therefore, we wanted to try testing this for The Navigators.
The control maintained the number of clicks that we used in each of the previous appeal emails – typically 1, occasionally 2. However, the treatment contained a total of 5 links to the donation page.
Our hypothesis was that giving the reader more opportunities to navigate to the donation page the more likely they are to click.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the number of links in email appeals for The Navigators' Eagle Lake campaign led to a significant increase in clicks from recipients. The treatment group, which had 5 links to the donation page, saw a 32.8% increase in clicks compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a confidence level of 97.5%. These results are valid - meaning it is recommended to use this tactic moving forward if your goal is to increase email clicks.
Further, we also wanted to see what the downstream metrics looked like on the donation page for this test. From email clicks to donations, the treatment saw a 10.5% decrease in transactions at a 40.4% level of confidence. While from email sends to donations, the treatment actually saw an 18.9% lift in transactions at a 52.4% level of confidence.
It is important to view both of these metrics as the different perspectives offer very different results. While these metrics did not validate, it is important to remember that when we push more traffic to a donation page, the instant conversion rate with decrease. But in actuality, there was an 18.9% lift in transactions.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of the number of links in email appeals. This suggests that providing recipients with more opportunities to navigate to the donation page can result in higher engagement and potentially more donations. It would be interesting to test different variations of the number of links to determine the optimal amount for maximizing clicks and conversions.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of testing and optimizing email strategies to enhance the effectiveness of fundraising campaigns for The Navigators.

experiments.title: How a relevant image in a high urgency email appeal impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a relevant image for Eagle Lake 2 email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because people give to people and it would establish a more personable connection to the camp.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 between June and July to raise money for the camps they host.
The Navigators audience is very comfortable and familiar with Abby Anderson, the main sender we use for their emails and texts. Therefore, we wanted to see whether an image of Abby at Eagle Lake camps when she was young would influence email clicks and increase donations as a result.
Our hypothesis was that the image of Abby would allow the recipient to feel more connected to Eagle Lake camps therefore increasing the likelihood that they would click the donation link, then give a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a relevant image of Abby at Eagle Lake camps in the email led to a significant increase in clicks for all traffic. The treatment group, which received the email with the image, saw a 48.1% increase in clicks compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 99.9% confidence level, indicating a strong impact of the image on recipient engagement.
These results are valid and therefore, we should consider using relevant images within appeal emails moving forward.
We also pulled number of donations out of the number of clicks within this test and saw an invalid 25.7 decrease in donations at an 85.9% level of confidence. As well as number of donations put of the number of email sends resulted in a 10.1% lift in donations at a 33.4% level of confidence.
We decided to view both of these metrics because based if we only viewed the number of donations out of clickthrough rate, it appears that the image of Abby decreased conversions, but in actuality, there was a 10% increase in donations. It only seems as though there was a drop because we pushed a larger group of people to the donation page.
Neither of these metrics validated, but the results do offer helpful learnings and context of The Navigators audience.
This experiment demonstrates the effectiveness of using personal, relatable images to establish a more personable connection with the audience and drive engagement. In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue incorporating relevant images of people or scenarios that resonate with the audience to increase interaction and response rates. Additionally, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of testing different elements in email campaigns to identify strategies that yield the best results and improve overall campaign performance.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post as a paid Facebook ad affects click-through rate and other downstream conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a Facebook ad that looks like an organic Facebook post for potential subscribers will achieve a higher CTR and lower CPC because it feels more human and not like an advertisement..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2023
This ministry client has campaigns that helps give relief to people around the world. We wanted to test into their Facebook ad click-through rate and see if we could get a higher down-stream conversion rate. IMB’s Facebook ads normally have designed graphics with a clear message, as you can see in Sample A below. The organic ad we created for this test has simple white font text on a navy background, it included no graphics unlike the control. We’ve tested running “organic posts” for other organizations and have seen a tremendous increase in click-through rate and a decrease in cost per click. We believe that the organic Facebook ad could create a higher click-through rate since it is simple and clear and looks like an organic Facebook post which humanizes the ad. This could also lead to a decrease in cost per click since the organic ad appearance does not seem as “markety” as the control graphic.
We tested this as an A/B test with a 50/50 split in targeting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a Facebook ad that resembles an organic post can significantly increase click-through rates and potentially decrease cost per click. The treatment ad, which had simple white font on a navy background and no graphics, outperformed the control ad with designed graphics and clear messaging.

The results of this experiment show a 51.3% increase in clicks for all traffic, with a high level of statistical confidence at 100.0%. This confirms the hypothesis that the organic-looking ad feels more human and less like an advertisement, leading to higher engagement from users.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing organic-looking ads against more traditional ad formats. This can help optimize ad performance and potentially reduce advertising costs. Additionally, considering the success of this experiment, it may be worth exploring the length of copy or color of organic ads.

experiments.title: How ‘skipping the form’ affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing one-click access to a free resource will lift click rates among subscribers because removing the sign-up process will demonstrate respect for their time and leverage trust to deliver as much value as possible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2024 - 07/31/2024
We always aim to improve user experience (UX) and deliver maximum value, especially to our subscribers. One way to create a better UX is to reduce friction, which is anything that makes taking the desired action more difficult.
But too often, we consider friction only from the viewpoint of how it impacts us and not our subscribers — what we should remember is that when someone abandons, they are also missing out on something of value.
So we wanted to know what would happen if we removed the sign-up process and maximized the value we delivered to our subscribers by respecting their time and providing one-click access to the resource we were sharing.
In the control, subscribers were sent to a landing page where they would complete a form to access the desired PDF. In the treatment, subscribers were sent directly to the desired PDF when they clicked the email link – no form fill required.
The only change in the email was a line that emphasized that the subscriber would not have to fill out a form to access the resource. Traffic was split ~50/50 between the treatment and control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Linking directly to the resource and skipping the form fill produced a statistically valid 28.5% increase in click-through rates (CTR).
Plus, while all 300 subscribers who clicked the treatment successfully accessed the desired resource, only about 18% of those who clicked the control completed the registration process (93), meaning the treatment was able to get the resource into the hands of 222.6% more subscribers. While we can certainly work to improve the landing page's conversion rate, achieving a 100% conversion rate is unthinkable.
Our subscribers win because they don't have to complete another form (they're already subscribers, we don't need to collect their contact information again and again), and we win because the treatment allowed us to deliver far more value to our audience, helping us fulfill our mission and driving toward better business outcomes—win/win.
The treatment version streamlined the user experience (UX) by eliminating the need for users to fill out forms, resulting in a significantly higher CTR. This indicates that simplifying processes for users can lead to a more positive response.
We are currently testing this concept with cold audiences and hope to share our learnings soon.
One additional thing to consider is how we will handle this in terms of contact scoring. But that is a solvable 'back-end' problem that is worth working through to deliver as much value as possible.

experiments.title: Does Longer Copy Affect Clicks in Email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing email length for an appeal email will achieve a higher click rate because the extra value proposition will increase motivation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding length, an additional value proposition, would increase the likelihood of somebody clicking through the the desired action.
This specific email that we tested was the first appeal of the FYE campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. In this experiment, increasing the email length for an appeal email did not achieve a higher click rate. On the contrary, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in clicks compared to the control group.

2. The extra value proposition included in the longer email did not motivate recipients to click through to the desired action as hypothesized.

3. The control group, which received the original length email, outperformed the treatment group in terms of click rate.

Future Implications:

1. When crafting appeal emails, it may be more effective to keep the message concise and to the point rather than adding extra content that may not necessarily resonate with the audience.

2. It is essential to test assumptions and hypotheses through A/B testing to ensure that changes to email content or format are truly impactful before implementing them across all communications.

3. Consider conducting further research or surveys to better understand the preferences and motivations of the target audience to tailor future email campaigns more effectively.

4. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future email marketing strategies and continue to iterate and test different approaches to optimize click-through rates.

experiments.title: How a pulling a donate button into the main navigation bar impacts clicks in mobile
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pulling the main donate button out of the dropdown menu and into the primary navigation bar for mobile users will achieve an increase in clicks and traffic to the main donation page because it increases visibility of the call-to-action button and reduces the friction of having to “find” the donate button.. because  it increases visibility of the call-to-action button and reduces the friction of having to “find” the donate button.. because  it increases visibility of the call-to-action button and reduces the friction of having to “find” the donate button.. because  it increases visibility of the call-to-action button and reduces the friction of having to “find” the donate button..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
For CaringBridge users in mobile, we noticed that the current mobile experience removed the donate button from the top navigation and instead it was placed with a hamburger dropdown menu. Unless a user opened this menu, they would not see the main donate button. Our hypothesis was that by pulling this donate button out of the hidden hamburger menu and making it more visible for mobile users, would generate more clicks and drive more traffic to the main donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that making the main donate button more visible for mobile users, by pulling it out of the hidden hamburger menu and into the primary navigation bar, significantly increased clicks and traffic to the main donation page. The treatment saw a  3,409.9% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, proving that the hypothesis was correct.
Donor Conversion Rate:
Control: 69,032 Samples | 148 Donations | .2% Conv Rate |
Treatment: 69,165 Samples | 145 Donations | .2 Conv Rate | -2.2 Difference | 15% Significance
What this reveals is that although this didn't impact donor conversion, it is an effective way of driving more traffic to the page. In future, we can pivot our focus to improving conversion rate of the main donate page.
This experiment underscores the importance of testing and optimizing mobile user experiences to maximize engagement and conversions. It is crucial to continue monitoring user behavior, conducting A/B tests, and implementing changes based on data-driven insights to continually improve the effectiveness of the nonprofit's digital presence and achieve its goals.

As well as a 254.1% lift in Hero Button Clicks on Treatment 2 which featured "How to Memorize Scripture: with a level of confidence of 99.9%. 

These results indicate that both treatment versions outperformed the control in terms of Hero Button Clicks 99.9% of the time.

experiments.title: How using simplified, invitational ad creative for a video series ad increased clicks to the landing page

rows.name: NA | Kingdom Mindset, normal ads - PO (March 2024)
NA | Kingdom Mindset, simple ads - PO (March 2024)

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a simplified style of ad creative for video series ads on Facebook will achieve an increase in clicks to the landing page because the ads are intriguing and make the reader want to visit the landing page to learn more. because  the ads are intriguing and make the reader want to visit the landing page to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2024 - 04/27/2024
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They promote these free video series on Facebook and wanted to see if using a simplified, “invitation” style of ad creative would lead to more clicks to the video series landing page.
For this experiment, we tested their standard style of ad creative against a version that had fewer design elements and was written to sound more like an invitation from the speaker in the video series, rather than an informal ad from an organization.
Our hypothesis is that the simple invitation ad design will increase clicks because it is more personal and engaging.
We ran this as an A/B campaigns test in Facebook with the ad budget split evenly. Both ad variants used the same text and CTA button, the only variable was the ad creative, and we ran this test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for one month, we found that the simple, invitational ad creative increased in clicks by 46.2%, with a level of confidence (LOC) of 100%. This indicates that this result is valid and that we'd get a similar result if we tested this again.
Aside from clicks, the treatment ads also resulted in a 26% increase in email sign-ups for the video series, with a 99.9% LOC. This shows us that the simple, invitation style of ad creative didn't just motivate people to click on the ads. It also made them more motivated to sign up for the series!
Our key learnings from this experiment are:
The simplified style of ad creative, designed to sound like a personal invitation, led to a significant increase in clicks and email sign-ups. This indicates that the treatment ad was more effective in engaging and attracting viewers to take action.Testing this kind of simple invitation ad with Focus on the Family's other video series offers may lead to similar results, but each series offer is different and more testing is needed to have a more definitive answer.

experiments.title: How sending a webinar invite from the host affects email click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending a webinar invite from the host will increase click rates by humanizing the email and creating a feeling of liking.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/25/2024
We all know and love our Audience Engagement Manager, Riley, and so do our subscribers. But for this test, we wanted to know what would happen if we sent a webinar invite from the webinar’s host (Nathan), rather than our usual sender, (Riley).
Our previous tests have shown that the more we can humanize our emails (I.e., make them look and feel as much like a personal 1:1 email as possible), the better our engagement rates.
So we wondered if sending the email from the host would make the email seem more like a personal invitation and less like a promotion — and how this would affect engagement rates.
For this test, we split the traffic 50/50 with roughly one-half of subscribers receiving the invite from Riley (with “we” language) and the other half receiving the invite from Nathan (with “I” language).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Sending the email from the webinar's host produced a 31.7% statistically valid lift in email click rates, smashing the control.

So this seems like a clear-cut learning -- by sending the email from the webinar's host, we humanized the interaction and earned a bunch more clicks, end of story, right? ... not so fast, my friend!

As we dug deeper into the data, we discovered an unexpected incongruency in the click rates and registration rates between the control and the treatment.

While the treatment (from the host) produced a significant 32% increase in clicks, it ultimately led to a 167% decrease in sign-up rates.

This one's a head-scratcher. Considering that the changes to the treatment were tightly aligned with our hypothesis, and no other changes were made (either to the email or the following landing page), we would not expect to see the treatment produce such a large lift in click rates but such a gigantic decrease in sign-up rates.

The only element we could isolate in our analysis is that the control email shares Nathan's title while the treatment shares it only in Nathan's signature, in gray font. Perhaps the demonstration of "authority" made a bigger impact in people's decision to register.

Clearly, humanizing the email improved click rates, which is what we set out to test ... what's less clear is why this tactic caused such a dramatic dip in registration rates, warranting closer analysis and a second test.

experiments.title: How using an iPhone Notes App style of Facebook ad creative impacted clicks to a video series landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the ad creative image to mimic a Notes App style for Facebook traffic will achieve an increase in clicks and email signups because the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2024 - 06/24/2024
Focus on the Family is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They promoted these free video series on Facebook and wanted to see if we could try some radically different ad creatives to improve clicks and email signups from their ads.
For this experiment, we tested using an organic, non-branded ad that resembles a screenshot of someone’s iPhone Notes App. This ad creative featured an impact-focused headline and three bullet point statements below.
The control ads for this test used the same headline and bullet points, but they would look like the typical branded ads we usually run on Facebook.
Our hypothesis is that the Notes App ads, with their authentic and eye-catching design, will significantly boost clicks and email signups. This is due to their unique appearance, which we believe will resonate more with our target audience on Facebook.
We ran this as an A/B campaigns test in Facebook with the ad budget split evenly. The Control campaign had the standard ads for two video series, and the Treatment campaign had the Notes App version of the ad creative for those same two video series. All other variables remained the same, and the test ran for a total of 2.5 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We expected the Notes App style of ad creative to perform better in this test, and we were surprised to see that our Control ads actually had the best results.
These are the results we saw:
The Treatment ads led to a -6.1% decrease in clicks to the video series landing page, with a 99.9% level of confidence (LOC).The Treatment ads also validated a -7.9% decrease in the number of emails acquired, with a 99.6% LOC.And the Treatment ads had a -24.0% decrease in donations, with a 98.8% LOC.The Control ads had a campaign ROI of 1.39, which was higher than the Treatment campaign's ROI of 0.94.
Our key learnings from this experiment are that the Notes App style ad creative did not perform as well as the standard branded ads in terms of clicks, email signups, and donations. Despite the hypothesis that the more authentic and eye-catching design would lead to an increase in engagement, the results showed a decrease in clicks to the landing page, emails acquired, and donations.
We have a couple of theories on why the Treatment ads performed worse in this test, and these are ideas that we can test into with future ads:
The Treatment ads lacked the Focus on the Family branding and logo, which could have decreased their credibilityThe Treatment ads did not incorporate any imagery to support the value or impact of the video series, but the Control ads used imagery to support their claims.Ex: For the "Reconnected" marriage video series ad, the image in the Control is a couple holding hands. This supports the message that the video series will help the reader reconnect with their spouse.The Control ads had more color to help them stand out on Facebook, whereas the white background of the Treatment ads caused them to almost "blend in" with the rest of the white space on the page.Ex: For the "Reconnected" marriage video series ad, the image in the Control is a couple holding hands. This supports the message that the video series will help the reader reconnect with their spouse.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a highly personalized and exclusive subject line for housefile recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because there is a higher perceived value insinuated for the content offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/24/2024
When sending a content offer to the housefile, we wondered if we could increase response and engagement of the message by increasing the perceived value of the offer through adding exclusivity and personalization in the subject line vs. a subject line that merely invoked curiosity around the contents and its potential usefulness or benefit to the visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings:

We saw a 58% increase in click rate at 99% level of confidence so the results of the experiment are valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue utilizing personalized and exclusive language in subject lines to increase engagement with email content and potentially improve overall campaign performance. This could involve tailoring subject lines based on recipient behavior or preferences, creating a sense of VIP access, or offering unique opportunities specifically for housefile recipients. By leveraging the power of personalization and exclusivity, nonprofits like Luther Seminary can effectively communicate with their audience and drive higher levels of engagement with their messaging.

experiments.title: How streamlining and simplifying email copy affects click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Streamlining an email by removing additional content (even when that content is value-focused) will increase click rates by improving the clarity of the email and focusing on the value derived from the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2024 - 05/30/2024
A lot of brands in the b2b space send heavily-designed emails. We can’t help but wonder if they’ve ever tested this approach, because we have consistently found that the less ‘markety’ and the more human our emails, the better our response.
So far our testing has instructed us to remove all branding elements, use everyday language, left-justify our copy, and generally design our emails to look as similar to a personal email as possible.
But for this test, we wanted to know how applying the same principle of simplification to an email’s content would affect click rates.
In the past we’ve improved click rates by cutting any non-essential or repetitive language, eliminating “hooks”, and removing any “warm up” copy or overlong salutations.
But what would happen if we also removed additional VALUE from the email beyond the resource we’re sharing?
The control in this test included some value-driven copy that could help the user understand more about using the content offer we were sharing. In the treatment, we removed this copy and presented the offer straight.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Streamlining the email by removing additional content produced a significant 48% lift in click rates when compared to the control, reinforcing our hypothesis that clarity and simplicity trump most other tactics when it comes to improving email engagement.
This is one test in a long series of tests to optimize our email engagement. And from our learnings, we have been able to develop a 'templated control' for the emails we send to our subscribers, based on their preferences.
Present a problem or common misconception. Provide an example of what to do instead. Explain a 'unique mechanism' of this recommendationPosition the offer as the "how to" CTAReinforce main benefit
So it's interesting to see how such a seemingly simple message is the product of such extensive testing! And we'll continue to find ways to refine this control over time
Overall, the results of this experiment highlight how focusing on ONE core message in your email can improve click rates and engagement.

experiments.title: How video ad creative impacts engagement n compared to static ad creative
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that video ad creative for a direct appeal campaign will be more engaging than an organic image and lead to higher engagement by increasing the visual appeal and clarity of the donation CTA
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2024 - 06/10/2024
Bible League Canada ran a direct appeal campaign on Facebook, inviting users to donate to support children’s Biblical education worldwide. In a prior test, we validated that using “organic” looking ad creative, we could increase engagement and donations for direct appeal campaigns. However, for this campaign, we could use an inspirational short video that demonstrated the need for Christian education worldwide. We hypothesized that this moving video could attract more users on Facebook and lead to better results.
We built two campaigns with matching ad copy; the only change was the ad creative. Our control campaign featured the “organic” looking static image we’d tested in the past against the video ad creative. We ran the test for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Video outperformed static image: The experiment demonstrated that using a video ad creative for direct appeal campaigns led to a significant increase in engagement, as indicated by the 84.1% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. This result supports the hypothesis that video ads are more engaging than static images for this type of campaign. This in turn led to a 300% increase in donations at a 92% Level of confidence.

2. Visual appeal and clarity: The success of the video ad creative in this experiment may be attributed to its ability to convey the need for Christian education worldwide in a more compelling and emotionally impactful way compared to the static image. The moving visuals likely captured users' attention and prompted them to take action.

It is worth noting that our CPM more than doubled for the video campaign and our cost per click went up 8%. However, the people we were paying to place the ad in front of were far more interested in the content, and thus, our click-through rate and instant donation page conversion rate both increased significantly, leading to the 300% increase in donations. (Our instant conversion rate increased 117%).

We will continue testing the impact of inspirational videos like this in other parts of the funnel and on other campaigns to see their impact.

experiments.title: How Conversion-Centered Design Affects Click Rates On A Landing Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning the NextAfter Institute membership page using conversion-centered design will increase click-through rates by using proven persuasion levers to hold attention and compel action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/12/2024
Conversion-centered design (CCD) aims to apply multiple (a) persuasive design techniques and (b) psychological triggers to get more people to take a desired action. This tactic can be helpful when you feel a page “needs a lot of work” and/or when a page does not receive enough traffic to validate smaller changes.
As we began to drive more traffic to a landing page promoting the NextAfter Institute Membership, we wanted to test how using CCD to make multiple changes would impact conversion rates. For this test, we developed a treatment that tested:
Structuring page to improve “skim-ability” – We used color to create on-page contrast and make it easier for the user to find the information they needed; we also narrowed the copy width to make it more readable
Making social proof more prominent – We embedded a widget that made it easy for users to read and scroll through our many positive reviews, giving them a more prominent placement on the page and placing them in two locations: near the top and near the bottom of the page
Using an outcome-driven headline – The headline is the most important copy on the page, so we shifted from a feature-focused headline to one that reinforced what those features will allow the reader to achieve, reinforcing the “how” with a subhead
Using long-form copy to make the case – At the top of the page, we used outcome-driven copy in big bold font to prompt immediate action but we reinforced that messaging with an in-depth sales letter that uses compelling language to illustrate the value propsotion concisely yet thoroughly
Drawing attention to benefits and outcomes – We used color contrast, huge fonts, bold type, testimonials, highlighted text, short paragraphs, and crossheads to draw attention to the benefits and outcomes of membership
We split traffic between the control and the treatment and ran the A/B test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing conversion-centered design techniques can significantly impact click-through rates.
By making multiple changes to improve the page design from the perspective of conversion rate optimization, we were able to increase click-through rates by 106.6% with a 100% statistical level of confidence.
In future experimentation, we should continue to utilize conversion-centered design principles to optimize our landing pages and drive conversions.
We can now improve on this design by experimenting with different creative combinations to refine our approach and improve our overall conversion rates. Additionally, we should consider applying these learnings to other areas of our website and marketing materials to enhance the effectiveness of our messaging and calls to action.

experiments.title: How an additional Call-to-Action early in an email impacts clicks and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: it clarifies the context of the email within the campaign early-on and ensures anyone who doesn’t finish the full email doesn’t miss an ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2024 - 05/06/2024
For MAF’s 2024 Plane Maintenance campaign, the 7th email in the campaign was written to include an insightful story about the types of repairs these planes need in order to deliver the gospel. However, with no call-to-action until the very end of the email we hypothesized that users who do not read the email to the end may not even realize that the email is part of the campaign. We believe that adding an additional call-to-action early in the email copy that contextualized the communication and links directly to the donation page would improve click-through and donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including an additional call-to-action and donation page link early in a campaign email for Mission Aviation Fellowship's housefile can significantly increase clicks and donor conversion. In this specific case, the treatment version that included an early call-to-action saw a 17.4% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 95.9% statistical level of confidence compared to the control version.

Downstream Donor Conversion:

Control: 11,847 Samples | 5 Donations | Conv Rate 0.0%

Treatment: 11,721 Samples. |9 Donations | Conv Rate 0.1% | 81.9% Relative Difference | 72.3% Significance. 

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that campaign emails clearly communicate the purpose and include a strong call-to-action early on to capture the attention of recipients who may not read the entire email. By providing a direct link to the donation page, recipients are more likely to understand the context of the email and take action immediately, leading to higher engagement and conversion rates. This experiment highlights the importance of optimizing email content to drive desired outcomes and should be taken into consideration in future email marketing campaigns for Mission Aviation Fellowship.

experiments.title: How leveraging a small image on a donate button to build affinity and alignment affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag to build affinity for potential donors to The Daily Signal will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/09/2024
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their home page, and most other pages, the only call-to-action to donate to support the organization is located at the bottom in the footer section among many other site links.
They’ve previously tested adding a donate button to the main menu of the homepage here: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/K1qG
That experiment resulted in a large increase in traffic to the donation page because we provided an easier, more prominent path to the donation page.
The team had noticed an eCommerce site that, when selling merchandise for a particular team, put that team’s logo on the “add to cart” page to leverage the natural affinity between the buyer and the team.
To continue testing the donate button to increase traffic to the donation page, they decided to test a similar concept of adding an American Flag icon to the button.
They believed the flag image would help align a donor’s motivation with their intended action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a small American flag to the donate button on The Daily Signal's website significantly increased the number of clicks to the donation page. The treatment group, which included the flag icon, saw a 53.6% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence.

Additionally, the treatment button saw a 22.8% lift in transactions with a 59.7% level of confidence. Although these results are not valid, they do indicate that overall more traffic to the donation page will increase the percentage of donations made.

Overall, this experiment suggests that incorporating patriotic or visually appealing elements can help build affinity with potential donors and encourage them to take action. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the use of imagery or design elements that align with donors' motivations to see if similar increases in conversion rates can be achieved.

Additionally, it is important to consider the placement of calls-to-action on the website. The success of this experiment may also indicate that strategically placing donation buttons in prominent locations on the site can lead to higher engagement and donation rates.

experiments.title: How showing a well known speaker on a Facebook ad impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a photo of a well-known speaker for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve a higher CTR  because the familarity principle suggests that people will develop a preference to what they are familiar with.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2024 - 04/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. A Hoover content offer, Dueling Populisms, showed a low CTR rate. Therefore, we wanted to test at the beginning of the funnel on the Facebook ad. Looking at previous content offers’ ad creatives; we saw ads with a photo of Victor David Hanson performed well because the audience was familiar with him. Since Dueling Populisms is written by Victor, we decided to apply that to this content offer as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:
Using a photo of a well-known speaker (Victor David Hanson) for Hoover's Facebook audience resulted in a 49.7% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This indicates that leveraging familiarity with a respected figure can significantly improve audience engagement. Cost per Click decreased by 51.2% and Cost per Registration decreased by 40.5% with a 100% level of confidence, showing that the treatment was not only more effective in driving clicks but also more cost-efficient in converting these clicks into registrations. Cost per Registration decreased by 40.5% with a 100% level of confidence.While this did not validate, it had a CPP decrease of 81.9%
In future experimentation, it is crucial to:
Continue leveraging the familiarity principle by featuring well-known speakers or personalities in ad creatives to drive higher engagement rates.Monitor cost metrics closely to ensure that any changes in ad creatives or audience targeting not only drive engagement but also remain cost-effective.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of understanding the preferences and behaviors of the target audience, and using this knowledge to optimize future advertising strategies for the Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How an email written at a 9th grade reading level impacts click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing copy at a lower reading level for people on ACI’s housefile will achieve a higher click-through rate because it will be easier for people to read and comprehend.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2024 - 04/26/2024
ACI was launching a new pledge to their housefile. While writing copy for this offer, we wondered if writing the copy at a lower reading level would impact click-through rate. We typically write copy at a 12th grade reading level for this client’s offers. For this experiment, we tried writing copy at a 9th grade level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that writing copy at a lower reading level for people on ACI's housefile did not result in a higher click-through rate. In fact, the treatment group, which received the copy at a 9th grade reading level, experienced a 33.7% decrease in clicks compared to the control group that received the copy at a 12th grade reading level. This outcome was statistically significant with a 99.9% level of confidence.

Moving forward, it is important to carefully consider and test hypotheses before making changes to copywriting strategies. While the intention was to make the content more accessible to readers, the results showed that the original approach might have been more effective in engaging the audience. Future experimentation should focus on testing different variables and closely monitoring the outcomes to make informed decisions about optimizing copy for better performance.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to a news platform site’s header affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the top header for site visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2024 - 04/10/2024
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their home page, and most other pages, the only call-to-action to donate to support the organization is located at the bottom in the footer section among many other site links.
In an effort to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we previously tested adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the category menu at the top of the page on the desktop version, and in the navigation menu of the mobile version. This experiment resulted in a 42.8% increase in donations compared to the control. However, with a 53.2% level of confidence, these results can’t be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.
In this attempt to increase traffic to the donation page, we wanted to test whether adding the donate button to a more notable location at the very top of the page would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. We also wanted to test whether adding a gift box icon to the donate button in order to match the “branded” style of the “Search” and “Menu” buttons next to it would lead to an increase in conversion rate.
The regular donate button and “branded” donate button appeared in the top header sitewide for both the desktop and mobile experiences. 50% of traffic saw the control with no button, and the two treatments received 25% of traffic each.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donate button to the top header for site visitors led to a significant increase in traffic to the donation page. The first treatment saw a significant 2,694% increase in clicks to the donation page compared to the control group with no button, while the second treatment saw an even higher 2,866.2% increase in clicks at a 99.9% level of confidence. This result suggests that making the donation button more prominent and easily accessible can greatly impact the amount of traffic led to the donation page.
However, in terms of donations, the first treatment lead to a 9.3% decrease in donor conversion rate (at an invalid 14.5% level of confidence), and the second treatment led to a near-valid 64% decrease in donor conversion rate compared to the control.
While invalid, these results may suggest that by more prominently displaying the donate button and increasing traffic to the donation page, people who do click on the button may be less motivated to give than someone in the control experience who seeks out the donation page in order to support the organization.
Future experimentation is needed to understand what motivates visitors of The Daily Signal to make a gift to support their work. Experiment ideas include testing alternative copy on the donation page to motivate more viewers of the site to give, and testing an alternate icon that aligns more strongly with the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How using a plain image in a content offer Facebook ad impacted clicks compared to a designed image using an overlaid CTA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a plain image in a content offer Facebook ad will increase clicks by making the ad appear more authentic because it looks like a post from another Facebook user
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2024 - 03/20/2024
Focus on the Family uses free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For one of their best-performing campaigns, they hypothesized that they could increase engagement by making the ad look like a post from another user. Users who share content don’t create “ads” with a custom photo with overlaid call to action text. Instead, they often post a plain image with some thoughts about it.
We created an ad treatment with no text or CTA overlaying the image. We used the ad copy itself to promote the free video series to users and invite them to sign up. We ran this against our control ad images that featured overlaid CTAs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an organic-looking Facebook ad design, which resembles a post from another user, can increase clicks significantly compared to traditional ad designs with overlaid call-to-action text. This approach creates a more authentic and engaging experience for the audience, leading to higher engagement with the ad content.
Moving forward, we should leverage this learning in future experimentation by incorporating more organic-looking ad designs in our campaigns. By focusing on creating content that mimics user-generated posts rather than traditional ad formats, we can build a stronger connection with our audience and drive higher click-through rates. Additionally, we should continue to test and optimize different creative approaches to further enhance our advertising strategies and maximize results.
For a future offer, we will test no ad copy and instead use an image with a clear, short call to action on a plain-colored background.
Impact on other metrics:
The treatment decreased the gross cost per click by $0.48 ($1.42 vs $190)There was no statistically significant difference in emails acquired during the experiment.

experiments.title: How letting lapsed members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, are appreciated affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Letting lapsed members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they will know their gift is helping support the mission
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if more lapsed members would click if they knew they would receive a Member’s Card no matter how much they gave. The treatment included the phrase, “Any amount you give is greatly appreciated” as part of the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that explicitly stating "Any amount you give is greatly appreciated" in the call to action message decreased click-through rates for lapsed members in The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment version with this added phrase saw a 34.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control version without the phrase. The conversion rate for the treatment email was also lower.

From this experiment, it is clear that the additional message about any amount being appreciated did not resonate positively with lapsed members. In future experimentation, it would be advisable to avoid incorporating similar messaging that emphasizes the size of the donation being irrelevant or downplays the importance of the gift. Instead, focusing on the impact of the donation in supporting the mission and achieving tangible outcomes may be more effective in encouraging lapsed members to re-engage and contribute.

Additionally, it is essential to consider the messaging context and align it closely with the organization's mission and values to ensure relevance and connection with the target audience. Experimentation should continue to test different messaging approaches, calls to action, and value propositions to identify what resonates best with lapsed members and drives higher engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How placing an additional call to action higher in the copy of a fundraising email for members affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding another call to action sooner in a fundraising email for current members will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they already support this organization and understand how their gift will be beneficial.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click if a call to action was placed earlier in the body of the email. Since members are familiar with the mission, values, and issues of the Heritage Foundation they wouldn’t need as much information to make an additional donation.
The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding a call to action sooner in a fundraising email for current members can significantly increase click-through and conversion rates.

2. Providing a clear and concise call to action early in the email can effectively prompt members to take action.

3. Current members of The Heritage Foundation are more likely to respond to donation requests since they already understand the organization's mission and values.

Note: The treatment email also received more conversations, though not at a significant statistical difference.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the placement and wording of calls to action in emails to optimize engagement and donation rates. Utilizing the knowledge that current members are more likely to donate, future emails can be structured to emphasize the impact of their contributions and make it easy for them to support the organization. Additionally, incorporating matching gift opportunities and other incentives can further motivate members to donate.
