experiments.title: How mentioning a match offer sooner in a fundraising email to non-members affects the click-through and conversion rate

experiments.summary:

We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked non-members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if non-members would click if the matching gift offer was placed earlier in the body of the email. The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action that mentioned the matching gift opportunity after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the matching gift offer sooner in a fundraising email did not increase the click-through rate for The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment saw a 7.6% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. This suggests that the incentive to give may not be as effective when emphasized early in the email.
Note: The conversion rate also decreased from the treatment email.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the placement of incentives in fundraising emails. The timing and presentation of matching gift offers and other incentives can have a significant impact on donor response rates. It may be beneficial to test different approaches to determine the most effective strategy for motivating recipients to donate. experiments.title: How adding 3 links to a fundraising email for non-members affects the click-through rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having three calls to action throughout an email for non-members will achieve a higher click-through rate and lead to an increase in donations because it provides more opportunity to take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2024 - 03/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. In the email, the President thanked non-members for their generosity and the issues America is facing. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if having three hyperlinked calls to action placed at the beginning, middle, and end of the email would encourage non-members to click more than having only one call to action at the end.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having three calls to action throughout an email for non-members resulted in a significantly higher click-through rate compared to having only one call to action at the end. The treatment group, which had three hyperlinked calls to action placed at the beginning, middle, and end of the email, saw a 100.2% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

Note: While the click-through rate increased the donation conversion rate was the same for both the control and treatment email.

In future experimentation, this learning should be applied by incorporating multiple calls to action strategically throughout emails or communication pieces. By providing more opportunities for recipients to take action, it increases the likelihood of engagement and conversion. Additionally, testing different placements and wording for these calls to action could further optimize the approach to maximize results. This experiment underscores the importance of experimentation and testing in marketing strategies to identify the most effective tactics for achieving goals.

Additionally, gathering feedback from the email recipients through surveys or direct responses could provide valuable insights into their preferences and help in tailoring email content to better suit their needs and interests.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits of a premium affect clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that explaining the benefits of a premium book for potential donors will achieve an increase in clicks because  the additional clarity on the premium will motivate them to click the link and learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test calling out the perceived benefits of the premium in an effort to pinpoint motivating factors for their audience. The control email called out the impact that their monthly gift would have on the organization whereas the treatment called out the benefits of reading the book that they would receive as a premium for becoming a monthly donor.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that explaining the benefits of a premium book for potential donors led to a significant increase in clicks compared to highlighting the impact of a monthly gift. The treatment saw a 27.8% lift in clicks at a 98.8% level of confidence, indicating that these results are valid. We can conclude that providing additional clarity and tangible benefits to potential donors can be a motivating factor for them to take action, and we should continue to utilize this motivation in future appeals. This test also suggests that using impact language may not be effective for their audience.
In future experimentation, we should continue to emphasize the benefits and values of becoming a monthly partner, such as offering premium gifts or exclusive content. This approach has shown to be effective in increasing engagement and click-through rates. Additionally, we should consider testing different types of premiums or incentives to see which resonates the most with our audience. By continually testing and optimizing our messaging and offerings, we can better understand what motivates donors and improve the overall effectiveness of our campaigns.

I believe we see a sharp decline in this specific format because a link is harder to see in the HTML version.

While this is still a valuable learning to see how the audience responds to this, I believe it will need to be retested to see if it produces the same result as a personal email.

experiments.title: How a fullwidth, plain-text email affects click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a full-width text format for our weekly email send will achieve an increase in email clicks because the email will seem more organic, personal, and less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2023 - 11/04/2023
Our weekly email sends have been the same format for quite a while. We tested into using plain text format to create a more human-looking email a while ago and were curious if we could take it one step further to  seem even more organic. Typically our email text is centered so that when it’s opened it’s still quite obvious it’s a bulk send, not a one-off email.
The only thing change in the treatment of the email is the format. The copy, subject line, sender, etc. all stayed the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a full-width text format for our weekly email send can significantly increase the number of clicks. The treatment group, which received the full-width text format, experienced a 27.6% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group.
This indicates that the full-width text format created the perception that the email was sent straight from Gmail rather than a bulk send. This likely caught the attention of recipients and encouraged them to click on the links within the email.
It is important to note that the treatment showed a significant 214% decrease in Clicks to Opens. This means that even though the treatment group had higher click-through rates, fewer people actually took the step to open the email. The treatment would have no impact on this though since the test was run on the email layout, not the content or email "envelope."
To use this learning in future experimentation, we might want to explore different formats that combine the organic feel of the full-width text with elements that entice recipients to open the email.
For example, we could experiment with subject lines that highlight the content or benefits of the email, as well as using personalization techniques to make the email feel even more tailored to each recipient.
Overall, this experiment has shown that the format of our weekly email can have a significant impact on recipient engagement. By continuing to test and iterate on different formats, we can improve our email performance and ultimately achieve our goal of increasing click-through rates while maintaining a high open rate.

experiments.title: How leading a membership ask email with the problem for non-members affects click-through rate and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading an email with the problem will increase email click-through rate because it will give the reader more context as to why they should renew their membership with Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2024 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran on the first email of the campaign for non-members to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how non-members would react to leading the email with a question that poses the problem Heritage is trying to solve rather than leading the email with a direct ask to become a member. In doing so, we believe users will be more likely to click through to the donation page and activate their membership because the leading question allows them to relate to the issue and want to be apart of the change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leading an email with a problem statement did not increase email click-through rates for new members. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 39% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. In addition, the treatment group saw a 61% decrease in donations with a 99.9% level of confidence.

Based on this result, it is clear that the approach of posing a problem as the lead in the email is not effective in motivating non-members to click through and make a donation. In future experimentation, it would be advisable to consider alternative approaches to engage non-members and encourage them to become members. This could involve highlighting the benefits of membership, showcasing successful outcomes of past campaigns, or offering exclusive content or promotions for members. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further research to understand the reasons behind the negative response to the problem statement approach, such as through qualitative interviews or surveys.

experiments.title: How a simplified message that focuses only on impact in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects conversions for lapsed members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the message and only focusing on the impact in the final email of the campaign will produce a higher conversion rate because the treatment email presents a more straightforward and concise message, which can be easier for recipients to understand quickly.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how a concise message that only focuses on their impact in the final email of the campaign would affect conversion rates. We believe a clear and simple call-to-action may lead to a better response as it reduces cognitive load for the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the message and focusing only on the impact in the final email of the campaign resulted in a higher conversion rate. The treatment email, which presented a more straightforward and concise message, saw a 37.4% increase in clicks compared to the control email with a 96.4% level of confidence. Additionally, we saw a 15.4% decrease in donor conversion with only a 43% level of confidence which tells us that the change in email copy did not affect the motivation to give on the donation page.
Based on this learning, future experimentation should prioritize clear and simple call-to-action messages that highlight the impact of the organization. By reducing cognitive load for the readers and making it easier for them to understand the value of their membership, we can improve the conversion rates and encourage more individuals to reactivate their memberships.
Additionally, it is important to continue conducting A/B testing to validate and refine these findings. By testing different variations of messages, designs, and strategies, we can further optimize our campaigns and ensure that we are effectively engaging our audience.

experiments.title: How a simplified message that focuses only on impact in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects conversions for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the message and only focusing on the impact in the final email of the campaign will produce a higher conversion rate because the treatment email presents a more straightforward and concise message, which can be easier for recipients to understand quickly.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for nonmembers to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how a concise message that only focuses on their impact in the final email of the campaign would affect conversion rates. We believe a clear and simple call-to-action may lead to a better response as it reduces cognitive load for the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the message and focusing on the impact in the final email of a campaign can lead to a higher conversion rate. The treatment email, which presented a more straightforward and concise message, resulted in a 9.5% increase in clicks for all traffic. Additionally, we saw a 6.2% decrease in donor conversion with only a 38.9% level of confidence which tells us that the change in email copy did not affect the motivation to give on the donation page.

To apply what was learned in future experimentation, it is important to continue to prioritize clear and simple call-to-action messages. By reducing cognitive load for the reader and making it easier for recipients to understand the message quickly, we can improve engagement and conversion rates. It is also important to monitor statistical confidence levels to ensure that the results of the experiments are reliable and can be confidently implemented.

experiments.title: How a new email sender impacts click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a new sender will produce a higher click-through rate, because it will increase curiosity among recipients, due to the email being sent from a new name.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2024 - 01/20/2024
Luther Seminary wanted to start sending housefile emails using a new employee. Instead of just switching to the new employee immediately, we tested how the housefile reacted to this new sender. We tested Luther Seminary’s normal housefile email sender vs. the new sender using a cultivation housefile email. For this test, we wanted to see how the new sender impacted the click-through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a new sender for housefile emails can significantly impact engagement. In this case, the treatment group saw a 21.6% increase in click-through rate and a 10.6% increase in opens compared to the control group.
This suggests that the new sender created curiosity among recipients, encouraging them to engage with the email. The results are statistically significant with a high level of confidence, indicating that this effect is likely to be real and not due to chance.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of using new senders on engagement metrics. This could involve using different employees or personas as email senders to see if the effect is consistent across different individuals or contexts.
Furthermore, it would be valuable to explore the long-term impact of using new senders. Do recipients continue to engage with emails from new senders over time, or does the effect diminish? Understanding the long-term effects can help refine the strategy for using new senders in housefile emails.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of considering the sender's identity in email marketing. It suggests that switching up the sender can generate curiosity and increase recipient engagement, ultimately leading to higher click-through and open rates.

experiments.title: How removing an image in an MMS message affects opt-out rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image from an MMS message for lapsed donors will achieve a decrease in opt-out rate because it looks less like marketing and more like a text a human would send.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running SMS and MMS ads as part of their kickoff campaign. They had leveraged texting because of the personal nature of the communication, two-way conversation ability, and high deliverability, and wanted to test a P2P MMS platform However, they wondered if their personal message was undermined by the image they sent alongside the message, since that’s not something that people usually send along with 1:1 communications.
They split their list in half and sent half of the list an MMS without an image to see if it increased conversion rate. This ended up converting the message to an SMS message, which should be noted.
However, they also wanted to monitor other metrics that might be affected by this, including opt-out rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing an image from an MMS message for lapsed donors resulted in a decrease in opt-out rate.

Based on this experiment, it can be concluded that lapsed donors may be more likely to disengage with a message stream that includes an image. The personal nature of the communication may be undermined by the image, as it might not be seen as a genuine message from a human.

When donors do not see a communication as "marketing", then you have the opportunity to transcend a transactional relationship and open a legitimate communication channel.

experiments.title: How leading a membership ask email with the problem for existing members affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading an email with the problem will increase email click-through rate because it will give the reader more context as to why they should renew their membership with Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran on the first email of the campaign for existing members in 2023 to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how existing members would react to leading the email with a question that poses the problem Heritage is trying to solve rather than leading the email with a direct ask to renew their membership. In doing so, we believe users will be more likely to click through to the donation page and renew their membership because the leading question allows them to relate to the issue and want to be apart of the change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leading an email with a question that poses the problem did not increase email click-through rate for existing members to renew their membership with The Heritage Foundation. In fact, the treatment group showed a 55.8% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. In addition, the treatment group saw a 23.5% decrease in donor conversion with a 97.6% level of confidence.

These findings suggest that existing members may not respond well to a problem-focused approach when it comes to renewing their membership. It is possible that they already have a strong understanding of the problem and are more interested in the direct ask to renew their support.

In future experimentation, it may be more effective to lead the email with a direct ask to renew membership rather than focusing on the problem. Additionally, it could be beneficial to test other strategies such as emphasizing the impact of their support or offering a specific benefit or incentive for renewing. These approaches may help to engage existing members and encourage them to click through and renew their membership.

experiments.title: How removing images on an email newsletter impacts click through rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing images for enewsletter readers will achieve a change in click through rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2024 - 01/26/2024
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station, and they have a weekly enewsletter featuring some of the top stories of the week that deploys every Thursday.
For this test, we wanted to see whether removing images and making the email look more like a human sent it made an impact on click through rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing images from the enewsletter resulted in a decrease in click-through rates. The treatment group, which received the enewsletter without images, had a 17.3% decrease in clicks compared to the control group.
Based on this result, it is evident that images play an important role in engaging readers and encouraging them to click on the content. Therefore, in future experimentation, it is recommended to continue including images in the enewsletter. However, it may be worth exploring different types of images or optimizing the placement and design of the images to maximize their impact on click-through rates.
Additionally, it is important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results. The experiment ran for only 1 day, which may not have allowed enough time for readers to adapt to the new format. Conducting experiments over a longer duration could provide more reliable insights.
Overall, the findings suggest that images are a valuable component of the enewsletter and should be used strategically to enhance reader engagement and increase click-through rates.

experiments.title: How Yes/No “Autonomy” Language Impacts Clicks on a CYE Exit Intent
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that yes/no autonomy language for users on a CaringBridge visit profile will achieve increase in click-thru because users are more likely to interact with a call-to-action that presents an easy “micro-yes.”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/11/2024
For CaringBridge’s CYE campaign, we hypothesized that Yes/No language on the call-to-action buttons would improve click-thru rate and send more traffic to the donation page. In the control, the call-to-action was “Donate Now” whereas in the treatment users were given a choice: “yes, I would like to give,” or “no, I’m not interested.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the hypothesis that yes/no autonomy language on the call-to-action buttons would improve click-thru rate and send more traffic to the donation page was proved to be incorrect. The treatment, which implemented the yes/no autonomy language, actually resulted in a 24.2% decrease in clicks for all traffic compared to the control.
This suggests that users were not more likely to interact with language that presents an easy "micro-yes" before being taken to the main donation page. In fact, it seems that the yes/no autonomy language may have confused or deterred users from clicking on the call-to-action buttons.
It should be noted that donor conversion rate wasn't captured for this experiment. It would be interesting to see the impacts downstream, whether less people making the commitment to "Yes, I would like to give" would still result in more conversions at the donation due to those users being more committed/motivated to give due to the micro-yes.

experiments.title: How a simplified product landing page that leads with value proposition impacts clicks to purchase
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified product landing page that leads with value proposition will increase clicks to purchase by increasing the perceived value of the product
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 12/05/2023
An organization was launching a brand new streaming series called RVL Discipleship: The Study. Unlike their traditional video series’ acquisition offers that are free with an instant donation opportunity, this would be a product sold through the Focus on the Family store.
The funnel at launch included a product landing page that pointed to the store page that pointed to the cart and checkout pages. The primary purpose of the product landing page was to increase interest in the streaming series and get visitors to click the button to purchase the series.
In reviewing the page, our team noticed that it led with a promo trailer and then offered a free episode. While there was some value proposition copy on the page, it was further down the page and overall, the user experience was very different than the experience of someone on one of their video series’ landing pages that we’ve refined through testing during the last three years. Instead of focusing on the product, there seemed to be almost as much time focused on explaining who the Ray Vander Laan was. We hypothesized that a treatment page that looked more like their video series’ landing pages, with a clear value-driven headline at the top and copy explaining the impact of the series would lead to more purchase clicks than the current design because it would provide greater clarity about the product and its impact on buyers.
We designed a new version of the page and used VWO to split traffic between both versions. We measured clicks to purchase on each and ran the experiment for about 2.5 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Simplifying the product landing page and leading with a clear value proposition can increase clicks to purchase.

2. The treatment page had a 47.2% increase in visits to the RVL Store Page for all traffic.

3. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 100.0%, indicating that the results are highly reliable.

4. By emphasizing the value and impact of the product, potential buyers are more likely to be interested and motivated to make a purchase.

Our next experiment will be removing the store page and cart pages from the funnel altogether so that visitors to the new landing page who click to purchase go straight to the store checkout page. We believe this will further remove friction and lead to more purchases of the product.

We did see 13 purchases on the treatment page while only 10 on the control, but our level of confidence for this increase in purchases was only 50%. More time and data would be needed to validate the impact on purchases.

experiments.title: How a specific donation ask in an email for non-donors impacted the email click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking non-donors for a gift of $10 or more instead of a gift of any amount will increase the click rate because of the added clarity in the copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2023 - 11/28/2023
Luther Seminary participated in Giving Tuesday and wanted to try to increase traffic to the donation page and hopefully also increase overall donations.
In order to achieve this, we wanted to see how adding a specified ask amount for nondonors would impact the click rate overall.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a specified ask amount for nondonors significantly increased the click rate overall. The treatment version, which asked for a gift of $10 or more, resulted in a 57.2% increase in cilcks with a high level of confidence.
This suggests that the added clarity in the copy, specifying the amount to be donated, is effective in motivating people to click and donate. It gives potential donors a concrete expectation and may make them more likely to take action.
However, for the overall donor conversion rate, there was a 4.4% increase in donation conversions at a 14.1% level of confidence. This means that while we can say this factor did lead to an increase in clicks, we can't with confidence say that this will always lead to an increase in donations.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing and refining the specified ask amount. Different donation amounts could be tested to see which has the greatest impact on click rate and donations. Additionally, exploring other ways to increase clarity and motivation in the copy, such as highlighting the impact of the donation, could also be tested.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of clear and specific messaging in fundraising campaigns. Providing a specific ask amount can help potential donors understand what is being asked of them and may increase their likelihood of taking action.

experiments.title: How adding clarity around the length of a survey impacted clicks on a donor email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding transparency about the length of a survey for donors will achieve an increase in survey participation because the expectations on the length of the survey are clearly communicated. because the expectations on the length of the survey are clearly communicated.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/06/2023 - 09/08/2023
When sending out a survey to donors, we wondered if including the number of questions in a survey within an email would enhance its appeal and clarity for recipients. We believe that by stating the number of questions upfront, recipients can gauge the time commitment required, making the survey seem more manageable and less daunting. This transparency builds trust and sets clear expectations, making the value proposition of participating in the survey more compelling. It assures recipients that their time is valued and that the survey won’t be overly lengthy or burdensome.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding transparency about the length of a survey can significantly increase survey participation. By clearly communicating the number of questions upfront, recipients are able to gauge the time commitment required and make an informed decision about participating in the survey.

The treatment group in this experiment experienced a 47.6% increase in clicks for all traffic, indicating that the transparency about the survey length was effective in capturing recipients' attention and motivating them to click. Additionally, the Clicks to Opens metric showed a substantial 42% increase, further suggesting that recipients found the survey more appealing and were more likely to engage with it.

To utilize this learning in future experimentation, it is important to continue incorporating transparency and clear communication in the design of survey invitations. Including information about the number of questions or the expected time commitment can help set realistic expectations and encourage more recipients to participate. It is also worth exploring other ways to enhance the appeal and clarity of survey invitations, such as emphasizing the value and impact of the survey or providing a brief overview of the topics covered.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of transparency and clear communication in engaging donors and increasing survey participation. By incorporating these elements in future experimentation, we can continue to improve the effectiveness of our survey invitations and ultimately gather more valuable insights from our donors.

experiments.title: How Facebook Audience+ impacted conversions

rows.name: PTV DA Christmas CD 2023 Advantage audience

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by using Facebook audience+ targeting for Facebook will achieve a higher conversion rate because it is a Facebook feature and Facebook tends to favor itself.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/08/2023 - 11/15/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Facebook is continually releasing new features to improve ad delivery and conversions.
For this test, we decided to test the Facebook Audience+ versus the traditional audience setup (open, housefile, web visitors, page likers, and LAL of all).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the traditional audience setup (open, housefile, web visitors, page likers, and LAL of all) performed better than the Facebook Audience+ targeting in terms of clicks, complete registrations, purchases, and cost per click.
The treatment using Facebook Audience+ targeting resulted in a 14.9% decrease in clicks for all traffic and a 497% decrease in purchases. The cost per click also increased by 111% and the cost per purchase increased by 879%.
Furthermore, certain segments of the audience also saw significant decreases in clicks, including the 45-54 age group (526% decrease), the 65+ age group (146% decrease), tablet visitors (351% decrease), and female visitors (157% decrease).
These results indicate that the Facebook Audience+ targeting did not perform as well as the traditional audience setup in terms of driving engagement and conversions.
In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue using the traditional audience setup for Facebook ads. It is important to remember that while Facebook may release new features and tools, it does not necessarily mean that they will always outperform the existing methods. Testing different targeting options and strategies is crucial to determine what works best for the specific audience and objectives.
Future test iterations, we will be testing the Facebook Advantage+ against open ad sets only while holding other ad sets the same.

experiments.title: How a timely and relevant subject line impacted clicks on a cultivation email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the relevancy of a subject line for cultivation email recipients will achieve an increase in email engagement because the timeliness of the message is increased.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2023 - 11/20/2023
When sending a cultivation email to the housefile, we wanted to see if we could increase clicks by calling attention in the subject line that the content of the message was seasonal and relevant. The control subject line drove curiosity “embracing the unpredictable” but we wondered if more people would engage with the email if we let them know it couldn’t wait to be opened or they would lose out on the timeliness of the article inside.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the relevancy of the subject line for cultivation email recipients resulted in a significant increase in email engagement, specifically the Clicks metric. The treatment subject line, which highlighted the timeliness of the article inside, outperformed the control subject line in terms of driving clicks and led to a 76% increase in clickthrough rate with 100% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it would be useful to explore subject lines that strike a balance between timeliness and curiosity. For example, a subject line that hints at seasonal and relevant content while also creating a sense of curiosity or intrigue might be effective in driving both opens and clicks.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of continuously testing and optimizing subject lines to maximize email engagement. By using data-driven insights, we can better understand what drives recipient behavior and tailor our messaging accordingly.

experiments.title: How clarity affects clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity of the ask for Facebook visitors will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because they understand what we are asking them to do..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/08/2023
Ben Carson’s American Cornerstone Institute was running ads promoting their Little Patriots Curriculum. On review, the ads that had performed best seemed to have a mismatch of creative and text. “Liberty the Eagle” was featured in the image, while the text asked the reader to pledge to teach kids founding principles.
Liberty the Eagle is a character in the Little Patriots curriculum, but that’s only encountered after they sign up — which means at the point of seeing the ad, the reader has no idea who Liberty is. They decided to replace this with a picture of Dr. Carson, a well-known figure. Once they did that, they realized they could change the “sign the pledge” ask to a “statement of belief” ask that is followed by the “sign the pledge” ask.
They launched two copy variants of the new ads to their audiences to see what worked best.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The importance of aligning the creative and text in an ad: The mismatch between the image of "Liberty the Eagle" and the text asking for a pledge to teach kids founding principles resulted in lower clickthrough rates. This suggests that the audience may not have understood the ask or the connection between the image and text.
2. Clarity in the call-to-action: Increasing the clarity of the ask for Facebook visitors led to a significant increase in clicks. This emphasizes the importance of clearly communicating the desired action or response from the audience.
3. The significance of statistical confidence: The treatment group showed a 218.6% increase in donations with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This high level of statistical confidence suggests that the results are reliable and not due to random chance.
To utilize these learnings in future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the creative and text in ads are aligned and coherent. Additionally, clear and explicit calls-to-action should be employed to improve engagement and conversions. It is also important to continue using statistical analysis to determine the significance of the results and validate the effectiveness of the changes made.

experiments.title: How a more direct value proposition affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that direct ask ads for Facebook users will achieve an increase in conversion rate because they already understand the story of the situation and the needs.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/20/2023
IMB was raising money for missionaries in the Holy Land. It was a high-urgency event, and they didn’t know which way of presenting the value proposition would resonate best. They created four versions of the ads:
A story-based approach
A more aggressive version of #1
A needs-based approach
A direct ask
Though they hypothesized that the direct ask would work best, they launched these ads across all audiences to determine which approach was most impactful.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the direct ask approach for Facebook ads was the most impactful in terms of generating clicks and achieving a high conversion rate. Despite the hypothesis that the direct ask approach would work best, it was important to run the experiment and gather data to confirm this assumption.
Based on the results, it is clear that Facebook users already understand the story and needs, and they respond well to a direct ask for support. This suggests that in future experimentation, the nonprofit should continue using the direct ask approach in their ads, as it has shown to be effective in increasing conversion rates.
Additionally, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different approaches and not relying solely on assumptions or hypotheses. By running experiments and analyzing the results, the nonprofit can make data-informed decisions and optimize their ads for maximum impact.
Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that the direct ask approach for Facebook ads resonates well with the audience and should be used in future experimentation and campaigns.

The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

2. Clicks to Opens, which indicates the engagement level of subscribers, also experienced a significant decrease with the treatment version. This suggests that the added link did not capture their attention or encourage them to take action.

3 Of those that received the treatment, only 30% clicked the first link while 70% clicked the second link.

4 Donations saw a 14% increase on the treatment but only a 74% level of confidence.

 

Based on these results, the addition of a second link did not increase engagement.

experiments.title: How adding clarity around the length of a survey impacted clicks from non-donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clearly communicating the length of a survey for non-donors will achieve an increase in survey participation because of the enhanced appeal and clarity of the time and effort involved..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/06/2023 - 09/08/2023
When sending a survey to a non-donor audience, we wondered if including the number of questions in a survey within an email would enhance its appeal and clarity for recipients. By stating the number of questions upfront, recipients can gauge the time commitment required, making the survey seem more manageable and less daunting. This transparency builds trust and sets clear expectations, making the value proposition of participating in the survey more compelling. It assures recipients that their time is valued and that the survey won’t be overly lengthy or burdensome.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Clearly communicating the length of a survey upfront enhances its appeal and clarity for recipients.

2. Providing the number of questions in the survey helps recipients gauge the time commitment required, making it seem more manageable and less daunting.

3. Transparency builds trust and sets clear expectations, making the value proposition of participating in the survey more compelling.

4. Clearly communicating the length of the survey resulted in a significant increase in both opens and clicks.

5. The treatment outperformed the control group with a 33.1% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with 99% level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, future experimentation should continue to focus on clear communication and setting expectations with recipients. Testing variations in the way the length of surveys is communicated, such as using different formats or emphasizing the time commitment in different ways, can provide further insights into what resonates most effectively with the target audience.

Additionally, the significant increases in Clicks for All Traffic indicate that clearly communicating the length of a survey motivates more people to click on the survey link if the perceived effort required seems low. Exploring other elements of the email or survey content that could enhance click-through rates can be valuable for future experimentation.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of transparency and setting clear expectations when communicating with non-donor audiences. By incorporating these learnings into future experimentation, Care Net can continue to optimize engagement and ultimately increase survey participation.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition of a homepage donation CTA impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding increased clarity to the value proposition language around the homepage CTA will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page by providing users with a better understanding of why they should make a gift and what their gift will actually do.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/17/2023
CBN is a Christian news, media, and distribution organization with a mission to preach the Gospel and demonstrate God’s love for the people of the world. The homepage of their website features an image with a Call-to-Action (CTA) that encourages readers to make a gift to CBN and support their work to share Christ’s love with the world.
For this test, we wanted to test into the impact of enhancing the value proposition language around this CTA by adding additional clarity, appeal, and credibility. We ran this test with the following variants:
Control: This variant featured a short and simple headline that said “Sharing the Love of Christ Around the World” above the “GIVE NOW” button
Treatment #1: This variant was designed to give readers a better understanding of how CBN shares Christ’s love by explaining that the reader’s gift to CBN will provide humanitarian aid and the Gospel to people. The headline in this variant said “Your gift right now will reach people with humanitarian aid and the Gospel of Jesus”
Treatment #2: Like Treatment #1, this variant added clarity to the headline, but it also added body copy that explained the problem more, starting with “Millions of people still lack access to the Gospel, hot food, and clean water but you can help change that.” It also reassured readers that 100% of their donation would support CBN’s mission. The final change made to this variant was that it would not display the hero image to mobile users. Instead, the headline and CTA would be displayed on a simple colored background to make it easier to read on mobile devices.
Our hypothesis is that the additional clarity provided in Treatment #1 and Treatment #2 would cause more users to click the “GIVE NOW” button and go to the donation page. We theorized that Treatment #2 would perform the best in this experiment because it added the most clarity to the value proposition language and was designed to make that language easier to read for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that enhancing the value proposition language around the Call-to-Action (CTA) on the homepage had a significant impact on user engagement. The treatment variant, which added clarity, appeal, and credibility to the value proposition, resulted in a 111.3% increase in clicks to the donation page.

These findings suggest that users responded positively to the added information about how their gift will be used to provide humanitarian aid and share the Gospel. Additionally, the inclusion of body copy that highlighted the problem and reassured readers about the use of their donation likely increased trust and motivation to give.

In preparing for this experiment, one concern we had was that the length of variation two could create too much friction and that web visitors would instead prefer the shorter copy of version 1. The results however found the opposite. The longer copy provided even more details about the impact of a donor's gift, leading to higher donor conversion. While running this experiment, we began testing these value propositions on the donation page as well. The visitors in those experiments were excluded from this one to keep the tests clean, but we will use those tests to further refine the value proposition provided for donating to CBN.

We attempted to dig into donor conversion on the main donation page, but had an issue with the UTMs being stripped on the traffic coming to the page from this CTA.

experiments.title: How an “Organic Status” Facebook Ad Affected Conversion Rate

rows.name: NA | The People's Pharmacy Cookbook Control
NA | The People's Pharmacy Cookbook Treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an “Organic Status” ad for Facebook users will achieve an increased conversion rate because  the ad will stand out amongst other sponsored posts..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/10/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station. We decided to test if an organic looking facebook post used as an ad increased clicks and conversion rates compared to their traditional looking ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning in this experiment is that Facebook users are more likely to click on an ad if it seems like it is an organic post. Because the ad looks like an organic status post, it stands out to users, so they don't immediately assume it's an ad and scroll past it. Making the ad stand out more to users increased the number of clicks by 49.5% with a 99.9% Level of Confidence & decreased the Cost per Click by 19.6% with a 99.9% Level of Confidence as well. I believe this positive effect was also impacted by the fact that the treatment seemed like a personal recommendation versus just a promotion of a product. It is important to note that these ads ran exclusively in-feed on Facebook. All other ad placements were turned off.
It is also interesting to note that the older the audience, the higher the conversion rate. This could be due to the fact that an older user may not be able to tell as easily a a younger user that it is an ad and not an organic post making them more likely to click on it.
Moving forward, we should continue to test this type of "Organic Status" ad on various types on content offers that appeal to varying types and ages of audiences. We should also try applying the strategy of making an ad look more organic in other ways, such as a normal status post, and maybe try an organic looking Instagram & Facebook story post as well.

experiments.title: How including gift array options in an email impacted clicks in a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a sample gift array for a direct email appeal will achieve an increase in engagement because there will be additional cognitive momentum to continue the process to the next step.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2023 - 07/13/2023
In a high urgency campaign, we typically keep the decisions isolated per each step. In the email step, we help the reader decide “Do I want to click?” and then at the donation page step we help the reader decide “How much do I want to give?” In one email, we wanted to test if adding gift array options at the email level would help more people begin thinking about both questions at once. The treatment version added a simple line of copy, letting the reader know that donations of all sizes and amounts were needed and welcomed and would qualify toward the campaign goal: “Whether you give $10, $100, $1,000 or more, every donation counts.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we learned by running this experiment is that adding a sample gift array in the email increased engagement: The treatment version, which included a line of copy mentioning various donation amounts, resulted in a 23.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that introducing a gift array at the email level can help in generating additional cognitive momentum and increasing engagement. We also believed this may have motivated more people who were considering making a gift but did not have all of the information needed to move forward with the decision, by reminding them that they could have the autonomy if they wanted to choose to give a gift of $10 or $100 and that all gifts would be counted.
Based on these learnings, future experimentation should consider incorporating sample gift arrays or suggested donation amounts in email appeals. This can potentially encourage donors to consider different giving options and increase their likelihood of clicking on the donation link. Additionally, these findings may also be useful in exploring the impact of dynamic gift arrays in other communication that are based on previous giving history—a tactic often used in direct mail letters that we could test applying in the digital space.

experiments.title: Will shortening a promotional email increase click-throughs?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening a promotional email for our general subscribers will achieve an increase in click-throughs because the email is more direct, human, and will create a higher sense of urgency by focusing exclusively on the upcoming ticket price increase.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2023 - 07/24/2023
At NIO Summit, there’s a lot going on. Beyond the in-depth sessions that dig deep into the most recent data and strategies in digital fundraising, there’s a whole theme that we lean into—heavily.
Each month when there’s a ticket deadline it’s often challenging to pick and choose which unique value of NIO to drive forward to the audience that will make them want to come. And often this leads to long emails with a lot of details.
After seeing a very parsed-down email from Sticker Mule about a deal on t-shirts that they were running, we wondered how that might apply to an upcoming ticket deadline promo we were planning to send to our subscribers.
We wanted to see if communicating all the many benefits of attending NIO Summit in a single email was creating mental friction. And whether a simpler, more direct email, focused on a single message might eliminate that friction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While both emails had about a 20% open rate, the treatment email resulted in a 138% increase in clicks. Beyond an increase in clicks, it’s important to note that 3 people who received the treatment email bought tickets to NIO Summit, while no tickets were bought by people who received the control email. 

This supports our hypothesis that our typical value-heavy promotional emails may be seen as cluttered and create mental friction. And that the shortened promotional email may increase click-throughs by creating more clarity and a higher sense of urgency.

We plan to test this same hypothesis in other contexts. Our next iteration will be to test this with an email releasing a new online fundraising resource. 

 

Winston's Analysis:

The key learning from this experiment is that shortening a promotional email to focus on a single message can lead to a significant increase in click-through rates. The treatment email, which was more direct and focused exclusively on the upcoming ticket price increase, resulted in a 138.4% increase in clicks for all traffic.

This suggests that providing a clear and urgent call-to-action, without overwhelming the audience with too much information, can be more effective in motivating subscribers to take action.

Furthermore, the experiment also revealed a 591% decrease in Clicks to Opens. This indicates that the shorter email may have created a higher sense of urgency, resulting in subscribers clicking on the email without spending much time engaging with its content.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of shorter, more focused emails on click-through rates. This can be applied not only to promotions for ticket deadlines but also to other types of email communications.

Additionally, it would be valuable to analyze the conversion rates and overall engagement of subscribers who clicked on the shorter email versus the longer email. This will help determine the quality of the clicks and the effectiveness of the treatment in driving desired actions.

Overall, the key takeaway is that simplicity and directness in email messaging can lead to higher engagement and click-through rates. Communicating a single clear message that creates a sense of urgency can be a powerful strategy in future email promotions.

experiments.title: How different types of graphics for Facebook ads affect click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a more inviting image with better value proposition for Facebook users will achieve a higher CTR because the treatment image is telling the user what value they will receive through these daily devotions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/10/2023
This ministry client wanted to work on increasing the click-through rate on their Facebook campaign for daily devotionals program.
We decided to do a radical redesign of the image and created a new image to test against the original to see if we could increase CTR and decrease CPC. The treatment image used a background image that was more inviting than the solid red background on the control.
The control image described WHAT the user can do (Connect with God every day), and the treatment image described HOW the user’s day can start by connecting with God (Start your day with purpose).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The control version performed better in terms of click-through rate compared to the treatment version. The treatment version had a 26.2% decrease in CTR with a 99.9% level of confidence. Despite using a more inviting image and emphasizing the value proposition, the treatment version failed to attract as many clicks as the control version.

When looking at the downstream metrics, the treatment also had:
A 4.7% decrease in email acquisition with a 31.7% LoC
 	A 0% conversion rate compared to the control with a 1.53% conversion rate (This is based on two gifts)

experiments.title: How using “Learn More” as a CTA impacted FB clicks on a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the level of commitment in the CTA language for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clicks and donation page views because the subtle ask to “learn more” introduces less friction than the direct ask to “donate now”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/29/2023
We were running a direct ask campaign on Facebook using the “Donate Now” button as the CTA and wondered if using a less direct CTA would help drive more donation page visitors. We kept all other elements of the ad the same and changed the button copy to “Learn More”. Our hypothesis was the “easier” option would create less friction by reducing the anxiety in the minds of the visitors of potentially making the full commitment without having enough information to make a decision first. If we could get them to give a “micro-yes” to learning more about the program we were asking them to support, we believed we could bring more visitors to the donation page from the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the CTA from "Donate Now" to "Learn More" resulted in a significant increase in clicks and donation page views. The treatment version, which had the "Learn More" CTA, saw a 48.8% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version with 97.7% level of confidence so the experiment results are valid. We also saw a 30% decrease in cost per click on the "Learn More" version.
This suggests that the direct ask of "Donate Now" may have created friction and anxiety for the viewers, causing them to hesitate or be reluctant to click on the ad. By offering a lower level of commitment with the "Learn More" CTA, viewers may have felt more comfortable and willing to engage with the ad.
When reviewing results, we also noted that one additional reason the treatment could have outperformed the control is that Facebook, by default, includes a subheader message when the "Donate Now" button is selected that says "Not affiliated with Meta" which could also be introducing more friction for visitors and introducing anxiety if the message appears suspicious or untrustworthy. This default subheader also replaces the available room to display the full headline so the treatment version has the benefit of showing the full headline of "Help equip future conservative leaders" which offers more clarity than the cut off version "Help equip future..."

experiments.title: How clarity in a subject line impacted email clicks

rows.name: Looking out for your child’s education
A free educational program for kids

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to a subject line for email recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because recipients will gain a strong initial idea of what the message is about and what it might offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/25/2023 - 06/08/2023
We want to test three different subject lines for ACI’s Little Patriots Pledge email to see which generates the most clicks. The purpose of the email is to send recipients to the pledge landing page where they would pledge to use and expand the Little Patriots curriculum.
The control subject line broadly mentions education
Treatment 1 subject line mentions a specific type of education
Treatment 2 subject line mentions an actual product – a “free educational program”
Let’s see which approach towards clarity in a subject line generates the most email clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Clarity proved to be the key to this subject line test. Each treatment produced a valid increase in email clicks. Treatment 1 produced a 145.8% increase in clicks while treatment 2 produced a 158.7% increase.

Our audience responded well to clarity. They appreciated knowing more information as they were considering opening the email, and surely the "free" callout in treatment two resonated strongly with them.

Interestingly, when looking at open rate, treatment 2 resulted in a decrease while treatment 1 resulted in a 2.6% increase with a 98% level of confidence. So treatment 2 produced a significant increase in opens and clicks.

Moving forward, we should implement more subject lines that focus on clarity, giving our audience up front transparency and value.

experiments.title: How language specific to existing contacts impacted Facebook clicks retest

rows.name: FBD 350 - Join Us Reach - May 21 member control
FBD 350 - Join Us Reach - May 21 member treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by making ad copy specific to FBD contacts for existing contacts on Facebook will achieve a higher conversion in clicks because they are more personalized to their relationship with First Dallas. because  they are more personalized to their relationship with First Dallas.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2023 - 05/20/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Leading up to Sunday services, First Dallas utilizes Facebook to share what the sermon will be and invites people to join in person.
This campaign also targets existing First Dallas contacts through the HubSpot/Facebook integration. We decided to test and see if copy that is specific to this audience would increase clicks on the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment for First Baptist Dallas is that creating ad copy specific to their existing contacts on Facebook can drive higher conversion in clicks. The treatment resulted in a 20.9% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a high statistical level of confidence of 97.4%. Additionally, Cost per Click experienced a 13.5% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, and there was an increase in clicks from both mobile and tablet visitors.
Based on these findings, First Baptist Dallas should continue to personalize their ad copy for their existing contacts on Facebook and consider testing different variations of personalized copy in future experiments. They should also explore other ways to personalize their messaging to their audience, such as tailoring images or targeting specific demographics. By continuing to experiment and optimize their Facebook ad campaigns, First Baptist Dallas can maximize their conversion and engagement rates.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post for a Facebook ad affects click-through rate and other downstream metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making an ad look like an organic post for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher click-through rate because it feels more human and not like advertising.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2023 - 05/02/2023
Several organizations have tested using an organic-looking post as a Facebook ad and have seen a significant decrease in cost per click and a significant increase in click-through rate compared to standard marketing ads.
ACI is testing this concept on its Pro-Life petition campaign in Facebook. To test this, we built two “organic” ads that consisted of just an image to make it appear as an organic post. We will compare the performance of these two ads to our original top-performing ad, which is a standard marketing ad with ad copy and an image.
The ads are sharing the same budget and are targeting the same audiences in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our organic posts did not provide the results we were looking for. Treatment 1 saw a 56.6% increase in click-through rate with a 100% level of confidence and treatment 2 saw a 10.8% increase also with a 62.8% level of confidence. While click through rate is improved, other initial metrics including CPM and CPC worsened, and end-of-funnel metrics (donations and GCPD) also worsened compared to the control.
Spend - Control ($1,445), Treatment 1 ($297), Treatment 2 (70)
CPM - Control ($3.60), Treatment 1 ($9.20), Treatment 2 ($5.45)
CPC - Control ($0.59), Treatment 1 ($1.01), Treatment 2 ($0.80)
% Sign Up - Control (34.2%), Treatment 1 (61.5%) - 35.6% increase in email conversion with a 100 LoC, Treatment 2 (53.5%)
ICR - Control (6.51%), Treatment 1 (3.16%) - 51.4% decrease with a 95.4% LoC, Treatment 2 (0.00%)
GCPN - Control ($2.09, Treatment 1 ($1.88), Treatment 2 (1.83)
GCPD - Control ($-2), Treatment ($32), Treatment 2 (null)
ROI - Control (1.06), Treatment 1 (0.45), Treatment 2 (null)
Based on the results of other organizations, this type of treatment typically produces higher CTR, lower CPC and CMP, and therefore lower GCPD. In this instance, the treatments resulted in higher CTR, higher CPC, and higher GCPD. CPMs for the control were also better than the two treatments.
The overall learning is that organic-looking ads did not perform at the beginning of the funnel - higher CPC and higher CPMs - nor at the end of the funnel - lower ICR and higher GCPD.
Image and likeness of ACI's Founder Dr. Ben Carson historically leads to high performance. The control ad for this experiment includes an image of Dr. Carson, which may contribute significant engagement at the beginning and throughout the funnel.

Another organization tested using an organic-looking post as a Facebook ad and saw a significant decrease in cost per click and a significant increase in click-through rate.
MSLF decided to test this concept on their Idaho Stockwater petition campaign in Facebook. To test this, they had one campaign with a typical-looking Facebook ad and a second campaign with just an image that looked like an organic post.
Each campaign had the same budget and was targeting the same audiences in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The organic post caused a decrease of 32.1% in click-through rate with a 99.9% level of confidence. Although our click-through rate decreased, the organic post positively affected most of the downstream metrics:

Spend - Control ($413, Treatment ($409)

CPM - Control ($40.20), Treatment ($11.82)

CPC - Control ($1.15), Treatment ($0.50)

% Sign Up - Control (69.7%), Treatment (76.7%)

Emails - Control (251), Treatment (628) - 10% increase in email conversion with a 98.6% LoC

ICR - Control (2.39%), Treatment (1.27%) - 41.4% decrease in donor conversion with a 63.8% LoC

Donors - Control (6), Treatment (8)

GCPN - Control ($1.65), Treatment ($0.65)

GCPD - Control ($69), Treatment ($51)

ROI - Control (0.33), Treatment (0.38)

Because traffic was so much cheaper to get at the Facebook ad level (CPM and CPC), we saw our downstream metrics perform better due to the volume of users going through the funnel. Although our ICR decreased by 41.4%, it did not negatively affect our GCPD which was $18 cheaper for the organic ads.

experiments.title: How anniversary language and monthly donor language affect clickthrough rate on a monthly giving popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding anniversary language and changing “partner” to “monthly donor” for return visitors will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/08/2022
CaringBridge had used popups to ask repeat visitors to consider giving a monthly gift. While the performance had built a file of recurring donors, they wanted to see if they could increase the clickthrough rate and conversion rate to monthly donor. They formed two hypotheses:
Using language highlighting their 25th anniversary would increase relevance and clickthrough rate and conversion rate
Using “monthly donor” rather than “CaringBridge partner” would increase the clarity of the ask and increase conversion rate.
They decided to test this with an A/B/C test. Their first treatment introduced the 25th anniversary language, but kept the “partner” language. The second kept the 25th anniversary language, but introduced “monthly donor” in place of “partner”.
They launched this to determine a winning variant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
They found that, on the basis of clickthrough rate, the added relevance of the 25th anniversary mention increased clickthrough rate by 16.2% with an 88.9% level of confidence. However, the combination of the 25th anniversary language and the "monthly donor" language increased clickthrough rate by 44.9%.
Previous testing had shown that an increase in clickthrough rate did not always correlate to more recurring donors, so they needed to also examine downstream data to see how that traffic converted.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post for a Facebook ad affects click-through rate and other downstream metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making an ad look like an organic post for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher click-through rate because it feels more human and not like advertising.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2023 - 05/02/2023
Several organizations have tested using an organic-looking post as a Facebook ad and have seen a significant decrease in cost per click and a significant increase in click-through rate compared to standard marketing ads.
ACI is testing this concept on its Pro-Life petition campaign in Facebook. To test this, we built two “organic” ads that consisted of just an image to make it appear as an organic post. We will compare the performance of these two ads to our original top-performing ad, which is a standard marketing ad with ad copy and an image.
The ads are sharing the same budget and are targeting the same audiences in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our organic posts did not provide the results we were looking for. Treatment 1 saw a 23.3% decrease in click-through rate with a 99.9% level of confidence and treatment 2 saw a 44.1% decrease also with a 99.9% level of confidence. Initial metrics and downstream metrics proved discouraging with the exception of petition sign up rate:
Spend - Control ($413, Treatment ($409)
CPM - Control ($10.88), Treatment 1 ($9.47), Treatment 2 ($5.65)
CPC - Control ($0.88), Treatment 1 ($1.16), Treatment 2 ($1.04)
% Sign Up - Control (45.9%), Treatment 1 (62.3%) - 35.6% increase in email conversion with a 100 LoC, Treatment 2 (59.4%)
ICR - Control (4.81%), Treatment 1 (3.18%), Treatment 2 (0.00%)
GCPN - Control ($1.93, Treatment 1 ($1.86), Treatment 2 (null)
GCPD - Control ($-2), Treatment ($31), Treatment 2 (null)
ROI - Control (1.04), Treatment 1 (0.46), Treatment 2 (null)
Based on the results of other organizations, this type of treatment typically produces higher CTR, lower CPC and CMP, and therefore lower GCPD. However, in this instance, the treatments resulted in lower CTR, higher CPC, and higher GCPD. CPMs did decrease with the treatments, though only marginally for treatment 1.
Notably, the sample size was out of whack. Next time we run this type of experiment, we should split the treatment(s) into a separate campaign and split the overall campaign budget between the control and treatment to ensure relatively equal spend. Still, the sample sizes were significant enough here to exceed the required sample size and reach a significant level of confidence.
The overall learning is that organic-looking ads did not perform at the beginning of the funnel - higher CPC than the control and lower CTR - nor at the end of the funnel - lower ICR and higher GCPD.
Image and likeness of ACI's Founder Dr. Ben Carson historically leads to high performance. The control ad for this experiment includes a video message from Dr. Carson, which may contribute significant engagement at the beginning and throughout the funnel. Video historically produces higher CPM and lower CPC, but in this case, CPC remained lower than the treatments and donor conversion metrics for the control vastly outperformed the treatments

experiments.title: How referencing last gift information for lapsed donor segment impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including last gift date and amount for lapsed donors will achieve a higher conversion of donors because donors are reminded of their last gift and are encouraged to make another gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2023 - 04/19/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
As we look to reactivate lapsed donors (no gift in over 12 months) we decided to test including information on their last gift date and amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by Pathway to Victory tested the inclusion of last gift date and amount for lapsed donors in their donation appeal email. The hypothesis was that reminding donors of their last gift would encourage them to make another donation. However, the results showed a significant decrease in Clicks to Opens for All Traffic (33.1%) with a high level of confidence (98.9%).
On the other hand, the treatment version saw a substantial increase in donations (2,669.6%) (1 donation vs 21 donations) with a level of confidence of 100%. This suggests that including last gift date and amount will cause recipients to make a donation.
When looking at last gift amount compared to the gift given, on average we saw a -$3 in their gift amount. However, the average length of reactivated donors was 3 years since last gift!

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad radical redesign impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by offering to pray for Facebook viewers will achieve higher click through rate because it is a tangible thing that people may be looking for .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/20/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each Sunday morning First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share live now ads to invite Facebook ad viewers to join the church on their online campus named iCampus.
Over the last few weeks, Facebook’s ad performance has been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to do a radical experiment by testing the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series vs asking if they have a prayer need.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment conducted by First Baptist Dallas involved testing two different types of Facebook ads: one that offered to pray for viewers and another that advertised the message and sermon series. The hypothesis was that the ad offering prayer would achieve a higher click-through rate. However, the experiment's results showed a 15.1% decrease in clicks for all traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group.
The key learning from this experiment is that offering to pray for viewers may not be an effective strategy for increasing click-through rates for Facebook ads. However, we saw increased prayer requests received and interaction with the iCampus site. So while the Prayer ad had a lower click conversion rate, we did see greater engagement through it.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of calls to action impacted clicks in a cultivation message
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a single call to action for cultivation message readers will achieve an increase in engagement because the isolated option will reduce potential friction caused by decision fatigue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/01/2023
When preparing a cultivation message around a specific topic of interest, we found the organization offered a lot of relevant resources. The control copy gave the reader a full menu to select from. They could listen to a podcast, read a post, or explore stories from people personally impacted by the program. We wondered if having this many choices could result in decision fatigue and cause the reader to not engage with ANY of the options. We developed a treatment to test this theory and isolated the choice to read a single article. We believed the treatment would help the reader have clarity by establishing a clear call to action. We split the audience 50/50 and measured the impact on clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we discovered through this experiment was the treatment resulted in a 95.8% increase in clicks with a 100% statistical level of confidence. The key learning from this experiment is that presenting a single call to action for cultivation message readers can significantly increase engagement by reducing decision fatigue. This means that the control copy that offered a full menu of options may have overwhelmed readers and resulted in lost engagement opportunities. In future experimentation, it is recommended to simplify the call to action and reduce options to reduce the risk of decision fatigue and improve engagement rates.
