experiments.title: How introducing a felt need affects clickthrough rate on site start remarketing ads
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addressing the “why” for potential site creators will achieve an increase in click thru rate  because we are addressing their personal needs and providing our services as the solution. . because  we are addressing their personal needs and providing our services as the solution.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/14/2023
CaringBridge was running remarketing ads in Facebook in order to recapture traffic that may have been interested to start a new site with CaringBridge. In an effort to increase click thru rate we proposed 3 different ad variations with 3 different motivations. We wanted to see which of these motivations would resonate more with their audiences.
The first treatment highlighted the privacy that a CaringBridge site offers. The second treatment addresses the felt need for the individual and how a CaringBridge site keeps you connected to others. The third treatment emphasizes how you can journal your progress on the site to continue getting support with others.
All three ads ran congruently on Facebook with the original remarketing ad which focused more on efficiency.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The winning variant for this experiment was treatment #2 with a valid 31.8% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 95.1% level of confidence. The other two ads had almost no difference in click thru rates. This informs us that treatment #2 more effectively connected with the reader's felt need with the first sentence of the ad being: "The moments you need help most are precisely when its hardest to ask for it." This is a sentence that introduces empathy and gets to the heart of why someone needs support in their health journey. In fact the first treatment may have introduced a level of anxiety because of the mention of publicly sharing their personal life.
It's recommended that we test similar language on the landing page itself in order to increase new site creations after people click through so that the messaging of their journey is consistent.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that plain-text emails will achieve a higher click rate than a html design (newsletter style) email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2023 - 03/15/2023
In effort to test new and valuable content to our active subscribers, we decided to send an email digest of recent experiments we’ve tested to our house file. Since we have over 4,800+ experiments in our library, sometimes it’s easy to lose track of the current experiments.
Since this was a new concept, we decided to test two variants of emails.
Each email contained the same content but one was a html design and the other was a plain-text design. To add context, we normally send plain-text emails to our house file.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Plain-text emails performed better in terms of clicks compared to html design newsletters.
2. Plain-text emails also had a higher open rate and click-to-open rate.
3. The experiment results had a high level of statistical confidence.
Moving forward, we should consider sending more plain-text emails to our house file as it seems to be a more effective approach for engaging our subscribers. We should also continue to test different email formats to see if there are any other changes we can make that will improve our email engagement metrics. Ultimately, we need to prioritize the preferences of our audience and find the best ways to communicate with them effectively.

First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each Sunday morning First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share live now ads to invite Facebook ad viewers to join the church on their online campus named iCampus.
Over the last few weeks, Facebook’s ad performance has been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to do a radical experiment by testing the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series vs asking if they have a prayer need.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by First Baptist Dallas showed that the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series outperformed the ad that asked for prayer requests in terms of clicks for all traffic. However, the results did not reach statistical significance due to a lack of minimum sample size.
The key learning from this experiment is that addressing the felt need of the viewer may not necessarily result in higher ad clicks. It is important to continue testing and experimenting with different ad copy and messaging to determine what resonates best with the target audience. Future experimentation should also focus on achieving a larger sample size to increase statistical significance in the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors such as timing and targeting in the ads to further improve performance.

experiments.title: How adding a supporting clickable image to a cultivation email impacted email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a clickable image in support of the email content will increase the number of email clicks because humans can process images faster than text and the image adds a visual representation of the topic at hand.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2023 - 03/07/2023
For a monthly cultivation email, The Illinois Policy Institute wants to determine whether including a related image within the email would be beneficial to increasing the number of email clicks. The control email consists of standard copy with no images or graphics, and the treatment version is the same copy but with the addition of a clickable image halfway through the copy. The image highlights a statistic that is pertinent to the email topic. The treatment copy includes the image in hopes of capturing the viewer’s attention for longer and more effectively. It also gives them an additional opportunity to click.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment which included a clickable and related image within the cultivation email produced a 21.1% increase in the number of clicks. The increase was statistically significant with a 99.9% level of confidence. The results tell us that the inclusion of the image led to a more comprehensive experience for the user as it provided a visual representation of the topic being discussed throughout the email. This improved the clarity of the email topic as it added evidential support that was easy for the reader to digest. In turn, the email approach reduced potential cognitive friction for the user which resulted in an increase in clicks. The increase in clicks can also be attributed to the fact that the image provided an extra opportunity for users to click.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including the price of a book for Facebook users will achieve a higher click conversion because the user knows the cost upfront.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2023 - 03/21/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
PTV decided to try out a new revenue stream by promoting items from their store for a set price rather than a generous gift. With this new revenue stream, we decided to test on the Facebook ad if showing the price of the item would increase click conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to determine if showing the price on a Facebook ad for a book would increase click conversion rate. However, results showed the control version had a 40.8% higher click conversion rate than the treatment. Complete Registrations, Cost per Click, and Cost per Registration also experienced decreases, while visitor groups, including 65+, mobile, tablet, desktop, female, and unknown, had significant decreases in clicks.
The key learning from this experiment is that including price on a Facebook ad did not increase click conversion rate but instead decreased it. In future experimentation, it may be worth testing other variables on Facebook ads, such as language, design, or audience targeting to increase click conversion rates. It is also essential to determine the target audience and tailor the ad accordingly. Overall, experimentation is a valuable tool for discovering what works best for promoting revenue streams in Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How images and video of a personality impacted clicks on an eBook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using images and videos of a well-known figure for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because this introduces more credibility and authority than using an eBook image alone.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/02/2023
When running ads for an eBook offer, we wondered if the cover of the eBook was appealing enough to drive interest from Facebook ad viewers. In previous testing on other offers, we had found success in using the organization’s founder’s name and photo. We wondered if we could achieve similar success by featuring images and videos in the ads that were relevant to the eBook’s contents. The first treatment had a background of an American flag to serve as a “trigger image” that can help with familiarity as users scroll. The second treatment was a 30-second video quoting Martin Luther King, Jr. and the third treatment was a 30-second video urging the American people to work together and not treat each other as enemies. We launched all versions of the ad and let them run for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we saw valid decreases in clickthrough rate for the first and second treatments, the third treatment that included a 30-second video urging the American people to work together and not treat each other as enemies achieved a 44.0% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. We believe this video saw a lift in engagement and effectiveness due to the relevancy of the messaging that gave a sneak peek into how the topic of the eBook was discussed. The video that produced the lift also had an enhanced sense of urgency compared to the other treatments that could have positively impacted performance. Future experimentation should focus on incorporating relevant and credible images and videos in the ad campaigns, accompanied by concise and compelling messages that resonate with the target audience.

experiments.title: How a more direct call to action in a Facebook ad impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that concisely communicating the call to action for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because it kicks off the cognitive momentum to keep things congruent with the landing page copy.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/02/2023
In a previous test on this offer, we tested short copy vs. longer copy and found that the longer copy version motivated more visitors to click the ad. In this next iteration, we wanted to test back into using shorter copy but installing the learning from the previous experiment which was that visitors needed clarity about the pledge they would be signing before they would commit to taking the next step. So this time, we kept the outline of the four pillars they would be agreeing to in the pledge in the copy, but removed all the extra lead-in copy. The treatment also introduced checkbox emojis to indicate each statement of the pledge they would be agreeing with if they signed.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that concise communication of the call to action in Facebook ads significantly increases clickthrough rates. By removing unnecessary lead-in copy and emphasizing the four pillars of the pledge with checkbox emojis, the treatment achieved a 15.2% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This suggests that visitors respond well to clear, concise messaging that ensures congruence with the landing page. In future experiments, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of concise messaging and strong calls to action that align with the landing page content.

First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each Sunday morning First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share live now ads to invite Facebook ad viewers to join the church on their online campus named iCampus.
Over the last few weeks the Facebook ad performance as been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to test the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series vs more of an invite to join iCampus.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment confirmed that the traditional ad with copy about message and sermon series performed better than more of an invitation to join iCampus. A clear takeaway would be to focus advertisement around the message, experience and energy within the church, rather than promoting direct digital connection via iCampus, as the traditional ad was much more successful in driving results. Ultimately, the key learning is that the content of the message matters a great deal when marketing faith-based institutions. Therefore, when creating ads for a similar type of nonprofit, the effectiveness of the ad should be centered around the message rather than a platform for connection.

experiments.title: How the use of a well-known figure influences action in an email

We believe that

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2023 - 02/10/2023
The American Cornerstone Institute was launching their new Little Patriots site. Due to some unexpected press coverage, they got a flood of traffic to the new site which brought it down. They wanted to use this as an opportunity to update their subscribers on the impact and let them know that the site is now accessible. This email proved to be an opportunity to drive more traffic to the program so they decided to run a test on what content would drive more interest. They decided to test two angles: “Popularity” vs. “Dr. Carson” with popularity being about the huge interest nationwide and Dr. Carson being about how his activities are tied to these efforts. This email was sent to a small subset of the subscriber file with the winner being rolled out the the entire audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that talking about Dr. Carson as the reason for the crash of the site was not more motiviating than just talking about the huge interest throughout the country. This suggests that users are more likely to respond to tangible and proven success stories versus abstract stories, even if they involve a figure they trust. This can help inform future experiments and help target audiences more accurately with messaging that’s more likely to resonate. We should focus on stories and results that are easy to understand, have visual appeal, and showcase authoritative figures that evoke trust and have real-world results.

experiments.title: How urgency language impacted Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Which element of urgency will drive response limited time or few left in stock?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/16/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
Each year PTV releases a new daily devotional book with 260 days of encouragement and teachings.
To boost response in the final days of the devotional being offered we decided to test whether an urgency of time or an urgency of exclusivity would drive results on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment provided key learnings around the use of urgency to drive response on a Facebook campaign. The results showed that creating a sense of exclusivity, or creating an urgency of few left in stock, yielded 30.2 % more clicks compared to the control which only used an urgency of time. This illustrates the value of using scarcity when crafting a message on a digital campaign. Going forward, creating a sense of exclusivity, combined with a sense of limited time can be used across all types of marketing campaigns to further drive response. If a campaign is not time-sensitive, encouraging users to act quickly due to limited availability can be an effective way to increase response rates.
In addition to driving a click response, we saw a significant impact on donations. The few left in stock generated a 49.6% increase in donor conversions with an 87.1% level of confidence (13 to 75 donations)!
For revenue, we saw a 22.6% increase with a 70.9% level of confidence.
While donations and revenue did not yield confidence levels over the required 95%, we did see directional lifts in a positive direction.

experiments.title: How an invite to church impacted Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by inviting people to church for Facebook viewers we will achieve a higher click conversion because it is more personal and invitational.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/20/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each week First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share the upcoming Sunday’s message and sermon series.
Over the last few weeks the Facebook ad performance as been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to test the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series vs more of an invite to attend church.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment has shown us that the more personal and invitational approach to the Facebook ad resulted in higher click conversions. We saw a 11.1% increase in Clicks when compared with the traditional approach. This demonstrates the potential impact of creating a more personal and warmer welcome to church via Facebook ads. For future experiments, we should continue to focus on creating more inviting and conversational advertisements to increase engagement and conversions.

experiments.title: How the use of story in an email appeal impacts click thru rate and donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that continuing the story on a donation page  for email recipients will achieve a higher click thru rate and donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2023
During Giving Tuesday the Navigators wanted to use a story for their email appeal. We wanted to see if we could get more people to click thru the email if we continued the story in the donation page as opposed to providing the entire story in the email. This would accomplish two things: reduce the length of the email (which we hypothesized would cause less people to click thru to give) and give people incentive to click thru so that they can land on the donation page, read the rest of the story and give. In turn, the treatment would increase click thru rate and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: We successfully increase click thru rate by 90% with a 100% level of confidence. However, when looking at donor conversion rate, we saw a valid 62% decrease with a level of confidence of 99%. So, while many more people were motivated to click thru, those were not the people who were motivated to give. These people could've just been interested in the story but had no intention to give a gift. It's recommended that this is retested to understand how to best use stories for the Navigators audience. It's recommended that the control is set as-is and the treatment would be split into two emails: one email that's pure cultivation with the testimonial/story (no ask), and a follow up email referencing the story and including a direct appeal. It could be that splitting up the experience in this way, captures all those who were interested in the story. It allows everyone to see the evidential and opportunity for them to give later.

experiments.title: How a $10 vs $15 ask for nondonors impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a $15 ask compare to a $10 ask for nondonors via email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2023 - 02/06/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Church Dallas. Through radio, television, books, and digital resources, PTV shares the truth of God’s Word in an effort to pierce a darkening culture.
For February 2023, Pathway to Victory highlighted one of their books “Choosing the Extraordinary Life.” Historically this offer has had a mediocre response to activating donors. Therefore in an effort to increase donor conversion, we decided to test a $10 vs $15 ask.
Several months ago we tested the PTV usual approach of a generic “generous gift of any amount” vs $10 and found these results https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4544
For this month, we decided to test a $10 vs $15 ask for nondonors to see if we could increase the entry point while still converting at the same level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
On the email level, we saw that clicks experienced a 20.2% increase with a 96% confidence level.
Digging into donations between the 2 variants we saw a 17.2% decrease in donations with a 58.6% confidence level.
For revenue, we saw a 12.8% decrease with a 65.9% level of confidence.
With the average gift for the $10 ask being $33.25 and the average gift for the $15 ask at $35.21.
What remained positive is the trend of not having gifts less than $5. When we tested this back in September with "generous gift" vs $10 we saw that the $10 version had fewer "low ball" donations as people met the goal of the amount presented or even exceeded.
For this test, we saw the same trends with
$10 ask gifts being
8 - $10 gifts2 - $15 gifts1 - $20 gift19 - $25 gifts5 - $50 gifts1 - $75 gift 4 - $100 gifts
For the $15 ask, we saw
1 - $7 gift2 - $10 gifts6 - $15 gifts5 - $20 gifts11 - $25 gifts1 - $30 gift1 - $40 gift2 - $50 gifts2 - $75 gifts1 - $100 gift1 - $250 gift
What is additionally interesting is that the gift array on the donation page is $25, $50, $100, and $250. Meaning that for a majority of these donations, the user typed in the different amount.
For gifts of $75 or over, the user receives a bundle of resources.
These results are based on a single email sent to PTV nondonors, we will continue testing amounts to find the sweet spot for PTV nondonors and their activation.

We do want to note that sending at 10am increased open rates with 100% level of confidence. However, we consider clicks to be a more meaningful measurement of engagement due to Apple Mail policies that interfere with our ability to record true open rates.

experiments.title: How Facebook ad imagery impacts clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which type of Facebook ad image will have the highest clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2017 - 12/15/2017
Illinois Policy Institute recently discovered that showing a cartoon image in a Facebook ad decreased clickthrough rate. They decided to create two new treatments based to test against the control. The first treatment included an image of Michael Madigan–the state legislator pushing for an increase in taxes. The second treatment included an image of a house, to align with the subject of property taxes. They wondered: Which Facebook ad approach will have the highest clickthrough rate? They launched a 3-part test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Illinois Policy Institute found that the first treatment using Madigan’s image increased clickthrough rate by 70%, while the second treatment using the house image actually decreased ad clickthrough rate by 23%. This experiment is a good example of how an active A/B testing approach can lead to incremental performance when done consistently. Despite experiencing a drop in the second treatment, the learning in the first treatment outweighed the impact and will now be implemented as the winner.

It’s apparent that the use of Madigan’s image increased audience motivation. Many Illinoisans are familiar with this particular legislator. When connected with the topic of a potential property tax hike and their ability to take action to stop it, response rate went up significantly.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition language to a direct appeal email impacted click through rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a value proposition-enhanced email for email recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2022 - 12/30/2022
As part of a high-urgency campaign for a public policy client, we emailed subscribers to their weekly newsletter, The Policy Shop, asking if they would make a donation in support of The Policy Shop. The email was short and sweet, including a cartoon, a thanks for being a part of The Policy Shop, and an ask for support.
We wondered how a more in-depth, value proposition-focused email would impact clicks (and donations). The treatment version highlights the problem(s) that The Policy Shop helps solve, provides background on The Policy Shop, provides evidentials on the impact of The Policy Shop, and tells the recipients how their gift in support of The Policy Shop will be used. The treatment also takes a more personal approach by greeting the recipient by name and including a raw donation link instead of a button and graphic.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in a valid 20.5% decrease in click rate. The brief email with a cartoon proved to be more click-friendly than the longer email with no graphic. Some users may have even clicked the cartoon to see what it led to before even reading the email and noticing that its purpose was to ask for support. What is far more telling with this experiment, however, is that the treatment produced 6 donations compared to none from the control. So although the treatment produced fewer clicks, it did produce a far better donor conversion rate, which is more valuable. This proves that the value proposition-heavy email was more valuable. Those who clicked the treatment email were more aware of the impact The Policy Shop has and why it needs support to continue to have such an impact. They had greater clarity on why The Policy Shop is so important. This clarity and motivation gained from the value proposition in the treatment could not be gained from the control. The experiment stresses the importance of value proposition copy and proves that value proposition should be a strong focus in emails going forward.

rows.name: Request your free devotional before tomorrow
Wanted you to have this before Sunday

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more casual subject line for housefile members will achieve an increase in click rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2022 - 11/29/2022
As part of DTS’s year-end campaign, we sent a free content offer to the housefile. We wanted to learn how their audience would respond to a subject line that sounded more casual like a one-to-one conversation with a human being compared to a subject line that used more formal language that is standard for the organization. Our hypothesis was that readers would find it more appealing to receive an email that sounded like it was from a friend rather than one that sounded like it was from the Seminary. We split the housefile 50/50 between the control and the treatment and evaluated the engagement based on clicks in the email for both segments.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed after running the email test, was there was a 19.7% decrease in clicks on the treatment version with 99.9% level of confidence so the experiment results are valid. We believe this decrease was due to another element of value proposition included in the subject line for the control version that was missing from the treatment. "Request your free devotional" gives the email subscriber a glimpse into the contents of the email and gives them more specificity about what they can expect to see inside if they choose to open and engage with the email. In future testing, we would keep the hypothesis of casual vs. formal tone but leave in the element of specificity on both versions to see how it impacts engagement.

experiments.title: How including an image of a well-known personality affects click thru rate in email prospecting
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of a well-known personality will increase click thru rate by building credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2022
In an effort to increase click thru rate in email prospecting for a public policy client, we wanted to measure the effect of adding credibility and interest to the offer by means of utilizing an image of a well known personality.
The offer deployed was a children’s book premium offer. The control version used the image of the children’s book, while the treatment version utilized the image of the well-known personality who wrote the book.
This test was set up in which ~50% of the audience would get the control version and ~50% of the audience would get the treatment version. However, once results were clear to email brokers they only deployed the winning version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 61.5% increase in click through rate (with a 99.9% level of confidence) on the treatment creative, we were pleased to conclude with clarifying results the validity of our hypothesis. By adding an image of a well-known personality, the image will serve to build credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
Additionally, while not statistically significant, but still significant to note, the relative difference in donors with increased click thru rate was 76.2%.
The control version received 4 donations while the treatment variant received 28 donors—a 76.2% increase. While the level of confidence was only 79.5% and did not meet the 95% threshold to validate, we still believe these results are still of importance to note.
These results are a positive indication that similar tests should be run for other public policy and faith-based clients that can utilize well-known personalities for marketing purposes.

experiments.title: How introducing “donors like you” language impacted clicks in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that casting an email recipient into the role of a donor before they donate will increase click-through rates by using the persuasion principle of consistency to prompt them to follow through.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/12/2022 - 07/27/2022
In an effort to increase click-through rates, we wanted to know how “casting” the recipient into the role of donor before they donate would impact engagement. In his experiment, we kept all other copy the same, switching only language that mentions needing “your help” to “help from donors like you”. We sent each version of the email to 50% of the house file to assess the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By casting the email recipient into the role of a donor, we were able to realize a 17.8% lift in click-through rates, with a 97.5% level of confidence, validating our hypothesis. We believe this lift occurred because by asking for help from "donors like you" we preemptively created a feeling of commitment which prompted more people to seek consistency by clicking through.

While past experiments have shown that "you" language increase engagement, we wanted to take this a step further by speaking to a future you that is already making a difference by donating to the cause at hand.

This treatment achieves two things en route to increasing engagement:
It lets the recipient know that they are just the kind of person the client needs to help them accomplish their goal, and that those who do donate are people who align with similar values and goals
 	By casting the recipient into the role of a donor before they donate, the potential donor wants to be consistent by translating their values into action. The thought pattern "I care about these issues and I am someone to whom this organization is looking to help, but am unwilling to take actions" creates an uncomfortable feeling of cognitive dissonance wherein the potential donor feels one way but does not back that up with their actions. This treatment gives them the opportunity to put their values into action, thus increasing engagement

experiments.title: How a subject line invoking curiosity impacted click rates on an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that enhancing curiosity in a subject line for housefile email subscribers will achieve an increase in email engagement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 11/10/2022
When crafting an email to the housefile announcing the goal of 100,000 survey submissions had been reached, we wanted to test the subject line to get a learning about our housefile audience and what they respond to in order to help build future subject lines to use in an upcoming campaign. Would they be more motivated to open and take the intended desired action from an email with a subject line that did not include curiosity and rather gave clarity, revealing the contents of the message? Or would they be more compelled to open and click a message that related to an issue they care about and promises a reveal. In this case, the control was “100,000 … and counting” referring to the 100K survey submission goal and the treatment subject line was “This is great news”
experiments.keyLearnings: We measured comparative open rates for each version of the email and saw a 4.3% lift in opens on the treatment version. However, we know that open rate is no longer a reliable metric due to inflated open rates being counted by audience members who are using Apple Mail. So we wanted to dig a little deeper to investigate the overall results on email engagement. We also measured the number of clicks on the Call to Action in the body copy for each version. Although the emails were identical aside from the subject line, we saw a 61.4% increase in click rate with 100% level of confidence on the treatment. We believe this is due to the positive momentum the subject line carries from "This is great news" into reminding people it wasn't too late to submit their survey. If the reader was curious to see what the great news was, they had an increased chance of seeing the call to action inviting them to complete the survey as well. And since the message leaned into aligning with the subscriber's values and highlighted social proof, they could now have a compelling reason to say "yes" to the ask and be a part of the continued great news.

Our original live broadcast Facebook Ad focused on the speaker and the title, but we thought people may be more motivated by different aspects of the focus topic. For the treatments, we developed an ad that focused on “Growing Your Donor File” and one that focused on “Empathy.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Treatment 1 had a lift of 146.9% compared to the control. This confirms our hypothesis that this audience responds stronger to the learning and results rather than the speaker. Although, it is possible that the results could be different if the speaker was someone they were more familiar with.

Comparing this result to the result of our previous test on the housefile lookalike audience reveals a drastic difference in the motivations of two audiences that are supposedly similar. The lookalikes were 110% more likely to click on the empathy treatment, while the housefile was 26% less likely to click on the empathy treatment.

experiments.title: How communal language affects clickthrough rate on a recurring giving popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language for repeat visitors will achieve an increase in monthly donors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/09/2021
CaringBridge had run a popup asking visitors on their 2nd and 8th visits to become a recurring donor for more than a year, which had built a strong base of monthly donors. The control asked donors to “consider something” before they go, and then talked about the need for recurring donors. They decided to try to include some “communal language”, that first thanked the donor for staying connected to the CaringBridge community and then solicited them for a monthly gift based on their firsthand experience of the value of the CaringBridge community.
They decided to test this new language through an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The communal language on the popup increased clickthrough rate by 18.7%. However, upon reviewing the conversion rate on the donation page from the two popup treatments, it was clear that the communal language attracted more clicks but decreased the motivation of the donors, as conversion rate dropped 75.2% from the increase in clicks. This is a reminder that all clicks are not created equal—more traffic does not necessarily result in more donors if those donors are not properly motivated and incentivized by the existing copy.

CaringBridge had a secondary hypothesis that the language on the donation page was not robust enough, and, with some improvement to the page copy, might be able to convert more of these "communal language clickers". They decided to run a monthly giving donation page test first and then re-test this with the winner.

Save the Children’s homepage gets a high volume of traffic from all channels. In an effort to increase traffic and increase donor conversion on the primary donation page, we tested into using congruent language on the hero banner as was being used on the donation page. We ran this for
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for two weeks, the treatment saw a significant decrease in clicks to the donation page. In fact, the treatment variation decreased clicks by 47%. As we dug into the data to see the impact on donor conversion, much to our surprise, there was no impact on overall donor conversion (.54% difference at 8% LoC) . A person would assume that if you are getting a higher volume of traffic from one of the variations (the control), you'd naturally see a positive impact on donor conversion. That wasn't the case. What this experiment tells us is this:
Communal language, like "together" is appealing to people as they land on the page - this helped lead to the increase in clicks to the donation page.
 	Using a direct CTA with a high level of clarity also increased the volume of people getting to the primary donation page.
The higher quantity of site visitors didn't result in a higher quality of visitors who were likely to convert.

experiments.title: How communal-based communication affects clickthrough rate on a matching gift popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language in the donation popup for website visitors will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/10/2020
CaringBridge was running a two-day match in the month of December as part of their year end campaign. They had previously tested into a highly effective popup during matches that used urgency and an hourly donor countdown to increase response during these time-sensitive campaigns. However, they wondered if more “communal” messaging that affirmed the donor in their relationship to the CaringBridge author and made a less direct ask would be more effective at getting people to enter the donation funnel.
They drafted a treatment that made some key shifts. First, it began by talking about the donor themselves—”You’ve been such a great friend by staying engaged through CaringBridge.” Then, it softened the ask by starting with “If it’s not too much to ask”, which gives the donor a sort of “release valve” from the pressure of “donate now”. Finally, the matching gift from the Board of Directors was positioned as an opportunity or service offering provided to the donor rather than something they were expected to respond to.
They split the traffic between the two popups and monitored downstream traffic separately to see how it affected both clickthrough rate and conversion, as they realized that the two do not always correlate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the communal language produced a 45.3% increase in clickthrough rate. There are a few reasons behind what might have contributed to the increase.

First, the intro of "You've been such a great friend by staying connected through CaringBridge" not only affirms the donor, it piques their interest. It's a compliment, rather than the presumptive "your donation will be matched" language on the control. This additional engagement could have contributed to more people reading the next sentence—"If it's not too much to ask, would you consider giving today so that our Board of Directors can match your gift?".

This next sentence presents the ask in a completely different way. First, the donor is given an initial choice: "is this too much to ask, or not?", before they read about the incentive. Second, the incentive is offered in a radically different way than the control. Where the control actually thanks the Board of Directors for matching, the treatment gives the donor the chance to be the hero by making the gift, and places the match in a position that is responsive to the donor's action.

This test illuminated the possibilities of "communal" language for CaringBridge—which is a very "communal" organization in the first place. This opens up the opportunity do to more testing on the donation page downstream from this ask.

experiments.title: How removing transactional language on a free trial offer impacted ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing transactional language for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in website visits.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2021
When reviewing ads for a free trial offer, we wondered if the language “no credit card needed” was a motivating factor for people to sign up or if it was distancing potential visitors by creating a more transactional mindset and reducing the appeal and overall value of the offer. We wanted to test removing the “no credit card needed” language to see how it impacted clicks on the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we thought that removing transactional language would come across as a more humanized approach and personal in nature, what we found was that removing the "no credit card needed" language caused a 25.9% decrease in traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence. What this data shows us is this language actually decreases anxiety and removes friction from the process, making it a lower perceived amount of effort to accept the offer because people are not worried about having to remember to cancel their free trial at a later date to avoid being charged any membership fees. Because we achieved valid results, we were able to take this learning from a Facebook ad and apply it to other areas and entry points where a free trial is offered on the website and add in this language to increase conversion rates.

experiments.title: How matching gift copy affects clickthrough rate on a modal popup
experiments.researchQuestion: How does matching gift language affect clickthrough rate on a modal popup window?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2018 - 12/28/2017
FamilyLife was in the final week of their year-end campaign, and launched a modal popup to point web traffic to the donation page. The popup contained a countdown clock as well as urgency-focused message. The default version of the popup had language that echoed the headline, but they wondered if including matching gift language would increase clickthrough rate.
They created a treatment that kept urgent language, but added in the fact that the gift would be matched dollar-for-dollar. They launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (with matching gift language) produced a 56% increase in clickthrough rate. This test revealed that while urgency is key to any message with a deadline (especially at the end of the year), incentive can provide additional lift for action oriented copy.
In the control, the potential donor is presented with four elements to inspire them to click through and give: an urgent headline, a countdown clock, additional copy above the button, and the button copy. But in the treatment, the matching gift language provides a fifth element, without leaving out any of the previous four—incentive.
Many times, calls-to-action have one of these elements, but not the other. This test shows the power of combining the two.

experiments.title: How inline acquisition offers in place of ads affected clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Will replacing the mid-roll advertising banner with an inline acquisition increase clicks for this placement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2017 - 11/09/2017
Focus on the Family currently uses DoubleClick for Publishers to manage ad fill throughout their website.  Most of the ads in rotation are for their own resources, giving opportunities, and some third-party offers. One such placement is a mid-roll banner targeting long-form article pages.  They hypothesized that an inline email acquisition offer that is contextually relevant for their audience may increase clickthrough and help capture more of the high volume of new visitors viewing these articles.
They already had a free online Marriage Assessment which has resonated well with visitors to their marriage and parenting content, so they split the traffic to articles in those categories and delivered an inline offer for the assessment in place of the mid-roll ad from DoubleClick to half the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The inline treatment increased the number of offer clicks by over 1,870%! This learning shows how the alignment of an offer, as well as its treatment, can be put to work to acquire more names. We hypothesize that a large portion of this impact is due to “banner blindness”—a web usability phenomenon where visitors consciously or subconsciously ignore banner-like information.  Banners also limit the amount of value proposition you can effectively communicate, which can decrease perceived value.
With high traffic and known content themes, we plan to test further into this placement—including moving it to the end of the content where we hypothesize we may be able to capitalize on the natural pivot/decision point created by completing the article.  Other potential tests include the headline, button, and value proposition copy as well as the inclusion of supporting graphical elements.

experiments.researchQuestion: My hypothesis was more visitors would convert on a CTA that was value driven rather than product driven. So, the value of a daily pro-life devotional that is emailed to you is that it reminds you to pray daily for the unborn.
experiments.summary:

Every Lent, we offer a 40-day Lenten Devotional Prayers for Life. This is always a popular offer and this year we wanted to have a sticky bar on our blog pages to draw visitors to the offer. We often use the CTA Get Your Free 40 Day Lent Devotional. I wanted to test this against a challenger variant that I have used in the past on Facebook. Get a Daily Reminder to Pray for the Unborn!
experiments.keyLearnings: People were 242% MORE motivated by the promise of a daily reminder to pray than simply by a free Lenten devotional. We will continue to test into ways of getting at the WHY of an offer in the CTA vs just WHAT the offer is.

experiments.title: How will a direct request in a subject line affect click rates?

rows.name: A surprise for Dr. Carson's birthday
Will you help me celebrate Dr. Carson's birthday?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a direct request in a subject line will improve click-through rates for a housefile send when compared to a less direct subject line.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2022 - 09/22/2022
In a recent housefile email send promoting the opportunity for subscribers to sign a birthday card for Dr. Ben Carson, we wanted to test how a direct request subject line that puts the call to action front and center affects open rates. We tested this against a subject line that was less direct in mentioning a “birthday surprise,” and made no other changes to the email. We sent each subject line to 50% of the house file to assess the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Including a direct request in the subject line resulted in a 4.5% lift in click rates with a 96.6% level of confidence. We believe this lift occurred because the direct request better aligned recipient expectations as to what the email is all about.

The direct request version of the subject line makes it clear that the recipient is being asked to help celebrate Dr. Ben Carson's birthday in some way while the less direct subject line merely mentions a "surprise," without alluding to the fact that it in some way involves the recipient.

As a result, the recipients who received the direct request subject line were more primed to "help" and thus more likely to click through on a call to action that matches this expectation. Recipients of the less direct subject line may have thought they were simply being informed of a surprise, making them less primed to participate in some way.

Humans crave consistency and this test suggests that by setting an early expectation and keeping that expectation consistent through to the CTA, you are more likely to see engagement with the offer being promoted.

experiments.title: How making an end-user click “See More” in a Facebook ad impacts click-through to the acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad would increase click-through and ultimately name acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2017 - 09/07/2017
Care Net offers a free online course called Pro-Life 101. They use paid Facebook ads to push the offer. Through previous testing, we had learned that longer Facebook ad copy increases name acquisition. To build upon those learnings, we tested ad copy that was even longer resulting in the end-user having to click “See More” to read the entirety of it.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version with the extended copy increased click-through by 82%! What this tell us is that people are not only more highly engaged with long ad copy, but even longer ad copy requiring people to click “See More” increases engagement and results in more people clicking through to the landing page and converting. The “See More” version provided additional information that the end-user needed to make the decision to click-through to the landing page and ultimately sign-up for the course.

experiments.title: How communal-based communication affects clickthrough rate on a matching gift popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language in the donation popup for website visitors will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/10/2020
CaringBridge was running a two-day match in the month of December as part of their year end campaign. They had previously tested into a highly effective popup during matches that used urgency and an hourly donor countdown to increase response during these time-sensitive campaigns. However, they wondered if more “communal” messaging that affirmed the donor in their relationship to the CaringBridge author and made a less direct ask would be more effective at getting people to enter the donation funnel.
They drafted a treatment that made some key shifts. First, it began by talking about the donor themselves—”You’ve been such a great friend by staying engaged through CaringBridge.” Then, it softened the ask by starting with “If it’s not too much to ask”, which gives the donor a sort of “release valve” from the pressure of “donate now”. Finally, the matching gift from the Board of Directors was positioned as an opportunity or service offering provided to the donor rather than something they were expected to respond to.
They split the traffic between the two popups and monitored downstream traffic separately to see how it affected both clickthrough rate and conversion, as they realized that the two do not always correlate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the communal language produced a 45.3% increase in clickthrough rate. There are a few reasons behind what might have contributed to the increase.
First, the intro of "You've been such a great friend by staying connected through CaringBridge" not only affirms the donor, it piques their interest. It's a compliment, rather than the presumptive "your donation will be matched" language on the control. This additional engagement could have contributed to more people reading the next sentence—"If it's not too much to ask, would you consider giving today so that our Board of Directors can match your gift?".
This next sentence presents the ask in a completely different way. First, the donor is given an initial choice: "is this too much to ask, or not?", before they read about the incentive. Second, the incentive is offered in a radically different way than the control. Where the control actually thanks the Board of Directors for matching, the treatment gives the donor the chance to be the hero by making the gift, and places the match in a position that is responsive to the donor's action.
This test illuminated the possibilities of "communal" language for CaringBridge—which is a very "communal" organization in the first place. This opens up the opportunity do to more testing on the donation page downstream from this ask.

experiments.title: How increasing the number of hyperlinks in an email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing the number of links in an email increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2016 - 01/16/2017
The treatment produced a 27% increase in clickthrough rate, which tells us a few things about our readers. First, it tells us that including more links incentivizes more of them to click, or take the first initial action at the moment that the value proposition hits home. Secondly, it tells us that we had previously been losing potential donors who didn’t make it all the way through the copy to the call-to-action.
This points to an important principle: our donors and prospects have varying levels of motivation. Some are ready to give and need very little convincing, while others are more deliberative and might need more copy to be incentivized to click. Typically, we attempt to address these varying levels of motivation through multiple emails, but this experiment points to the opportunity that we might be able to reach multiple levels of motivation in the same communication.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had always included a “focused link” in their email appeals, which gave readers a single link to click to take action. Historically, this had been a button, but previous testing had shown that a text link performed better.
They wanted to test the effect of giving donors more places to click throughout the email—essentially linking up any text that contained a CTA, so if the donor was motivated, they could take action right then. They produced a treatment that had the exact same copy, but included more clickable links.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2016 - 03/30/2016
The stripped-down email (with no template) produced an 80% increase in clickthrough rate. This tells us something about trust — and giving is an act of trust. It may imply that people are more likely to take action when they feel that the email is coming from a person, rather than an organization.
There were still elements of the treatment email that weren’t entirely personal — the big blue button, and the logo. This prompted further testing to see if the removal of these elements produced an additional lift.

CaringBridge received a matching gift for their spring fundraising campaign from a board member. Since the majority of their audience was unfamiliar with the board member, Rik Lalim, they originally created a templated email that was explicitly “CaringBridge” to ease users’ potential anxiety. But they had previously tested emails that had no formatting and emulated “normal” email, and experienced great success. They decided to create a treatment version that stripped away all the formatting and photography.
They then split their email file and ran a test to see which attracted more responses

experiments.title: How communal language affects clickthrough rate on a recurring giving popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language for repeat visitors will achieve an increase in monthly donors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/09/2021
CaringBridge had run a popup asking visitors on their 2nd and 8th visits to become a recurring donor for more than a year, which had built a strong base of monthly donors. The control asked donors to “consider something” before they go, and then talked about the need for recurring donors. They decided to try to include some “communal language”, that first thanked the donor for staying connected to the CaringBridge community and then solicited them for a monthly gift based on their firsthand experience of the value of the CaringBridge community.
They decided to test this new language through an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The communal language on the popup increased clickthrough rate by 18.7%. However, upon reviewing the conversion rate on the donation page from the two popup treatments, it was clear that the communal language attracted more clicks but decreased the motivation of the donors, as conversion rate dropped 75.2% from the increase in clicks. This is a reminder that all clicks are not created equal—more traffic does not necessarily result in more donors if those donors are not properly motivated and incentivized by the existing copy.
CaringBridge had a secondary hypothesis that the language on the donation page was not robust enough, and, with some improvement to the page copy, might be able to convert more of these "communal language clickers". They decided to run a monthly giving donation page test first and then re-test this with the winner.

experiments.title: How increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2018 - 01/31/2018
CaringBridge’s marketing team sought to increase the amount of users who got to their “ways to help” page, where there were several options to deepen engagement and provide more support to their friend or family member.
They thought that a slight copy change would align the “Ways to help” button on the journal page with the user’s intent. Instead of “Ways to help”, they proposed “Help [FirstName]” to encourage more people to click through. They thought this might work because they hypothesized that people who were reading this page were (consciously or unconsciously) desiring to help their friend or family member.
They were concerned about possible reactions to this test, so they only included 10% of site traffic in the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new copy produced a 19% lift in clickthrough rate for the “Ways to help” page—an impressive lift considering the small change. This points to more opportunities to align site copy with user intent throughout the site. With the volume of traffic that CaringBridge has, there is tremendous potential to increase clarity and adjust user experience accordingly.

experiments.title: How isolating a quantified ask affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: When isolated, what impact does the quantified ask have on clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2017 - 03/27/2017
The first treatment, with just the quantified ask, produced a 26% increase in clickthrough rate. However, in further testing, it failed to produce any increase in conversion and produced a spike in refunds.
The second treatment, with the headline change only, produced a 10% decrease in clickthrough rate, which showed that the cumulative lift in previous testing had more correlation with the presence of the quantified ask, but was boosted by the headline as well.
This prompted further testing to see if they could clarify the message on the donation page to reduce confusion and refund rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge tested a radical redesign that produced a big lift in donor conversion. This was a valuable finding, but they wanted further clarity on the lift, since two factors were changed. In the first experiment, they changed the headline of their tribute widget and added a “quantified ask”, with the offer that a $30 gift would keep their friend or loved one’s site online for a month.
They were curious which element had the most impact between these two. So they created two treatments: one that reverted to the old headline and included the quantified ask, and one that reverted to the old body copy (without the quantified ask), but kept the winning headline.
They launched an A/B/C test to validate a winner and determine the impact of each element on conversion.

experiments.title: How increasing intensity of color on a widget affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the intensity of the tribute widget attract more attention and increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2017 - 10/25/2017
CaringBridge had long sought to increase clickthrough rate on their tribute widget, which started the gift process for . One of their designers hypothesized that the current tribute widget blended in too much with the rest of the site and needed some “pop”. Using the intense CaringBridge brand purple, she created a treatment designed to attract more attention, and hopefully clickthrough rate.
CaringBridge tested the new tribute widget in both desktop and mobile views and launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment tribute widget reduced clickthrough rate by 27.6%, which further reduced donations and revenue downstream. We believe this can largely be attributed to the decrease in readability due to the white text now being placed on the intense purple.  In breaking down the data by device, most of the decrease came from mobile devices, in which this effect is intensified due to the smaller screen size.
This was just the first test in a series of tribute widget tests designed to increase clickthrough rate.

experiments.title: How streamlining the tribute widget affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a streamlined widget with added social proof inspire more people to begin the donation process?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2015 - 04/24/2015
Most gifts to CaringBridge start with the “tribute widget”, a small box that appears on all Site Journal pages and profile pages. We noticed one day that the tribute widget had a lot of information that could be extraneous and distracting from the intended action, which was to begin the gift process.
The CaringBridge team developed a streamlined version of this widget that removed a “most recent quote”, along with avatars for signed in users. To increase clarity, the team put pre-filled text into the box to instruct the user what to write. Also, we added social proof into the widget, dynamically pulling the number of previous donors to inspire the user to join them.
experiments.keyLearnings: The streamlined widget with added social proof resulted in nearly 52% more people beginning the donation process. We closely monitored donation page conversion rates as well, and there was no change. Clarifying the value proposition by removing extra graphics and copy and reducing anxiety through social proof resulted in a significant lift in dollars raised.

experiments.title: Does personalizing an email focusing on a premium resource increase clicks for Pathway to Victory?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding personalization for email subscribers will achieve a higher click through rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2021 - 12/28/2021
Pathway to Victory, a ministry of First Baptist Dallas, presented us with an opportunity to test some personalization during their highly-successful December match campaign. As an organization that is very premium-focused, their digital offers throughout the campaign focused around receiving a 2022 Daily Devotional book, along with other assorted premiums based on giving level, coupled with the largest match campaign in the ministry’s history. Despite having a relatively large email file, Pathway to Victory rarely used personalization on their emails. Therefore, we saw an opportunity to test whether adding a Dear First Name salutation to an email, and A/B test that only to the portion of the file that had a first name on file already (approx. 50% of the total file), would increase click-through rates. We tested this approach with an email in the middle of December.
experiments.keyLearnings: While open rates between the two emails were the same, adding the personalized salutation increased the number of clicks to the landing page by 26%. Given that we have seen that adding a simple personalization to emails did not negatively affect any email metrics, we plan to continue testing this, as well as add more than just a personalized salutation in future emails.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing copy for a digital audience will achieve increased click through and donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2022 - 03/16/2022
As part of the campaign promoting the Perfect Ending offer for Pathway to Victory, we continued to test whether removing images and updating copy so that it is not so premium-focused would impact click throughs and donor conversion rates. For this specific appeal, we also included a quote from Dr. Jeffress’ recent sermon on the war in Ukraine.
experiments.keyLearnings: By removing images and adding in a quote from a recent sermon that was relevant to the premium offer, click through rates increased by over 50%.

experiments.title: How using congruent language on the homepage banner impact clicks and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will congruent messaging impact clicks and donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2021 - 03/08/2021
Save the Children’s homepage gets a high volume of traffic from all channels. In an effort to increase traffic and increase donor conversion on the primary donation page, we tested into using congruent language on the hero banner as was being used on the donation page. We ran this for
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for two weeks, the treatment saw a significant decrease in clicks to the donation page. In fact, the treatment variation decreased clicks by 47%. As we dug into the data to see the impact on donor conversion, much to our surprise, there was no impact on overall donor conversion (.54% difference at 8% LoC) . A person would assume that if you are getting a higher volume of traffic from one of the variations (the control), you'd naturally see a positive impact on donor conversion. That wasn't the case. What this experiment tells us is this:
Communal language, like "together" is appealing to people as they land on the page - this helped lead to the increase in clicks to the donation page. Using a direct CTA with a high level of clarity also increased the volume of people getting to the primary donation page.
But...
The higher quantity of site visitors didn't result in a higher quality of visitors who were likely to convert.
While the treatment variation didn't "win" in total clicks to the donation page, we hypothesize that the congruency in copy between the homepage and the donation page DID help since there was no improvement in donor conversion when it wasn't used; yet traffic was higher with the control. Continued testing around these principles is needed to better understand the motivations of site visitors to the primary donation page.

experiments.title: How elaborating on specific organizational initiatives affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an email that elaborates on specific organizational initiatives increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2017 - 12/13/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary recently began their calendar year end campaign, focused three pillars: The Call to Teach, The Will to Engage, and The Heart to Go. As they prepared to send an appeal on email focused on the second pillar–The Will to Engage– they wondered if an email that elaborated on the strategic initiatives they’ve created to engage culture would increase the clickthrough rate to the donation page. They created a treatment version and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
They found that the treatment version did increase clickthrough rate by 276%. It’s clear the treatment version significantly increased recipient motivation, but why? There are a few important elements worth analyzing in the second email. First, the introduction of the treatment version takes a more personal approach to the topic of cultural engagement. It highlights Dr. Bailey’s daily schedule of leaving his office and driving through the city of Dallas. This simple change immediately draws the reader into Dr. Bailey’s point of view and shows why this initiative is personal to him. Humans are storytellers. We love to have a concrete image to hold in our mind’s eye.
Second, the treatment version elaborates on three specific ways Dallas Theological Seminary is addressing culture and empowering individuals outside of the seminary walls. By describing at length these three initiatives–The Table Podcast, the Faith and Work Initiative, and a plethora of free online courses–DTS is reminding their audience where their financial support goes. It’s also important to note the subtle difference in the call-to-action. Instead of clicking to ‘make a secure donation’ this treatment email invites individuals to ‘invest in these initiatives.’ This is an approach that Dallas Theological Seminary will continue to work into the rest of their calendar year end campaign.

experiments.title: How more contextualized ad media affects click-through-rates on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using more contextualized imagery on a Facebook ad will increase click-through rates by adding clarity and appeal to an offer in order to increase its perceived value.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/18/2022
After turning off some underperforming Facebook ads, we wanted to introduce a new treatment to test against our previous champion. For this treatment, we wanted to know if more contextualized ad media would lead to higher click-through rates than a simple 3D render of the ebook associated with the offer. To test this concept, we created new ad media which placed the eBook (dealing with critical race theory) over an image of children in a classroom while also adding some value prop copy that aimed to demonstrate WHAT the viewer would learn by signing up for the ebook.
For this experiment, we changed only the ad creative and ran the test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 27.5% DROP in click-through rates, with a 100% level of confidence. When analyzing this experiment, we identified a potential reason for these results:

Decreasing the size of the ebook in the treatment image, along with placing it over a busy background may have created friction for the viewer by making it unclear what they should do.

This may also have reduced the visual impact of showing what is being offered (the ebook), prompting viewers who may have otherwise been enticed by this offer to keep scrolling, not realizing a free ebook was being offered

The image may also have failed to make the intended impact because it is dark in tone, with heavy shadows and much of the subject matter obscured by the ebook, potentially making it difficult for the viewer to quickly discern what the image is trying to convey.

Overall, we believe that these factors combine to make the treatment ad rather "invisible" to the viewer, failing to do enough to catch the reader's attention, get them to stop scrolling, and click through to the offer.

experiments.title: How replacing a static image with a video on a Facebook ad affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting an ad in video format will increase click-through rates for Facebook users by better catching their attention and better communicating the value one receives by signing up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2022 - 10/18/2022
Many of Dallas Theological Seminary’s Facebook ads for their free online courses are similar, using a color-rich background including the names of the courses and the teachers who present them. In an effort to combat low click through rates, we hypothesized that although viewers were seeing ads for different courses, because these ads looked so similar, it was causing viewers to assume they were seeing an ad for the same course time and time again, eventually prompting them to tune the ads out.
We also wondered if the static ads were inadequately communicating the value one could receive by enrolling in one of Dallas Theological Seminary’s free online courses. We wanted to know if we could increase click-through rates by presenting a video ad that would 1) Catch viewers attention and 2) Better communicate the value of registration.
For this experiment, we changed only the ad media, splitting traffic between he control and treatment over a span of 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 93% LIFT in click-through rates, with a 100% level of confidence.
This experiment validated our hypothesis that a more interactive media format would result in more viewers clicking the ad. We believe that this treatment was successful for two primary reasons:
First, we believe that the new media format broke up the monotony of similarly formatted ads being shown to the same people. A different ad from a familiar organization, we believe, cued viewers that a new offer was being advertised.
Second, we believe that the video allowed viewers to see for themselves the quality of the coursework and the expertise of the teacher (these courses are adapted from on-campus seminary lectures) which increased the perceived value of the offer by improving its credibility and exclusivity, which likely provided more incentive for the viewer to click.
Moving forward, we will attempt to gather more video samples that we can use to promote more courses in their catalog.

experiments.title: How including lengthier copy impacted clicks on an email offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that extending the value proposition of an offer for email recipients will achieve a higher clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/25/2021
We were pushing out a Christmas Devotional offer and wanted to re-send to any recipients who had not yet opened or clicked on the original offer to download. The control copy was short and to the point so we wanted to test whether adding additional language—highlighting the value of the resource and emphasizing why this offer would be of particular use to the recipient—would impact clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was the treatment copy actually reduced clicks by 20%. While the treatment copy uses a more humanized approach and introduces a personal recommendation, the call to action changed from "Click below to get your 2020 Christmas Devotional" to "Let me know what you think!". In future experiments, we would want to test including the communal-style language in the body copy while keeping the call to action at the end of the email succinct and clear.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity in a Facebook ad affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the clarity of the prospect-level value proposition increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2017 - 01/23/2017
The treatment with the dark text and the book on the left produced a 95.6% increase in clickthrough rate as compared to the control, beating the other three treatments (which also produced lifts).
This experiment shows the importance of clarity in the ad, even at the initial point of contact. The high contrast in the text makes it jump out, especially in Facebook where the majority of the platform is white. Also, leading with the book image draws the reader’s eye into the text, rather than the other way around.
This experiment will help future ad campaigns begin with more optimized design.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Buckner International was offering a free eBook by their president, Albert Reyes, called “The Jesus Agenda”. They hypothesized that their control ad, with a photo of Jesus, might lack clarity in the prospect-level value proposition, since it didn’t show the viewer what they were getting.

They drafted three new treatments: one with the eBook and dark text, another with the eBook and lighter text, and one with the eBook right-aligned and the text on the left. They launched a four-way test to determine which one produced the highest clickthrough rate.

experiments.title: How using more personalized copy increases click through rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing copy for the email audience will achieve a higher response rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/29/2022 - 05/06/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry whose donor base has been built on premium offers–books, DVD series, etc. Most of their email file has been accustomed to receiving graphic-heavy emails, with images of the book, etc. For the last of their April 2022 How Can I Know premium offer, we wanted to test whether a more standard, graphic-heavy email would perform better in terms of click throughs and donations compared to a highly personalized, text-only email that did not heavily focus on the book. We only sent this email to those that had not purchased How Can I Know in April.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test were interesting, as we had tested a graphic versus plain-text email earlier in the campaign, with the plain-text version generating a higher click-through rate. In this case, the graphic version had a 40% higher click-through rate. However, the results of the test when looking at donations is flipped: the plain-text, personalized copy version had a 83% higher donor conversion rate than the graphic version did, with a 98% level of confidence in test results.

We believe that this is due to the way the copy was written for the plain-text version; it was a much more humanized approach, diving deeper into the questions asked and answered by the book. The graphic version of the email was written more as a purely promotional vehicle for the book itself. Therefore, while less people clicked through to the donation page for the plain-text version, those that did were more motivated to actually complete their donation when they got there.

We will definitely continue this kind of testing for premium offers moving forward for Pathway to Victory.
