experiments.title: How image clarity and ask intensity affected clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Does an image of the 3D eBook in the ad produce more clicks than an image of children? How do people react to a soft ask vs. a direct ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2017 - 05/03/2017
The Buckner team realized that users were 40% more likely to respond to an image including the 3D eBook than one that does not. This stems from the clarity and directness of the image’s alignment with the ad- people know exactly what they’re getting when they click the ad. Copy 2 was also more effective because they’re not asking them to commit to something at the ad level, as in Copy 1. The direct ask in Copy 1 is better suited for people who have already clicked through to the landing page. The softer ask in Copy 2 (asking them to learn more) paired with a direct image led to a 40% more successful click through rate.
They also learned that placing a strong statement (“The foster system is broken”) that people will either agree/disagree with at the beginning of the ad leads to more qualified traffic (and a more likely converter). If the user agrees with the initial statement, then sees the opportunity to learn more paired with a very clear image of what they’re going to receive if they click the link, they’re more likely to continue to the landing page. If they disagree, they’re immediately diverted.
experiments.keyLearnings: Buckner International was promoting a new free ebook to grow their email file targeted towards people interested in the foster care system. They wanted to figure out what made people click through to the landing page from the ad and download the guide. They created four different treatments to test this- 2 images paired with 2 different approaches to copy. One of the images is a photo of the 3D eBook, and the other image is a photo of children. The copy differences hinge on a direct vs. soft ask: “commit to prayer” vs. “learn more about the opportunity”.

experiments.title: How focusing ad copy and creative on relatable messaging impacted click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that more communal, digestable, and relatable content for ad engagers will achieve an increase in click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/10/2022
TGC is offering its free Secular Creed eBook in acquisition. The Facebook ad promoting the offer was receiving a low click-through rate,  so we wanted to test two new ad variants to see if they would see in increase clicks. We wondered if the existing ad is difficult to process, as it uses a lot of big words, the messaging could be confusing, and overall, it does not do a good enough job of considering the audience.
Treatment #1 took a new direction with the ad copy and the ad image. The copy begins with a more engaging, personal question that asks for their personal opinion instead of asking if they want to learn something. Overall, it uses communal language (“we” and “us”) instead of general language to get the message across that we share a particular outlook. The image is just the ebook as opposed to the ebook with a long quote to follow.
Treatment #2 uses the same copy as treatment #1, but implements video creative instead of a static image. The video is a two-minute motivational excerpt from a speech made by the author on topic with the value prop in the ad copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was little difference between the results of the control and treatment 1, with treatment 1 experiencing a 5.9% decrease in clicks with only a 54.6% level of confidence. However, treatment 2 resulted in a 142.5% increase in clicks with a 100% level of confidence.
There was little statistical difference between the control copy and the treatment copy, which means the video creative in treatment 2 made all the difference. Strategically selecting a 2-minute excerpt from the author of the eBook that aligned with the value prop and put the problem and solution into perspective motivated people to click the ad.
Given the success of this test, it would be wise to, when applicable, leverage eBook authors and leverage video of them that can reach people on a deeper level.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the value of giving monthly vs incentives impacts click thru rate in a high urgency campaign email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on the value of monthly giving rather than the incentives of monthly giving  for prospective donors  will achieve a higher click thru rate and a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a high urgency campaign to acquire new recurring donors. Most of the campaign copy was geared towards the incentives for a monthly donor (i.e. special perks, exclusive access, etc.). In one of the last emails of the campaign we wanted to test adjusting the language so that it emphasizes the value of giving monthly vs a 1 time gift. Our hypothesis is that prospective donors will be more motivated to give when they understand why monthly giving is valuable.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 125% valid lift in click thru rate with a 99% level of confidence. This informs us that RTB's audience is more engaged in the email and are more likely to click thru when they're given the value of monthly giving. However, as we dug deeper we saw 11 donations come from the control and 9 donations come from the treatment. This translates into a 18% decrease in donations with a level of confidence of 34.5%. This is not a statistically significant decrease but we were expecting an increase in donor conversion rate. This might be because the actual donation page also had a heavy emphasis on incentives and the messaging of value was not consistent and carried through in the next step.
It's recommended that the donation page copy is tested next time to determine if this language truly motivates more donors to take action.

experiments.title: How adding urgency and clarity in the opening line of an ad and relatability through the ad image impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency and clarity in the opening line of an ad and relatability through the ad image for ad visitors would increase click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/09/2022
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition was experiencing a low click-through rate, so we tested a few different ad angles to see if they would increase clicks. We wondered if grabbing the reader’s attention right off the bat with a statement that they could “get behind” and clearly understand what was at stake would increase click-through rate. We also wondered if the existing ad image was experiencing user fatigue, and therefore tested two new images to see how they impact click-through rate. We ran an a/b/c test. The copy for the “b” and “c” variants differs from the “a” variant only in the opening sentence of the copy.
The “b” variant has a different image than “a”, using an image with symbols that represent Texas and that almost any Texan would recognize. The “c” variant has a different image as well, showing a person riding a horse in open Texas land – an image that we hypothesized Texans would relate to and distinguish themselves through.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both treatment 1 and 2 led to an increase in ad clicks. Treatment 1 saw a 38.9% lift in clicks with a 96.9% level of confidence and treatment 2 saw a 83.4% lift in clicks with 99.9% level of confidence. This tells us that users did appreciate the clarity in the opening line of the ad copy, as they learned immediately what the ad was asking of them (to sign a petition) and what the petition was for (to keep Texas Texan). Also, by pointing at this this petition was "critical", users likely felt more of a sense of urgency to click the ad to reach the critical petition. As these variables were not isolated, we don't know for sure whether the clarity or urgency factored more into their decision to click through to the landing page.

Ultimately, treatment 2 experienced the biggest life of the three variants. This tells us that the ad image in treatment 2 resonated the most with users. They're asked to complete a petition to "keep Texas Texan", and the image of a man riding a horse through an open field on a dirt path rightly represents Texas. Users were more likely to click an ad image that offered authenticity than an image that was more "cartoony". That's because users see Texas and Texans as "authentic".

This ad approach is worth testing with other ads and for other offers. The results and learning would be clearer, however, if the ad variants are isolated. We don't know for sure if users were more motivated to click because of the copy change or the image change.

experiments.title: How adding a graphic call out for an Angels and Demons Facebook ad impacted click throughs for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a graphic call out for a Facebook audience will achieve a change in clickt-throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2022 - 01/31/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. One of their most popular offers is a brochure discussing angels and demons. This brochure is offered, and mailed, for free to anyone that wants to sign up for it on the PTV website.
In this test we wanted to see if adding in a free offer graphic call out made the ad stand out a bit more on Facebook, and thus result in a higher click-through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a 10% increase in email sign-up conversions and a 99% level of confidence, this treatment showed how just a simple graphic change on a Facebook ad, emphasizing the free nature of the offer, has a measurable impact on offers.

experiments.title: How including clarity in the subject line impacted clicks

rows.name: Christian living in the internet age

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing clarity in the subject line for housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/13/2022
When sending a new offer to the housefile, we wanted to find out how recipients would respond to an email subject line that clearly stated the contents inside. Since TGC regularly sends articles and newsletters that include a subject line that highlight the theme, we wanted to draw attention to the fact that this email was different than usual because it included a newly launched free content offer that was available to download. We sent the control version “Christian living in the internet age” that introduced the topic and developed a treatment that featured the specific resource and CTA “Download our latest eBook ‘Digital Discipleship'”. We split the subject line and sent to 25% and 25% of the housefile recipients.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we saw was a 51.3% increase in clicks with 100% level of confidence on the treatment subject line that included the specific contents of the email. We believe the treatment subject line increased the appeal of the offer and helped call attention to subscribers that this email included something different than what they usually find in their inbox from TGC that they wouldn't want to miss. The treatment subject line improved the relevance of the message, which led to a more engaged audience because those who opened the email were already primed to take the next step had a higher motivation to click the link because of their initial interest in the offer. For our next test when delivering a content offer, we will test a CTA that uses language such as "Get" and include the word "free" to see if we can drive additional clicks.

experiments.title: How previewing a pledge affects click-through rates on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that previewing a pledge on a Facebook ad will increase click-through rates by engaging the viewer and prompting them to click/tap to make their selection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/18/2022
After turning off some underperforming Facebook ads, we wanted to introduce a new treatment to test against our previous champion. For this treatment, we chose a concept that has shown success in the past: previewing a choice from the offered asset in order to entice the viewer to engage and click through to the asset itself. For this experiment, we changed only the ad creative and ran this test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 22.7% drop in click-through-rates with a 99.9% level of confidence. When analyzing these results, we identified a couple of factors that may have contributed to this decline.
First, while such a treatment has worked well for survey offers in the past, we feel that this approach may not be as effective for a pledge because we are not "asking" them to complete any action, i.e. when previewing a survey question, the preview "asks" the viewer a question ... but in the context of a pledge, the viewer simply sees a statement with a checked box beside it. This might make it unclear to the viewer that they are being asked to engage, create friction and fail to do enough to get them to stop scrolling.
Similarly, because the treatment presents a statement with a box already checked, it may simply cue viewers to agree and move on. In the control, action is encouraged by agitating urgency around the issue, likely doing more to encourage the viewer to stop, consider the situation, and want to learn more/take some action.
Lastly, we also noticed that in an attempt to strengthen the call to action, we added more copy and reduced the font size—"sign the pledge" appears much larger and eye-catching on the control than it does on the treatment, which could, again, making it clear to the viewer that there is an action to take.

				gift).

22.7% decrease in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How clarity affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does donor-centric language and a more personal connection increase the number of clicks compared to more succinct copy?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2014 - 11/25/2014
The treatment delivered 51.7% fewer clicks than the control. This says a lot about not only the audience viewing this, but the context in which the copy appears. It tells us that this audience values clarity over persuasion — a quick, short ask trumps a longer, more personal ask that attempts to draw in their emotions.
It also suggests that this page is highly functional – since it is a “dashboard” of sorts, people use it to get from one place to another, which suggests that future copy should be concise and to the point.
experiments.keyLearnings: CaringBridge had a donation ask on the homepage that registered users saw when they were logged in. We wanted to increase clickthroughs on this ask to generate more donations. We created a treatment that was highly donor-centric — it gave them a virtual “pat on the back” and reminded them of all the ways that they help those they care about.

experiments.title: How “free” language affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the word “free” increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2018 - 03/27/2018
Hoover Institution leveraged a free eBook to grow the size of their email file. They were curious about the language used in the ads—the control ad had a call-to-action of “Get the facts on healthcare now”, but they knew from previous experiments that the word “free” was a powerful motivator. They set up a second ad with the call-to-action “Get the free eBook now” and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new ads with “free” in the call-t0-action produced a 20.7% decrease in clickthrough rate. There are at least two possible reasons for this:
The target audience is more interested in something with perceived authority and factual basis than something that is free, possibly due to the intellectual reputation of Hoover.The rest of the ad copy mentions "facts", so the "free" language might produce a break in congruency rather than a lift due to incentive.
While a valid decrease was confirmed in clickthrough rate, there was no statistically valid decrease in conversion rate, which means that additional testing is needed on the ad and landing page.

experiments.title: How the email design influences clickthrough rate
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified email design increase the proportion of people clicking to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2017 - 03/07/2017
The simplified email resulted in a 14.8% increase to the percentage of visitors going to the landing page. Our hypothesis is that the lack of design seemed to be more genuine and less “marketing” oriented. As a result, the subscribers were more willing to read and engaged with the email once they opened it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate were launching a new digital prayer book to kick off the Lenten season. As part of this launch, they wanted to give their house email file an opportunity to request the resource. This would serve as both cultivation of the subscribers and a new opportunity for them to give a gift once they had requested their PDF.

Historically, the email template used in donor communications was heavily designed with an organizational wrapper. We wanted to see if sending the same email message but in a stripped-down design would have an impact on the percentage of people that clicked on the link to download the prayer book.

experiments.title: How a simpler, more direct value proposition affects clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a shorter, more direct value proposition affect clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2017 - 04/10/2017
The shorter ad with the direct question produced a 122.1% increase in clickthrough rate, which showed that the elements of this ad increased the clarity of the value proposition. Longer copy can be more effective, but only if if clarifies the value proposition. In addition, the direct question to provoke the reader to respond by taking the intended action.
This success prompted further testing to isolate the variables of length and the direct question to understand how each of these played into the success of the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Heritage Foundation was promoting a quiz around the “Gadsden Flag” that led to a membership offer with the flag as a premium. They had created two versions of a Facebook ad, as they had seen some research that longer, more engaging copy could increase clickthrough and conversion rate. The control, in this case, had much longer copy that talked about the history of the flag and made an offer to the reader to test their knowledge. The treatment copy was much shorter, and asked a direct question to the reader: “How much do you know about the Gadsden Flag?”

They split the traffic to determine which ad attracted more clicks.

experiments.title: How ad creative affected clickthrough rate and follow-on conversion rate

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2015 - 10/19/2015
While the difference in clickthrough rate between the two ads was nonexistent, there was a massive disparity in conversion rate. The ad that incentivized visitors to see the video now resulted in 90% more conversions than the one that highlighted exclusive access. This proves the point that we must be measuring every layer of the conversion process to determine the impact of ad creative.
experiments.keyLearnings: Heritage Action was running a campaign to acquire email addresses by leveraging exclusive video content from one of their events. They tested two ads against each other with identical creative but differing calls-to-action. One used the CTA “Watch the video now”, while the other said “Get Exclusive Access Now”. The two ads had an identical clickthrough rate of 3.8%, so we dug deeper to see if the ad creative (which went to the same landing page) resulted in different conversion rates after the click.

The Leadership Institute has found that using Mark Levin’s authority in both copy and imagery has increased course signups for their online course ‘Conservatism 101.’ They wondered: Will more forthright, biting ad copy impact clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad? They launched an A/B test to find out.

experiments.title: How authority, active language, and clarity affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does increased authority, active language, and clarity affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 11/09/2017
Leadership Institute recently re-launched a course about the history and principles of conservatism, called Conservatism 101. As they prepared to market this course through Facebook, they developed four different treatments for the advertisements. Testing proved that the third and fourth treatment produced a significant lift to clickthrough rate, but they wanted to gather more data to validate if one of these resulted in more downstream conversions—in this case, more sign-ups for the course.
The primary difference in the two treatments was the image and the copy within. The first treatment had a patriotic background that described what the course was: “Conservatism 101: An Introduction to the American Conservative Tradition”. The second, however, featured a picture of Mark Levin, a popular radio host and lecturer in the course. This image contained call-to-action copy as well: “Join Mark Levin in this new free course, Conservatism 101.”
The team isolated these two ads to determine which ad was more effective at driving downstream conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The second treatment, with Mark Levin and the call-to-action copy in the image produced a 63.7% increase in downstream conversions. This showed that the increased authority and active call to action was able to shape motivation to affect downstream actions. Ultimately, Leadership Institute didn’t just want more clicks—they wanted more clicks with a higher motivation.

This test prompted a new round of testing that used the winner as the control to continue to increase results.

experiments.title: How emotional language, clarity and authority affect ad clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does emotional language, clarity and authority affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 11/09/2017
Leadership Institute recently re-launched a course about the history and principles of conservatism, called Conservatism 101. As they prepared to market this course through Facebook, they developed several different treatments for the advertisements. The first treatment posed a question: “What are conservatives actually trying to conserve?”, which implied that the course would answer that question. The second treatment introduced emotional language that outlined the threat to conservatism and made a call for conservatives to rediscover their principles. This treatment also included a call to action (CTA) in the top copy: “Start today by enrolling in Conservatism 101 now.” The third treatment took a similar tack with emotional language, but sought to align with the mindset of the reader by declaring the threat to be invalid. This treatment heightened the clarity and authority around the course offer, introducing a list of featured speakers, and introduced line breaks to better isolate the CTA: “Activate your free course today and discover—or rediscover—the principles and beauty of conservatism.” The final treatment used the same copy as the third treatment, but introduced a picture of Mark Levin with the CTA “Join Mark Levin in this new free course: Conservatism 101.”
Since Facebook is able to serve multiple ads simultaneously and gather data on each, they launched this A/B/C/D test to determine which ad produced the most clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After a few days, LI had enough data to determine a winner. Both the third and fourth treatment produced a clear, statistically significant increase in clickthrough rate over the other treatments. Treatment 3 produced a 56.7% increase over the baseline due to a combination of increased clarity and authority as well as visual clarity around the call-to-action. Treatment 4 produced the largest increase of all—a 108.7% increase over the baseline due to the copy adjustments from treatment three and the new image and embedded CTA, which reinforced the authority of the top copy.

In their next experiment, Leadership Institute isolated the top two performing ads to determine which one produced the most conversions on the following landing page.

experiments.keyLearnings: This told us that we don't necessarily need to "soften" the ask with a story. The story might introduce friction as an estate commitment is a personal decision, not one based on third-party influence.

experiments.title: How a softer call-to-action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call to action generate a higher clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2016 - 03/21/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their Membership Card to non-members giving them the opportunity to become a member.
Anticipating that the direct ask to “become a member” would receive fewer (but more motivated) clicks from the email, they wanted to test whether the added step and call-to-action to “confirm the name on your card” would generate more clicks, and result in more donations.
Since they weren’t making a direct ask, they created a different offer flow for this treatment. They created a two-step form for the treatment that started with a simple “confirm your name” step (pre-populated with the visitor’s name), and then took them to complete their donation. Because the visitor was indicating their intent to become a member by completing the first step, they removed much of the sales copy on the donation page in order to remove friction from the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The softer call to action generated a 54% increase in clicks, but a 51.6% decrease in donations. This revealed a key insight: all traffic is not the same. Visitors come with different motivations, and the traffic to the treatment flow was not at all "sold".

This prompted a second test to see if reintroducing the value proposition copy on the follow-on donation form would affect the results.

But we wanted to see if the send time had a direct affect on click through rate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a valid result of a 38.8% relative increase in click through rate, we discovered that the people on our house file are more ready to engage with a live broadcast offer in the mornings rather than the evenings.

While setting up the advertising and promotions for an upcoming live broadcast, we wanted to determine if our various audience groups for our Facebook Ads would respond differently to various ad treatments. We have 3 main audience groups that we ran this test with: our housefile, housefile lookalikes, and nonprofit professionals.
Our original live broadcast Facebook Ad focused on the speaker and the title, but we thought people may be more motivated by different aspects of the focus topic. For the treatments, we developed an ad that focused on “Growing Your Donor File” and one that focused on “Empathy.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Growing Your Donor File” treatment greatly out performed both the control and treatment 2, lifting click rate by 277% with the nonprofit professionals audience. This learning will allow us to increase our total leads for this specific live broadcast. For future live broadcasts, it shows us that it will be more effective to focus our advertising on the learning and results that someone will get out of it, rather than the speaker and their qualifications.

experiments.title: Will previewing the content of a blog post in an email lead to more clicks through to the post?

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/19/2021
For our 2021 NIO Cultivation series, we were curious whether it would be more effective to preview the content we were sharing in the email itself, or simply just share the link and let the recipient do the work to view the content. In this particular email we were sharing a blog post. The control of the email previewed one of the findings from the blog post and shared images of an experiment, where the treatment was a short message simply just passing along the link to the post.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It's difficult to isolate the exact variable that led to the decrease in traffic on the treatment version of the email. The location of the link in the depth of the email could have played a factor. Because the treatment version did not link to the article until the very end, people may have abandoned the email by that point. Another factor could be that the shorter version created mystery about the content, encouraging recipients to click in and learn more.

 

Moving forward in this cultivation series, we will follow the shorter email structure. But we will definitely need to test this again and better the isolate variables.

experiments.title: How a text link instead of a button impacted email clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a hyperlinked page URL for prospects will achieve an increase in email clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2021 - 02/04/2021
In testing what generated the most clicks when rolling out a new eBook offer to their housefile list, The Fund for American Studies wanted to know whether the approach of using the landing page URL with a hyperlink would definitively be better than using a button in the email promotion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 98.5%, we observed a +17.5% increase in email clickthrough rate.

The reality is that when you write an email from an individual, the reader expects the person that wrote them to not use "marketing techniques" (like in this case, a button).

Therefore, the believability that an actual person on the other side of the email actually wrote them goes up when you treat the clickable link in emails like how a real person would send them — in this case, not as a button, but rather as a page URL that is hyperlinked.

When possible, continue to use clickable URL's instead of fancy marketing elements within emails — like a button.

Moving the call to action closer to the top of the email resulted in a 72% increase in traffic to the donation page. However, despite this increase in traffic, the treatment did not result in a statistically significant increase in the overall number of gifts given.
What this would indicate is that we could shorten our email copy to get the call to action closer to the top and move the additional copy to the donation page. This would let us maintain the level of expressed value proposition while still increasing click through rates. This is a hypothesis that will need to be validated in future email appeals.

Over the last several months, the tone and style of Buckner International’s monthly email appeals had evolved to be longer and more casual in nature. One of the side effects of this evolution was that the call to action and link to the donation page were pushed further down in the copy.

There was a hypothesis that this may be negatively impacting the overall click through and response rates for their appeals. We decided to isolate this variable of the appeal and run an experiment where we moved the call to action up in the email. We then monitored both click through and response rates.

experiments.title: How increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2018 - 01/31/2018
CaringBridge’s marketing team sought to increase the amount of users who got to their “ways to help” page, where there were several options to deepen engagement and provide more support to their friend or family member.
They thought that a slight copy change would align the “Ways to help” button on the journal page with the user’s intent. Instead of “Ways to help”, they proposed “Help [FirstName]” to encourage more people to click through. They thought this might work because they hypothesized that people who were reading this page were (consciously or unconsciously) desiring to help their friend or family member.
They were concerned about possible reactions to this test, so they only included 10% of site traffic in the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new copy produced a 19% lift in clickthrough rate for the “Ways to help” page—an impressive lift considering the small change. This points to more opportunities to align site copy with user intent throughout the site. With the volume of traffic that CaringBridge has, there is tremendous potential to increase clarity and adjust user experience accordingly.

experiments.title: How testing a new offer in a “Dear Reader” ad affected clicks and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a relevant, time-sensitive resource for website visitors will achieve increase clicks and donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2020 - 10/24/2020
As we prepared to launch a new offer to the housefile and in acquisition, we were faced with a dilemma. The new eBook offer was relevant and time-sensitive, but we didn’t want to kick out an offer that was already performing strongly and miss out on additional conversions by losing a month of run time. So instead of replacing the current Dear Reader eBook offer outright, we decided to make the new offer compete against the old offer for the spot.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found is that the new offer had lower performance than the current offer across the board. We saw a 32.8% decrease in conversion rate for the new eBook offer, which you can see broken down by segment below, resulting in fewer engagements in both clicks and donations. These results helped us reach a conclusion to keep the existing offer in place rather than rolling out the new eBook offer in this high-traffic Dear Reader spot. New is not always better! For future new offers, we will plan to run additional A/B tests to determine which offer will lead to maximum results instead of giving away valuable real estate to an offer with unknown performance and risk losing potential revenue and have to make up for lost time later. This experimental approach with initially rolling out to only 50% of site visitors will help us focus less on if the offer is fresh and instead care more about if the offer works. 

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 52.4% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 29.7% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 61.9% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 49.1% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	All Traffic visitors had a 32.8% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 23.0% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 40.4% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 34.5% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 46.6% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.

0% increase in traffic
				× 32.8% decrease in conversion rate

experiments.title: How increasing the number of hyperlinks in an email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing the number of links in an email increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2016 - 01/16/2017
The treatment produced a 27% increase in clickthrough rate, which tells us a few things about our readers. First, it tells us that including more links incentivizes more of them to click, or take the first initial action at the moment that the value proposition hits home. Secondly, it tells us that we had previously been losing potential donors who didn’t make it all the way through the copy to the call-to-action.
This points to an important principle: our donors and prospects have varying levels of motivation. Some are ready to give and need very little convincing, while others are more deliberative and might need more copy to be incentivized to click. Typically, we attempt to address these varying levels of motivation through multiple emails, but this experiment points to the opportunity that we might be able to reach multiple levels of motivation in the same communication.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had always included a “focused link” in their email appeals, which gave readers a single link to click to take action. Historically, this had been a button, but previous testing had shown that a text link performed better.

They wanted to test the effect of giving donors more places to click throughout the email—essentially linking up any text that contained a CTA, so if the donor was motivated, they could take action right then. They produced a treatment that had the exact same copy, but included more clickable links.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
27.1% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How the email design influences clickthrough rate
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified email design increase the proportion of people clicking to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2017 - 03/07/2017
The simplified email resulted in a 14.8% increase to the percentage of visitors going to the landing page. Our hypothesis is that the lack of design seemed to be more genuine and less “marketing” oriented. As a result, the subscribers were more willing to read and engaged with the email once they opened it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate were launching a new digital prayer book to kick off the Lenten season. As part of this launch, they wanted to give their house email file an opportunity to request the resource. This would serve as both cultivation of the subscribers and a new opportunity for them to give a gift once they had requested their PDF.

Historically, the email template used in donor communications was heavily designed with an organizational wrapper. We wanted to see if sending the same email message but in a stripped-down design would have an impact on the percentage of people that clicked on the link to download the prayer book.

				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How isolating a quantified ask affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: When isolated, what impact does the quantified ask have on clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2017 - 03/27/2017
The first treatment, with just the quantified ask, produced a 26% increase in clickthrough rate. However, in further testing, it failed to produce any increase in conversion and produced a spike in refunds.
The second treatment, with the headline change only, produced a 10% decrease in clickthrough rate, which showed that the cumulative lift in previous testing had more correlation with the presence of the quantified ask, but was boosted by the headline as well.
This prompted further testing to see if they could clarify the message on the donation page to reduce confusion and refund rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge tested a radical redesign that produced a big lift in donor conversion. This was a valuable finding, but they wanted further clarity on the lift, since two factors were changed. In the first experiment, they changed the headline of their tribute widget and added a “quantified ask”, with the offer that a $30 gift would keep their friend or loved one’s site online for a month.

They were curious which element had the most impact between these two. So they created two treatments: one that reverted to the old headline and included the quantified ask, and one that reverted to the old body copy (without the quantified ask), but kept the winning headline.

They launched an A/B/C test to validate a winner and determine the impact of each element on conversion.

0% increase in traffic

				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How the position of the ask impacts email engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the call to action up in the email increase overall email engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2017 - 04/03/2017
Moving the call to action closer to the top of the email resulted in a 72% increase in traffic to the donation page. However, despite this increase in traffic, the treatment did not result in a statistically significant increase in the overall number of gifts given.
What this would indicate is that we could shorten our email copy to get the call to action closer to the top and move the additional copy to the donation page. This would let us maintain the level of expressed value proposition while still increasing click through rates. This is a hypothesis that will need to be validated in future email appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Over the last several months, the tone and style of Buckner International’s monthly email appeals had evolved to be longer and more casual in nature. One of the side effects of this evolution was that the call to action and link to the donation page were pushed further down in the copy.

There was a hypothesis that this may be negatively impacting the overall click through and response rates for their appeals. We decided to isolate this variable of the appeal and run an experiment where we moved the call to action up in the email. We then monitored both click through and response rates.

experiments.title: How a different offer on a confirmation page affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Which confirmation page offer will lead to more click-throughs?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2017 - 12/01/2016
When people completed their registration for a NextAfter live broadcast, we wanted to give them an opportunity to engage with other resources that we offer. Our original control prompted registrants to take our free FCORM assessment, but we hypothesized that this might not be the right next step for most people.
For the treatment, we created a page that presented multiple eBook offers that addressed a variety of topics. Our primary goal with this experiment was simply to get people to click through to explore more of our content.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our treatment saw a 156.7% increase in clicks, confirming our initial hypothesis that the FCORM offer was not the right next step. Our general audience is more motivated to learn practical insights that inform their marketing and fundraising.

Additionally, Google Analytics data tells us that those who clicked through the FCORM offer abandoned the site within 1 or 2 steps. Of those who clicked through the treatment page, 51% went on to download at least one other offer, and 41% viewed at least 5 more pages before exiting the site.

experiments.title: How the design of a fundraising email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stripped-down fundraising email increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2016 - 03/30/2016
The stripped-down email (with no template) produced an 80% increase in clickthrough rate. This tells us something about trust — and giving is an act of trust. It may imply that people are more likely to take action when they feel that the email is coming from a person, rather than an organization.
There were still elements of the treatment email that weren’t entirely personal — the big blue button, and the logo. This prompted further testing to see if the removal of these elements produced an additional lift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge received a matching gift for their spring fundraising campaign from a board member. Since the majority of their audience was unfamiliar with the board member, Rik Lalim, they originally created a templated email that was explicitly “CaringBridge” to ease users’ potential anxiety. But they had previously tested emails that had no formatting and emulated “normal” email, and experienced great success. They decided to create a treatment version that stripped away all the formatting and photography.

They then split their email file and ran a test to see which attracted more responses

experiments.title: How additional ad copy affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does reducing copy in the image affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2015 - 11/30/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their Christmas devotional series as a free offer on Facebook. Previous testing had shown that using copy in the image of the ad increased clickthrough rate and conversion rate. But as they reviewed their ad, they wondered if there was actually too much copy in the ad, and reading it bogged the reader down and kept them from clicking.
They made a treatment that removed the subheadline from their ad image, leaving only a single call-to-action with no supporting copy in order to simplify the value proposition.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The ad with reduced copy produced a 19.8% increase in clickthrough rate, which indicated that less copy simplified the value proposition enough to attract more interest from people who saw it.

experiments.title: How streamlining the tribute widget affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a streamlined widget with added social proof inspire more people to begin the donation process?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2015 - 04/24/2015
Most gifts to CaringBridge start with the “tribute widget”, a small box that appears on all Site Journal pages and profile pages. We noticed one day that the tribute widget had a lot of information that could be extraneous and distracting from the intended action, which was to begin the gift process.
The CaringBridge team developed a streamlined version of this widget that removed a “most recent quote”, along with avatars for signed in users. To increase clarity, the team put pre-filled text into the box to instruct the user what to write. Also, we added social proof into the widget, dynamically pulling the number of previous donors to inspire the user to join them.
experiments.keyLearnings: The streamlined widget with added social proof resulted in nearly 52% more people beginning the donation process. We closely monitored donation page conversion rates as well, and there was no change. Clarifying the value proposition by removing extra graphics and copy and reducing anxiety through social proof resulted in a significant lift in dollars raised.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects click rate in a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will drive the most clicks from a Facebook Ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2016 - 08/12/2016
We released a research study on nonprofit value proposition and advertised it through Facebook Ads to people affiliated with NextAfter including emails on file and website visitors.
There are many different aspects of the value proposition that we could focus on for the promotion of the study, so we wanted to test 3 primary value propositions to see what resonated the most with our audience.
The control focused on the depth of the study. Treatment 1 focused on the 4 key components of a value proposition. And treatment 2 focused on the end result of an effective value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the audience that has already engaged with us in some way, it was 254% more effective to focus on the results of more names, donors, and dollars. This will inform the way the we promote and communicate this study to our website traffic and engaged contacts.

This also points to an underlying motivation of our engaged contacts: these people are looking for results in their fundraising.

experiments.title: How a different motivation affects click-through rate on a follow-up offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Which confirmation page will drive more traffic to niosummit.com?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2016 - 10/17/2016
When NextAfter site visitors downloaded the eBook Why Should I Give to You?, we wanted to drive them to the Nonprofit Innovation & Optimization Summit website.
We wondered if visitors would be more motivated to check out the conference by an overall summary of what they would take away, or through specifically featuring some of the top slated speakers.
We configured both confirmation pages, and split the traffic 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focusing on the speakers drove 29.1% more traffic to niosummit.com with a 95.5% level of confidence. This learning will be applied to future confirmation pages when promoting NIO Summit. This will also serve as a baseline for other NIO Summit promotions to test against.

experiments.title: How removing the call-to-action at the top of a landing page impacts name conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the design and navigational elements increase the visitors getting to the assessment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2017 - 10/30/2017
The treatment decreased the amount of people getting to the assessment by 28.6%! While we hypothesized that the simplified page would help decrease friction and motivate more people to click-through because the focus of the page was on the value proposition, we learned that:
People coming to this page were already highly motivated to take the assessment. The additional value proposition focus wasn’t needed to convert these people.
By removing the call-to-action at the top of the page, we made it harder for these highly motivated people to click-through to the actual assessment.
We recommend building upon these results and learnings and continue testing separate elements on this page to get a better understanding of the motivation of the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focus on the Family has a Marriage Assessment on their website to help couples grow and strengthen their marriage. Before an end-user gets to the actual assessment there is a landing page with value proposition and a call-to-action. In an effort to increase conversion on this page of people getting to the assessment, they developed a treatment. They hypothesized that by simplifying the page – including removing the call-to-action at the top of the page – they might be able to motivate more people to take it by leading with the value proposition of the assessment. The goal of the treatment was to eliminate any elements on the page that would cause potential friction in the conversion process. They split the traffic coming to the assessment page and tested the control against the treatment.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
28.6% decrease in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How adding a call-to-action to the top of a simplified landing page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding the button back in to the top of the page increase acquisition on the simplified page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2017 - 11/30/2017
By adding the button back in to the top of the page, we still weren’t able to increase the traffic going to the assessment. We saw a slight decrease in conversion – not as significant as when the button was completely removed – but still a dip.
We hypothesize that this audience is so highly motivated that they don’t actually need this assessment landing page. We propose testing removing this in-between page altogether and sending people straight to the assessment to get more people to take and complete it.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focus on the Family offers a Marriage Assessment on their website. In a previous test on the assessment landing page, we learned that a simplified page where we removed elements of friction actually decreased conversion because we removed a button at the top of the page that would take this highly motivated audience directly to the assessment. To build upon those learnings, we hypothesized that we could increase conversion by keeping and testing the simplified version again but simply just adding back in the button to the assessment at the top of the page. We split the traffic and tested it.

experiments.title: How including clarity in the subject line impacted clicks

rows.name: Christian living in the internet age

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing clarity in the subject line for housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/13/2022
When sending a new offer to the housefile, we wanted to find out how recipients would respond to an email subject line that clearly stated the contents inside. Since TGC regularly sends articles and newsletters that include a subject line that highlight the theme, we wanted to draw attention to the fact that this email was different than usual because it included a newly launched free content offer that was available to download. We sent the control version “Christian living in the internet age” that introduced the topic and developed a treatment that featured the specific resource and CTA “Download our latest eBook ‘Digital Discipleship'”. We split the subject line and sent to 25% and 25% of the housefile recipients.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we saw was a 51.3% increase in clicks with 100% level of confidence on the treatment subject line that included the specific contents of the email. We believe the treatment subject line increased the appeal of the offer and helped call attention to subscribers that this email included something different than what they usually find in their inbox from TGC that they wouldn't want to miss. The treatment subject line improved the relevance of the message, which led to a more engaged audience because those who opened the email were already primed to take the next step had a higher motivation to click the link because of their initial interest in the offer. For our next test when delivering a content offer, we will test a CTA that uses language such as "Get" and include the word "free" to see if we can drive additional clicks.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
51.3% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How adding preview links to a digest email increases click throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding preview links for digest email recipients will achieve more click throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2022 - 03/23/2022
After getting some inspiration from the Marketing Brew’s daily emails, I wondered if the same idea of adding preview links above the full content of a digest email would provide more click throughs.
The goal was to create ease for those who don’t want to read the full email but get straight to the content, but also provide more context about the content for those who want it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was a 30% increase in click throughs on the version of the email with the preview links. Further, looking at the heat map on version b of the email, the highest percentage of link clicks were on the preview links. We can gather that most subscribers prefer to just click direct to the content and do not need more context.
Next, we should test preview links with no further context vs. preview links with context.

				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How adding in more information about an offer to Facebook copy impacted click-through rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing Facebook copy for a Facebook audience will achieve a change in click-throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2022 - 01/31/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. One of their most popular offers is a brochure focusing on the most popular Parables of Jesus.
For this experiment, we tested both a different copy treatment with a different approach to what the brochure offers, as well as images that showed the inside of the brochure.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By changing the copy and images so they gave a Facebook audience more insight into what they were going to be receiving, we saw a 159% increase in click throughs with 100% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that the control copy was too generic to interest people and get them to fill out a form on the landing page with all their information (the resource is mailed); once they got an 'inside' view of the content, they were much more likely to click through on the offer.
This is an experiment we ran across multiple offers like this for Pathway to Victory, with similar results. The next iteration of this test would be to focus on isolating which component of the test (copy, image, etc) really led to the experiment results.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).

experiments.title: The Impact on Email Clicks of Asking For Less Money in an Appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for $10 instead of $30 will increase the click through rate of an appeal
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2022 - 07/25/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. For their July Appeal email, they wanted to see if they could increase donor conversion and email clicks by asking for less. Instead of asking for $30 in the copy, they decided to test asking for $10.
They believed that, with the inflation and financial difficulties families are facing right now, a lower appeal amount will decrease donor friction and increase the click through rate of the appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We had a 40% increase in clicks to the donation page by asking recipients for less. Unfortunately, once on the page, we weren't able to validate an increase in donor conversion for the treatment. We received 22 donations from the email asking for $30 and 25 donations from the email asking for $10. A 13% increase in donations, however only a 32% LOC, so the results were invalid.
We did however show that donors are much more likely to click through on an appeal if asked for less. Further testing is needed to see if we can also improve donor conversion with this approach.

For NIO Summit 2022 we ran a flash sale and want to test the subject line to see which one would lead to more click-throughs. We wondered which language would create more urgency around the deadline: “Today only” vs. “discount ends at midnight”.

experiments.title: How the “lowest cost per click” conversion event impacted full video promotional ad views on Facebook Ads Manager
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the optimizing our video ad campaign for lowest cost per click for prospective and known audiences we were targeting in our ad campaign will achieve only increase clicks, but not increase full video completion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/10/2022
As a part of the NIO Summit 2022 promotional paid advertising campaign to sell event tickets, we wanted to experiment with what kind of video conversion event would produce the best balance between impressions, reach, clicks, and full promotional video views.
Our natural thought was to promote the video ads via “reach” campaign types, but our desire as direct response marketers was to test that natural inclination against a more “direct response” like conversion event of “lowest cost per click.”
How would the video ad start and finish rates play out while balanced against clicks and reach?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 100% level of confidence, we observed that full video views on the "lowest cost per click" campaign surprisingly produced a staggering increase of full video views in the amount of +851.9% over the "reach" optimized campaigns.
It's worth noting that we did reach 85,286 with the "reach" optimized campaign when compared to just 10,287 prospects in the "lowest cost per click" optimized campaign.
Alternatively, we did see 9.5x more clicks (+7805% increase in ad link clicks, with a 100% level of confidence) in the "lowest cost per click" optimized campaign, though.
So, to summarize — although we reached 8.2x more people with the "reach" campaign, we saw 9.5x more link clicks with the "lowest cpc" campaign (and 1.14x more "full video views" as noted in the data above) over the "reach" optimized campaign.
Ultimately, it comes down to what you value and your ultimate goal ... if you're looking to just get your video ads in front of the largest number of people — you should optimize for the "reach" event type. However, if you're mostly interested in driving site visitors or full video views based upon who you reached, through your video campaigns — it makes sense to optimize for the "lowest cpc" event type.

experiments.title: How including a preview of a survey question in a Facebook ad impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in a Facebook ad graphic for potential survey takers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/18/2022
We were running a survey offer on Facebook and wanted to develop a treatment to increase clickthrough rate. We wondered if replacing the stock imagery with a text-based ad that gave a preview of the first question on the survey would offer more clarity to ad viewers about what types of questions they would be asked to complete. We also changed the generic headline “Tell us what you think” to a more direct and specific CTA of “Take the survey” to offer additional clarity about the desired action we wanted them to take from the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running both versions of the ad was that the treatment design led to a 452% increase in clickthrough rate compared to the control with 100% level of confidence. Not only did we see a lift in the first step of the process, but we also saw a 307% increase in email acquisition. We weren't able to just send more people to visit the landing page with the treatment, we were also able to generate more survey responses. In addition to the lifts in both traffic and conversion, we also saw a 99% decrease in our cost per acquisition. We believe the additional clarity in the text-based ad reduced friction and confusion about the desired next step which made it easier for ad viewers to begin taking the survey and created cognitive momentum to complete the full process.

experiments.title: How emphasizing exclusivity in a Facebook ad graphic impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal by highlighting exclusivity  for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/19/2022
We were running an eBook cover image for a new offer from The Heritage Foundation and wondered if we could make the offer more appealing by highlighting the exclusivity of the resource. This research report is very unique as it is only available from this organization and no one else, but this was not communicated anywhere in the ad. We created a treatment graphic that placed emphasis on the fact that this offer was exclusive and we reiterated the CTA “Learn More” directly on the graphic to help it stand out in the newsfeed.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running both versions of the ad was that the treatment produced a 21.6% lift in click rate with 98.4% level of confidence so the test results are valid. We believe this experiment saw positive results because the enhanced appeal of making the offer more exclusive made it more desirable because ad viewers were now aware they could not get a resource like this from any other organization or get access to this information anywhere else. We will continue testing this concept on other exclusive offers from The Heritage Foundation and see if we can achieve similar results in other acquisition spaces—such as email prospecting—using this learning.

experiments.title: How simplifying a cultivation email template impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying an email template for online course takers on the housefile will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2022 - 07/29/2022
When sending a cultivation email offering multiple free courses to the housefile, we developed a control that had hyperlinked images and a course description that included the number of sessions for each course and the name of the instructor. Because we had previously tested into a simplified template with this audience for a monthly newsletter, we also developed a treatment that simply introduced the courses as individual hyperlinks in bullet format. We split the file 50/50 and sent the email to see which version of the email would generate more clicks to the registration page.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was the treatment that had no images led to a 71% increase in click rate with 100% level of confidence, validly generating more traffic to the course registration pages. Interestingly enough, when we followed the control vs. the treatment visitors further downstream, we noticed that overall donations to these 3 courses experienced a 49% decrease for course registrants who came through the treatment version of the email with 98% level of confidence, making it a valid decrease. So although on the surface, it looks like we were able to convert more traffic from the email, we saw inverse results when we analyzed donor conversion rate in this experiment. Our hypothesis is that because the course images and descriptions could potentially add more perceived value in the mind of the course subscriber and better align with the landing page and donation page design experience, the control was able to produce more donations.

experiments.title: How a video affects click-through rates when compared to a survey preview for a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a video ad will increase click rates on a Facebook ad for a survey offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2022 - 02/27/2022
Recently we have seen success using video format ads and survey preview ads, so naturally, we wanted to test them against each other to see how they would perform head-to-head.
For this experiment, we pitted a new video treatment against our survey preview control, left all other ad elements unchanged, and ran the two ads for 30 days to compare results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 30 days, the results of our test were conclusive, with the video ad producing a 77.6% decline in click-through rates when compared with the survey preview, with a 100% level of confidence.
This further validates our past learnings that previewing a survey in the ad creative is likely to increase click rates by catching the user's attention and engaging them visually on an issue they care about, leading them forward into the offer where they want to share their thoughts.
We'll now look to globalize this across survey offers for our other public policy clients.

experiments.title: How the power of not “breaking a streak” affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting a streak of giving for key multiyear donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/22/2022
CaringBridge was running an appeal to celebrate their 25th anniversary as a company. They had read research about how people hate breaking streaks and strive for continuity, and wanted to test the inclusion of this language in their appeals with a group of consistent donors.
They took a “control” email from the campaign and kept it largely the same, but changed the subject line from “There’s Still Time to Help Mark the 25th Birthday of CaringBridge, [FirstName]” to “Don’t Break the Streak, [FirstName]!”. Then, in the copy they highlighted the fact that the donors had previously given to CaringBridge and asked them to continue their streak of generosity.
Finally, the email thanked them for their continued, generous giving.
They split the list of donors in half to see how they responded to the two different messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment group that received the "continuity" messaging showed a 65.4% increase in conversion rate, bolstered by an 81% increase in clickthrough rate.
This shows evidence of "commitment bias", where people have a tendency to remain committed to our past behaviors, particularly those exhibited publicly, even if they do not have desirable outcomes.
This is seen in the for-profit space in concepts like Duolingo streaks or Snapchat streaks, but also is applicable to charitable giving through concepts like this.
It should be noted that this likely has limited application as there is a strong possibility for diminishing returns if overused—but the results can be powerful!

experiments.title: How using value prop language in an email impacted click-through and donation conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop language for an email audience will achieve a higher click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2022 - 05/24/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Most of the language used in email appeals has traditionally been focused on the premium offer itself, with mentions of the book, what it contains, and different ways to get the book. For this experiment we wanted to test whether making the email more value-prop focused, with a reference to the book, would impact click-through and donor conversion rates.
This test was run with multiple emails; the results in this experiment are for the 1st email send, including a resend. Both emails pointed to the exact same landing page, which focused on the premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment were extremely interesting. While there was a 106% increase in click-through rates total, with a 100% level of confidence, when looking at the experiment through a donation conversion lens, the results were different.
When looking at open-to-donation conversion rates, there was a 29% lift in donation conversion rates with the value prop version, with a 70% level of confidence. However, when looking at how many people clicked a link and actually completed the donation, there was a 37% DECREASE in donation conversions, with a 92% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that this result is that while the value prop version was driving more clicks to the landing page, without having any value prop language on the page itself, those potential donors lost their interest in making a donation and receiving the premium.
This then led to the next round of testing, which incorporated value prop language onto the landing page itself.

experiments.title: How asking an open-loop question on an ad image affects click through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking an open loop question on the ad image for Facebook users will achieve increase donation rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2022 - 06/06/2022
The Heritage Foundation wanted to improve CTR on their Big Tech Survey campaign. The original ad had long, issue-focused copy and a statement on the ad image telling the audience to take their survey to fight back against big tech.
in an attempt to earn more clicks, Heritage tested two ad treatments: one with the same image/statement but shorter body copy and a second with a new image and an open loop question to encourage people to click to find out what they knew.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the test for 30 days, Heritage observed that treatment #2 (with open loop question on ad image) produced a dramatic lift of 304% with a 100% LoC. This indicates that by opening a loop of curiosity on the most prominent part of the ad, Heritage was able to inspire more people to click through, presumably because they wanted to test their knowledge — confirming the hypothesis that creating curiosity would result in a lift in clicks.
This test also supports the general supposition that making changes to the ad's most prominent element - the ad image - may have a greater impact than making similar changes to the body copy, which is less prominent.
Next, Heritage may choose to test a single element of this concept — either the ad image or the value prop language — to see which element has a bigger impact when changed. But for now, we can conclude that:
Making changes to the ad image has a larger impact on performance than changing the body copyOpening a loop of curiosity is likely to encourage more people to click through

experiments.title: How leveraging a well-known eBook author affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leveraging a well-known ebook author will achieve higher click-through rates on a Facebook ad for a content offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2022 - 07/12/2022
In an effort to improve click-through rates for a Fund for American Studies content offer, we wanted to test how adding a callout and image of a well-known author (in this case, Rand Paul) would impact click-through rates.
We hypothesized that featuring a U.S. Senator would add authority to the ebook, catch people’s eye, and inspire more people to click.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 30 days of running this experiment, we were able to confirm our hypothesis by producing a 58.9% lift in click-through rates with a 99.6% level of confidence.
This experiment shows that "name recognition" can be a powerful motivator for content offers. This may be especially true in this instance because the well-known name in this offer is associated as an ally and "force for good" by the target audience.
When prospects recognize the name and face of someone they respect, they may be more likely to accept a content offer as credible + valuable.
We can now test different treatments against this concept to continue to improve performance.

experiments.title: How giving subscribers part of a content offer in the promotional email impacted clicks to the offer signup page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing subscribers with a sample of a content offer in the promo email will increase clicks to the offer signup page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/25/2022
KCBI is a leading Christian radio station and ministry in Dallas, Texas.  About a year ago, they created a “verse a day” subscription offer for their subscribers. People who enrolled received a verse or two in their inboxes each morning. They wanted to promote the offer to their file again but wanted to see if this time they could get more clicks to the landing page by providing subscribers with a sample verse like the ones they would get. We hypothesized that this would increase the appeal and clarity of the subscription offer and cause more people to visit the signup page. Simply saying, “we will give you a verse or two each morning” is far more vague than showing them the types of verses and content they will get each day.
experiments.keyLearnings: While both emails featured conversational and personal copy, the treatment email allowed subscribers to experience the content they'd be receiving if they signed up. This increased the appeal and clarity of the offer and led to more visits to the signup page. We will continue to test into ways of allowing people to "try before they buy" so we can reduce the mental friction that comes from not knowing what an offer will be like.

experiments.title: How removing a video thumbnail impacted email engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the video image from the email for ACI subscribers will achieve increased click through rate due to the increased deliverability rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2022 - 05/23/2022
Experimentation with similar organizations has found that the more “stripped down” an email is, the better overall deliverability is which often results in improved engagement. This is due to how providers like Gmail and Yahoo categorize emails with images in them.
With this in mind, we decided to run an experiment for the most recent Cornerstone Conversation email. The entire purpose of the email is to drive engagement for people to view the YouTube video. We wanted to see if removing the YouTube image would improve deliverability enough to drive additional clicks to the video.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By removing the video screenshot from the email, we observed a statistically significant 16.9% decrease in clicks. Additionally, we did not see any increase in deliverability or opens from the removal of the image. In this case, it would appear that the single image didn't impact the deliverability of the email or result in the email being added to the "Promotions" tab which highly designed emails can often do.
In subsequent email, we will be sure to include a screenshot of any videos or even audio recordings to help improve click through rate. Additionally, we'll want to test utilizing Dr. Carson's likeness in emails to see if that helps further drive engagement on emails where a donation is the primary call to action.

We were looking to increase click through rates on a free online course offer on Facebook and wanted to see if using a video clip of the course content in the ad creative would increase clarity around the offer as well as enhance the appeal of the offer by giving visitors a glimpse of the content to spike their curiosity.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the video ad was the treatment produced a 111% increase in click rate with 100% level of confidence. We believe the increased appeal and clarity introduced in the video led to more interest in learning more about the offer and led additional visitors to click through to the landing page. The course is also taught by the Seminary's President so highlighting this in the video could have also increased credibility and legitimacy of this specific course. Based on these results, we will identify more video clips that can be used to promote free online courses to see if we are able to achieve similar lifts.
