experiments.title: How the placement of an offer on article pages impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Within an article, where is the best placement for an offer to increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2017 - 11/30/2017
By placing the offer at the end of the article, we were able to increase conversion by 101%! This tells us a couple of things:
1. Placement off an offer on a page matters significantly2. People are more highly motivated to want/get the offer after they have experienced the full value of the content on the page. The offer acts as a natural next step in the mind of the end-user.
Now that we know where to place offers within article pages to get the highest conversion, we recommend testing the kind of offer we present in that space.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focus on the Family gets a high volume of traffic on their article pages. In a previous experiment, we tested putting an offer (content or donation) on the article pages to convert that traffic into names and potentially donors for the organization. The content offer was highly successful. For this experiment we built upon those learnings and hypothesized that the placement of the offer may have an impact on conversion. Would an offer in the middle of the page or at the end of the article convert more site visitors? We split the traffic coming to the page to test this and find out.

experiments.title: How an inline ask in place of a midroll ad affects clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Will replacing the mid-roll advertising banner with a value-focused inline donation ask increase clicks for this placement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2017 - 11/09/2017
Focus on the Family currently uses DoubleClick for Publishers to manage ad fill throughout their website.  Most of the ads are for their own resources, giving opportunities, and some third-party offers.
One such placement is a mid-roll banner targeting long-form article pages.  They hypothesized that a value-focused inline donation ask may garner more clicks that the ad banners due to the limited amount of value proposition copy in the ads and the effects of “banner blindness”—a web usability phenomenon where visitors consciously or subconsciously ignore banner-like information.  Since the value of a donor is higher than the other offers in rotation, they also saw significant value if clickthrough could be increased.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The inline ask saw a 74.9% decrease in clicks compared to the normal banner ads.  While there were broader appeal offers mixed into the ads that makes the comparison indirect, we hypothesize from low donor conversion rate that the original motivation of this audience may not align well with a direct donation ask.  This result stands in stark contrast to the results of our similar test of an inline email acquisition offer in this same placement.
We’re reminded that motivation is both the strongest factor of conversion as well as the most difficult to influence.  We plan to continue testing the placement and type of offers used on these long-form article pages to improve alignment with the content and visitor motivation.

experiments.title: How a plain, stripped down, email impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a stripped down email help improve email engagement?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2019
Back to the Bible offered people on their emailing file the opportunity to sign-up for a free Bible Reading Plan. The control email that was being sent used a marketing template and lots of images. We hypothesized that a stripped down email could help improve email engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings: By sending a plain text email, we were able to increase email engagement by 19%. Not only that, but we also saw an increase in opens - even though the subject lines were identical. This tells us that we had increased deliverability and inboxing. People appreciate hearing from Arnie - as a person. The tone and copy of the email was believable that he actually sent the email to the file. By stripping the email of the marketing elements (images, template, etc) we were able to reinforce this sentiment as well.

experiments.title: How Adding Negative Search Terms Affected CTR in Google Ads
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding “phrase match” negative search terms increase our CTR?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2020
We saw an opportunity to improve our click through rate for The Heritage Foundation in Google Ads.
We saw that the Ad copy was relevant and optimized for performance however, our click through rate and search impression share was lower.
We observed that our brand terms for our organization overlapped with other (non-competing) organizations as a universal term that is widely used.
We ran a search term analysis Google Ads Script – N-Gram Analysis by BrainLabs that compiles the search terms into phrases to discover patterns in search terms. We then selected the phrases that didn’t meet our target performance goals and we added these phrases to our negative search terms list as “phrase match” negative keywords.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding “phrase match” negative search terms effected the CTR by limiting the Ad showing on lower performance search terms. In addition to raising the CTR:
Search Impression Share increased by 17%Cost Per Conversion lowered -33.59%Average Cost Per Click lowered -21.80%

experiments.title: How the headline on a blog post Facebook ad affects landing page views

rows.name: Headline Using Post Title and Basic CTA

experiments.researchQuestion: Which style of Facebook ad headline will drive more traffic to the blog post?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2019 - 11/08/2019
In this experiment, we were launching Facebook ads to drive traffic to a newly updated year-end fundraising blog. In developing the ads, we wondered what type of headline would be most effective at driving visits to the blog post. We have seen strong call-to-actions be effective for conversion focused ads, but wondered if that would hold up in an ad purely designed to drive traffic.
Version A used simply the post title as it might appear if someone shared the post organically. Version B used a headline that packed the specific value of the blog post into an action statement. Version C took a hybrid approach of putting the blog title first, followed by a simple call-to-action separated by a pipe.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The hybrid approach was the overwhelming winner, increasing landing page views (people that made it to the page and loaded it) by 190%. This shows us a few things...
A simple blog post title is not enough to call a Facebook viewer to take an action, even though this is the more natural appearance of an organic post.The value heavy CTA, although we have seen it be effective for driving someone to convert on a specific offer, is not nearly as powerful for driving pure traffic. In this case, our goal is to drive traffic to build an audience, not get a specific download or opt-in instantly.The hybrid approach clearly communicates the topic, and it clearly communicates the medium. The more action oriented headline could cause confusion about what the actual content is on the other end. But the hybrid approach makes it abundantly clear what is expected of the Facebook viewer: "Read the post".

experiments.title: How increasing relevance in a call-to-action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing relevance in a call-to-action affect clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/05/2019
Gospel for Asia was running a microdonation campaign to promote their Bridge of Hope program on Facebook. While the program was performing well, they felt that there was room for optimization. After a review of the creative and the data, one simple optimization opportunity that stood out was the call-to-action on the Facebook ad. The CTA on the control said “Give them hope!”—which seemed very broad, and something that many nonprofits could say. The team proposed a CTA that was very specific to the story—”Give a child a childhood!” They hypothesized that this would drive more clicks to the donation page, which would give more donation opportunities. If conversion rate held, it could deliver more revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment CTA with increased relevance produced a 35.8% increase in clicks to the donation page. While this eventually will affect revenue, the team didn't want to run an underperforming control any longer and rolled the winner out before attempting to validate on revenue.
This test, very simply, shows that congruence between message and CTA is incredibly important and can have a tremendous impact on motivating potential donors to take action. Further testing must be done on this funnel.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity in email copy affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will copy with a clearer call-to-action increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/02/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending a mid-month update on their fundraising appeal for the month. The offer was a book by Joni Earackson Tada. The control email highly incentivized the click by asking the reader to “find out why” the sender (Greg Laurie) admires Joni, the author of the book. While this might drive a lot of clicks, we’ve seen this approach drive much lower conversion rates, as those who click through are not as motivated to accept the offer.
We devised a treatment that moved some of that copy into the email, incentivizing the click by  giving them a timely call-to-action to find out how to get their own copy of the book before Mother’s Day.
We split the file and ran a test to see which email won.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 58.7% decrease in clickthrough rate, which tells us that tipping our hand at the book offer reduced motivation to click through. However, the treatment produced a 55.1% lift in donor conversion on the following page, which tells us that those who did click through were more motivated to accept the offer.
Because of the disparity in clickthrough rate, the control conversion path produced more overall revenue, despite the lower conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding an image in an email strengthens the value proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding an image of the person in the email story increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2018 - 07/03/2018
Buckner International was sending their third campaign email of a series dedicated to a matching gift for the Family Pathways program. In this email, they tell a story about a single mom named Marissa who was helped by the program. They wanted to test if including a photo of Marissa and her daughter would increase clickthrough rate and build people’s emotional connection to her. All other copy was the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: Adding the image of Marissa increased clickthrough rate by 76%. Giving people a visual image to connect her story to, rather than just copy about her story, made the story more real to people and motivated them to click through to the donation page. We should test this concept with other email appeals.

experiments.title: How a target impression share bid strategy decreased ROI
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a target impression share bid strategy increase donor conversion rate or revenue metrics?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2019
Within the Google Ads platform, we decided to test a target impression share bid strategy to see if we could increase our level of competitiveness and generate more clicks and donors more effectively than with a typical impression-based bid strategy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With 98.7% level of confidence, we saw a -9.0% decrease in ad clicks.
Combined with this decrease in clickthrough rate from our ads, we also saw a spike in cost per click value (+107% increase in CPC, with 100% level of confidence).
Further, we noticed that the target impression share bid strategy (which is designed to focus on “maximizing opportunity” from the Search Network) did not increase revenue (+2.3% in revenue with a 53.4% LoC). Finally, it showed a directional decrease in donor conversion rate, as well (-17% in donor conversion rate with a 51.4% LoC).
In summary, do not use a target impression share bidding on a brand keyword strategy, as it only serves to boost your costs dramatically, with no evidence that it will increase your results.

experiments.title: How additional cultivation impacts digital engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will additional cultivation-focused emails increase the overall engagement (email open and click) for the ADF subscribers?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2019 - 10/12/2019
During a data analysis performed for ADF at the beginning of the calendar year, we discovered a handful of trends related to the email file that were concerning:
3 out of 4 ADF subscribers had not opened an email within the last six months
New and existing donors were twice as likely to disengage as non-donor subscribers
ADF was sending twice as many solicitation emails as it was cultivation and stewardship-focused emails. Simply put: the subscribers were being asked twice as much as they were given back to.
While the digital fundraising program had been growing, these trends illustrated a potential problem in the near future if they were not corrected. The historical success of ADF’s online fundraising was closely correlated to the size and engagement of the email file.
Given the ambitious fundraising goals, we couldn’t reduce the number of solicitations. So, instead, we decided to test out an additional cultivation series that would increase the number of emails sent to the file. The additional emails would focus on educating and feeding back into the various segments. There would be no additional solicitations.
At a tactical level, this email series consisted of simply-designed emails sent each Friday by one of the authors on the blog (Sarah K). They would include a link to a blog post or a video and we emphasized getting replies to the email.
To validate the impact of this series, we split the email file in half, paying special attention to ensure that there was equal representation of active, lapsed and non-donors and that the average revenue donor was similar between the splits.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 6 months, we observed a 52% increase in the likelihood for a subscriber to engage with digital communications. (An engagement is considered an open or click.) We also observed a 41% increase in online revenue from existing donors. When we dug into the unique segments, we observed the following:
Non-donors saw a 65% increase in their engagement. In the last 3 months of the experiment, this fell to a 51% increase when compared to the control segment.Lapsed donors saw a 50% increase in their engagement. In the last 3 months of the experiment, this dropped slightly to a 47% increase when compared to the control segment.The active donors saw a 28% increase in their engagement. In the last 3 months of the experiment, this dropped slightly to a 27% increase when compared to the control segment.
Based upon all of these trends, we can derive two key takeaways:
While the overall engagement dipped slightly the longer people were in the program, overall engagement remained high for the segment involved in the cultivation series.The lapsed donors and non-donors were the segment that saw the greatest improvement in their engagement.

experiments.title: How first-name personalization affects email engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalization increase email engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2016 - 11/28/2016
National Breast Cancer Foundation had a recommendation that they start including first and last names on their email capture forms. Since this would take some development work to update all of their forms, they wanted to see if it made a difference in email engagement.
They had enough names on their list who had first names (from other sources) that they were able to run a test on a post-campaign email. This email had two calls to action—a donation ask and a Facebook share link. They split the file between the emails that had no personalization and the emails that had personalization and measured the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The segment of the file that had personalization had a 270.1% increase over the emails with no first names. This told NBCF that while overall engagement was still lower than they had liked, it was well worth it to update their email capture forms and start collecting first and last names along with the emails.
Based on this data, they planned an additional test to see how asking for first and last name affected email signup rates.

experiments.title: How a personal message of impact affects clickthrough rate on a recurring donation ask
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a personal message of impact affect clickthrough rate on a recurring donation ask?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/22/2019
CaringBridge launched a new effort to acquire recurring donors. Since the most likely people to become recurring donors were people who had visited the site multiple times, they created an exit intent popup that fired as someone left CaringBridge after their second visit (and only their second visit). The first iteration of this popup was a “faceless” message that made a direct ask and sent the user to a recurring-only donation page. They hypothesized that making the ask from a mom who had used CaringBridge would increase clickthrough rate and get more people to the page, thus generating more recurring donors. They launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The personal ask treatment reduced clickthrough by 93%. Interestingly, the mom-centric ask had almost a 30% conversion rate, but clickthrough was so much lower that it only generated 1/3 as many gifts. CaringBridge will continue to test value propositions both on the exit-intent popup and the recurring donation page to optimize this funnel. Additionally, they will also test conversion rates as they roll the popup out to more traffic—including people who have visited CaringBridge five or more times.

experiments.title: How a simplified newsletter affects click rate over a templatized version, part II
experiments.researchQuestion: Which newsletter template gets more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/08/2019
In a previous test, DTS was sending out their top three DTS Voice articles to their subscribers as a cultivation opportunity, and wanted to see whether a simplified template (links only in bullet points) or a more designed layout, with imagery and CTA’s along with blurbs about the content, performed better. They used the same content pieces in each email.
After the original test, we hypothesized that that the CTA buttons were potentially causing friction more than the design of the newsletter, and that that was the cause of the original lift from the second version.
In this test, we tried linked text in the templatized version vs. the original simplified template.
experiments.keyLearnings: Even with the adjustments made to the templatized version, the simple newsletter still received 82% more clicks than the control. Moving forward, we'll use the simple template to get this content to DTS Voice subscribers in the most effective way.

experiments.title: How minimal content impacts subscriber engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified message that focuses on the links rather than the reason to click increase overall clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/06/2019 - 09/10/2019
The primary goal of Alliance Defending Freedom’s weekly cultivation series is to engage subscribers with content that is central to ADF’s mission. With this in mind, past optimization efforts have centered around finding the optimal tactics to lift open and click through rates. All of the emails are sent from the same sender, “Sarah”, and are written to appear as one-to-one as possible.
In recent discussions, a hypothesis was proposed that we could potentially increase the number of visitors clicking by reducing the amount of content we put into the email. The theory was based upon the thought that we may be spending too much time “selling” the content instead of letting the visitors get to the information.
To test this hypothesis, we created a treatment version of the weekly email with a dramatically simplified message.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that we were able to increase overall click through rate by 13% by reducing the amount of effort we spent selling the content in the email. If this proves to be a universal tactic, this could significantly help speed up the content creation process. However, this concept will need to be further tested to ensure it wasn't an anomaly.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).

We have a hypothesis that the use of CTA buttons in the first email contributed to the lower click rate, so future testing will involve linked text instead of buttons on the templatized version.

experiments.title: How creating a congruent experience impacted visitor engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a mobile experience that is more congruent with the visitors’ original source (Facebook) increase their engagement with the content?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2016 - 11/07/2016
Alliance Defending Freedom maintains a blog section of their website that is updated at least once per day. A recent data analysis discovered that, while there was significant volume to the blog, the engagement of this audience was low. On average 85% of all traffic left within the first 10 seconds of arriving on the site.
After digging deeper into the website metrics, we discovered that mobile and desktop traffic were suffering the most from low engagement. Since the primary goal of the blog is to engage visitors, we decided to run an experiment designed to get more visitors to stay longer on the site. Our primary measure of success would be the number of visitors that stayed on the site longer than 60 seconds.
Our hypothesis was that there was a disconnect between the environment that the majority of visitors were coming from (in this case, Facebook) and the environment they were landing on. As a result, we decided to test out a mobile blog experience that was more congruent with the Facebook’s style of browsing.
We stripped out many of the images, reduced the navigation and secondary calls-to-action, and then gave visitors a small amount of the content with the remainder hidden behind a “Read More” button.  The goal of the “Read More” button was to expose them to the additional content found at the bottom of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The mobile-optimized blog yielded a 76% increase in the number of visitors that stayed on the site longer than 60 seconds. This equated to 1,656 more people that consumed ADF’s content in a 3 week period.
While the 60-second mark was the primary success metric for this experiment, looking at some of the earlier times yielded even larger lifts in engagement. We saw a 182% increase in the number of visitors that stayed at least 30 seconds and a 366% increase in the number that stayed longer than 10 seconds.
To us, this demonstrates the importance of visual congruency for the visitor. If we are able to match the environment they are coming from, the visitors will be more likely to engage with and consume the content.

experiments.title: How a donor-based appeal (instead of a goal-based appeal) affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a donor-based appeal (instead of a goal-based appeal) affects conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/27/2019
CaringBridge had a matching gift offer as part of their year-end campaign. They had recently developed a dynamic thermometer that would update as a goal was reached and had leveraged it to increase conversion. However, they wondered if the large goal amount was too vague, and possibly caused too much cognitive friction in the mind of the reader. Did it make the reader wonder if the large goal could be achieved? Did it make the reader do mental math to approximate how much had been raised? Most importantly—did it communicate to the reader that their gift could have an impact?
They decided to run an experiment with a donor-based appeal, breaking their day down into hourly goals and keeping a dynamic countdown of how many donors were needed to meet the hourly goal. Their hypothesis was that smaller goals would bring it more within reach of the donor, and that communicating a goal in terms of how many donations—rather than how much money—was needed would tell the donor how they could materially affect the goal. Notably, the treatment did not include the match language at all.
They launched an A/B test to understand the impact of this treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version produced a 25% increase in conversion—at scale, a tremendous increase in revenue. This suggested that the donor-based goal was not only easier to understand, but more appealing. This approach will be tested again in future appeals to refine our understanding of this increase.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of calls to action affects sharing
experiments.researchQuestion: Which presentation of the social sharing buttons will prompt the greatest amount of sharing?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2015 - 12/02/2015
As part of their Thanksgiving stewardship campaign, Alliance Defending Freedom wanted to find a way to increase the sharing of the Thanksgiving video. By default, the landing page incorporated sharing features on every landing page. This makes it easy to share the content but there isn’t specific language asking for the share as a call-to-action.
We wanted to see what impact specifically asking visitors to share the message would have.  Additionally, we wanted to see whether giving multiple sharing options would increase their likelihood to share or if it is better to focus on just a single social network.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced two interesting learnings:
We discovered that simplifying the call-to-actions so that we were only asking for one action, Share to Facebook, we were able to increase the number of total shares by 230%. This is a principle that has proven out in multiple experiments. When we give visitors multiple options, they are more likely to choose none of them.The other major takeaway was that just asking for a social share wasn’t enough to motivate the action. There was no statistically significant difference between the control and the multiple sharing options with copy advocating for the social share.

experiments.title: How a celebrity signer affects open and click rates

rows.name: Students freeze up when they hear this

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a subject line that mentions a conservative celebrity increase open rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/12/2018
The Leadership Institute has access to many high profile people in the conservative movement. They wanted to see if mentioning these people would impact their online presence. They decided to test a subject line that states Mike Huckabee has involvement with LI vs a vague subject line about something that makes students freeze when they hear it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The subject line that included Mike Huckabee garnered an 18.3% open rate, while the vague subject line about students earned a 20% open rate. This is a lift of 9.5%. There was a 121.3% lift in clicks for the email that had a student focus in the subject line.
This experiment tells us that LI email recipients care more about what is being discussed on campus than about a conservative celebrity hosting an LI employee on their show. However, these results could change depending on the celebrity signer. In the future, the Leadership Institute should look into how the subject line and the copy work together to generate opens and clicks.

experiments.title: How adding a personal story impacted email engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a personal story included in the email increase subscriber engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/12/2019 - 07/16/2019
Alliance Defending Freedom was continuing the longitudinal experiment to increase cultivation to a targeted segment of their email house file. The goal of these emails was to simply engage the audience and provide them with ongoing content and stewardship. With that in mind, we wanted to find ways to increase that engagement.
One hypothesis we developed was that we could potentially increase the personal-feel of these emails by including stories from the sender’s own life. We believed this personal touch might increase the likelihood that someone would click on the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Surprisingly, the personal story added to the beginning and end of the cultivation email resulted in a 36% decline in click through rate. After reading through the emails with a fresh perspective, our hypothesis for this decrease is that adding the personal story shifted the content of the email from subscriber-focused to sender-focused. The control starts out the email asking the subscribers a question while the treatment starts out talking about the sender (Sarah) themselves.
This is a good reminder that we need to craft every email with the goal of keeping the focus on the benefit to the subscriber.

experiments.title: How location based imagery affects click-through rate of a Facebook Ad campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will location specific imagery lead to greater landing page click-throughs in a geo-targeted ad campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2019 - 06/18/2019
In this experiment, we were advertising online fundraising workshops hosted by NextAfter Institute. Each specific location had it’s own geo-targeted Facebook Ad campaign. The intention was to keep the workshop in their mind during the course of the sales period and drive them back to the landing page to consider attending.
We knew our sample size would be too small to optimized for ticket sales, but we hoped to run an experiment to optimize our landing page click-throughs.
The control ads used a picture of a course instructor teaching in a room full of attendees. Common advertising recommendations are to use people in imagery to make it relatable. But we wondered if using images specific to someone’s city would actually be more a powerful driver to show that the training was more specific and local to them.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment ad set resulted in a 64% increase in click-throughs to the landing page at a 99% level of confidence. From this, we can learn that local-recognition is a more significant motivating factor than showing imagery of what the workshops actually look like. This will be our new default ad-set to start testing on to try and drive even more traffic to these workshops.
It's worth noting that these results are an aggregate of 6 different geo-targeted campaigns including Chicago, Dallas, Boston, Toronto, Vancouver, and London. Each campaign ran for 2-3 months between March 2019 and June 2019.

However, in the redesign, the donate button had been given the same visual treatment—or equal weighting—as the rest of the navigation items. Their team hypothesized that calling the button out by giving it a white background would increase the traffic to the donation page and might result in additional revenue from people who previously couldn’t find it. They launched an A/B test with this simple addition to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new donate button attracted 15% more traffic to the donation page. It is of note that conversion rate on the donation page did not increase—motivation stayed the same. However, there was a significant increase in revenue, though not statistically valid, due to more people finding the page.

experiments.title: How different banner value propositions affect clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will different banner value propositions affects clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2018 - 01/02/2018
Compassion International had a large acquisition spend on BibleGateway.com. A previous landing page experiment had produced a significant lift in donor conversion, but they hypothesized that the banner creative might be optimized to produce an additional lift. They produced four different banners to test different value propositions and see if any of them increased donor conversion.
The control asked the reader: Do you share a birthday with a child in poverty? It then gave them the opportunity to select their birthdate. They felt that this lacked context and incentive to click through.
They created a second treatment that gave the reader the opportunity to “make your birthday count” by sharing it “with a child in poverty”.
A third treatment gave the reader the opportunity to “find their match”, and a fourth introduced suspense, by suggesting that they share a birthday with a child in poverty—”who is it?”
They launched a four-way test to see how these treatments increased clickthrough rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: None of the banners exceeded the control’s clickthrough rate, which meant that without a much higher on-page conversion rate, they wouldn’t increase conversions. Only one ad (“who is it?”) came within reach of the control in terms of conversion rate. However, the landing page might be optimized to dovetail with the message of the banner and increase congruency, which inspired future experiments.

experiments.title: How increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2018 - 01/31/2018
CaringBridge’s marketing team sought to increase the amount of users who got to their “ways to help” page, where there were several options to deepen engagement and provide more support to their friend or family member.
They thought that a slight copy change would align the “Ways to help” button on the journal page with the user’s intent. Instead of “Ways to help”, they proposed “Help [FirstName]” to encourage more people to click through. They thought this might work because they hypothesized that people who were reading this page were (consciously or unconsciously) desiring to help their friend or family member.
They were concerned about possible reactions to this test, so they only included 10% of site traffic in the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new copy produced a 19% lift in clickthrough rate for the “Ways to help” page—an impressive lift considering the small change. This points to more opportunities to align site copy with user intent throughout the site. With the volume of traffic that CaringBridge has, there is tremendous potential to increase clarity and adjust user experience accordingly.

experiments.title: How Facebook ad content at year-end affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which type of Facebook ad content will drive the most traffic at year-end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 12/31/2017
Compassion International was advertising on Facebook promoting one-time donations leading up to the end of the calendar year. They wanted to test different types in the content of the ad to see which drove the most traffic to their donation page. They launched three different types of ads, testing three hypotheses. The first ad was centered around the tax-deductible nature of the gift, with a generic image. The second ad featured a story of a boy who suffered horrible burns and was treated through the generosity of Compassion donors. The third ad highlighted urgency, with only a few days left before the end of the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The story-focused ad around the young boy produced a big lift in clicks, even though it contained a direct ask. However, the overall number of donors was quite low—and the story-focused ad didn’t produce a proportionate amount more donors, despite the big increase in traffic. This tells us that people who clicked this—even though they clicked a link that said “make your donation now”—weren’t ready to give.
There are two alternatives to this:
Increase motivation by increasing the force of the value proposition in the ad copy. This gives you the chance to get more motivated traffic.
Increase conversion by optimizing the landing page. If people are clicking but not converting, then maybe the landing page isn’t doing its job. Better copy and a stronger value proposition here (or less friction) could increase conversions from your traffic.
Most likely, a combination of both is needed. But due to the short time to test in year-end, it’s not immediately testable.

When launching a Facebook ad campaign for an upcoming webinar, we wondered if the traditional “Learn More” headline was strong enough of a headline to prompt someone to click and register. We thought it was possible that packing more value proposition into the headline could create more clarity up front, and result in greater conversion rates.
We created a treatment using “Optimize Your Donation Pages with These 21 Elements” as the new headline, and ran the test. The results below are from the ad set targeting the NextAfter.com Traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 72% increase in clickthroughs to the landing page, and was approaching validity on a 70% increase in conversions.
This shows us that focusing the ad headline on the value proposition with this audience helps create additional clarity about the offer, and increases the liklihood that someone will register.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of calls-to-action affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will limiting the number of final calls-to-action increase the overall visitor engagement on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2015 - 10/07/2015
By reducing the number of final calls to action to just one action, we were able to increase the number of visitors exploring the ADF website by 119.5%. What is interesting about this lift is that the final call-to-action was to explore a different website created by Focus on the Family, not to explore the ADF website. We also observed a 28% lift to those clicking on that final call-to-action but the biggest lift came from those clicking on the links within the article itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Alliance Defending Freedom creates a daily blog post that they use to promote discussion and knowledge of America’s religious freedoms. The majority of these posts end with a call-to-action or multiple calls-to-action that link to areas of their website or to various other organizations that promote the values ADF strives to protect.  Since the goal of each post is to drive visitor engagement, we wanted to find a way to increase the number of visitors that participate in the call-to-action.
We had the hypothesis that, by limiting the number of final calls-to-action to just one choice, we may be able to increase the engagement of the visitors. We took the blog post that was scheduled for 10/6/2015 and created a new treatment. The new treatment contained the same number of links throughout the page but we moved two of the bullet points from the final “Take Action” section and placed them inline with the other copy.

experiments.title: How “personalized” ad copy affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalized ad copy increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2015 - 10/06/2015
The Texas State Historical Association launched a free eBook offer about Texas’ involvement in the Civil War. They wanted to test whether personalizing ad copy would increase clickthrough rate, so they made two variations of the same ad, with a slight but significant word change. The first set gave viewers the offer to get “the” free eBook, while the second personalized the offer with get “your” free eBook. We hypothesized that personalizing the offer would increase clickthrough rate and result in more email addresses acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment ad copy decreased conversion by nearly 25% — which showed us that viewers of the ads didn’t respond nearly as well to a personalized offer. This indicated that viewers of these ads didn’t feel that the book was already “theirs”, and that creating distance between the user and the offer enticed more of them to claim it. There are plenty of tests that show the opposite result, which means that it is always beneficial to test against “best practices”.

Our original control prompted registrants to take our free FCORM assessment, but we hypothesized that this might not be the right next step for most people.
For the treatment, we created a page that presented multiple eBook offers that addressed a variety of topics. Our primary goal with this experiment was simply to get people to click through to explore more of our content.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our treatment saw a 156.7% increase in clicks, confirming our initial hypothesis that the FCORM offer was not the right next step. Our general audience is more motivated to learn practical insights that inform their marketing and fundraising.
Additionally, Google Analytics data tells us that those who clicked through the FCORM offer abandoned the site within 1 or 2 steps. Of those who clicked through the treatment page, 51% went on to download at least one other offer, and 41% viewed at least 5 more pages before exiting the site.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects click rate in a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will drive the most clicks from a Facebook Ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2016 - 08/12/2016
We released a research study on nonprofit value proposition and advertised it through Facebook Ads to people affiliated with NextAfter including emails on file and website visitors.
There are many different aspects of the value proposition that we could focus on for the promotion of the study, so we wanted to test 3 primary value propositions to see what resonated the most with our audience.
The control focused on the depth of the study. Treatment 1 focused on the 4 key components of a value proposition. And treatment 2 focused on the end result of an effective value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the audience that has already engaged with us in some way, it was 254% more effective to focus on the results of more names, donors, and dollars. This will inform the way the we promote and communicate this study to our website traffic and engaged contacts.
This also points to an underlying motivation of our engaged contacts: these people are looking for results in their fundraising.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects the click rate on a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook Ad treatment will generate more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2016 - 08/10/2016
At the launch of our value proposition research study, Why Should I Give to You?, we ran Facebook across our typical Facebook audiences. We wanted to determine what components of the value proposition resonated most with different audience groups early on.
The control focused on the depth of the study, treatment 1 focused on the 4 key components of a value proposition, and treatment 2 focused on the result of an effective value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that with our NPO Professionals target audience, the control that focused on the depth of the study resonated the most and drove the most clicks. Future tests will look at different variations of smaller variables within the control ad to perpetually optimize the click rate.

experiments.title: How different audience motivations affect click rate in a Facebook ad (Part 3)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad variation will the housefile audience respond to more?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2016 - 08/04/2016
While setting up the advertising and promotions for an upcoming live broadcast, we wanted to determine if our various audience groups for our Facebook Ads would respond differently to various ad treatments. We have 3 main audience groups that we ran this test with: our housefile, housefile lookalikes, and nonprofit professionals.
Our original live broadcast Facebook Ad focused on the speaker and the title, but we thought people may be more motivated by different aspects of the focus topic. For the treatments, we developed an ad that focused on “Growing Your Donor File” and one that focused on “Empathy.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Treatment 1 had a lift of 146.9% compared to the control. This confirms our hypothesis that this audience responds stronger to the learning and results rather than the speaker. Although, it is possible that the results could be different if the speaker was someone they were more familiar with.
Comparing this result to the result of our previous test on the housefile lookalike audience reveals a drastic difference in the motivations of two audiences that are supposedly similar. The lookalikes were 110% more likely to click on the empathy treatment, while the housefile was 26% less likely to click on the empathy treatment.

NextAfter has a research blog we run Facebook advertising on to reach new audiences with our nonprofit fundraising optimization content. We wondered if simply altering the call to action on one of these ads would affect the click rate.
The control call to action was simply the name of the blog, while the treatment turned the call to action into an actual action saying “Learn How to…”
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that altering the call to action to be focused on ‘learning’ creates a 74% drop in click rate. It’s possible that the call to action of “Learn How to Humanize Your Email Fundraising” did not provide enough clarity about the nature of the content. In contrast, the control of “5 Ways to Humanize Your Email Appeals” provided a clear description of the type of content, how long it was, and exactly what aspect of fundraising it applied to.

With this test on the housefile lookalikes audience, we found that the “Empathy” treatment outperformed the control and treatment 1, lifting click rate by 110%. This further confirms our learning from our test with the nonprofit professionals audience that it is more effective to focus our ads on the topic and results that will come from the live broadcast rather than the speaker.
This also shows us that even similar audience groups can have varying motivations: the “Empathy” treatment was the winner with this audience, but the “Donor File” treatment was the winner with the nonprofit professionals audience.

experiments.title: How personal tone and copy affects clickthrough rate for an email fundraising appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing marketing elements from the email and increasing the personal tone of the copy increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2016 - 05/20/2016
Americans for Prosperity had scheduled a send for an appeal offering membership to their exclusive Torchbearer club. When we read the email copy, we saw that it was centered around AFP’s results, which felt disconnected from the weight of the ask — a monthly recurring donation. We hypothesized that sending an email that looked like it came from a person, rather than an organization, would increase clickthrough rate and response.
We hypothesized that a monthly recurring ask requires a more personal tone, and should be fashioned as an invitation, rather than an ask. This treatment went beyond just copy approach too — we removed any elements that seemed like “marketing”. The control version of the email had several graphical elements that we thought might instantly clue the reader in to the intent of the email, before they were able to consume the value proposition in the copy. The email started with an AFP Torchbearer logo. There was also a large green “donate” button at the top of the email, and a large branded “donate” button at the end.
The treatment removed these graphical elements and took a radically different copy approach. We started by addressing the reader with a personal greeting. We gave context for the communication, and put the organizational results into context as well — like a person might write them. Then, we extended an invitation to Torchbearers — not an ask. If we were presenting Torchbearers membership as something worthy of honor, then we wanted to present the invitation in a manner that reflected that weight. We also cut and pasted a link directly into the email — just like a person would do. Finally, we offered to answer questions, which meant that the reader could reply. This action further reflected the personal tone, and gave the reader the feeling that they had a personal contact on the inside of the organization.
We then tested the two emails in equal segments to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The email with the personal tone produced a 91.3% increase in clickthrough rate, which led to a directional (though not statistically valid) increase in conversions for the Torchbearer program.
This is another data point in our research that underlies the principle of the Fundraiser’s Creed: people give to people, not organizations or marketing machines.

But we wanted to see if the send time had a direct affect on click through rate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a valid result of a 38.8% relative increase in click through rate, we discovered that the people on our house file are more ready to engage with a live broadcast offer in the mornings rather than the evenings.

Increasing personalization reduced clickthrough rate by 17.2% — which tells us that the additional language made the value proposition of the tribute widget less clear. Clarity trumps persuasion, which is proven yet again.
experiments.keyLearnings: CaringBridge wanted to run a test on their tribute widget to see if increased donor-centric language would improve clickthrough rate, which leads to a donation opportunity. They designed a treatment that addressed the donor (rather than making a “demand”) and told them what their donation would do — making it relevant to the page they were on. This was a two-layer test, as they were willing to sacrifice clickthrough rate if it led to more donations and revenue overall.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2015 - 07/09/2015
The Stanford Graduate School of Business was promoting a new eBook to build its email list. They had found success advertising on Facebook with ads that featured quotes from entrepreneurs included in the eBook. However, they wanted to test a more direct-response style ad that featured the book and tested the power of the word “free”. The new ads were run alongside each other to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The ad featuring “free” delivered a 146.1% higher clickthrough rate than the ad that simply featured the book. This ad was rolled out across the board — and not only did it increase clickthrough rate, it also increased conversion, lowering cost-per-name by more than 58%. “Free” can be a powerful incentive when paired with the right offer.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2015 - 10/22/2015
On the Texas State Historical Association’s website, they had a navigation option dedicated to member acquisition. The link was found toward the end of the navigation and directed traffic to a page with more information on the benefits of membership.
We had a hypothesis that this one button and subsequent page did not sufficiently address the needs of the various segments. In this situation, we had both potential members and existing members visiting the website. We decided to test out a new navigation with two options that would address their distinct needs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By splitting the Membership navigation into two options, we were able to directly address the two distinct segments (existing and potential members).  This change led to a 15.6% increase in the number of visitors to the giving pages.
There was also a secondary benefit of the two navigation options. We were able to create two distinct pages that were better able to address the needs of the two different segments with targeted value propositions and calls to action.

In a series of Facebook ads, the Texas State Historical Association (TSHA) was promoting a free eBook version of their popular Texas Almanac. In exchange for their email address, the visitors would be able to download the 756 page book.  Our goal for this test was to drive as much traffic to the site as possible to take advantage of this free offer.
In our ads, we wanted to emphasize the how this was the first time TSHA was offering the Alamanc for free and that they were the only organization that could do so. As a result, we wanted to see which of these attributes would drive the most interest.
experiments.keyLearnings: Exclusivity proved to be the more motivating element of the offer given the 134% lift it provided to the click through rate of the ads. This tone will be used in future ads and will be incorporated throughout the landing page.

experiments.title: How “personalized” ad copy affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalized ad copy increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2015 - 10/06/2015
The Texas State Historical Association launched a free eBook offer about Texas’ involvement in the Civil War. They wanted to test whether personalizing ad copy would increase clickthrough rate, so they made two variations of the same ad, with a slight but significant word change. The first set gave viewers the offer to get “the” free eBook, while the second personalized the offer with get “your” free eBook. We hypothesized that personalizing the offer would increase clickthrough rate and result in more email addresses acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment ad copy decreased conversion by nearly 25% — which showed us that viewers of the ads didn’t respond nearly as well to a personalized offer. This indicated that viewers of these ads didn’t feel that the book was already “theirs”, and that creating distance between the user and the offer enticed more of them to claim it. There are plenty of tests that show the opposite result, which means that it is always beneficial to test against “best practices”.

As part of the monthly cultivation for their email file, the Texas State Historical Association was giving away one of their new eBooks. We had previously utilized long-form emails to describe the eBooks in-depth in order to better explain why the reader would want the book. We had the hypothesis that by emphasizing the visual of the book and keeping the email relatively vague, we would be able to drive more traffic and eBook downloads.
experiments.keyLearnings: The email that emphasized the visual of the eBook and reduced the copy in the email ended up lifting the click through rate by 12.4%. This proved that the visual of the eBook was enough to sell the offer and that the increased copy added more friction than value to the process.

We wanted to find out what factors of the book were most appealing to the audience so we ran an experiment on the imagery used in the ad. Since the author of the free book is well known, we tested to see if his image would drive more interest than highlighting the book itself.
experiments.keyLearnings: The popularity of the author resulted in a 52.2% increase in the number of visitors to the page. In this situation, the book did not have the same value and appeal to the viewers as the author himself.

Hillsdale College was in the middle of a fundraising campaign and had an email that quoted Ronald Reagan. They had heard over and over again that using images in email was a “best practice”, and in this case, might offer extra credibility that would strengthen the incentive to click through. So they devised two emails — one that used text to convey the quote and one that used a more graphical treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that what is commonly accepted as "best practice" might not, in fact, be the best email to send. Hillsdale was able to attract many more people to the landing page by keeping the "personal" nature of the email consistent.

experiments.title: How a personalized email appeal affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a personal tone email increase click-through-rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2016 - 04/04/2016
In the beginning days of the SBA List’s  moneybomb, we wanted to test the tone and approach to messaging so that we could then implement the learnings to the remainder of the campaign.
SBA List’s regular appeals are more rhetoric in nature. Using this as the control, a treatment was made that took a more personal approach. While keeping the value proposition of the email intact, we completely rewrote it with a conversational tone. We also stripped all the elements of branding out of the email and removed the donate button at the bottom of the email to make it seem like it came from a person, rather than email marketing platform. Finally, we inserted personalization throughout the appeal and removed the President’s picture that is normally in their appeal emails.
Then, it was split-tested with the email file, dividing it in half to make sure we could validate a result.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new email produced a 313.2% increase in click-through-rate. This provided insightful learnings about the SBA List audience. They want to feel like they are being personally engaged and valued enough that they would get a personal email from the president. They want to feel like the appeal is only written to them. That they are personally being asked in a 1-1 relationship to support the organization.
This is difficult to do in direct mail, but email provides many great ways to personalize communications. Moving forward we will find additional ways to be even more “high-touch” with the audience, including on the donation form.

experiments.title: How the intensity of the call-to-action affects email clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: What kind of CTA will get more non-donors to click?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2016 - 04/10/2016
In efforts to learn more about what motivates non-donors to click-through in an email, SBA List did a test of the CTA in one of their email appeals during a money bomb. The soft ask made no mention of giving a gift and the hard ask had a direct ask for a gift. Would a soft CTA get a higher click-through-rate than a hard CTA with non-donors since it didn’t directly ask them for a giftt? They did an A/B split of the non-donor segment of their list.
experiments.keyLearnings: The hard ask had a 150.5% increase in click through from the soft ask. Non-donors were more motivated to click-through when told directly what to do. Clarity of the hard CTA trumped persuasion in the soft CTA. While the test was not validated on this, the hard ask also received a 24% increase in revenue. These learnings will be used moving forward when sending appeals to non-donors.

experiments.title: How urgency affects clickthrough rate in website popups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity of the call-to-action increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2016 - 05/16/2016
Run with Water was raising entry fees before their race event. They used a ‘welcome mat’ pop-up to let visitors know and encourage them to register for the race. However, they hypothesized that time-sensitive language that highlighted the user’s benefit might increase urgency and increase race registrations instead of the cause-based call-to-action. So they created a treatment that highlighted the deadline and presented the benefit to the user, rather than cause-based copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with urgency-based language delivered a 24.8% increase in clickthrough rate. This shows that while cause-based copy might inspire some people (like existing donors or constituents familiar with the cause), clarity trumps persuasion. While we must continually test, research shows that plainly stating the benefits can often increase conversion in place of inspirational copy.

experiments.title: How the kind of ask in an email affects click through and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call-to-action would get more people to click-through to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2016 - 09/26/2016
Jews for Jesus had a high-urgency campaign during the month of September. They sent a series of seven different emails during a three week period. About halfway through the email series they wanted to see if they could get more people to click-through to the landing page and ultimately give more donations; so they set up a test for their email that tested the call-to-action. They tested a hard ask vs. a soft ask. All other copy, design, and layout remained the same. The only difference between the two emails was the CTA.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The soft ask had an increase in clicks by 49.8%. With out directly asking people for a gift, more people were motivated to click through to see how they could help.  While the soft ask was able to get more people to the landing page, these people didn’t convert to donors as well as people coming to the page from the hard ask. The people clicking through from the hard ask email were already highly motivated to give. We were not able to validate off of donations, but initial results were showing a 31% increase in donations for the hard ask.
What we learned is that by having a soft ask in the email, we were able to increase the quantity of people to the landing page, but not the quality.

experiments.title: How increasing relevancy in the subject line affected clickthrough rate

Timeframe: 11/05/2015 - 11/05/2015
Jews for Jesus was sending a monthly appeal to their house file. They had previous success with personal-style emails from the Executive Director, and wanted to test whether topical subject lines would achieve a higher clickthrough rate than their typical “personal update” style subject lines. Typically, subject line is just measured against its one job: getting the open. But they wanted to test the effect of a treatment subject line to see if it affected clickthrough rate as well. The two emails had different subject lines, but the same body copy and CTA.
experiments.keyLearnings: Not only did the subject line test increase open rate, it also inspired more people to click through to the content. By increasing relevancy in one area, we can affect performance metrics in another. It’s important to note that the increase in clickthrough rate was not just a factor of increased open rate. The open rate increase was only 21% — but 88% more people clicked through. This proves that a subject line that increases motivation right from the start can increase every email performance metric.

experiments.title: How personal email copy affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a personal tone with personalized copy increase clickthrough rate?

Jews for Jesus had a process in place to convert direct mail letters to eAppeals, which they sent on a monthly basis to their email file. While this raised money and kept digital donors engaged, they hypothesized that the tone of a direct mail piece wasn’t really aligning with the medium of email.
So while keeping the value proposition of the email intact, they completely rewrote it with a conversational tone. They also stripped all the elements of branding out of the email to make it seem like it came from a person, rather than email marketing software. Finally, they inserted personalization throughout the appeal.
Then, they split-tested this with their email file, dividing it in half to make sure they could validate a result.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new email produced a 109.2% increase in clickthrough rate. This provided insightful learnings about the Jews for Jesus digital donor:
They respond when they feel personally engaged.
They respond to emails that feel like they come from people, rather than email marketing machines.
This is difficult to do in direct mail, but the email provides many great ways to personalize communications. This points a way forward for all emails to be “high-touch”.

The majority of CaringBridge’s online revenue comes from an area on each journal page that is internally called the “tribute widget”. The CaringBridge team wanted to see if they could clarify and strengthen the value proposition presented in this widget. The control had a “command” to the visitor, and made a lot of assumptions about their knowledge of where the donation went and how to use the widget itself. So they developed a treatment that suggested an action to the visitor, clarified the impact of their gift, and offered a helpful note about how to begin the process.

experiments.title: How a video screenshot in a cultivation email impacts click-through.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email would get more people click through to watch the video?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2017 - 02/06/2017
Live Action produces undercover investigative videos they send out to their email file to encourage constituents to watch the video and then share it with their friends and family. They normally have a screenshot of the video in the email copy. In an earlier test, they learned that when an image isn’t included in appeals emails, they get an increase in clicks and engagement. They hypothesized that this approach might work in the same way for their cultivation emails. They developed a treatment for investigative video email that did not include a screenshot in the email copy and tested it against the control that had the image in it.
experiments.keyLearnings: What they found is that the treatment with out the image in the email actually had a 10.7% decrease in clicks from the control. In cultivation emails, where the primary call-to-action is to watch a video, the use of an image in the email copy motivates more people to click. This is opposite of how people respond to appeal emails when an image is used. This can be attributed to the fact that the image in a cultivation email reinforces the CTA where in an appeal email, the use of an image becomes a point of friction to the giving process.

experiments.title: How the email presentation impacted click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified version of the email increase the number of subscribers getting to the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2016 - 12/09/2016
As part of a monthly appeal, Live Action wanted to test out the standard email design that had been in use for the last several months. This email template included the standard Live Action branding and an image of the Live Action president Lila Rose. The hypothesis was that if we could make the email feel more genuine, we could engage a greater proportion of the audience in the appeal.
To test this hypothesis, we stripped out the majority of the design elements and also softened the copy so that it did not have as many of the popular direct mail tactics such as bolding significant portions of text and including an ask array in the email (i.e. “would you consider making a $25, $50, or $100 gift.”).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The simplified design increased the subscriber click through rate by 208%. This increased traffic resulted in a 50% lift to the number of donations made to to the treatment.
This experiment demonstrated the importance of facilitating a one-to-one relationship in mass-marketing emails. It is our belief that people give to people and when they feel marketed to, they are not as likely to engage with an organization. By making this email look more genuine, more people engaged in the message. As we plan for future iterations of testing, this is a concept that we want to continue to explore with further testing.

Live Action sends out a weekly email to their housefile with the top pro-life news stories from Live Action News. The current email format is highly branded, comes from Lauren Merz, and includes mainly images with links to stories.
They have had success in the past with emails coming from Lila Rose, the founder. They wanted to test whether a more personal, stripped-down approach to the newsletter would increase click rate in the emails.
experiments.keyLearnings: The stripped-down approach from Lila increased open rates by nearly 70%, however, removing the imagery decreased click rate by 18%. People are drawn to anything with Lila’s name on it, and responded well to receiving these stories from Lila, but clickthrough rate declined without the added pull of an image to tie to each story.

experiments.title: How removing an image from an email impacts clicks-through
experiments.researchQuestion: Would removing the picture from the email motivate more people to click-through to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2017 - 12/23/2016
During Live Action’s year-end campaign, they sent a series of emails asking people to support their mission and give a gift to their organization. One of the emails in the campaign was a story about a baby boy named Jaxon whose parents decided against abortion when they found out he had a disease. To reinforce the message and bring a more personal connection to the story they included a picture of baby Jaxon in the control email. We hypothesized that by removing the picture, we would be able to make the email feel more genuine and believable that a person, and not an organization, had sent this email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The email version without the picture of the baby had an increase in clicks by 54%. By removing the image in the email, we were able to make the email look and feel more authentic – as if the sender personally sent the message.
This reinforces our learnings from a previous test that people are more highly motivated to engage with us when they feel like they aren’t being mass-marketed to, but rather communicated with from a one-to-one basis.

experiments.title: How copy length in an email affects the force of the value proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: How does reducing the length of email copy affect the force of the value proposition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2016 - 12/19/2016
Live Action sent many email appeals as part of their year-end fundraising campaign. One email in particular had a complex value proposition that took a significant amount of copy to fully explain. Since the holidays are a busy time for many people, they wondered if people would take the time to read the entire email. However, they knew that shortening the copy of the email risked removing crucial parts of the value proposition—and that would significantly affect donor conversion.
They created a treatment that removed sections of the copy, and re-edited the copy to make sure it presented a unified value proposition. However, this presented one more caveat: removing an early section of copy removed a link from the email, so the treatment had one less “clickable” opportunity.
They created two test segments and sent a test to each to determine which version attracted more clicks (the intial metric) and donors (the ultimate metric).
experiments.keyLearnings: The email with the shorter copy, despite having one fewer link, produced a 24.7% increase in clickthrough rate. That showed, at least initially, that the value proposition could still be effectively communicated with fewer words. However, they looked deeper into the data to determine if the shorter copy produced highly-motivated clicks that turned into donors on the landing page.

experiments.title: How personal email copy impacts click-through
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a more conversational and personal email improve click-through?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2017 - 02/13/2017
Live Action has the largest online presence for the pro-life movement. They are experts in the pro-life space and present their content with professionalism. Lila Rose, their founder and president, is the voice for a lot of their communication, including their monthly email appeals. They hypothesized that because the email was coming from Lila, the tone of the emails wasn’t matching the personal communication medium that email is.
While keeping the value proposition from the control email intact, they completely rewrote a treatment email with a conversational tone and inserted personalization throughout the copy. They also stripped all the elements of branding and the picture of Lila out of the email to make it seem like it came from a person, rather than email marketing software.
They split their email file in half and tested.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email produced a 31.8% increase in click-through. What this tells us is that:
Response will increase when people feel personally engaged.
Live Action can continue to communicate with their audience in a professional way, but when it comes to email, donors want more authentic and approachable communication. People give to people. People want to feel that Lila is personally emailing them rather than an email marketing machine.

experiments.title: How clarity in the subject line impacts click-through

rows.name: Former managers: Planned Parenthood is not health care

experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line would get more people to click-through?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2017 - 02/27/2017
For the release of a new investigative video, Live Action sent an email to their house file and included a link for people to view it. In an effort to get more people to open and click through to watch the video, they tested two different subject lines. The control took a personal approach by including the recipients name in the subject line. The treatment was much more matter of fact and told people exactly what the email was about. They split their file and tested.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Their wasn’t a significant difference in open rate between the control and the treatment, but the treatment got a 20% increase in click-through. What we can learn from this is that:
Clarity trumps persuasion. While the control subject line was personalized and this can be a highly motivating tactic, it was also very unclear and may have caused some friction. The treatment told people exactly the topic of the email.
The treatment subject line helped reinforce the email value proposition and strengthened the message, resulting in more people clicking-through from the treatment.

experiments.title: How simplifying copy in an email can impact click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email would increase click-through?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2016 - 12/23/2016
During Live Action’s Calendar Year End campaign, they sent a series of emails asking for support to their year-end goal. One of their emails addressed what would happen in the first 100 days of the new administration and the kind of impact it would have on the preborn. The email appeals were traditionally lengthy, full of detail, and offered two opportunities for people to click through to the donation page – one at the top of the email and one at the bottom. They developed a treatment that simplified the copy and moved the call-to-action to the bottom of the email. They tested the two different emails against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version decreased click-through by 64.5%! What we can learn from this experiment is that people needed the additional value proposition that was present in the control to motivate them enough to click-through to the donation page. The significant more clicks on the control could result in significant more donations and revenue. While we know that simplified message can help improve conversion, we need to be mindful that we aren’t stripping the email of critical messaging and weakening the value proposition.

experiments.title: How changing the location of the argument affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more personalized email with content that describes the problem but not the solution generate more clicks than a more heavily designed email that is geared towards driving visitors to engage with the information on the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/10/2014 - 09/12/2014
As part of their September appeal, FAIR was using the results of their most recent study to demonstrate the need for greater immigration reform. Their Control email took the standard approach of demonstrating the argument, both the problem and solution) within the email while making a direct ask for support at the bottom of the email. As an alternative to this approach, we developed a new treatment that changed the look and feel of the email to look more personal and only discussed the problem within the email.
experiments.keyLearnings: When presenting both the problem and solution within the email, the visitors did not have the same motivation to visit the website.  The new treatment was able to drive 70.9% more visitors to the website in order to learn about FAIR’s solution to the problem presented within the email.
