experiments.title: How a simpler, more direct value proposition affects clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a shorter, more direct value proposition affect clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2017 - 04/10/2017
The Heritage Foundation was promoting a quiz around the “Gadsden Flag” that led to a membership offer with the flag as a premium. They had created two versions of a Facebook ad, as they had seen some research that longer, more engaging copy could increase clickthrough and conversion rate. The control, in this case, had much longer copy that talked about the history of the flag and made an offer to the reader to test their knowledge. The treatment copy was much shorter, and asked a direct question to the reader: “How much do you know about the Gadsden Flag?”
They split the traffic to determine which ad attracted more clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The shorter ad with the direct question produced a 122.1% increase in clickthrough rate, which showed that the elements of this ad increased the clarity of the value proposition. Longer copy can be more effective, but only if if clarifies the value proposition. In addition, the direct question to provoke the reader to respond by taking the intended action.
This success prompted further testing to isolate the variables of length and the direct question to understand how each of these played into the success of the ad.

experiments.keyLearnings: By using active language, Heritage's new ad attracted more interest from people who saw it. Nonprofit organizations commonly use challenge grants during fundraising campaigns -- but "issuing a challenge" from the board is a much stronger way to approach the same action.

experiments.title: How a soft ask in an email appeal can motivate more people to convert.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a soft ask help increase click-rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2016 - 12/23/2016
For National Breast Cancer Foundation’s year-end campaign they sent a series of appeals. One of their appeals was focused on providing mammograms to women in need. They have found that it is difficult to get their email file to convert through email appeals. In an effort to optimize this challenge, they hypothesized that it might be better to use a soft ask as the call-to-action rather than a direct one. The control email said, “Here’s a link where you can give.” They developed a treatment that said, “Here’s a link where you can learn more.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email increased click-rate by 100%. What this tells us is that even if though the value proposition in the email was strong, people were not ready to give once they got to the CTA. That said, they were motivated enough to learn more about how they can get involved and help.

Every audience is different. It is important to test key elements like the CTA in an email appeal. Knowing that the NBCF audience already had a low conversion rate, the soft ask was a strategic way to motivate more people to engage and get them to the donation page to potentially give.

The month of October is Breast Cancer Awareness Month. They get a significant amount of traffic to their homepage during this month. They have a pop-up on the homepage that they display in an effort to convert the site traffic into donors. They tested the value proposition of this pop-up from something tangible (provide 1,000 more women with screen services) to a more general value proposition (help women facing breast cancer).
experiments.keyLearnings: The more general value proposition increased clicks to the donation page by 14%! The general value proposition had a higher appeal. Providing help to women facing breast cancer is something that people can understand, there is no explanation needed and they want to give towards that. The more specific value proposition was a great approach, but it lacked clarity about what “screening services” were, creating friction in the mind of the end user.

experiments.keyLearnings: The softer ask increased clickthrough rate by 78%. It’s likely that the “learn more” call to action encouraged more people who weren’t initially planning to give to the donation page, while people who were already motivated to give a gift were more likely to click a link from a direct ask. This leads to a higher click-through rate, but does not necessarily translate to more gifts.

experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified email design increase the proportion of people clicking to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2017 - 03/07/2017
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate were launching a new digital prayer book to kick off the Lenten season. As part of this launch, they wanted to give their house email file an opportunity to request the resource. This would serve as both cultivation of the subscribers and a new opportunity for them to give a gift once they had requested their PDF.
Historically, the email template used in donor communications was heavily designed with an organizational wrapper. We wanted to see if sending the same email message but in a stripped-down design would have an impact on the percentage of people that clicked on the link to download the prayer book.
experiments.keyLearnings: The simplified email resulted in a 14.8% increase to the percentage of visitors going to the landing page. Our hypothesis is that the lack of design seemed to be more genuine and less “marketing” oriented. As a result, the subscribers were more willing to read and engaged with the email once they opened it.

experiments.title: How emotional language, clarity and authority affect ad clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does emotional language, clarity and authority affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 11/09/2017
Leadership Institute recently re-launched a course about the history and principles of conservatism, called Conservatism 101. As they prepared to market this course through Facebook, they developed several different treatments for the advertisements. The first treatment posed a question: “What are conservatives actually trying to conserve?”, which implied that the course would answer that question. The second treatment introduced emotional language that outlined the threat to conservatism and made a call for conservatives to rediscover their principles. This treatment also included a call to action (CTA) in the top copy: “Start today by enrolling in Conservatism 101 now.” The third treatment took a similar tack with emotional language, but sought to align with the mindset of the reader by declaring the threat to be invalid. This treatment heightened the clarity and authority around the course offer, introducing a list of featured speakers, and introduced line breaks to better isolate the CTA: “Activate your free course today and discover—or rediscover—the principles and beauty of conservatism.” The final treatment used the same copy as the third treatment, but introduced a picture of Mark Levin with the CTA “Join Mark Levin in this new free course: Conservatism 101.”
Since Facebook is able to serve multiple ads simultaneously and gather data on each, they launched this A/B/C/D test to determine which ad produced the most clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After a few days, LI had enough data to determine a winner. Both the third and fourth treatment produced a clear, statistically significant increase in clickthrough rate over the other treatments. Treatment 3 produced a 56.7% increase over the baseline due to a combination of increased clarity and authority as well as visual clarity around the call-to-action. Treatment 4 produced the largest increase of all—a 108.7% increase over the baseline due to the copy adjustments from treatment three and the new image and embedded CTA, which reinforced the authority of the top copy.
In their next experiment, Leadership Institute isolated the top two performing ads to determine which one produced the most conversions on the following landing page.

experiments.title: How authority, active language, and clarity affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does increased authority, active language, and clarity affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 11/09/2017
Leadership Institute recently re-launched a course about the history and principles of conservatism, called Conservatism 101. As they prepared to market this course through Facebook, they developed four different treatments for the advertisements. Testing proved that the third and fourth treatment produced a significant lift to clickthrough rate, but they wanted to gather more data to validate if one of these resulted in more downstream conversions—in this case, more sign-ups for the course.
The primary difference in the two treatments was the image and the copy within. The first treatment had a patriotic background that described what the course was: “Conservatism 101: An Introduction to the American Conservative Tradition”. The second, however, featured a picture of Mark Levin, a popular radio host and lecturer in the course. This image contained call-to-action copy as well: “Join Mark Levin in this new free course, Conservatism 101.”
The team isolated these two ads to determine which ad was more effective at driving downstream conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The second treatment, with Mark Levin and the call-to-action copy in the image produced a 63.7% increase in downstream conversions. This showed that the increased authority and active call to action was able to shape motivation to affect downstream actions. Ultimately, Leadership Institute didn’t just want more clicks—they wanted more clicks with a higher motivation.
This test prompted a new round of testing that used the winner as the control to continue to increase results.

experiments.title: How a subject line’s perceived impact on the subscriber influences click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will produce the greatest email engagement (opens and clicks)?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2017 - 03/17/2017
In an effort to educate their subscriber base on the public’s sentiment toward the state’s current budget crisis, the Illinois Policy Institute sent an email with the results of an independent 3rd party poll. The results of the poll aligned with the message IPI had been promoting since the current session began. It also supported the budget plan the the organization had developed and was working with state legislators to pass.
The goal of the email was to educate and engage the subscriber file so we decided to test out two subject lines that highlighted the contents of the message: “tax hikes” and “overspending.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
After sending to half of the email file, we found that “overspending” decreased the overall click through rate by 23%. It also negatively impacted the open rate by 5%. It is our hypothesis that this behavior is due to how the words are perceived by the subscriber. A “tax hike” is something that would impact them directly while “overspending” is something that they may not be in favor of but does not appear have any direct impact on them.
This serves as an important reminder that all aspects of the messaging should focus on how the topic will impact the recipient (when possible). We need to address not only why a message is important but why it is important to the reader.

As part of their monthly appeal strategy, Hillsdale College ran an experiment to a segment of their house email list to determine the best messaging approach for the appeal.  A clear winner was identified and the resulting email was scheduled to be sent out to the remainder of the email list with a subject line A/B split test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The "Message of hope" ended up with 10.1% less opens and 35.5% less clicks. The specificity of the control subject line, "Hillsdale's plan to reverse damage," proved to be more effective in inspiring recipients to open the email and then click through to the landing page.   It is important to remember that specificity yields clarity, and that clarity trumps persuasion.

experiments.title: How Facebook ad imagery impacts clickthrough rate

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2017 - 12/15/2017
Illinois Policy Institute recently discovered that showing a cartoon image in a Facebook ad decreased clickthrough rate. They decided to create two new treatments based to test against the control. The first treatment included an image of Michael Madigan–the state legislator pushing for an increase in taxes. The second treatment included an image of a house, to align with the subject of property taxes. They wondered: Which Facebook ad approach will have the highest clickthrough rate? They launched a 3-part test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Illinois Policy Institute found that the first treatment using Madigan’s image increased clickthrough rate by 70%, while the second treatment using the house image actually decreased ad clickthrough rate by 23%. This experiment is a good example of how an active A/B testing approach can lead to incremental performance when done consistently. Despite experiencing a drop in the second treatment, the learning in the first treatment outweighed the impact and will now be implemented as the winner.
It’s apparent that the use of Madigan’s image increased audience motivation. Many Illinoisans are familiar with this particular legislator. When connected with the topic of a potential property tax hike and their ability to take action to stop it, response rate went up significantly.

experiments.title: How text on a call-to-action button affects engagement and clickthrough rate.

rows.name: Show me how my friends score

experiments.researchQuestion: Does clarifying the intent of the social share button by indicating the outcome increase social sharing (and in doing so, increase name acquisition)?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2014 - 11/11/2014
Heritage Action For America launched a name acquisition campaign through an online mini-game in 2012 called “How Conservative Are You?”. The game let people answer 10 questions about legislation, then compared them to their Congressman. The game also had a social element too — if shared on Facebook, the user’s friends could take the quiz and then compare scores. Heritage Action wanted to promote social sharing because of the multiplier effect it had on name acquisition.
We launched a test on the button that asked the user to share. The control asked them a question — “How conservative are your friends?” While that might pique the interest of the user, it’s unclear what the button does. We launched a treatment that teased the outcome of the social share — “Show me how my friends score”. We also clarified copy around the button to indicate what was happening.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment delivered a 214.7% lift in social sharing — which meant that not only did more people share it, more of their friends saw it. This brought a tremendous lift in traffic to the site, and more names were acquired as a result. It served as a great learning and reminder that at the process level of value proposition, using “outcome” language to earn the click can increase overall CTR.

experiments.title: How stripping out branding in an offer email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing branding from a marketing email increase response rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2017 - 09/19/2017
Heartlight Ministries was promoting their new free online course to their housefile. They had developed a heavily branded email template that, despite being signed by Mark Gregston (their founder), felt impersonal. They hypothesized that they could get a better response–measured in clickthrough rate–by stripping out much of the branding and sending an email that felt more personal in nature. They developed a plain-text treatment and split the active email file to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The unbranded email produced a 19.7% increase in clickthrough rate. There are multiple factors that contributed to this lift:
The unbranded email significantly reduced the vertical space required to begin to read the content, which could have led to more people reading to the call-to-action.The unbranded email had more contrast between the text and the background, which made it easier to read.The design of the email better reflected the context of the email, which was a genuine free course offer that felt personal in nature and needed a "person" design to reflect that.
This experiment shows that Heartlight might want to do further testing on email to validate that the stripped-down template continues to produce better results.

experiments.title: How the template of an email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the template of an email affect click rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2017 - 09/18/2017
Heartlight Ministries was offering a new free online course to their housefile, and they wanted to test whether using a plain-text email over their general email template would produce more clicks. All copy remained the same in both the templated email and the plain text email.
experiments.keyLearnings: The plain text email increased click rate by 14.6%. This could be due to the more personal feeling of Mark actually talking to that person and offering them something, like you would to a friend, versus the Heartlight “brand” offering them the free course. This approach could also increase the housefile’s trust in Mark as the leader of the organization.

experiments.title: How inline acquisition offers in place of ads affected clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Will replacing the mid-roll advertising banner with an inline acquisition increase clicks for this placement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2017 - 11/09/2017
Focus on the Family currently uses DoubleClick for Publishers to manage ad fill throughout their website.  Most of the ads in rotation are for their own resources, giving opportunities, and some third-party offers. One such placement is a mid-roll banner targeting long-form article pages.  They hypothesized that an inline email acquisition offer that is contextually relevant for their audience may increase clickthrough and help capture more of the high volume of new visitors viewing these articles.
They already had a free online Marriage Assessment which has resonated well with visitors to their marriage and parenting content, so they split the traffic to articles in those categories and delivered an inline offer for the assessment in place of the mid-roll ad from DoubleClick to half the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The inline treatment increased the number of offer clicks by over 1,870%! This learning shows how the alignment of an offer, as well as its treatment, can be put to work to acquire more names. We hypothesize that a large portion of this impact is due to “banner blindness”—a web usability phenomenon where visitors consciously or subconsciously ignore banner-like information.  Banners also limit the amount of value proposition you can effectively communicate, which can decrease perceived value.
With high traffic and known content themes, we plan to test further into this placement—including moving it to the end of the content where we hypothesize we may be able to capitalize on the natural pivot/decision point created by completing the article.  Other potential tests include the headline, button, and value proposition copy as well as the inclusion of supporting graphical elements.

experiments.title: How adding a call-to-action to the top of a simplified landing page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding the button back in to the top of the page increase acquisition on the simplified page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2017 - 11/30/2017
Focus on the Family offers a Marriage Assessment on their website. In a previous test on the assessment landing page, we learned that a simplified page where we removed elements of friction actually decreased conversion because we removed a button at the top of the page that would take this highly motivated audience directly to the assessment. To build upon those learnings, we hypothesized that we could increase conversion by keeping and testing the simplified version again but simply just adding back in the button to the assessment at the top of the page. We split the traffic and tested it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By adding the button back in to the top of the page, we still weren’t able to increase the traffic going to the assessment. We saw a slight decrease in conversion – not as significant as when the button was completely removed – but still a dip.
We hypothesize that this audience is so highly motivated that they don’t actually need this assessment landing page. We propose testing removing this in-between page altogether and sending people straight to the assessment to get more people to take and complete it.

experiments.title: How matching gift copy affects clickthrough rate on a modal popup
experiments.researchQuestion: How does matching gift language affect clickthrough rate on a modal popup window?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2018 - 12/28/2017
FamilyLife was in the final week of their year-end campaign, and launched a modal popup to point web traffic to the donation page. The popup contained a countdown clock as well as urgency-focused message. The default version of the popup had language that echoed the headline, but they wondered if including matching gift language would increase clickthrough rate.
They created a treatment that kept urgent language, but added in the fact that the gift would be matched dollar-for-dollar. They launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (with matching gift language) produced a 56% increase in clickthrough rate. This test revealed that while urgency is key to any message with a deadline (especially at the end of the year), incentive can provide additional lift for action oriented copy.
In the control, the potential donor is presented with four elements to inspire them to click through and give: an urgent headline, a countdown clock, additional copy above the button, and the button copy. But in the treatment, the matching gift language provides a fifth element, without leaving out any of the previous four—incentive.
Many times, calls-to-action have one of these elements, but not the other. This test shows the power of combining the two.

experiments.summary:

Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their Christmas devotional series as a free offer on Facebook. Previous testing had shown that using copy in the image of the ad increased clickthrough rate and conversion rate. But as they reviewed their ad, they wondered if there was actually too much copy in the ad, and reading it bogged the reader down and kept them from clicking.
They made a treatment that removed the subheadline from their ad image, leaving only a single call-to-action with no supporting copy in order to simplify the value proposition.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The ad with reduced copy produced a 19.8% increase in clickthrough rate, which indicated that less copy simplified the value proposition enough to attract more interest from people who saw it.

experiments.title: How images in an email affect clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will additional imagery increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2016 - 05/04/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their newest online course on Genesis to their house file. Many of the people in their house file had already taken one of their first two courses.
Previous testing had shown that their prospects responded well to very personal invitations. However, they wanted to try a more graphical approach that presented the new course in a more dramatic way and gave the reader more to click on.
They created a treatment that inserted an image from the course into the copy (linked to the registration page) and put an image of Dr. Mark Bailey, the sender, next to his signature.
They split the file and sent an A/B email test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that when we are introducing a new course to our donors, we must be somewhat visual about it. Though previous testing had shown the power of a personal invitation, imagery can help attract attention and win more clicks and registrations.

experiments.title: How elaborating on specific organizational initiatives affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an email that elaborates on specific organizational initiatives increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2017 - 12/13/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary recently began their calendar year end campaign, focused three pillars: The Call to Teach, The Will to Engage, and The Heart to Go. As they prepared to send an appeal on email focused on the second pillar–The Will to Engage– they wondered if an email that elaborated on the strategic initiatives they’ve created to engage culture would increase the clickthrough rate to the donation page. They created a treatment version and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
They found that the treatment version did increase clickthrough rate by 276%. It’s clear the treatment version significantly increased recipient motivation, but why? There a few important elements worth analyzing in the second email. First, the introduction of the treatment version takes a more personal approach to the topic of cultural engagement. It highlights Dr. Bailey’s daily schedule of leaving his office and driving through the city of Dallas. This simple change immediately draws the reader into Dr. Bailey’s point of view and shows why this initiative is personal to him. Humans are storytellers. We love to have a concrete image to hold in our mind’s eye.
Second, the treatment version elaborates on three specific ways Dallas Theological Seminary is addressing culture and empowering individuals outside of the seminary walls. By describing at length these three initiatives–The Table Podcast, the Faith and Work Initiative, and a plethora of free online courses–DTS is reminding their audience where their financial support goes. It’s also important to note the subtle difference in the call-to-action. Instead of clicking to ‘make a secure donation’ this treatment email invites individuals to ‘invest in these initiatives.’ This is an approach that Dallas Theological Seminary will continue to work into the rest of their calendar year end campaign.

experiments.title: How adding specificity and urgency affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing specificity and urgency in the call-to-action increase clickthrough rate and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/22/2017 - 10/24/2017
Compassion International served Facebook ads to people who had browsed their site that showed them dynamically generated ads based on the children they had viewed. While results were positive for the campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase clickthrough rate and conversion rate by adding specificity to the ads.
They created a treatment that was identical to the first ad with one small change—in the call-to-action, instead of “Sponsor a child today”, they changed the copy to “Sponsor this child right now” to increase specificity and urgency.
They launched an experiment to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 233.4% lift in clickthrough rate, but resulted in fewer downstream conversions (though this data was not valid). This suggests that while the ad might increase user motivation to click, something on the following landing page is not aligning with that motivation and is causing cognitive friction in the mind of the prospective sponsor. This shows that the experiment needs more data and possibly downstream conversion testing to discover what is causing the depression in conversion.

experiments.title: How personalization affects clickthrough rate in a child sponsorship Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: How will personalizing the ad with the child’s name affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2017 - 11/09/2017
Compassion International experienced a large increase in clickthrough rate when they added increased urgency to the calls-to-action in their Facebook advertisements. They wanted to see if they could further increase results by including the name of the child in the call-to-action. Although these ads were dynamically generated, they were able to match the name with the photo of the child in this area of the advertisement.
Their treatment replaced “this child” with the actual name of the child. They ran both ads simultaneously to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The increased personalization actually reduced clickthrough rate by 40.2%. There are several reasons why this might have happened:
Since these were not remarketing ads, the end user had no previous relationship with the child and was less incentivized to click. Thus, the ads were “too specific”.
The removal of the word “child” from the CTA removed a primary motivating factor.
The CTA lost congruency with the copy above when the copy shifted from talking about “a child” to a very specific name.
As it currently stands, Compassion International is not able to accurately track conversions directly back to the ad. This concept might be re-tested with a landing page that features the child who was selected. Although clickthrough rate might be lower, in that case, conversion rate might be higher.

experiments.title: How clarity affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does donor-centric language and a more personal connection increase the number of clicks compared to more succinct copy?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2014 - 11/25/2014
CaringBridge had a donation ask on the homepage that registered users saw when they were logged in. We wanted to increase clickthroughs on this ask to generate more donations. We created a treatment that was highly donor-centric — it gave them a virtual “pat on the back” and reminded them of all the ways that they help those they care about.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment delivered 51.7% fewer clicks than the control. This says a lot about not only the audience viewing this, but the context in which the copy appears. It tells us that this audience values clarity over persuasion — a quick, short ask trumps a longer, more personal ask that attempts to draw in their emotions.
It also suggests that this page is highly functional – since it is a “dashboard” of sorts, people use it to get from one place to another, which suggests that future copy should be concise and to the point.

We believed that increasing personalization by including mentions of the patient’s name along with a more emotional ask would cause more people to click the link and begin the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that clarity trumps personalized persuasion in this specific banner. The new banner included more copy and a much more indirect ask. This tells us that visitors on the page were already motivated to give – we didn’t need to keep selling. We just needed to ask, clearly.

experiments.title: How streamlining the tribute widget affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a streamlined widget with added social proof inspire more people to begin the donation process?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2015 - 04/24/2015
Most gifts to CaringBridge start with the “tribute widget”, a small box that appears on all Site Journal pages and profile pages. We noticed one day that the tribute widget had a lot of information that could be extraneous and distracting from the intended action, which was to begin the gift process.
The CaringBridge team developed a streamlined version of this widget that removed a “most recent quote”, along with avatars for signed in users. To increase clarity, the team put pre-filled text into the box to instruct the user what to write. Also, we added social proof into the widget, dynamically pulling the number of previous donors to inspire the user to join them.
experiments.keyLearnings: The streamlined widget with added social proof resulted in nearly 52% more people beginning the donation process. We closely monitored donation page conversion rates as well, and there was no change. Clarifying the value proposition by removing extra graphics and copy and reducing anxiety through social proof resulted in a significant lift in dollars raised.

CaringBridge received a matching gift for their spring fundraising campaign from a board member. But they had previously tested emails that had no formatting and emulated “normal” email, and experienced great success. They decided to create a treatment version that stripped away all the formatting and photography.
They then split their email file and ran a test to see which attracted more responses
experiments.keyLearnings:
The stripped-down email (with no template) produced an 80% increase in clickthrough rate. This tells us something about trust — and giving is an act of trust. It may imply that people are more likely to take action when they feel that the email is coming from a person, rather than an organization.
There were still elements of the treatment email that weren’t entirely personal — the big blue button, and the logo. This prompted further testing to see if the removal of these elements produced an additional lift.

experiments.title: How increasing the number of hyperlinks in an email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing the number of links in an email increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2016 - 01/16/2017
CaringBridge had always included a “focused link” in their email appeals, which gave readers a single link to click to take action. Historically, this had been a button, but previous testing had shown that a text link performed better.
They wanted to test the effect of giving donors more places to click throughout the email—essentially linking up any text that contained a CTA, so if the donor was motivated, they could take action right then. They produced a treatment that had the exact same copy, but included more clickable links.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 27% increase in clickthrough rate, which tells us a few things about our readers. First, it tells us that including more links incentivizes more of them to click, or take the first initial action at the moment that the value proposition hits home. Secondly, it tells us that we had previously been losing potential donors who didn’t make it all the way through the copy to the call-to-action.
This points to an important principle: our donors and prospects have varying levels of motivation. Some are ready to give and need very little convincing, while others are more deliberative and might need more copy to be incentivized to click. Typically, we attempt to address these varying levels of motivation through multiple emails, but this experiment points to the opportunity that we might be able to reach multiple levels of motivation in the same communication.

experiments.title: How increasing intensity of color on a widget affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the intensity of the tribute widget attract more attention and increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2017 - 10/25/2017
CaringBridge had long sought to increase clickthrough rate on their tribute widget, which started the gift process for . One of their designers hypothesized that the current tribute widget blended in too much with the rest of the site and needed some “pop”. Using the intense CaringBridge brand purple, she created a treatment designed to attract more attention, and hopefully clickthrough rate.
CaringBridge tested the new tribute widget in both desktop and mobile views and launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment tribute widget reduced clickthrough rate by 27.6%, which further reduced donations and revenue downstream. We believe this can largely be attributed to the decrease in readability due to the white text now being placed on the intense purple.  In breaking down the data by device, most of the decrease came from mobile devices, in which this effect is intensified due to the smaller screen size.
This was just the first test in a series of tribute widget tests designed to increase clickthrough rate.

experiments.title: How making an end-user click “See More” in a Facebook ad impacts click-through to the acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad would increase click-through and ultimately name acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2017 - 09/07/2017
Care Net offers a free online course called Pro-Life 101. They use paid Facebook ads to push the offer. Through previous testing, we had learned that longer Facebook ad copy increases name acquisition. To build upon those learnings, we tested ad copy that was even longer resulting in the end-user having to click “See More” to read the entirety of it.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version with the extended copy increased click-through by 82%! What this tell us is that people are not only more highly engaged with long ad copy, but even longer ad copy requiring people to click “See More” increases engagement and results in more people clicking through to the landing page and converting. The “See More” version provided additional information that the end-user needed to make the decision to click-through to the landing page and ultimately sign-up for the course.

experiments.title: How presenting a giving opportunity in a blog post can increase traffic to a donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the addition of a donation opportunity increase traffic to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2017 - 10/13/2017
Care Net has a high volume of traffic visiting their blogs. In an effort to convert more of these blog readers into donors, they hypothesized that putting a donation opportunity at the end of each blog post might help increase traffic to their donation page. To test this, they added a donation opportunity that spoke directly to the blog reader by saying, “Dear Reader…”. They included value proposition copy in it and added a button with a call-to-action at the end of the text that said, “Make Your Gift”.
experiments.keyLearnings: Making a direct solicitation in the form of a "Dear Reader" ad resulted in a 138% increase in donations. It is a good reminder that we need to prompt visitors to do our desired action but we also need to make sure we fulfill upon the original reason that they came to the site first. By having the ad at the end of the article, this allowed the visitor to first read the content they were looking for before presenting them with the next step.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity in a Facebook ad affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the clarity of the prospect-level value proposition increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2017
Buckner International was offering a free eBook by their president, Albert Reyes, called “The Jesus Agenda”. They hypothesized that their control ad, with a photo of Jesus, might lack clarity in the prospect-level value proposition, since it didn’t show the viewer what they were getting.
They drafted three new treatments: one with the eBook and dark text, another with the eBook and lighter text, and one with the eBook right-aligned and the text on the left. They launched a four-way test to determine which one produced the highest clickthrough rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the dark text and the book on the left produced a 95.6% increase in clickthrough rate as compared to the control, beating the other three treatments (which also produced lifts).
This experiment shows the importance of clarity in the ad, even at the initial point of contact. The high contrast in the text makes it jump out, especially in Facebook where the majority of the platform is white. Also, leading with the book image draws the reader’s eye into the text, rather than the other way around.
This experiment will help future ad campaigns begin with more optimized design.

experiments.title: How image clarity and ask intensity affected clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Does an image of the 3D eBook in the ad produce more clicks than an image of children? How do people react to a soft ask vs. a direct ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2017 - 05/03/2017
Buckner International was promoting a new free ebook to grow their email file targeted towards people interested in the foster care system. They wanted to figure out what made people click through to the landing page from the ad and download the guide. They created four different treatments to test this- 2 images paired with 2 different approaches to copy. One of the images is a photo of the 3D eBook, and the other image is a photo of children. The copy differences hinge on a direct vs. soft ask: “commit to prayer” vs. “learn more about the opportunity”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Buckner team realized that users were 40% more likely to respond to an image including the 3D eBook than one that does not. This stems from the clarity and directness of the image’s alignment with the ad- people know exactly what they’re getting when they click the ad. Copy 2 was also more effective because they’re not asking them to commit to something at the ad level, as in Copy 1. The direct ask in Copy 1 is better suited for people who have already clicked through to the landing page. The softer ask in Copy 2 (asking them to learn more) paired with a direct image led to a 40% more successful click through rate.
They also learned that placing a strong statement (“The foster system is broken”) that people will either agree/disagree with at the beginning of the ad leads to more qualified traffic (and a more likely converter). If the user agrees with the initial statement, then sees the opportunity to learn more paired with a very clear image of what they’re going to receive if they click the link, they’re more likely to continue to the landing page. If they disagree, they’re immediately diverted.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of calls-to-action affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will limiting the number of final calls-to-action increase the overall visitor engagement on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2015 - 10/07/2015
Alliance Defending Freedom creates a daily blog post that they use to promote discussion and knowledge of America’s religious freedoms. The majority of these posts end with a call-to-action or multiple calls-to-action that link to areas of their website or to various other organizations that promote the values ADF strives to protect.  Since the goal of each post is to drive visitor engagement, we wanted to find a way to increase the number of visitors that participate in the call-to-action.
We had the hypothesis that, by limiting the number of final calls-to-action to just one choice, we may be able to increase the engagement of the visitors. We took the blog post that was scheduled for 10/6/2015 and created a new treatment. The new treatment contained the same number of links throughout the page but we moved two of the bullet points from the final “Take Action” section and placed them inline with the other copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: By reducing the number of final calls to action to just one action, we were able to increase the number of visitors exploring the ADF website by 119.5%. What is interesting about this lift is that the final call-to-action was to explore a different website created by Focus on the Family, not to explore the ADF website. We also observed a 28% lift to those clicking on that final call-to-action but the biggest lift came from those clicking on the links within the article itself.

				gift).

119.5% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a direct ask affects traffic to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a soft call-to-action or a more direct call-to-action drive more traffic and donations from the blog?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2015 - 12/02/2015
This past Giving Tuesday, Alliance Defending Freedom took a different approach than most nonprofit organizations. Instead of making a direct ask to their constituency, they utilized a message of stewardship to their constituency. They created a blog post that explained the impact the donors’  support had over the past year with specific examples of people that had been helped. This post was sent out to both their social media audience and their email file.  At the end of the post, there was a short value proposition statement on why their donations were so important and a button with the soft call-to-action of “I Support Religious Freedom” that directed them to a donation page.
We had the hypothesis that since it was Giving Tuesday, we could be more direct in our call-to-action. We created a new treatment that changed the wording on the button “Make your Gift This Giving Tuesday” in order to add clarity around the action we wanted them to take.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The genesis for this experiment came from the learnings of two prior tests. In those tests, we learned that ADF’s blog traffic responds better to softer calls-to-action while their email traffic responds better to direct asks within the email. These two experiments proved that the blog and email file were two disparate audiences with differing motivations. Since this page was going to receive traffic from both audiences, we wanted to discover which tactic would prove most successful.
We learned that the direct call-to-action reduced traffic to the donation page by 56.5% and this reduction in traffic then resulted in 29% less gifts*. This reinforces the need to use softer language with additional value proposition for the blog audience.
* The 29% decrease in gifts was not yet statistically valid but was quickly approaching validity by the time the test was finished.

experiments.title: How decreasing a page’s loading time affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the loading time of a webpage impact the visitors likelihood to engage with the content?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2016 - 02/22/2016
At a recent planning meeting with Alliance Defending Freedom, we were having discussions on ways to improve visitor engagement with their blog posts. A single blog post will see several thousand visitors every day but we were having difficulty getting them to engage beyond that one article. After analyzing the data in Google Analytics, we made two critical discoveries:
93% of the visitors to the blog did not stay on the page longer than 10 seconds
The average load time of the web page was 14 seconds.
This meant that the vast majority of visitors did not stay on the site long enough for the content to actually load.  Based upon these findings, we decided to run an experiment to see what the impact of speeding up the blog would have on visitor engagement. We created a “flat” version of the same blog that was hosted directly on a content delivery network (CDN). The end result was a 55% reduction in load time going from 14.8 seconds to 6.7 seconds.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The “flat” version of the blog was able to increase the visitor engagement by 26.4%. (We defined visitor engagement as a user clicking on anything on the page or scrolling down the site).  Additionally, we saw a 17% increase to the time spent on the page.
Increasing the load time of the actual website will be no easy task but this experiment allows us to quantify the impact of speeding up the site. Knowing this will help prioritize the work with the existing day-to-day tasks that must get done.

				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How adding a call-to-action onto a banner increased visitor engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Does including the call-to-action on the homepage banner for all visitors increase the number of people that explore the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2016 - 05/04/2016
In an effort to drive visitor engagement, specifically to increase the number of visitors exploring the website, ADF had run some experiments recently on their homepage. One particular test targeted mobile visitors and their engagement with the “jewel widget” found on the home page. On this experiment, we discovered that we could increase the number of people exploring the website by 11% if we added a call-to-action on top of each banner (it is normally only visible on mouseover.) We decided to run this same experiment but open it up to all visitors, instead of just mobile, to see if it would have the same impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: It would appear that placing the call-to-action so that it is always visible on the home page increased the number of visitors exploring the website by 22%. By adding this language so that it is always visible, we increased the clarity of the desired action. Prior to this experiment, the only way to know the desired outcome of the click was to hover over each block which, it seems, was not immediately evident to all users.

experiments.title: How relevancy and recency impact visitor engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will related content drive more engagement and exploration than recent content?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2016 - 11/30/2016
Alliance Defending Freedom had recently made a radical shift in their blog to better engage their mobile visitors. They created an entirely separate website that would mirror the experience most mobile visitors were coming from (Facebook). This change significantly increased engagement for the high volume of traffic that visit their blog each day.
The next iteration in this test series was to see if we could get the visitors to explore the ADF website in order to learn more about the organization. One hypothesis was that if we showed the visitors content that was more relevant to the post they had just read, they would be more likely to continue exploring the site.
To test that hypothesis, we created a version of the blog that replaced the most recent blog posts with related content.
experiments.keyLearnings: The related content ended up decreasing the number of visitors that read other articles by 20%. This was a big discovery since there was significant effort being put into making this a feature available on the desktop version of the blog. This will allow ADF to focus their efforts on website improvements that can drive deeper engagement.

experiments.title: How the “Next Article” button impacted visitor engagement – V1
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a “Next Article” button increase the percentage of visitors that explore the ADF website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2016 - 12/20/2016
As part of the efforts to increase visitor engagement for ADF, we wanted to find a way to encourage visitors to explore the website. With a majority of visitors coming on mobile devices to the blog, we focused our attention on that audience.
One idea was to mirror many of the mobile news sites that had started placing a button at the bottom of each page that moved the visitor on to the next article. This feature would seem to create a binary decision instead of making them select from a menu of potential articles like was the current setup.
experiments.keyLearnings: Based upon the results of this experiment, the “Next Article” button seemed to decrease the number of visitors that read another article (and at a high rate!) Our expectation was that it would either increase or have no impact so this was a surprising results. We have decided to re-launch this test using a different way of setting it up to validate whether there was an instrumentation error impacting the results.

experiments.title: How 3rd party validation can impact visitor engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will incorporating 3rd party validation increase the likelihood of a visitor to read another article?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/06/2017 - 01/27/2017
On the mobile version of ADF’s blog, we have focused significant efforts into finding ways to increase visitor engagement. The majority of visitors are coming from social media and often do not usually explore the site beyond the one blog post that they read.
Our previous tests had been unsuccessful in increasing the percentage of visitors that continue to explore the site. But, by analyzing their results, we had developed a new hypothesis that we could increase engagement by increasing the perceived popularity of the posts. We believe that by ordering the recent posts with the most popular at the top would increase the likelihood of a visitor reading the article.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not a huge lift, we did see a statistically significant 2.5% increase to the number of visitors that read another article. This would imply that visitors were more likely to be influenced to read a post if they knew that other visitors had done the same thing. This is a good example of subtly using 3rd party credibility to increase engagement.

experiments.title: How the call-to-action in an email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging approach for the year end campaign is the most appealing to the Heritage Foundation audience?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2014 - 12/17/2014
At the start of their end of year campaign, Heritage crafted four different emails that would offer insights into what motivated their donors visit the campaign landing page.  The goal was that by testing these different approaches, we would be able to gain insights as to what messaging approach was most appealing to the visitors to help craft the rest of the campaign messages.
experiments.keyLearnings: The shorter email with a softer call-to-action was able to drive incremental amounts of traffic with additional gifts (although the response rate did not have a level of confidence above 95%).

experiments.title: How popularity of an article influenced visitor engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will combining relevancy with popularity increase the engagement of visitors on the blog?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2017 - 03/22/2017
For Alliance Defending Freedom, we had previously run experiments that found more relevant blog posts (those in the same content category) were less influential at getting visitors to explore the blog than the most recent posts. We also found that the most popular articles could slightly increase the percentage of people that read another article compared to the most recent posts. We had also found that the tactic used to display the articles (sidebar compared to a list at the bottom) did not have any impact on increasing engagement.
However, in the continued efforts to increase reader engagement, we wanted to see if we could radically alter the visitor experience by combining all three of these (relevancy, popularity, and design). We decided to launch a three-way test targeting desktop visitors to the blog. We would show them the standard control which was the most recent articles and then two versions of the most popular articles based upon the category of the post they were reading.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In both cases, the most popular and relevant content (regardless of where it was displayed) decreased the percentage of people that explored the website. Due to the differences in the treatments, this may have been the result of differing factors.
In the case of the sidebar, our hypothesis is that visitors ignored that area of the website due to “banner blindness.” In the case of the content at the bottom, it is our hypothesis the drop in engagement may be due to the lack of imagery. We wanted to emphasize the popularity so we used the space normally reserved for imagery to show the posts’ position in popularity. This may have decreased the appeal of the article.

experiments.title: How an ‘Up Next’ Slideout Impacts Mobile Clickthrough Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will providing a single article that is “Up Next” increase clickthrough rate on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2017 - 06/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom noticed that their most engaged users–and consequently, the ones that were most likely to become supporters–primarily spent their time reading Alliance Defending Freedom’s blogs. Noticing this as a major step in the donor journey, they decided to test a ‘Up Next’ slideout designed to keep the site visitor engaged with a new article immediately after finishing one. If they could keep their visitor engaged just a little bit longer, they knew they could increase that user’s affinity for their mission–and perhaps, their willingness to support Alliance Defending Freedom. They set up an A/B test to test this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings: Users that were motivated enough to finish reading the entire blog article responded favorably to the ‘Up Next’ slideout. Over the course of two weeks, Alliance Defending Freedom noticed that the treatment did, in fact, increase engagement by nearly 30%. It’s evident that readers wanted more to get more content. By removing the friction of searching for the next blog to read or deciding which topic to choose, Alliance Defending Freedom found that visitors stayed engaged for a longer period of time, especially when the next article was chosen for them. This finding has prompted further side-by-side funnel testing to determine what kind of funnel is the most effective for user engagement. There is also room for similar tests measuring results all the way through the funnel, to view the effects of email acquisition and donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How Mission-Specific Content Impacts Clickthrough Rate on a Mobile Slideout
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase mobile traffic to the ‘Issues’ section of the site without decreasing ‘next article’ clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 08/25/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom found that the ‘Issues’ slideout increased traffic to the ‘Issues’ portion of the site by 49%. We also found that we were able to direct this traffic without negatively impacting the number of visitors that explored the website or read another article.
This test confirms our hypothesis that we can direct traffic to a desired portion of a site, without decreasing ‘next article’ clickthrough rate. In other words, this test proves that we can funnel visitors directly to the organization’s stated mission and immediately bring clarity to the organization’s value proposition without disrupting their online experience. This test has prompted further desktop-only tests, as well as similar site-specific slideout experiments.
experiments.keyLearnings: Alliance Defending Freedom knows that site visitors who spend more time in the ‘Issues’ section of their site have a better understanding of their mission. Earlier this year, they tested into using a ‘Up Next’ slideout on their blog pages. They wanted to determine if they could direct more traffic to the ‘Issues’ section of their site without decreasing clickthrough rate on their mobile slideout. To test run this test, they created an ‘Issues’ slideout as a treatment to see if they could effectively redirect traffic on mobile browsers.

experiments.title: How a softer call-to-action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call to action generate a higher clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2016 - 03/21/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their Membership Card to non-members giving them the opportunity to become a member.
Anticipating that the direct ask to “become a member” would receive fewer (but more motivated) clicks from the email, they wanted to test whether the added step and call-to-action to “confirm the name on your card” would generate more clicks, and result in more donations.
Since they weren’t making a direct ask, they created a different offer flow for this treatment. They created a two-step form for the treatment that started with a simple “confirm your name” step (pre-populated with the visitor’s name), and then took them to complete their donation. Because the visitor was indicating their intent to become a member by completing the first step, they removed much of the sales copy on the donation page in order to remove friction from the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The softer call to action generated a 54% increase in clicks, but a 51.6% decrease in donations. This revealed a key insight: all traffic is not the same. Visitors come with different motivations, and the traffic to the treatment flow was not at all “sold”.
This prompted a second test to see if reintroducing the value proposition copy on the follow-on donation form would affect the results.

The Heritage Legacy Society sent a prospecting email to ask Heritage Foundation members if they planned to include the organization in their estate planning. They didn’t know the best way to approach this through email, so they tested two distinct approaches: a direct ask, supported by facts about tax planning, against a story-based approach with a personal testimony from a Heritage Legacy Society member.
experiments.keyLearnings: This told us that we don’t necessarily need to “soften” the ask with a story. The story might introduce friction as an estate commitment is a personal decision, not one based on third-party influence.

experiments.title: How “less is more” affects video clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How much “selling” copy is needed to earn the click to watch a video?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2015 - 12/15/2015
Hillsdale College included a video in their year-end fundraising campaign to show a glimpse into the daily lives of students on campus. The video was not attached to a financial ask, but was intended to set up an ask that came a few days later. The video was excellent — but only if the recipient actually watched it, which they cannot do in the email client. Therefore, Hillsdale wanted to maximize traffic to the landing page, where the video could actually be viewed.
Video alone provides a strong call to action, especially when paired with a thumbnail image (as previous testing had proven). But how much copy is needed to “sell the click”? The control email had a lot of copy, so they wanted to test to see if a shorter email would increase clickthrough rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The email with shorter copy had a 26.9% higher clickthrough rate. This told Hillsdale that they didn’t need to go overboard selling the click — they just needed to be straightforward and reduce friction.

experiments.title: How a softer call to action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call to action like “Learn More” drive more traffic than an “activation” ask like “Get Free Access Now”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2015 - 11/16/2015
Hillsdale College had launched a new online course about the life and writings of acclaimed author and apologist C.S. Lewis. They wanted to promote these on Facebook, as they had done very successfully with other courses. But they wanted to better understand which calls to action drove the greatest amount of traffic to the page. Previously, they had used “activation” language like “Start Your Free Course Now” or “Get Instant Access Now”. They decided to test whether their audiences were actually motivated at that point in the funnel to “activate” — or if a softer call to action like “Learn More” would drive more traffic and conversions. So they set up two ads and set out to gather enough data to determine a result.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results tell us that at the first step in the journey, some of our audience is “sold” enough to be able to take that first step with an “activation” message. However, that leaves a lot of prospective users on the table who might want to learn more about the course before committing to sign up for it.

experiments.title: How adding branding to a prospecting email affected clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a branded header increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2015 - 06/16/2015
Hillsdale College was renting email lists to promote their popular Constitution 101 course. Most internal Hillsdale emails used a more “personal” style that didn’t include much branding. They wanted to test whether adding a branded header logo to the email would increase clickthrough rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment shows that while the brand might “get in the way” with the existing audience (who is already familiar and converted), new audiences may need an “introduction” to the brand in order to gain trust, familiarity, and increase response rate.

Hillsdale College was in the middle of a fundraising campaign and had an email that quoted Ronald Reagan. They had heard over and over again that using images in email was a “best practice”, and in this case, might offer extra credibility that would strengthen the incentive to click through. So they devised two emails — one that used text to convey the quote and one that used a more graphical treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that what is commonly accepted as “best practice” might not, in fact, be the best email to send. Hillsdale was able to attract many more people to the landing page by keeping the “personal” nature of the email consistent.

experiments.title: How the specificity of a subject line affects the click rate

rows.name: A new course for you: American Heritage

experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will produce the most visitors to the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2015 - 05/31/2015
When they were announcing their new online course, Hillsdale College wanted to offer the free course to their existing email file.  The majority of their email file had previously taken an online course so they wanted to frame this new offering around their previous experience.  As a way to increase the number of participants, we ran a test of two subject lines to see which would increase the number of people visiting the website.
The control in this test was letting the email list know that they could take another online course. The subject line read, “Your next course is ready”, which was the best performing subject line from past experiments. The treatment added specificity to the copy based upon the hypothesis that increased clarity would improve results. That subject line read, “A new course for you: American Heritage”.
experiments.keyLearnings: The increased clarity ended up dropping the unique number of website visitors by 15.1%. The increased clarity of the subject line reduced the number of constituents that opened the email.  While those that opened the email were more likely to visit the website, this increase in open to click rate was not enough to overcome the overall decrease in open rate.

experiments.title: How conveying value in the donate button affects traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call to action can drive the most visitors to the donation page: “Donate Now”, “Make my Gift”, or “Support Imprimis”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2015 - 05/22/2015
Imprimis is the free monthly speech digest of Hillsdale College and is dedicated to educating citizens and promoting civil and religious liberty by covering cultural, economic, political, and educational issues. It has an audience of over 2.8 million so we saw this as a great opportunity to optimize the pathway to a donation.
Historically, the donate button was found at the top of the site with the text of “Donate Now.” We didn’t believe that really conveyed any value or ownership in their gift so we wanted to try two different treatments.
experiments.keyLearnings: None of the other buttons were able to drive a single visitor to the donation page. Upon further reflection, the “Support Imprimis” button was the only one that conveyed any value to the visitors. If they enjoy the publication, this is something that they would be interested in doing. The “Donate Now” and “Make my Gift” are actions that we want them to take but it is not necessarily providing any value or context to the visitor.

experiments.title: How personalization affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does increasing the personalization in an emotion-based ask increase the number of clicks over the existing need-based ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2014 - 11/25/2014
We learned that clarity trumps personalized persuasion in this specific banner. The new banner included more copy and a much more indirect ask. This tells us that visitors on the page were already motivated to give – we didn’t need to keep selling. We just needed to ask, clearly.
experiments.keyLearnings: During appeals, CaringBridge often displays an additional site banner on its journal pages to present a donation ask to the reader. We believed that increasing personalization by including mentions of the patient’s name along with a more emotional ask would cause more people to click the link and begin the donation process.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2015 - 01/26/2015
As part of their monthly appeal strategy, Hillsdale College ran an experiment to a segment of their house email list to determine the best messaging approach for the appeal.  A clear winner was identified and the resulting email was scheduled to be sent out to the remainder of the email list with a subject line A/B split test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Message of hope” ended up with 10.1% less opens and 35.5% less clicks. The specificity of the control subject line, “Hillsdale’s plan to reverse damage,” proved to be more effective in inspiring recipients to open the email and then click through to the landing page.   It is important to remember that specificity yields clarity, and that clarity trumps persuasion.

Timeframe: 12/15/2014 - 12/17/2014
At the start of their end of year campaign, Heritage crafted four different emails that would offer insights into what motivated their donors visit the campaign landing page.  The goal was that by testing these different approaches, we would be able to gain insights as to what messaging approach was most appealing to the visitors to help craft the rest of the campaign messages.
experiments.keyLearnings: The shorter email with a softer call-to-action was able to drive incremental amounts of traffic with additional gifts (although the response rate did not have a level of confidence above 95%).

experiments.title: How simplifying an email affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a simpler email that looks like a personal message from the president increase the number of visitors to the page over the standard marketing template?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2014 - 12/24/2014
Hillsdale produced a stewardship video to thank supporters while giving them an inside look at work that goes occurs on a daily basis. This was to be sent out two days before Christmas in hopes of gaining a handful of donations and to set the stage for the end of year campaign that would be following the week later. The standard email sent from Hillsdale is a well-designed template that looks like it comes from the college. We wanted to see if a simpler email would give the impression of more personalized which would then increase results.
experiments.keyLearnings: The simpler email increased click rate by 19.6% because it had the perception of coming from an individual instead of an organization. A request from an individual will often have more meaning and will carry more weight than a request coming from an organization.

experiments.title: How employing active language affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does active language incentivize more people to click through?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2014 - 08/14/2014
The Heritage Foundation has an annual fundraising campaign backed by a challenge grant from their board of Trustees. In 2014, Steve Forbes wrote an introductory paragraph on the landing page about why the board was matching gifts. Heritage wanted to tease the quote in the ad, but wanted to test the active language of Forbes “issuing a challenge” versus the more passive language of “announcing a challenge grant”.
experiments.keyLearnings: By using active language, Heritage’s new ad attracted more interest from people who saw it. Nonprofit organizations commonly use challenge grants during fundraising campaigns — but “issuing a challenge” from the board is a much stronger way to approach the same action.

experiments.title: How an engagement device affects clickthrough rate versus a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a survey ask instead of a direct donation ask increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2016 - 08/29/2016
Hillsdale College wanted to send a digital follow-up to a direct mail appeal to a segment of their donors who had both a mailing address and an email address on file. These “chase” appeals had generated a significant amount of revenue in the past, but they wanted to try a new approach.
Previously, these emails just mirrored the direct mail package and made a direct ask for a donation. However, the direct mail package for their August appeal included a survey. They hypothesized that they could get better engagement by asking the donors to take a survey (that led to a donation) rather than putting a direct ask in the email. Ultimately, they hypothesized that this increase in clickthrough rate would result in more people reaching the donation page, and higher donor conversion. But first, they needed to get more clicks.
They recreated the survey online, split their email file, and send one segment a direct ask and the other a link to take a survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The survey, as expected, delivered a 693.1% increase in clickthrough rate. A soft ask almost always delivers more clicks than a direct donation ask, because the motivations are entirely different.
However, more clicks doesn’t always mean more donations, since people aren’t specifically clicking to make a donation. That warranted another test to see how the survey affected donor conversion.

experiments.title: How Facebook ad image text affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more specific language in the image of a Facebook ad increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2016 - 06/07/2016
Hillsdale College launched a free digital offer for their popular eBook Liberty and Learning, with a donation ask after the download was processed. As they created the ads for this offer, they wanted to test copy in the Facebook ad to determine how “ready to download” their traffic was.
The first ad assumed that people who saw the ads weren’t very convinced and needed to “learn more” first. This language was highlighted in the image, which is the most visually engaging part of a Facebook ad.
The second ad gave more context to the offer, explaining that it was a “free eBook”. While this gave more clarity to the offer, it also made an assumption that someone was “ready to download” when they clicked.
They launched an A/B test of ads to validate a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 20.6% increase in clickthrough rate, which showed that people who saw the ad were more likely to take initial action when presented with more specific language in the ad. This is aided be by Hillsdale’s precise targeting, but it also tells us that clarity around the offer entices more people to take the first step.
However, clickthrough rate might be deceiving, so Hillsdale measured conversion rate as well to make sure that the lift held all the way through the marketing funnel.

experiments.title: How length of copy affects clickthrough rate in a video email
experiments.researchQuestion: How much “selling” is needed to earn the click to watch a video?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2015 - 12/15/2015
Hillsdale College included a video soft-touch ask as part of their year-end fundraising campaign. This email was not designed to directly raise money, though there was a form on the page. It was meant to inspire readers to watch the video and prepare them for an ask later in the week. Therefore, it was critical to get as many people to the landing page as possible — which meant that Hillsdale needed to optimize the email’s clickthrough rate.
The original version of the email used lots of copy to “sell” the click. We developed a hypothesis that a video, when shown to a relevant audience, wouldn’t need nearly as much “selling” copy to win the click. We developed a treatment with dramatically less copy, moving the screenshot (which linked to the landing page) closer to the top of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test once again proves that clarity always trumps persuasion. If we can be straightforward about what we are wanting the user to do, it often outperforms a longer-form, more persuasive approach.

experiments.title: How call-to-action visibility affects email clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second button to the top of the email increase click through rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2016 - 05/13/2016
The Heritage Foundation was partnering with Townhall Media to generate email signups through an eBook giveaway. Townhall generated new signups by sending sponsored emails to their list.
Early results showed acceptable open and conversion rates, but lower than expected clickthrough rates.
We hypothesized that the email openers were abandoning the email because the value proposition and call to action were not immediately visible upon opening. By adding a second call to action button at the top of the email we anticipated a higher number of readers would remain engaged with the email and ultimately click through.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the second button increased click through rate 40.4%. The new button at the top of the email received some clicks, but the CTR on the unchanged links lower in the email increased as well (19%, 11%, and 27% respectively). This confirmed our hypothesis that more readers would stay engaged with the email and ultimately click if there was a call to action visible immediately upon opening.

experiments.title: Does Calling Out the Giving Tuesday Donor Goal in the Sticky Bar Increase Engagement?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the Giving Tuesday donor goal for IMB homepage visitors will increase engagement with the web asset, as making the goal visible taps into goal-oriented behavior. Visitors are more likely to click, explore, and give when they understand exactly what their participation contributes to.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2025
