During IMB’s 2025 Giving Tuesday campaign, we tested the addition of a donor goal to the homepage sticky bar. Our hypothesis was that clearly displaying the goal would increase engagement with the sticky bar—measured by higher click-through rates—and drive more motivated traffic further down the giving path.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding a donor goal to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a notable increase in engagement, yielding a 29.9% rise in clicks across all traffic. This suggests that prominently displaying a clear Giving Tuesday goal can attract more visitors to the giving pathway. However, because the confidence level fell below the standard 95% threshold, these engagement results should be interpreted as directional rather than definitive for future decision-making. When examining downstream giving behavior, both the control and treatment produced 9 total gifts.
Transactions per session: Treatment showed a 1.3% lift (2.1% LOC)
 	Transactions per click: Treatment showed a 22% decrease (43.5% LOC)
In other words, while the treatment succeeded in motivating more users to click into the donation experience, it did not increase their likelihood of completing a gift once they arrived at the donation page. The sticky bar drove more traffic to the page, but not more donors. Revenue: To prevent outliers from skewing insights, gifts over $500 were removed. Following this adjustment, the treatment resulted in a 93.7% decrease in revenue (94.1% LOC) compared to the control. This suggests that although engagement was higher, the additional traffic did not translate into higher-value giving behavior. In future experiments, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations of the donor goal messaging to see if different phrasing or formats may have a more significant impact on user engagement. It is also important to continue monitoring and analyzing results from experiments to continually improve strategies for engaging with website visitors and driving them further down the giving path.

experiments.title: How adding an image of the premium in an appeal email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the image of the premium to the appeal email for the housefile will increase the click-through rate because the cover image is unique and would encourage users to click through.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2025
Time of Grace offers a new premium each month. They send out appeal emails for this premium. The cover for their October premium was particularly distinctive and stood out to the audience.
Therefore, we wanted to test including the image in the email. We wanted to see how that would affect click-through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that adding the image of the premium to the appeal email did not achieve the desired increase in click-through rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 25.5% decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a 91.2% statistical level of confidence. While these results are not considered valid, they are approaching validity, indicating that further testing would be necessary to confirm the effect.
In terms of donations, however, we did see an increase in the treatment, but not with a 95% level of confidence.
Control - 11 Donations
Treatment - 17 Donations
LOC = 74.9%
Future studies should investigate how images in emails influence user behavior. Further testing can reveal whether certain images or situations are more effective. Keep analyzing results to improve email marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with donor centric language affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the stickybar for website visitors will achieve an increase in clicks because having language where the donor is first and focusing on what their donation does would encourage users to click more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/27/2025
For CaringBridge, during their 2 day match in June, we wanted to test changing the language on the stickybar. This was an appeal stickybar. The language change was to put the donor “first”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the language on the stickybar to put the donor first and focus on what their donation does resulted in a 12.2% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that the revised language resonated with website visitors and motivated them to engage with the call to action.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of the language to further optimize for donor engagement. Additionally, conducting more experiments with larger sample sizes to achieve a higher statistical level of confidence would help ensure the validity of the results and the effectiveness of the changes implemented. It is important to continue testing and refining strategies based on data-driven insights to maximize impact and drive successful outcomes for Caringbridge.

experiments.title: How including specific goals and stats in an email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including stats and goals for 2025 will increase click-through rate because specific and measurable outcomes demonstrate the tangible impact of a donor’s gift, making the appeal more compelling and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
This experiment involved testing email copy for The Navigators’ donor appeals in their calendar year-end campaign. The test was conducted within an email campaign sent to existing and prospective donors, comparing two variations: the Control copy, which used general language about the impact of donations, and the Treatment copy, which included specific statistics and measurable goals for 2025.
This experiment was prompted by the desire to understand whether more detailed and concrete information about The Navigators’ progress and goals would resonate more effectively with donors. The idea stemmed from the assumption that presenting measurable outcomes and future plans would create a stronger emotional and logical connection, leading to higher engagement and click-through rates. Additionally, recent donor feedback highlighted a preference for knowing the direct impact of their contributions, which further inspired the inclusion of these stats and goals in the Treatment copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including specific statistics and measurable goals for the future in donor appeals can significantly increase the click-through rate compared to using general language about the impact of donations. The Treatment copy, which contained detailed information about the organization's progress and goals for 2025, outperformed the Control copy with a 30.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with an 87% level of confidence.

Additionally, we saw the treatment group increase donor conversion rate by 27% but with only a 48% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of including specific and measurable outcomes in donor appeals to further validate these findings. It would also be valuable to explore how different types of statistics and goals resonate with donors, as well as the optimal way to present this information for maximum effectiveness. Additionally, considering donor feedback and preferences when crafting email copy and appeals can help tailor messages to better meet their expectations and motivations for giving.

Overall, these results suggest that donors respond positively to concrete data and future plans, indicating that this strategy may enhance donor engagement and support for The Navigators. Further experimentation and refinement of this approach can help optimize future fundraising efforts and drive continued success in connecting with donors.

experiments.title: How changing the FB creative for an eBook offer impacted click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the creative for avila’s audience will achieve an increase in CTR and engagement because it will increase intrigue .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/19/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of Avila’s newest offers is “What is Purgatory?”. This is an eBook for the viewer to learn more about Purgatory. We wanted to test using creative that was not the ebook. The original ebook had a more traditional, artistic cover, we wanted to see if making the creative a bit more vague would increase intrigue and make people click on the ad. And even further if it would increase engagement and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the creative for Avila's audience led to an increase in clicks for the "What is Purgatory?" eBook offer, indicating that the more vague creative increased intrigue and interest among viewers. While the results were not statistically significant at the 95% level, the 8.8% increase in clicks with an 89.5% statistical confidence suggests that the treatment was effective in capturing the audience's attention.

In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue testing different creatives to further validate the initial findings and explore other creative approaches that may resonate with the audience. It is important to analyze the data from each test comprehensively to understand the impact of various creative elements on engagement and ultimately donations. Additionally, considering incorporating qualitative feedback from the audience to gain insights into their preferences and interests when developing future creatives.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts lapsed donors during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing value prop for lapsed donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates. This specific experiment targeted lapsed donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
- The experiment aimed to test the impact of reinforcing the value proposition for lapsed donors in an email during WUNC's Fall Fund Drive.
- The treatment group, which received the additional call-out reinforcing WUNC's value proposition, showed a significant increase in clicks for all traffic, with an 81.8% improvement.
- The statistical level of confidence was 89.5%, indicating that the results are approaching validity, but further testing is recommended for certainty.
-Moreover, while there were only 3 gifts total, the addition of the value prop did increase conversion rates by 100%.

experiments.title: How a more conversational tone on an email offering an ebook impacts click through rates for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a more conversational tone for non-donors will achieve an increase in click throughs and email sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/03/2024 - 10/08/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Income Inequality, we wanted to see if a more conversational approach to email copy made an impact on click throughs and email sign ups for a non-donor audience
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a more conversational tone in email copy for non-donors resulted in a 6.1% increase in click-through rates. While this indicates a positive impact, the statistical level of confidence of 83.8% is below the threshold of 85% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following points:

1. Ensure a higher level of statistical confidence: To draw reliable conclusions, it is crucial to have a statistical level of confidence of at least 85%. This will help in making informed decisions based on the experiment results.

2. Continue testing conversational tone: The positive trend observed in this experiment suggests that a conversational tone may resonate well with non-donor audiences. It could be beneficial to further test this approach with a larger sample size to validate the impact.

3. Explore other variables: Apart from tone, it may be worthwhile to test other variables such as subject lines, call-to-action language, or design elements to optimize email performance for non-donor audiences.

4. Collect qualitative feedback: In addition to quantitative data, gathering qualitative feedback from the audience can provide valuable insights into their preferences and behaviors. This could help in refining future experiments and strategies.

Overall, while the experiment showed a positive trend, it is essential to prioritize statistical significance and continue testing different approaches to effectively engage non-donor audiences and drive conversions.

experiments.title: How using more focused copy on a donor email offering up a content offer impacted offer performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing copy for donors will achieve a change in email performance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Personal Freedom and the Moral Case for Capitalism, we wanted to see whether copy that focused more on the offer, and not use pop culture references, impact email and content offer performance. This experiment was for an active donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that focusing the copy on the offer itself can have a positive impact on email performance for donors. The treatment group, which received the email with copy focused on the offer without using pop culture references, saw an 11.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group.
Moreover, the treatment version saw a 55% increase in email sign-ups, with a 100% level of confidence. And it also saw an 11% increase in donations after someone signed up to get the offer, but only with a 35% level of confidence.
We can then say that for an active donor audience, for this specific content offfer, using more focused copy led to an increase in all KPIs. Future experiments could look at making sure there is congruency at the landing and donation page level.

experiments.title: How a cleaner weekly email design impacts the click rate of readers
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning Winston’s Weekly for subscribers will achieve a higher email click rate because there will be more information and resources available to them. because there will be more information and resources available to them.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/11/2024
We’ve been running Winston’s Weekly for a little more than a year now. The weekly email shares a case study from our experiment library and breaks it down to provide fundraisers with a deeper look at fundraising tests to inspire their own testing and optimization.
Looking at the design, it felt partially designed and partially plain text. We thought that using more design elements might create a cleaner, more readable way to present the information.
Pulling some inspiration from the newsletter Case Studied, we broke each header section into its own blocks in the design. We added a resources section at the bottom that links to the week’s experiment and other relevant resources. We also added a section about our services with a button to learn more.
Unlike the control version, this treatment version provided more opportunities and more reason to click out of the email to our website.
The treatment resulted in an email that was almost twice the length of our typical email but presented the content in a much cleaner way.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The redesigned version of Winston's Weekly did not perform as well as the original version, with a 27.5% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was 92.4%, meaning the results are approaching validity but cannot be considered completely reliable. The additional information and resources added to the treatment version did not lead to a higher click rate as hypothesized.

I'm interested in doing another email redesign in the control format with just the addition of the resources section and the section about NextAfter's services to see how that would impact clicks.

It could also be interesting to do another email redesign in a plain text format, but removing the gray background and using clearer headings like in this treatment to see if that gets more people to stick around till the end and click.

Future Experimentation:

1. Further testing is necessary to determine the impact of the redesigned layout on subscriber engagement.

2. Consider conducting additional A/B tests with variations in design elements to identify which specific changes lead to improved performance.

3. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future iterations of Winston's Weekly, taking into account subscriber preferences and engagement metrics to guide design decisions.

experiments.title: How adding relevancy to email copy impacted clicks and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding relevancy to an offer for non donors will achieve an increase in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/03/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and Non Donor audiences. This specific test was on their non donor audience. For this test on one of their most popular offers, we wanted to see whether email copy focused on relevancy outperformed the control copy, which has been used multiple times.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding relevancy to an offer for non donors led to a 5.6% increase in clicks compared to the control email. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 80.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. Therefore, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Donations had a 64% increase on the treatment with a 80.7% level of confidence. The control had 11 gifts, while the treatment with the relevancy copy had 18 gifts. It would be interesting to further test relevancy copy to see if this continues to impacts donations and makes it feel more personal to the donor.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is at least 95% to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different ways of adding relevancy to offers for non donors to see if it consistently leads to increased engagement. By conducting experiments with a higher level of confidence and testing various approaches to relevancy, the Hoover Institution can gather more reliable insights to inform their email marketing strategies.

experiments.title: Does a content offer work better as a first text?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending a content offer for poeple to opt into text will achieve a higher engagement rate and a lower unsubscribe because offering something of value is an easier sell to get people involved .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2024
KCBI recently incorporated texting into their program and we wondered what the best route to activate people in this channel would be.
So, we ran a test. Would offering a content offer, or something of value, be a better route to get somebody to opt in to text vs. a generic welcome message.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the offer did receive overall less clicks.. the most important part of the test is knowing how many people actually stayed on the file instead of unsubscribing.

By offering something of value in the first text we actually saw a decrease in unsubscribed contacts.

 

Test: United We Kneel eBook

1,461 sent messages, 42 unsubscribes: 2.87%

 

Control: General Message

1,463 sent messages, 88 unsubscribes: 6.02%

The test underscores the importance of offering valuable, targeted content to retain subscribers. While the eBook offer may not have driven as many clicks initially, its value proposition likely made recipients more inclined to stay subscribed, rather than seeing the email as just another message in their inbox. This outcome suggests that even with fewer clicks, offering relevant, engaging content can effectively reduce unsubscribe rates.

experiments.title: How adding a logo at the top of an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a logo at the top of emails for subscribers will achieve higher email engagement because the logo creates confidence that the email is legit.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/25/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
3 years ago we tested out of images within emails, however we decided to retest a logo on email to see if a little branding adds validity to the email and if it can impact clicks and downstream donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a logo at the top of emails for subscribers resulted in a 23.0% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. Although the results suggest potential benefits of including a logo for branding and legitimacy purposes, the statistical level of confidence of 85.0% falls below the accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

In regard to donations we saw a 28% decrease in gifts at a 81% level of confidence

So, while the email saw more engagement, it did not translate to more gifts. 

Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making. It is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations to validate the impact of adding a logo to emails. Additionally, considering other factors such as email content, layout, and targeting can provide more insights into improving email engagement and donations. Ultimately, the key takeaway is to prioritize rigorous testing and analysis to make informed decisions for optimizing email marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How a banner style Facebook ad impacted clicks to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that running a yes/no ad during a high urgency campaign for a direct mail audience will achieve increased clicks to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/10/2021
During a high urgency campaign, we wanted to target direct mail recipients with digital ads to prime and reinforce the campaign messaging in order to drive additional highly motivated and qualified traffic to the donation page. We wanted to test our control copy which included the deadline and the Challenge Gift against a treatment that made a specific ask to give a monthly gift and become a new Cornerstone Partner. We also developed a second treatment that asked a question to frame and align the donor’s values and beliefs with the organization and used more sliding door copy that presents what happens if we do not equip Christian leaders with Scripture. We wanted to activate the power of the “micro-yes” through this ad and bring those visitors to the donation page while they are already nodding their heads in agreement with the mission of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: While neither treatment had enough run-time to see valid results since it was a short, time-bound campaign, the ad that had "Learn more about becoming a Cornerstone Partner" as the call to action increased traffic by 65.6% with a 91.8% level of confidence. The second treatment with the banner graphic also drove clicks up 21.8% but only had a 62.7% level of confidence. We will look for additional opportunities to test the banner graphic in acquisition ads to see how the belief alignment impacts traffic and conversion rate for both online courses and instant donation pages.

experiments.title: How Adding “Yes/No” language to Facebook Ads Impacts Performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding “yes/no” buttons to creative for questions posed in ad will achieve more clicks because it taps into the binary nature of close-ended questions. because it taps into the binary nature of close-ended questions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2023 - 10/31/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think-tank for Stanford University.
Using “Yes/No” language buttons on a static creative piece has improved performance for some other clients, and the NextAfter team believes Hoover’s audience will react positively and improve CTR.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from utilizing "Yes/No" buttons on static ads within Facebook are as follows:

1. Adding "yes/no" buttons to ad creative for questions posed in the ad resulted in an 11.6% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that using close-ended questions with binary options in ad creative can effectively drive engagement.

2. The statistical confidence level is below 95%, but the results approach validity. This suggests that further testing is required to confirm the effectiveness of the "yes/no" buttons.

3. The experiment also revealed some negative impacts. Complete registrations experienced a 442% decrease, indicating that the "yes/no" buttons might have discouraged users from completing the registration process.

4. Cost per click and cost per registration saw significant increases of 115% and 1228%, respectively. This suggests that "yes/no" buttons may have attracted more clicks, but these clicks may not have resulted in a higher number of registrations.

5. Specific audience segments, such as 65+ visitors, tablet visitors, and male visitors, saw significant increases in clicks, indicating that the "yes/no" buttons may appeal more to these demographics.

Based on the insights gained, it is highly recommended to conduct further experiments to validate the effectiveness of employing "yes/no" buttons in ad creative. To understand if there are any usability issues or how they can be mitigated, it is essential to investigate the negative impact on complete registrations. Additionally, analyzing the metrics of cost per click and cost per registration will help determine the overall effectiveness and ROI of implementing "yes/no" buttons in future campaigns. Through careful and controlled experiments, the Hoover Institution can obtain more confidence and better understand the use of "yes/no" buttons to enhance ad creativity and prompt desired user actions.

experiments.title: How Adding More Than One Link In Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a second link for the FYE email will achieve a higher click rate because there will be more opportunity to click and go to the survey page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/22/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campagin every year. For this campaign the organization will publish ads, web elements, and email in an effort to support the campaign.
For this test we wanted to see if we could increase traffic to a survey page from their email channel. To do this we tested having one link vs. two link in the email body. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a second link in the FYE email resulted in a 5.2% increase in clicks for all traffic. While this indicates a positive impact on click rates, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

It is also important to note that once a user completes the survey, the user gets redirected to a donation page. While we don't have the data to see if increasing the number of links increased the completion rate of the survey, we can see that people who did receive the treatment had a 11% in giving at a 41% LoC (40 gifts vs 45 gifts).

experiments.title: How Adding An Image To an Email Effects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image for the edu FYE email appeal will achieve a higher click rate because the image will resonate with the user to continue reading the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campaign each year that consists of a full email campaign to their Education housefile.
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding an image to the email copy led to more link clicks to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we learned that adding an image to the email decreases the click rate if you look at the overall impact. To make this test even, we split the housefile into donors, nondonors, and lapsed donors. Each segment did produce a decrease in clicks, however, the treatment version with the image created a 47% lift in unsubscribes.

Looking at transactions from each email, the control also had a significant increase in transactions and revenue.

experiments.title: Does Changing the Position of an Image Affect Clicks in Email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image at the bottom of an email for the FYE EDU appeals will achieve a higher click rate because the image will be reinforcing the copy that was just read.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Earlier in Moody’s FYE campaign we tested adding an image towards the top of an email to see if it could catch peoples attention and make them want to read more of them email. The results showed that it did the opposite and made people click out or unsubscribe.
For this test we wanted to see if placing an image at the bottom of an email copy could reinforce the message and create a higher click rate in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image at the bottom of an email for FYE EDU appeals did not achieve a higher click rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, if you look at revenue you can see a slight increase in the generosity of the donors when the image is present in the email. Both the control and treatment received the same number of donations, however the gift the average gift is up slightly when compared to the control. It is important to note that the donation size is very small, and the control did receive large outlying gifts that had to be taken out to not skew the average gift.

It would be interesting to test this tactic again to get a larger pool of donors to see if this is something that can be validated.

experiments.title: How an autonomy-supportive treatment impacted clicks on a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing more autonomy  for the housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there will be an enhanced level of motivation that follows the increase in the copy’s autonomy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2024 - 07/09/2024
When running a fiscal year end campaign, we were curious if giving the email readers more autonomy in the email would lead to higher motivation to give and increase clicks in the email.
First and foremost, autonomy support begins with an orientation. As much as we might wish to reduce autonomy support to an algorithmic set of tactics (e.g., offering choice, avoiding pressure or guilt), true autonomy support starts with an attitude: we are not persuading donors, but rather inviting them to support a mission we believe they would value, and informing them about the ways they can help.
Having said that, when we start with “make this match-back or premium more autonomy supportive,” we are already a few steps behind. Within the “autonomy paradigm,” one would not even offer a premium or match-back to entice or seduce donors.
Nonetheless, we believe our treatment copy has made the appeals less pressuring or controlling and, in some instances, supportive of autonomy. This is demonstrated by the removal of controlling language such as “I still need your help” and “We need you”, simplifying sentences to enhance readability and clarity, using “your support” and “your contribution” to emphasize the reader’s autonomy, maintaining urgency without using pressuring language, reducing redundant phrases to streamline the appeal, preserving the core message and call to action, replacing the sense of urgency with a focus on making the strongest effort as the campaign comes to a close, and emphasizing the reader’s autonomy and the impact of their contribution.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted to test the impact of introducing more autonomy in emails for a fiscal year end campaign at Care Net showed that the treatment version resulted in a 57.5% decrease in clicks. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.9% so the results are not valid.
One key learning from this experiment is that while the hypothesis that increased autonomy in email copy would lead to higher motivation and increased clicks seemed plausible, the actual results did not support this. It is essential to recognize that autonomy support is more than just the language used in the emails; it is about the overall attitude of inviting donors to participate in a mission they believe in.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to consider the holistic approach to autonomy support rather than just focusing on the language used in the emails. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that experiments are run for a long enough duration to gather sufficient data for meaningful analysis and to have a higher statistical level of confidence before drawing any conclusions.

experiments.title: How zooming in on ad creative impacted cost per registration

rows.name: Hoover Digest Spring 2024 Creative Test Control
Hoover Digest Spring 2024 Creative Test Treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the ad creative to show more of the eBook cover  for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve an increase in the CTR because it engages the viewer with a highly colorful and visual image and provides consistency throughout the offer funnel with the eBook cover.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2024 - 06/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. Currently, the Hoover Institution is running one of its seasonal offers, the Spring Digest. It showed a low CTR. We wanted to test the beginning of the funnel to see how this would impact clicks and, ultimately, registrations. Since the covers for the Digest are visually appealing, we decided to zoom in on the eBook and delete some of the negative space around the eBook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Adjusting the ad creative to show more of the eBook cover resulted in a 7.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 81.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85%. Therefore, the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
3. The Cost per Click experienced a significant 18.3% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, indicating that the treatment had a positive impact on this metric.
4. The Cost per Registration also experienced a significant decrease of 16.2% with a 99.9% level of confidence, revealing that there was an increase in conversions with a lower cost.
In future experimentation, it is important to:
1. Ensure that experiments are run for a longer period to gather more data and increase the statistical validity of the results.
2. Consider other factors that may have influenced the outcome, such as seasonal trends or changes in the target audience.
3. Continue to test different variations of ad creatives to optimize performance, but always prioritize statistical significance in order to make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How aligning a premium offer with seasonality during CYE impacts clicks and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a seasonally-aligned premium offer in the Dear Listener for visitors on FamilyLife’s podcast pages will achieve an increase in clicks to the premium donation page because the seasonally-aligned premium offer will feel more relevant and appealing to the viewer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2023 - 12/01/2023
During their Calendar Year-End (CYE) fundraising season, FamilyLife has used “Dear Listener” elements on their podcast pages to convert their web visitors into donors. Before CYE began, we had running a Dear Listener that promoted a card game premium for married couples. But we wondered if aligning the premium offer with the time of the year would make it more relevant and acquire more donors.
The premium offer that FamilyLife was promoting during CYE was a 52-week devotional book. This book was included as an incentive in their CYE campaign emails and on the campaign’s donation page. We decided to run this offer as our Treatment to see if it would get more users to engage and make a gift to FamilyLife.
Our hypothesis was that the devotional premium would achieve an increase in clicks to the premium donation page because it would feel more relevant to the web visitors; this is because December is a popular season for people to get devotionals that they can begin in the new year.
We ran this as an A/B test in VWO for ~1.5 weeks. The Control Dear Listener promoted the game and pointed to the game’s donation page, and the Treatment Dear Listener promoted the devotional book and pointed to its donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for a week and a half, we found that the seasonally-aligned devotional premium actually to a -31.4% decrease in clicks to the donation page, with an 87% level of confidence. This test didn't meet the 95% level of confidence that's required to consider it a fully valid result, but we turned the test off early due to the poor trending results and the importance of the Calendar Year-End fundraising season.
There are a few different angles that we can test with this idea in the future:
Would we see a different result if we tested a seasonally-aligned offer during a different time of year (ex: Valentine's Day, Easter, etc.)?Would we see a different result if the control and treatment are the same type of premium offer (ex: both are books, or both are games, etc.)?

experiments.title: How using donor autonomy in a CTA impacted clicks on a survey
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving survey takers s a choice rather than a command for housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there is an improved sense of autonomy in the process, giving the potential donors ownership.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/18/2024 - 05/20/2024
The concept behind donor autonomy is that people like to make choices for themselves, rather than being told what to do. When applying this concept to online behavior, we wondered if using language in a CTA that gave the email recipients the chance to indicate their willingness to participate, rather than instructing them with a command would have any influence on the click rate to complete a survey. We split traffic 50/50 between each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that giving survey takers a choice, rather than instructing them with a command, resulted in a 5.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. This suggests that there is a positive impact on engagement when potential donors feel a sense of autonomy and ownership in the process. However, the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, indicating that further testing would be necessary to validate these results.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of language that offer choices to recipients rather than giving commands. In our next test, we will build on the concept of donor autonomy by including the option to say "no" rather than only including one place to click and say "yes" to the request, which may lead to an even greater sense of agency for the email recipient and potentially increased engagement.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomy in a CTA impacted clicks on a survey

rows.name: Click here to begin your survey now
I'm willing to give my feedback

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving survey takers s a choice rather than a command for housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there is an improved sense of autonomy in the process, giving the potential donors ownership.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/18/2024 - 05/20/2024
The concept behind donor autonomy is that people like to make choices for themselves, rather than being told what to do. When applying this concept to online behavior, we wondered if using language in a CTA that gave the email recipients the chance to indicate their willingness to participate, rather than instructing them with a command would have any influence on the click rate to complete a survey. We split traffic 50/50 between each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that giving survey takers a choice, rather than instructing them with a command, resulted in a 5.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. This suggests that there is a positive impact on engagement when potential donors feel a sense of autonomy and ownership in the process. However, the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, indicating that further testing would be necessary to validate these results.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of language that offer choices to recipients rather than giving commands. In our next test, we will build on the concept of donor autonomy by including the option to say "no" rather than only including one place to click and say "yes" to the request, which may lead to an even greater sense of agency for the email recipient and potentially increased engagement.

experiments.title: Does Humanizing Facebook Creative Improve Click-Through-Rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: Utilizing targeted imagery increases engagement and downloads by helping Facebook audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook. It fosters a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/30/2024
This study was conducted on Facebook for WUNC, a public radio station in North Carolina, to optimize click-through rates and enhance overall conversions for a promotional campaign. The experiment aimed to refine the effectiveness of an advertisement for an eBook about outdoor activities in North Carolina. The experimental design compared the performance of an ad featuring the eBook’s cover image against an alternative ad featuring a person engaged in outdoor recreation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that utilizing targeted imagery, specifically featuring a person engaged in outdoor activities, can significantly increase engagement and conversions for promotional campaigns such as eBooks. In this case, the treatment ad featuring a person saw a 12.7% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control ad featuring the eBook cover image, with a high level of statistical confidence at 95.2%.
These results suggest that incorporating images of people enjoying the outdoor activities showcased in the eBook can help the audience visualize themselves benefiting from the content, thus fostering a stronger connection and driving action. In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using targeted imagery that resonates with the target audience to enhance engagement and conversion rates. Additionally, it could be beneficial to further test different types of imagery and messaging to continuously optimize the effectiveness of promotional campaigns.

experiments.title: How reminding lapsed members about the value of the membership card in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the click-through rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if lapsed members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including reminder text about the value of a membership card can significantly increase the click-through rate for lapsed members. The treatment in this experiment saw a 41.9% increase in Clicks for All Traffic, indicating that reminding members of the benefits they receive can be a compelling reason for them to donate again.

Note: While the treatment email had a higher click-through rate, it also had a slightly lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test and refine the messaging around the value proposition of a membership card. This could include highlighting specific benefits, such as exclusive content, special discounts, or access to events. By effectively communicating the value that members receive in exchange for their donation, we can increase the likelihood of them re-engaging with the organization.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to further test different variations of the value proposition messaging to optimize the impact on member reactivation. By continuing to experiment and analyze the results, we can uncover the most effective ways to encourage lapsed members to donate and renew their support for The Heritage Foundation.

experiments.title: How does focusing on an urgent need rather than a premium in the final email of a campaign impact clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of urgent language instead of an incentive for potential donors will achieve an increase in clicks because  the sense of urgency and a deadline will act as a motivating factor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test urgent language in the email vs the incentive of a premium. For the majority of this campaign, we have focused on the incentive of a book premium. However, for the final email our hypothesis was that by the final email if they have not converted to a monthly donor by this point, then the premium is not incentive enough for them to give.  Therefore, the urgent language may be motivating due to the presence of a deadline.
The control included the premium and the treatment included the urgent language.
This test ran as a 50/50 A/B test to The Navigators housefile audience in an effort to activate more recurring donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the urgent language in the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign did not result in an increase in clicks from potential donors. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a drop of 13.3% in clicks at a 81.0% level of confidence. While these results did not validate, they do suggest that the urgent language was not a motivation factor for The Navigators audience to click the link, and that they potentially respond better to the presence of an incentive or premium.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the impact of urgent language and deadlines on donor behavior. It is possible that in this instance, potential donors may have already been motivated by the premium incentive in previous emails and adding urgent language only served to create confusion or negatively impact their decision-making process.
Moving forward, it may be beneficial to continue testing different incentives and messaging strategies to see what resonates best with potential donors. Additionally, ensuring that experiments are properly powered and have a high level of statistical confidence will help to draw more reliable conclusions for future decision making.

experiments.title: How removing images and html styling impacted click through rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that stripping html from an email for the Today in the Word Audience will achieve a higher CTR because it will appeal more personal and less spam like.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could improve the CTR of an email by changing the layout to make it feel like it is a personal email. To do this we stripped all of the HTML, besides the header logo at the top.
In doing this it makes the email seem less like a marketing email and more like a 1 on 1 email.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that stripping HTML from an email for the Today in the Word Audience can significantly increase click-through rates (CTR). The treatment, which made the email feel more personal by removing all HTML except for the header logo, resulted in a 10.9% increase in clicks for all traffic.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with lapsed members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for lapsed members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired action is cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/22/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign.  The email was a personal communication informing lapsed members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask lapsed members to “reactivate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage lapsed members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for lapsed donors resulted in a 28.0% increase in the click-through rate. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.
Note: While the click-through rate increased the conversion rate decreased by 50%. The sample size was not large enough to attain the required level of confidence of 95% or above.
In the future, it is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to obtain more reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to continue testing different variations of the call to action to determine the most effective approach for encouraging lapsed donors to re-engage. By using what was learned from this experiment, we can refine our strategies and optimize our communication to better connect with our audience and achieve our goals.

experiments.title: How leading a membership ask email with the problem for lapsed members affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading an email with the problem will increase click-through rate because it will give the reader more context as to why they should renew their membership with Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran on the first email of the campaign for lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how lapsed members would react to leading the email with a question that poses the problem Heritage is trying to solve rather than leading the email with a direct ask to reactivate their membership. In doing so, we believe users will be more likely to click through to the donation page and reactivate their membership because the leading question allows them to relate to the issue and want to be apart of the change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that leading an email with the problem did not increase click-through rates for lapsed members to reactivate their membership with The Heritage Foundation. The treatment actually resulted in a 19.3% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the accepted threshold of 85%, meaning the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making. Additionally, the treatment group saw a 46.9% decrease in donor conversion with an 85.3% level of confidence.

Based on these findings, it is important to reconsider the hypothesis and approach for future experimentation. It may be necessary to use a different strategy to engage lapsed members and entice them to reactivate their membership. This could include focusing on the benefits and value of being a member, highlighting success stories or achievements of the organization, or providing exclusive content or offers. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that future experiments have a sufficient statistical level of confidence to ensure the validity of the results.

experiments.title: How using an image of the content offer in a housefile email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image to the email for potential downloaders will achieve a higher click-through rate because users will be able to see what they’re requesting before clicking out of the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2024
The Navigators sent a free content offer to their housefile list through email. The content offer was a free 12-month calendar and users had to provide their name, email, and home address on the landing page to receive a physical copy. Following the landing page, they were redirected to an instant donation page where they were asked to make a gift to support the Navigators work.
Within the email, they wanted to test how adding an image of the calendar would affect click-through rate from the email to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image of the calendar to the email did not have a positive impact on the click-through rate to the landing page. In fact, the treatment group showed a 3.8% decrease in clicks compared to the control group with an 80.3% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size and a higher statistical level of confidence to determine the true impact of adding an image to the email. Additionally, other factors such as the content of the email, the subject line, and the timing of the email should be taken into consideration in future experiments. It is also important to continue testing different variables to optimize the email and landing page conversion rates for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How posing a lower, $10 ask for non donors impacts email engagement and giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a specific, lower ask amount for non donors will achieve an increase in click-through rates and ultimately gifts because having a specific, low amount can reduce decision friction and potential anxiety.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2023 - 12/22/2023
During calendar year end, we wanted to activate more nondonors on RTB’s file. In order to lower the threshold for those who have never given, we posed a $10 ask as a test. The control received no specific amount while the treatment received a $10 ask in their email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a specific, lower ask amount of $10 to non-donors can result in an increase in click-through rates compared to not mentioning a specific amount. The treatment version of the email showed a 20.5% increase in clicks for all traffic with a level of confidence of 87%.

Additionally, only two gifts were generated from this email both of which came from the treatment and  both at an amount of $25 which is higher than the initial proposed amount.

It's suggested that this test is ran again with the $10 ask as the starting point of the gift array that the treatment group receives. That way, there's further congruency in the ask and can potentially activate more donors.

experiments.title: How does addressing someone directly and asking them a personal question affect click-through rate for a high urgency email?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors will achieve a higher click-through rate because it keeps the user engaged and helps them feel more relatable to the situation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2023
The Navigators launched their calendar year campaign with an email that started with a direct statement about how Christians face problems more now than ever before. We decided to test how opening an email with a personalized question to draw them in would affect click-through rate to the donation page.
In previous testing, we’ve seen a more personalized tone perform better because it allows the user to relate to the problem at hand and causes them to engage with the email longer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors can have a positive impact on click-through rates. The treatment group, which received the personalized question at the beginning of the email, saw a 20.3% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group with an 87.2% level of confidence.

Because of these results, we believe that opening an email with a personalized question rather than a direct statement will keep users engaged with the email causing them to be more likely to click the link to donate.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of personalized tones and messaging to optimize click-through rates. Additionally, it would be important to analyze other metrics such as conversions and donations to understand the full impact of the personalized approach. By continuously testing and iterating, we can refine our email strategies to effectively engage potential donors and drive meaningful action.

experiments.title: How a more generalized ask in a sticky bar for Giving Tuesday impacted click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing the message in terms of a broader impact will resonate with more people and lead to a higher click-through rate because it is more emotionally engaging.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/28/2023
On Giving Tuesday, Dallas Theological Seminary made a concerted effort to acquire more monthly donors, called Cornerstone Partners. They’ve typically taken an approach that outlines the number of new Cornerstone Partners needed before the midnight deadline.
This year, they wanted to see if a more general ask would lead to higher click-through rates because the goal number could be perceived as overwhelming for someone reading.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment version of the message, which framed the ask in terms of a broader impact, did not lead to a higher click-through rate compared to the control version. In fact, the treatment version saw a significant decrease in clicks by 61.1% with a 93.1% level of confidence.
These results suggest that the hypothesis was incorrect and that framing the message in terms of a broader impact may not resonate with more people or be more emotionally engaging. Therefore, it may not be an effective strategy for increasing click-through rates and acquiring more monthly donors.
In future experimentation, it would be necessary to further test different messaging strategies to determine what resonates most with the target audience. It is important to take into account the specific characteristics and motivations of the donors to understand what messaging would be most compelling to them.
Additionally, it would be advisable to conduct experiments with larger sample sizes and run the experiment for a longer period to increase the statistical validity of the results. This would help ensure that the findings are more reliable and generalizable.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different messaging approaches and continuously refining strategies to optimize donor engagement and acquisition.

experiments.title: How unique value propositions impacted clicks for a direct ask sticky bar
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the giving motivation of equipping the next generation of Christian leaders for website visitors will achieve an increase in click conversions because that value will resonate the strongest with our audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 06/30/2023
DTS is amid their fiscal year end fundraising campaign. With 15 days remaining, we want to add a website sticky bar as an additional revenue opportunity. We’ve built two different sticky bars unique in value proposition. The control uses the value proposition of “reaching lives across the globe with the truth of the Bible” and the control used the value proposition of “equipping the next generation of Christian leaders.” We want to see which value resonates the strongest with our website audience and generates the most clicks to the FYE donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment sticky bar resulted in a 33.3% decrease in clicks with a nearly valid 90.6% level of confidence. The treatment produced 22 clicks compared to 52 to the control.
We noticed the treatment heading in the wrong direction so we ended the experiment after 11 days in order to send 100% of traffic to the control variant.
It seems the control value proposition of "reaching lives across the globe with the truth of the Bible" resonates the most with our online audience. There are other factors to consider with the experiment, however.
The treatment led with "SPECIAL OPPORTUNITY" while the control led with "DEADLINE APPROACHING". Perhaps the control was more effective in this case as it notified users of an important deadline immediately. The treatment lead-in is much more broad which could lead users to overlook it or move casually past it. Also worth noting is the treatment mentioned the Challenge Gift amount of $600,000 while the control only referenced the Challenge Gift without stating the amount.
It's possible that including the $600k amount turned visitors off as they may have felt as if their modest gift would not make a significant difference in the overall goal.
These are all isolated elements that would be worth testing into to get more refined results. Though, in this high urgency scenario, we wanted to act fast and get our best two sticky bars copy versions out to the public.

experiments.title: How a mysterious subject line + conversational email copy impacted email clicks

rows.name: Version A - Subject Line: The taxes you pay

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a mysterious subject line coupled with conversational email copy for email recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because more people will open the email to find out the meaning of the mysterious subject line and the conversational appeal of the email will lead more people to click to find out more about the focus article of the email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2023 - 06/09/2023
IPI is sending a monthly cultivation email to their housefile email list. In order to learn about our audience and what motivates them to open emails and click within emails, we’ve set up a subject line + body copy experiment.
1/4 of our list will receive
control subject line and control body copy
treatment subject line with control body copy
control subject line with treatment body copy
treatment subject line with treatment body copy
The treatment subject line plays into curiosity and intrigue, while the treatment body copy takes an especially conversational approach, sharing the perspective of the sender while also asking for the thoughts and feedback of the reader.
We want to see which combination generates the most clicks within the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Treatment #2 (treatment subject line + control email copy) produced a nearly valid (89.9% level of confidence) 11.9% increase in email clicks. Treatments #1 (control SL and treatment email copy) and #3 (treatment SL and control email copy) produced negative results with invalid level of confidences.

Thus, treatment #2 was the big winner here. Our audience has responded well to mystery subject lines in the past, and we now have more evidence that this approach to subject line framing is effective. These type of subject lines creative intrigue and curiosity, prompting recipients to open the email to find out what's inside. The control subject line did not have the same curiosity, as it exposed the topic up front.

When we look specifically at open rates, treatment #2 produced a 49.3% increase in open rate with a 100% level of confidence; treatment #3 produced a 24.5% increase in open rate with a 100% level of confidence.

Ultimately, we should lead many more cultivation emails using mysterious subject lines.

As for the body copy element, perhaps the conversational approach consisted of too much fluff, as the control – less conversational – variant was a bit more to the point and brief, and resulted in higher click rates. We may want to be more considerate of our recipients' time and focus more on clarity and being direct when sending future cultivation emails.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign for Grace Gone Wild impacted conversion on Facebook

rows.name: PTV DA Grace Gone Wild traditional
PTV DA Grace Gone Wild radical

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by promoting the book with an exciting creative, that matches the cover for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher conversion of donors because there is excitement around the topic and interest.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2023 - 06/22/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
Grace Gone Wild is a book published by PTV. In the past, this book has not be very successful. Therefore in an effort to increase conversions we decided to test a radical approach to the Facebook creative to see if we could increase conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment was conducted to test whether promoting the book "Grace Gone Wild" with an exciting creative that matches the cover on Facebook would increase conversions.
The results showed that the treatment had an 11.7% decrease in clicks for all traffic, indicating that the control version had a higher conversion rate. However, since the statistical level of confidence was just below 95%, further testing would be necessary to validate the results fully.
Another significant observation was that the cost per click increased by 67% with a 100% level of confidence. This information could be beneficial in future experimentation.
The key learning from this experiment is that an exciting creative alone may not necessarily increase conversions, as evidenced by the decrease in clicks for all traffic in the treatment. The language used in the promotional campaign may also be a factor worthy of testing in future experiments.
Based on these results, we should continue to conduct further experimentation to explore the most effective messaging and creative strategies for promoting the book, "Grace Gone Wild."

experiments.title: Which value prop is more affective in a sponsorship ask for PFI–religion or justice?
experiments.researchQuestion: An appeal to someone’s sense of justice will work better at converting PFI’s audience into sponsors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2020 - 12/02/2020
On Giving Tuesday, PFI was going to send out a sponsorship ask to their general donors. Since they are a religious organization that also deals with the justice system, we wanted to test whether an appeal based on religious reasons or on the basis of justice was more motivating.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not have enough results to validate, and need to be careful in using such a small scaled response as proof that the religious element is more motivating. We will continue to test as their file grows.

experiments.title: How a direct ask tied to a wider impact affected clickthrough rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a gift with far-reaching impact for a prospecting audience will achieve an increase in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2022 - 12/20/2022
As part of a calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, Illinois Policy Institute focused on raising funds for a new documentary film they are producing and releasing in 2023. Since this is a new project, we were curious to learn what would motivate donors to give their support. We wanted to test a narrow, specific ask – making a gift of $25 to cover the cost of tickets for people to view the documentary versus making a gift of $25 to help fund the cost of promoting the documentary on social media platforms. We split the audience 50/50 between the control and treatment emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found after sending the email was a 5.9% increase in click-through rates on the treatment email with 84.8% level of confidence so the results of the test are not valid. We believe the lift in click-through rate could be due to the fact that the control language suggests their gift will make an impact locally in their own state, but the treatment language expands the scope of the project to reach a national audience. This could be appealing to potential donors and could have motivated additional clicks. But since the confidence level is only 84.8%, we will have to continue testing the narrow ask vs. the wide ask to understand how the prospecting audience will respond to a tangible ask like a ticket for an audience member to view the documentary compared to a less tangible ask such as general promotion of the documentary.

When reviewing donor conversion rate results among the two variants, the treatment produced 14 donations to the control's 16 donations. The treatment saw a 12.9% decrease in donations at just a 29.4% level of confidence. Again, we will have to continue testing this approach (narrow ask vs. wide ask) to pick up on clicks and donations trends, and to reach a valid conclusion.

experiments.title: How updating the call-to-action button on a Facebook ad impacts click-throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the call to action button for potential course takes will achieve a change in course sign up rates because the button relates to the action we are asking them to take. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2023 - 03/03/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For the launch of a new course led by Dan Burke, the founder of Avila, we wanted to test whether a change in the call-to-action on a Facebook ad would make an impact on click-throughs.
Our standard control for most of Avila’s ads has been to use a ‘learn more’ call to action; in this experiment, since we were asking people to sign up to take the course, we tested learn more versus a ‘sign up’ call to action button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the call-to-action button from 'Learn More' to 'Sign Up' did not lead to an increase in click-throughs for Avila Foundation's new course. The treatment resulted in a 6.7% decrease in clicks, which was statistically significant with a 94.9% level of confidence.
However, when viewing this experiment through the lens of how many people actually signed up for the course, the results are different. While there was a higher number of clicks on the 'learn more' control version, it actually had a 6.3% less sign-up rate once those people got to the landing page. Thus, we can see a correlation between asking people to sign up as the call-to-action, and them actually going through the process. While this only had a 61% level of confidence, it does show a directional lift in response.
Continued testing for this kind of experiment is needed. Future variations could include making the copy of the ad itself correlate more to the call to action, and/or testing within the ad headlines as well so they correlate to the call-to-action button.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by including a dynamic donor countdown to a banner ad affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic donor countdown to the emergency banner for potential donors will achieve increased clicks to the donation page because people respond to an urgent message.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2021 - 05/08/2022
If we added a dynamic donor countdown to the emergency banner, donor conversion will increase because donors respond to urgent messaging. We used an emergency alert banner vs. an emergency banner with a countdown as our variable treatment.
Note – Dynamically counts down from 25. After a gift, the number will decrease to confirm with donors that the counter is valid.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped due to lack of significant results.
• Unique banner click rate was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Donor conversion was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Revenue per visitor was lower for the variant (-5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Recommendation is not to add the dynamic counter to the emergency banner.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity with more descriptive menu language affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using more descriptive language in a dropdown menu will achieve a greater click-through rate by providing a more personal motive to click.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2022 - 10/05/2022
If we change the language for “Join Team Tomorrow” in the navigation dropdown, there will be a greater click-through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• Test stopped after several weeks due to a lack of signifiant results
• No significant difference in the click-through rate for any of the three treatments. Directionally, "Find a Cause" had a higher CTR (31% lift 85% confidence level - possible retest in the future).
• The overall test did not generate sufficient clicks. The next test iteration will individually list the four focus areas and have direct links to their corresponding donation forms.
• The treatment version using language like "Find a Cause" was more successful in motivating users to click, suggesting that works better than a generic request like "Join Team Tomorrow".
• The results of this experiment show that changing the language for "Join Team Tomorrow" on the website navigation dropdown resulted in a 30.9% increase in clicks for all visitors with 85.1% statistical confidence.

experiments.title: How placement of a premium link impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by moving the placement of the link higher in the email copy for email subscribers will achieve an increase in conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that generates a majority of its revenue through monthly premium offers. Throughout the month, Pathway to Victory will email its file 4-5 times a month about the premium featured.
For the first email about the November 2022 premium, we decided to look at the placement of the link to get the premium. This particular premium is one that PTV has featured before and has seen great success. The success of this offer presented us with the opportunity to test having the link to the premium higher in the email copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did see an increase in email clicks, 16% with a 92.5% level of confidence, we did not see a significant increase in donations or revenue.

In fact we saw a very slight decrease of donation conversions by 2% with a 6% level of confidence.

 

So while we did see an increase in email engagement this did not translate to an increase in donations or revenue.

 

Further testing ideas for this offer via email include, specific dollar amount ask, other placement tests and images of the premium in the email copy.

experiments.title: How adding specifics to a subject line impacted open rates for Pathway to Victory

rows.name: Join me for the Bible Prophecy Tour of Israel
Join me for the experience of a lifetime

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being specific in the subject line for the email file will achieve a change in response rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2022 - 07/19/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. One of the offerings they have is a Tour of Israel, led by Senior Pastor Dr. Robert Jeffress and his wife.
As part of the ongoing promotion for this tour, we are testing different subject lines to see which one gets a better response rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The difference in subject lines--one referencing the Tour of Israel and the other one being more vague but intriguing--had a negligible impact on open rates and click-through rates. With only a 0.79% increase in open rates, and a decrease of 4.9% in the experiment version in click through rate, this experiment indicates that further testing and finessing of the subject line is called for.

experiments.title: How previewing the survey affects click-through-rates on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that previewing a survey for FB users will achieve higher click-through rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2020 - 07/09/2022
