In an effort to improve click-through-rates for a FB ad pointing people to a survey on the Border Crisis, we wanted to test if a radical redesign centered on previewing the survey, rather than highlighting the problem, would inspire more viewers to click. For this experiment, we mocked up a survey question on the ad image so that the viewer would be prompted to click to select their answer
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 25.6% lift in clicks with an 84% level of confidence. These results lead us to believe that by previewing the survey and thus focusing on the solution in the form of an action, rather than the problem, more viewers were inspired to act.

While these results are promising, because this experiment altered several elements of the ad: previewing the survey in the ad image + shortening the body text to focus on the desired action, we can not say with certainty which change had more of an impact on this lift.

Our hunch is that the image, being more visible when people scroll their feeds, played more of a role ... but to further validate these results, we will next want to test a new image using the same body copy as the winning ad above.

experiments.title: How tightening copy and replacing a button with a hyperlink affected click rates on an auto-responder
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that tightening the copy and replacing a button with a hyperlink for email recipients will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/15/2022
The Gospel Coalition developed a content offer which provided a free e-book download when people signed up. To further cultivate this audience and provide even greater value to their experience, a discussion guide was sent the next day to anyone who downloaded the ebook. We wanted to know how different tones and link presentations might affect click rates and developed a treatment which would be run through an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced a 9.2% directional lift in click rates with an 87.2% level of confidence indicating that the shorter, more direct copy of our treatment (compared to the more conversational, interrogative copy of the control) did have a measurable positive effect on click rates.
More subtly, the treatment copy was structured to provide the "why" (value proposition) of downloading the question guide at the beginning of the email, directly after the salutation. This is based on the theory of influence that states that providing a clear 'why' within a request is more likely to trigger a response of compliance—in this case by clicking to download the question guide "to help you get the most from this insightful resource."
This value proposition is later reinforced near the CTA (call to action) by illustrating that the question guide will a) help readers apply the text's lessons to their own life and b) provide a framework for discussing the text with others.
The treatment also hypothesized that a simple hyperlink may also help improve click rates over a button which, while very visible, may create additional friction at the CTA.
These results show that providing a "why" and streamlining the language within an email promotion can trigger a more favorable response from recipients by creating a slippery slide which carries them effortlessly from salutation to CTA while providing enough benefit for recipients to click through.
In the future, we will test these elements individually to help isolate the variable that produced the lift.

experiments.title: How removing the organization from a sender name affected click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the organization from the sender name for email recipients will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2022
A new president was announced within the organization, meaning their email list would be receiving emails from a sender name they may not recognize. To mitigate confusion and prevent disengagement, unsubscribes, and SPAM complaints, the organization name was appended to the sender name on emails sent to their list. We wanted to know how this was affecting click rates and decided to test if we would see a lift in click rates by once again removing the organization from the sender name. For this experiment, we ran an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced a 101.5% lift in click rates, with an 84.7% level of confidence. Despite being unable to validate this lift, the results provide a strong indication that there may be a link between sender name and click rates. This is especially interesting because the sender name is most often thought to affect open rates, but its impact on click rates is less commonly documented.
These results reinforce the knowledge that the more personal and human your outreach (especially via email), the better chance you have of connecting with donors and inspiring them to act. Whenever possible, organizations should seek to humanize their outreach and appeals—this includes ensuring that emails are sent from real people within an organization and should appear in an inbox as much like a personal email as possible. The results also show how even a small change made to one part of a campaign can have a significant affect on downstream performance.

experiments.title: How removing images from a premium-based offer impacts click-throughs and donor conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing copy for a digital audience will achieve higher click-throughs and donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2022 - 03/16/2022
Encouragement from a Place called Heaven was a premium-based offer that Pathway to Victory used for a very select audience in March. For the digital appeal, we tested removing the images of the premium offer, as well as humanizing the copy, all with the war in Ukraine raging in the background. We also included a quote from Dr. Jeffress’ Sunday sermon in the treatment version.
experiments.keyLearnings: By removing the images and humanizing the copy, as well as referencing the upheaval caused by the war in Ukraine, the treatment version had a 38% increase in click-throughs. Donation conversion rates on both versions were the same, due to the smaller sample size for this appeal.

experiments.title: How a question versus a problem at the beginning of an email affects click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Introducing a problem at the beginning of the email will increase the readers interest in the solution
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/18/2021
KCBI was launching a new offering of a Verse of the Day email. This is an important offering and very inline with the organizations mission. In order to discern what motivates their readers to read on and to engage with the email we tested a beginning to the email that was a questions “What do you seriously need each morning?” against starting the email with the problem of mornings and finding time to spend with God, then offering the solution to making their mornings better. This was a 50/50 split test with the same subject line “What you need every day.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The question at the beginning of an email increased the click rate by almost 12%. Our hypothesis is that the question started people on the path of thinking what is it that they need in the morning and then the solution was offered. Beginning with the problem, told them that they struggled to get what they need, rather than asking what they needed and engaging them in the options. The question also matched the subject line of "What you need every day" and so there was a smooth transition from the envelop of the email to the beginning of the conversation. The reader was more prepared for what came next. We also think there's something to an upbeat tone when talking to KCBI's audience.

experiments.title: How a simple text email affects click rates on a content offer email
experiments.researchQuestion: Design elements will reduce open rates and click rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/10/2021
BRI typically sends emails that have some design elements. So we wanted to test a completely simple text template to make sure that the the design elements weren’t affecting open rates or click through rates. With a recent housefile email offer, we tested the control template against a simple text email.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that the design elements were not harming the conversion rates at any level of statistical validity. The click rates were lower for the simple text by 10%, and the level of validity shows a directional lift. However, the open rate was higher by 1.2% for the simple text, but a level of confidence of 59%, which means that life is entirely inconclusive. Lastly, the donation conversion rate on the simple text was 194.5% higher than the designed email, with a 95% level of confidence. And yet, we only had 34% of the donations needed to really find this test truly conclusive. All in all, this test is a mixed bag and shows that we need to continue to test as the file grows, to see if there are any conclusive effects from this style of email.

experiments.title: How email copy length affected click-through-rate on an appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening copy for housefile emails will achieve improved click-through-rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
For a membership renewal campaign, we wanted to look at ways to optimize the email copy for The Heritage Foundation appeals. The test is intended to determine whether shorter or lengthier email copy results in higher click-through-rates. The version with the higher click-through-rate sends more users to the landing page linked in the email.
The control email consists of seven short paragraphs while the treatment email consists of five short paragraphs. The sender and subject line are identical for each email, and the “Renew” and “Sign up to renew” links are included in each email.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the experiment resulted in a 7.2% increase in clicks, the level of confidence was only 80%. The treatment (shorter copy) did ultimately generate more clicks than the control (lengthier copy). To further increase the level of confidence, it may be wise to conduct another test in which the shorter copy is paired with a personalized salutation.

experiments.title: How the use of the word “you” affects click through rate of an eBook offer on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: The use of the word “you” will increase conversion because it is addressing and engaging the reader specifically.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/23/2021
As we were launching a new eBook offer for Prison Fellowship International, the original language for the funnel copy included a lot of reader centered language focused on the outcome for the reader and the purpose for the reader, which often included the use of the word “you.” As that was not typically the communication style for PFI, they preferred to use more general language about how the eBook benefits people in general. To ensure that this did not affect the outcome, we wanted to test this in the Facebook marketplace.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that the click through rate was 8% lower on the reader centered language. And that was a key metric for this funnel, because the cost per click was high, making the funnel more difficult to scale. Also, the ultimate goal was to acquire new donors. And the donor conversion for the more general language brought in two times more donors. For these two key reasons, we discontinued the test before reaching validation to place spend on the more efficient funnel.

experiments.title: How paragraph formatting in an email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will utilizing shorter paragraphs to increase readability affect clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2020 - 11/03/2020
In this experiment, the initial email copy draft utilized long-form paragraphs. And many copy drafts arrive in this type of format before they get put on a landing page or in an email. I normally edit them to have more breaks and spacing in an effort to improve readability. But I wanted to see if editing the formatting to utilize shorter paragraphs actually made a difference.
My hypothesis was that this type of formatting would improve the clarity and understanding of the offer – and ultimately lead to more clicks and conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Breaking the copy up into smaller paragraphs led to a 19.7% increase in clicks at a 94.4% level of confidence. This is high enough for me to be confident in making these types of copy edits for emails moving forward.

The operating hypothesis here was that smaller chunks of copy can actually make it easier to read – which enhances the clarity of what’s being communicated in the email. And if it’s clearer what we’re asking people to do, we’ll have more click throughs – and ideally more conversions – as a result.

More testing will be needed to verify the downstream impact on conversion, as well as how this affects clarity on a landing page.

experiments.title: How a grid layout affects clickthrough on a membership benefits page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a grid layout lead to more clicks into the checkout process?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2019 - 01/26/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if a grid style layout would be effective at showing visitors the various benefits of our 3 membership tiers, and thus lead to more clickthroughs to the checkout process.
The control used a bulleted list to describe the benefits of each tier. The treatment used a large grid with Xs and checkmarks to visually show what was included with each tier.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we didn’t reach a 95% level of confidence, the experiment had been running for a full month and has been leaning towards validity. The consequences of being wrong in this case a fairly low. But we will conduct follow-up testing to see if we can confidently improve clicks and conversions in the membership sign-up process.
The results indicate that this more visual approach is more effective at demonstrating the member benefits and showing the value of membership. It’s possible that more info is need on the page to clarify what some of the benefits are. And we also need to ensure in future testing that we can track all the way through to a purchase.
It also looks like a new element was added into the control variant during the test. And it’s likely that this skewed the final result.

experiments.title: How removing the email template impacts clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a plain text email generate more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/03/2018
During Hurricane Florence, BGEA experimented with sending a plain text style email against their standard marketing email template to see if it generated more clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In reviewing the two creatives that were involved in this experiment, we initially thought that the marketing email generated more clicks, but after further slicing the web analytics details, we identified that the marketing template had an additional clickable link to an article (which was hyperlinked on the header image) that the plain text version did not include.
Overall clicks were higher on the marketing template, but when you isolate the click traffic on the PRIMARY objective (which is calling the recipient to give a gift), we actually saw that the treatment generated 1,025 clicks against the control’s 526 clicks on the “Give Now” option (which represents a 94% increase in click activity).
Subsequently, the clickthrough traffic on that primary call to action (Give Now) saw a higher conversion rate (16.6%), as well as a 53.1% higher overall revenue than the control.
Further experimentation is required to validate this hypothesis, but initial results look promising.

experiments.title: How an animated video ad image increased ad clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using an animated video ad from Facebook increase ad clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2019 - 10/14/2019
As a part of optimizing The Fund for American Studies’ eBook offer, we wanted to increase ad clickthrough rate to improve traffic through the funnel. To experiment with boosting ad clickthrough, we decided to
experiments.keyLearnings:
Directionally, we observed an increase of ad clickthrough rate by +7.7%, however we did not validate the experiment (achieving only an 81% level of confidence).
We turned off this experiment because after running for two weeks, we achieved the (directional) lift in ad clickthrough rate and traffic, however the email registration rate decreased (-28.5%, LoC: 100%), as did the donor conversion rate (-41.6%, LoC: 65.7%).
Our hypothesis is that although the video ad produced more traffic, with the decrease in email signups and donations, it was likely erroneous click activity after watching (or attempting to rewatch) the ad.
This approach has worked well for some offers and causes, and we should consider retesting this tactic at some point down the road.

experiments.title: How leading with a question impact email click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does leading an email with a question increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2019 - 07/23/2019
Alliance Defending Freedom was sending out one of their weekly cultivation emails as part of a longitudinal experiment to improve email engagement. In the previous week, we had observed a surprising result in an experiment. When starting the email with a personal anecdote, we saw a decreased level of engagement. We had a hypothesis that this may be due to the way the two emails differed in their introductions. The control started with the question to the reader while the treatment started with the story.
Since there were several other factors that could have led to the observed result, we decided to isolate the question factor and run another A/B split. The only difference in the control and treatments email would then be whether they begin with a question or a statement.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach a statistical level of confidence, we were quickly approaching a validation with a 90% level of confidence. This would suggest that leading with a question is a better way to capture visitor attention that the standard introduction. However, we would need to test this further to validate the concept.

experiments.title: How email design affects click-through in a digest or newsletter email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will additional design elements in a digest email lead to greater click-through?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2019 - 03/18/2019
Email appeal experiments had given us a predisposition to think that email with button, images, and minor design elements are ineffective. But we had really only discovered that to be true in the specific context of a donation appeal.
In this experiment, we wondered if basic design elements could actually lead to greater click-throughs. The control was a plain-text style email in the format we had tested into, and the treatment added section images and clickable html buttons.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this didn’t quite reach our desired level of confidence of 95%, it was very close to validating a 32% increase in clicks. With this type of a result, we’re inclined to test it again to get greater confidence in the learning. There’s the possibility of a novelty effect – it improved clicks simply because it is different, and not necessarily because it is better long term.

experiments.title: How a video ad affects the cost of traffic acquisition through Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a video ad drive more traffic to the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2017 - 03/07/2017
NextAfter runs a series of Facebook ads that promotes their research to a house file look-alike audience. The intent is to get people to self identify that they are interested in nonprofit content, before they would be targeted with an acquisition ad.
We thought that using a video advertisement could capture the attention of more people and generate more clicks. So we developed 3 ads.
The control was an illustration like what we have used in the past. Treatment 1 was used a still image of the experiment break-down video. And Treatment 2 was a 15 second video teasing the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The still image of the video started to approach validity with a 36.3% increase in clicks, but the video itself made virtually no difference in click rate what-so-ever.
The most significant metric in this test to us was the Cost-Per-Click (CPC). Regardless of what the click rate is, we want to get the most traffic for the lowest spend possible.
Oddly enough, treatment 1 had a 40% higher CPC than the control even though it had a higher click rate. Treatment 2, the video ad, had a 65% higher CPC than the control even though the click rate was essentially the same.
This test shows us that it is more cost effective for us to use the illustrated ads than it is to use this type of video to drive new traffic to our website.

experiments.title: How removing details in an email affects traffic to the landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the date and time from the email drive more traffic to the registration page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2017 - 02/15/2017
When promoting a webinar, we typically try to include the date and time in the emails that we send in order to provide as much clarity as possible to our recipients. But we wondered if putting the date and time in the email was actually disqualifying some people right away because they already knew they would be busy. We thought these elements were creating unnecessary friction at this stage in the registration process.
Our hypothesis was that by removing the date and time from the email, we could drive more click-throughs to the registration page. And once someone was on the landing page, we displayed an exit-intent pop-up offering to send a recording even if they couldn’t make the time work with their schedule.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a lift of 15.3% in click-through rate. Unfortunately, our experiment did not quite reach our desired level of confidence of 95%. It came just short at 92.4%.
After analyzing conversion rates, we saw that there was no statistically significant difference between the control and treatment when it came to how many people actually registered.

experiments.title: How the use of video in a Facebook Ad affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook Ad variation will get more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2016 - 08/12/2016
At NextAfter, we’re trying to distribute our research blog to the wider nonprofit audience in an effort to educate the marketplace about the effect that optimization can have on fundraising.
As part of this effort, we were running a series of Facebook Ads to our housefile lookalike audience. The initial click rates were lower than desired, so we wanted to test a new treatment.
Our hypothesis was that using a video advertisement could generate a higher click rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The video Facebook Ad received a 71.5% lift in clicks. The reasons for this increase could be:
The movement and action of the video was more effective at catching the eye of Facebook users scrolling through their newsfeed, causing them to pay more attention to the content of the ad. Or…
The video demonstrated and reinforced the value proposition of the blog in a way that helped increase clarity and appeal.
We will continue to test video in Facebook Ads to try and determine exactly what affect it has on Facebook Ad audiences.

experiments.title: How adding a brand name in the sender line affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a brand name to the sender line increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2016 - 03/07/2016
NextAfter sent an email promoting their new eBook “Turning Likes Into Donors” to a rented email list of nonprofit development professionals. The email was sent from Tim Kachuriak, and meant to look like a personal email. However, we wondered — if one of the recipients of the email wasn’t familiar with Tim, would they be likely to open and click the email? We developed a test that added “NextAfter” to the sender line to give the recipient better clarity in regards to the sender and launched an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The added brand name reduced clickthrough rate by 20.7%. This shows that the presence of a brand name actually decreases the likelihood that the user will engage. This may be because the brand is as unknown as the name. This experiment also may suggest that people are more willing to engage with a name they don’t know, but not if it’s evident that they are being marketed to.

experiments.title: How a single word can affect click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the word “Free” affect clickthrough?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/21/2013 - 10/28/2013
The TSHA created a new online resource center as a way to acquire email addresses. In order to attract visitors to the page, they placed banners throughout their site. The goal of this test was to identify which message produced the most traffic to the resource center landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a 29% lift in the number of clicks, it appears that the word “free” is indeed a powerful word. The cost in the mind of the visitor that is associated with clicking on the banner is diminished when the perceived value outweighs the cost. By communicating in the ad that access to the resource center is free we were able to reduce the cost burden and thereby increase the number of visitors to the resource center landing page.  While one test is not enough to speak definitively about the subject, the concept of “free” on influencing visitor behavior should not be underestimated.

experiments.title: How directly connecting the value proposition to the ask impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a stronger value proposition increase clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2017 - 05/23/2017
As Mother’s Day was approaching, Live Action sent a fundraising email asking people to give a gift in honor of a mom in their life. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase clicks from the email to the donation page by better connecting the Mother’s Day holiday to the value proposition of Live Action. We developed a treatment with this strengthened value proposition and tested it against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t validate, but the data we did collect shows that by strengthening the value proposition of the email, we were able to increase clicks by 22%. Here is a great takeaway from this experiment: When we present an ask, more people are willing to click through (and hopefully give) when we can connect the ask to a strong reason to give. If we don’t do this, the ask sounds and feels transactional. People give not just to give, but to have an even greater impact. A strong value proposition can clearly communicate this and result in more people being motivated to give.

experiments.title: How mentioning fundraising efforts in an email affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Does telling the reader exactly what they’re about to click on make an impact on clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2017 - 07/22/2017
The Leadership Institute ran a matching grant campaign to raise funds for field representatives to send to college campuses in the fall semester. They wanted to test their email copy to see if going into detail about the grant would inspire more people to give. They tested copy that did not mention the grant against copy that did mention the grant.
experiments.keyLearnings: The email copy that did not mention the matching grant generated a 6.8% click rate, while the email copy that did mention the matching grant only generated a 2.9% click rate. The level of confidence is 87.3%, so the experiment can’t be validated — although it’s likely that with more samples, the test could validate in favor of the copy that did not mention the matching grant.

experiments.title: How a new value proposition focus in a sales email affects mystery donor audit applications

rows.name: What are your 2019 online fundraising plans?
Here’s a great way to increase giving for 2019 and beyond

experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing the value proposition on the lack of time to plan and research lead to greater applications?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2018 - 12/20/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if a different value proposition approach to a mystery donor audit sales email would lead to greater applications. The control we had tested into already focused on how expert opinions are really just that : opinions. If your fundraising isn’t based on research, data, and testing, then you’re just relying on intuition.
The treatment focused on the lack of time to the necessary research to create a plan for 2019. We could step in with this mystery donor audit and do the leg work for you to help you create a strategy for your next year of fundraising. Both the subject line and body copy of the email were included in the treatment variables.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version saw a 70% decrease in clicks through to the landing page. While we didn’t reach full statistical confidence, we were approaching confidence at 89.5%. While we didn’t have enough applications to validate off of conversions (3 for the control and 1 for the treatment), this decrease in clicks is a strong indicator that focusing on time constraints is not the right value proposition focus.

experiments.title: How social pressure in the copy affects change in conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: Does pressuring rather than encouraging someone to improve their knowledge cause a change in conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2018 - 04/25/2018
This experiment was tested on Hillsdale College’s online course page. They wanted to know if copy that pressured the reader to engage would perform better than the more encouraging treatment, which asked the reader to enhance what they already know. The control makes the reader feel like they need to be better or face ridicule, while the treatment encourages the reader. The hypothesis was that the more direct language would pressure the reader to make the conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The copy that applied more pressure to the reader saw a .52% click rate while the more encouraging copy saw a .57% click rate. Since there was no statistically significant difference in results, we can conclude that the more direct copy had no change in conversion results.

experiments.title: How communicating a smaller amount to goal impacts click rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Which amount would increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2018 - 10/03/2018
During Boys Town September High Urgency campaign focused on kindness/bullying, we communicated to people that we had a goal to distribute 400 Praise Boxes by the end of the campaign. In one of the final emails to people, we gave them an update on the progress of the campaign and how many Praise Boxes we still needed to reach our goal. We hypothesized that a smaller number might increase motivation and get more people to click on the link to help us reach our goal. We tested this against a larger number to the 400 goal.
experiments.keyLearnings: The smaller goal amount increase conversion by 18%. This experiment didn’t validate, but it was directional at 85.9%. The larger amount to goal communicated to people that we really did need their help in these final days. More people were interested in seeing how they could help. Scarcity isn’t always a more motivating factor as we saw with this experiment.

experiments.title: The impact of adding a second CTA as a P.S. in email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second signup CTA as a P.S. lead to a higher click-through rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/25/2018
We decided to market our free online course to subscribers who had not yet enrolled. In crafting our emails, we wanted to know if adding a second signup CTA as a P.S. would lead to more people clicking through to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While our results were just below a 95% level of confidence, we saw a clear directional decrease in conversion with the addition of the P.S. Our hypothesis is that the P.S. did not feel natural to the flow and thus made the email seem less personal to the recipients.

experiments.title: How a question in a banner ad affects clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking a question in a banner ad increase clicks to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/12/2018
In this experiment, we were advertising applications for the Nonprofit Innovation & Optimization Awards using a sticky bar on the NIO Summit site. The control used pretty straightforward copy saying you could be recognized for innovative fundraising. The treatment asked a question that we thought might be disarming and more inviting.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t reach a level of confidence of 95% like we would have liked, but it starts to indicated that the control was the better performer. This could be a result of a loss of clarity in the treatment. By asking a question, the specifics of the awards got moved to smaller text that may have gone unnoticed – leading to less clicks.

experiments.title: How a direct call to action influences clicks on a sticky-bar
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call-to-action drive more traffic to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/11/2018
In this experiment, we were launching a t-shirt design contest for the 2018 NIO Summit. We placed a sticky bar on the homepage of www.niosummit.com to let visitors know about the contest. We tested two different calls to actions: Enter Your Design vs Learn More
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Learn More” call-to-action drove 53.4% more traffic to the landing page, although the experiment did not reach the 95% level of confidence we would have liked. Nevertheless, this is a strong indicator that a softer call-to-action for general traffic on the homepage is more effective at increasing clicks.

experiments.title: Do images in an email next to blog links increase engagement?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing images next to blog links in an email increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2018 - 05/01/2018
In the effort to continue to optimize the Weekend Update email, we wanted to see if further reducing the images in an email would impact the open and click rates of the send. Based on prior testing, we knew that reducing the image to text ratio of a message increased open rates significantly.
experiments.keyLearnings: Reducing the images in the weekend update to simply a small signature logo increased clicks by 10.4% and opens by 3.79%. Neither reached 95% significance, but both are close enough for a directional learning.

experiments.title: How a direct ask in an exit intent affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct ask increase traffic or donor conversion rate for an exit intent banner?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2017 - 06/01/2017
Toward the end of a matching gift campaign, Buckner International wanted to test out an exit intent on their main website promoting the challenge. Since this was the first time this approach had been used, we wanted to find out which approach (a “learn more” or a direct ask) would have the greatest impact on a visitor’s likelihood to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the direct ask approach reduced to the number of visitors to the campaign page by 50%. While it did not reach the 95% level of confidence we would strive for, it was quickly approaching validity before the campaign ended. However, the goal was not necessarily to get clicks, it was to get donations.
When we looked at the number of actual donations, there was not a statistically significant difference between the two treatments. We were able to drive more traffic to the page but this did not result in more total donations. For future tests such as this, we will need to go with a more radically different treatments to achieve validity.

experiments.title: How clarifying the call-to-action for mobile visitors affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Does including the call-to-action on the homepage banner for mobile devices increase the number of people that explore the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2016 - 04/20/2016
Alliance Defending Freedom has a unique homepage banner that allows for multiple stories, cases, and appeals to be featured. The banner shows the featured image and includes the headline on top of it. When a user hovers over the image, more details of the article appears including the category and a call to action such as “Learn More” or “Double Your Impact.”
While discussing ways to increase the engagement of the visitors to the site, it was realized that mobile users would never see the call-to-action. It is only visible on hover which is a function not available on phones and tablets.  We decided to run a test targeting mobile devices to see if moving the call-to-action below the headline on the image would encourage more users to explore the website.
experiments.keyLearnings: By moving the call-to-action on top of the banner; we were able to increase the number of people exploring the website by 11.5%.  Our hypothesis is that without the call-to-action, the visitors were unclear on what they should do next. It made it more obvious that the banners should be clicked on and it clarified what their next steps would be on the subsequent page.

experiments.title: How the page layout impacts visitor engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a sidebar impact visitor engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/30/2017 - 02/21/2017
With the ADF blog, we wanted to find new ways to increase overall visitor engagement. Engagement is defined as anything from donations and email acquisition to visitors exploring the site. One way that the blog increased visitors exploring the site was by listing recent articles at the bottom of each post. We had the hypothesis that this may not be seen by a significant volume of visitors if they didn’t finish each post. To overcome this, we decided to test out “sticky” sidebar that would remain on the right side of the page for desktop visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 3 weeks, the sidebar did not produce any significant impact to either emails acquired or visitors exploring the website. The trend seemed to indicate that it may be negatively impacting these metrics so we decided to shut off this experiment in leu of others that we felt may be more impactful.

experiments.title: How supporting copy length on a Facebook ad affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the length of the supporting ad copy increase clickthrough rate on a facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2017 - 12/12/2017
Oral arguments for Jack Phillips–a client of Alliance Defending Freedom–began on December 5th. As the news covered this national story, Alliance Defending Freedom decided to launch a Facebook ad campaign urging people to sign a letter of support for Jack. Since they knew some people were unfamiliar with Jack, they wondered: Will increasing the length of supporting ad copy on a facebook ad increase clickthrough rate? To test this, they created a longer treatment and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Alliance Defending Freedom that there was no statistical difference in email acquisition between the control and treatment ad. As we look at this test, it’s important to consider the timing of this campaign. Due to the relevant and captivating nature of this material, this campaign experienced a well above-average clickthrough and email acquisition rate. It’s likely that this played a factor in the A/B test (i.e. people were already pretty motivated to clickthrough, so there was less room for incremental optimization).

experiments.title: How acknowledging listeners impacts engagement with our web asset during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading with a thank-you to Radio Player listeners will increase engagement and downstream donor metrics because it acknowledges their presence, deepens their connection to Moody Radio, and invites generosity at the moment they’re already engaged.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2026 - 03/09/2026
To increase engagement on Moody Radio’s player, we tested a more direct, listener-focused headline. We believed that showing gratitude upfront would build connection and boost engagement and giving.
Thanking listeners upfront recognizes their presence, reinforces their role, and prompts generosity. This approach meets the audience while they’re already tuned in, making it a good time to invite deeper support.
This test ran in the lead-up to Spring Share. We evenly split traffic across variations to ensure a reliable comparison.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that leading with a thank-you message for Radio Player listeners did not produce a measurable lift in engagement or downstream donor metrics.
While the treatment version saw a 4.8% decrease in Content Offer CTA clicks compared to the control, the result was not statistically significant (33% confidence vs. the 95% threshold). This means we cannot reliably attribute the performance difference to the headline change.
However, looking further down the funnel provides important context. Both the control and treatment groups generated 11 gifts each. This suggests that although the thank-you message drove fewer clicks, the traffic it did generate may have been more qualified or motivated to give.
Therefore, these results should not be used to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence.
Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test variations of the messaging or explore other strategies to increase engagement and donor metrics among Radio Player listeners.

experiments.title: How radically simplifying the email format affects engagement and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that radically simplifying email formatting for an owned-audience email housefile will achieve greater immediate and downstream engagement because readers will perceive greater authenticity and trust, since the email appears to have been sent from an individual rather than a list.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2026 - 02/11/2026
Luther Seminary has been sending emails without many design elements to their housefile for years, believing that the more a message looks as if it was sent by an individual, the greater the trust and engagement will be. While their standard email template does not appear to have many design elements, there is still a bit of extra white space and unconventional line spacing that could subconsciously lead a reader to associate the message with marketing.
We wanted to see if fully simplifying email formatting would boost engagement. For the test, we removed all padding. We matched font, size, and color to a typical personal email.
We determined that click-through rate would gauge impact. We also measured offer sign-ups and donations to assess how a front-end change affected downstream behavior.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Top of Funnel: Email Clicks Radically simplifying email formatting for an owned-audience email housefile did not lead to greater immediate engagement. The treatment actually led to a slight -2% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control, though the difference was not statistically significant and therefore unreliable.

Mid Funnel: Offer Sign-Ups. The control email yielded 317 offer sign-ups, and the treatment yielded 316. The radically simplified email format had no impact on downstream offer sign-ups for email subscribers.

Bottom of Funnel: Donations. The treatment email resulted in a 40% increase in donations, a 48% increase in overall revenue, and a 5% increase in average gift, compared to the control, at a 85.8% level of confidence.

Key Takeaway: While the experiment did not yield the top-of-funnel increase in click-through rate as predicted, the impact on downstream donations warrants further testing. The greater authenticity of the radically simplified design could affect donor perceptions of the sender and carry through to important downstream actions. More experiments could demonstrate a clearer link and answer additional questions, such as: How does a radically simplified email format impact engagement in other email types, such as a direct appeal or cultivation message?

experiments.title: Does adding a donor goal countdown on the homepage for CYE increase engagement and donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the dynamic donor goal in the campaign lower third for Today in the Word’s Homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks as well as downstream metrics because it shows visible progress toward a shared goal, which creates urgency and social proof—signaling that others are giving and that each gift meaningfully moves the mission forward right now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2025 - 12/31/2025
During Today in the Word’s Year-End Campaign, we tested whether adding a donor goal to the website’s lower third would increase clicks and downstream donor conversion. The lower-third goal was calculated using the ministry’s overall revenue goal and average gift amount, then dynamically counted down as gifts were completed through the donation form.
The test ran for the full duration of the campaign, with traffic split evenly between the control and treatment experiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dynamic donor goal to the lower third of the Today in the Word homepage resulted in a 4.4% increase in Lower Third slide-up CTA clicks across all traffic. However, the level of confidence for this result was 30.4%, which falls well below the standard 95% threshold, meaning the finding is not statistically valid and should not be used to inform future decisions on its own.

The treatment group also generated an 18.7% increase in completed transactions at a 55.3% level of confidence. While this result does not validate a definitive lift in clicks or gifts, it is directionally positive and suggests that the presence of a donor goal does not demotivate the Today in the Word audience—and may help reinforce giving behavior among engaged users.

Future experimentation should focus on increasing sample size and extending test duration to improve statistical confidence and account for normal fluctuations in donor behavior.

Overall, while these results are not conclusive, the positive directional trends indicate that further testing is warranted to better understand whether a donor goal in the homepage lower third can meaningfully drive engagement and downstream donor conversion..

experiments.title: How including an image of the premium in a web asset donation ask impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the premium image for Moody Radio website radio player visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because  it visually reinforces the offer and increasing its perceived value to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Moody Radio’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding a premium image to the website Radio Player.
Both the control and treatment included identical premium language; however, only the treatment featured the premium image.
The test ran for two weeks, with traffic evenly split between both variants.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment showed that adding an image to the Moody Radio Player led to a 20.6% decrease in clicks overall (77.1% level of confidence). Because this outcome was unexpected, we took a deeper look at the data to better understand what may have driven the decline.
To isolate potential drivers, we analyzed click performance by device type.
On mobile, the treatment resulted in a 12.7% decrease in clicks (48% level of confidence).
On desktop, clicks declined by 44.8% (72% level of confidence).
On tablet, clicks dropped by 52.7% (62% level of confidence).
These results suggest that the image had the greatest negative impact on desktop and tablet users, where the decline in engagement was most pronounced.
Transaction data followed a similar pattern. The control generated four gifts, while the treatment generated just one—representing a 75% decrease in transactions (82% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How clarity about donor dollars on the homepage impacts engagement and downstream donor metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that transparent “100% of your gift” language for IMB homepage visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because it establishes immediate trust in how donor dollars are used and creating a seamless message connection between the homepage and the giving page. because  it establishes immediate trust in how donor dollars are used and creating a seamless message connection between the homepage and the giving page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/14/2025
To strengthen IMB’s homepage value proposition and increase clicks to the donation page, we tested adding a statement to the homepage graphic that clearly explains where donor dollars are used.
This message about how gifts are applied was also featured on the main giving page, creating stronger congruency between the microdecision to click “Donate” and the experience donors encounter once they arrive on the giving page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using transparent language like "100% of your gift" did not yield the expected increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics. Despite the treatment version emphasizing how donor dollars are used, it actually resulted in a 13.2% decrease in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version.
Since the statistical level of confidence is only 68.3%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making. Therefore, it is important not to draw conclusions or make changes based on these results alone.
However, was the treatment more effective at motivating donors?
The short answer: No.
The treatment produced a 23.1% decrease in transactions at a 47% LOC including all transactions made.
However, in order to see the most accurate results for this test, we removed all gifts over $500, and saw the treatment actually has a 60% decrease in overall transactions at a valid 95.1% LOC.
In future experimentation, it is essential to consider factors such as the language used, the placement of the message, and the overall messaging strategy. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of the message or to explore other elements of the homepage that could impact engagement and donor behavior. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence in the results before making significant changes is crucial to making informed decisions.

experiments.title: Does emphasizing collective impact strengthen a donor’s sense of shared mission, or does it unintentionally reduce their sense of personal responsibility to give?
experiments.researchQuestion: Including collective impact language in the Fall Share Email will help recipients achieve an increase in engagement and downstream conversion, as donors will be invited to see their gift as part of a larger, shared mission.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/09/2025
In an effort to better understand what motivates Moody Radio’s audience to engage with an email and ultimately give, we tested how emphasizing collective impact influences donor behavior. Specifically, we wanted to see whether highlighting the power of “together” encourages greater involvement—or if it unintentionally reduces a donor’s sense of personal responsibility to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that using collective impact language in Fall Share Email 5 led to a 15.5% increase in click engagement across all traffic. However, the results reached only a 65.0% confidence level, which falls below the standard 95% threshold for statistical significance. As such, these findings should be viewed as directional rather than conclusive.

When examining donor conversion, the results were mixed:
Control: 10 transactions, $462 in revenue
 	Treatment: 7 transactions, $905 in revenue
While the treatment generated
fewer gifts overall
, it produced a
higher average gift size
, suggesting that collective impact framing may increase perceived value or motivation among those who choose to give.
Moving forward, continued testing is recommended to validate these initial trends. Future experiments could include
larger sample sizes
,
alternative expressions of collective impact
, or
different audience segments
to understand better how this message framing influences both engagement and giving behavior. Additionally, evaluating the
context and tone
in which collective impact language appears may help determine how best to align this approach with Moody Radio’s audience motivations and communication strategy.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for recurring donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for recurring donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for recurring donors did not lead to an increase in click-through rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.6% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. The statistical analysis showed a 63.4% level of confidence, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable results and that any conclusions drawn are statistically significant. Additionally, it is crucial to carefully consider the design and implementation of changes such as adding a button, as they may not always have the desired effect. It is also recommended to conduct further testing and analysis to understand the reasons behind the unexpected outcome and to inform future decision-making. Experimentation is a valuable tool for learning and refining strategies, and it is important to approach it with a curious and open mindset to adapt and improve based on the findings.

experiments.title: Does impact-focused or campaign-focused language lead to more engagement and an increase in downstream metrics?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the impact-focused language for Moody Radio radio player visitors will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the donors to connect emotionally to the impact of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2025 - 08/07/2025
For Moody Radio’s 48 Hours of Impact campaign, we wanted to test whether campaign-focused copy or impact-focused copy resonated more with donors. Moody has historically been very campaign-focused with their appeals, since their audience is most likely accustomed to this type of appeal. We wanted to know whether the additional value of their gift would be more impactful than traditional language.
Control: Campaign-focused
Treatment: Impact-focused
This test ran on the Moody Radio radio player for about two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the impact-focused language did not perform better than the campaign-focused language in terms of generating clicks for Moody Radio's 48 Hours of Impact campaign. The treatment actually saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Furthermore, we used custom metrics in VWO to track gifts that came from the Radio Player.

Control:
13 transactions, $1950
7 transactions, $925

experiments.title: How adding a signature block with image in a direct appeal to the housefile impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the email signature block for housefile appeal receiver’s will achieve an increase in engagement with the email because it adds a personal touch—allowing readers to put a face to the name and therefore connect more with the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2025 - 08/05/2025
IMB has been working to optimize the email templates used to communicate with their housefile, with the goal of adding more personal touches to make each message feel like it’s coming from an individual rather than the organization as a whole. To support this effort, we tested adding a signature block with the sender’s photo to a direct appeal email. The control group received our standard typed sign-off, while the treatment group received the signature block with the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment group, which received the signature block with the sender's image, experienced a decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 73.4%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered reliable for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it is essential to carefully monitor and analyze the data to determine the impact of any changes made to the email templates. While personalizing emails with a signature block may seem like a good idea intuitively, it is important to test this hypothesis rigorously to validate its effectiveness before implementing it on a larger scale. Further experimentation with different variations of personalization elements in emails could help determine the most effective strategies for increasing engagement with the audience.

experiments.title: How an autoresponder sent from a person versus the organization impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the autoresponder coming from a person for Grace Upon Grace eBook requesters will achieve an increase in clicks because people are more likely to engage and trust personalized, human communication.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2025 - 07/09/2025
For Moody Radio, we wanted to test how an autoresponder sent from a notable person within the organization versus from the organization directly would impact email engagement.
This test ran for a little over a month with traffic being split equally between the control and treatment versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the autoresponder sent from a notable person within the organization resulted in a 2.1% decrease in clicks compared to the autoresponder sent from the organization directly. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 25.9%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not conclusive and should not be used for future decision-making.

While we know that opens are no longer reliable, we did pull the results of both opens and click-to-open rates to understand the range of where each email performed.

Control: 65.48% open rate, 64.93% click to open rate

Treatment: 61.23% open rate, 67.96% click to open rate

These results suggest that the treatment email, coming from Wes Ward, resulted in a slightly lower open rate of the email, but led to a slightly higher click-to-open rate.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, considering factors such as the timing of the emails, the messaging, and the audience segmentation could help to identify what truly drives engagement and clicks. It is also important to test different variables in future experiments to gain a better understanding of what resonates with the audience and drives the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio website traffic will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Radio.
This test ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio did not lead to an increase in clicks, as initially hypothesized. In fact, we observed a 4.2% decrease in clicks across all traffic, with a statistical confidence of 36.3%. This is well below the accepted 95% threshold for validity.

Additionally, downstream metrics showed a 4.4% decrease in transactions, with a confidence level of only 13.3%.

These findings suggest that the gift array version of the footer did not effectively drive more users to the donation page and may have resulted in less motivated traffic. While these metrics did not meet the validity criteria, they point to the possibility that there may be more effective ways to encourage donations from Moody Radio listeners beyond the gift array format.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider that what may seem like a logical approach to increasing user engagement, such as allowing users to select a gift amount before reaching the donation page, may not always lead to the desired outcome. It is essential to test all changes thoroughly before implementing them on a larger scale. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are statistically sound and reach a high level of confidence before drawing conclusions.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that he gift array version of the footer  for Moody Bible website traffic will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Bible’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Bible.
This text ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the gift array version of the footer for the Moody Bible website traffic showed a 4.3% increase in clicks compared to the standard one-button call-to-action. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 21.1%, which is below the 95% threshold needed to consider the results valid.
Furthermore, the treatment saw a 26.1% lift in transactions at a 43.7% LOC, meaning that the treatment led to more people engaging with the footer bar as well as led to more motivated traffic to make a gift.
However, the treatment actually resulted in a 20.7% decrease in revenue at a 56.7% level of confidence (pulled out of the number of transactions)
Control: 11 gifts, $3747
Treatment: 14 gifts, $3780
These results indicate that the treatment was more effective at getting people to engage and make a gift, but ultimately, the control produced a higher average of revenue per donor.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a statistically significant level of confidence is achieved before making decisions based on the results. This means conducting the experiment for a longer duration, increasing the sample size, or making adjustments to the experiment design to improve the reliability of the results.

experiments.title: How adding an image to an email impacted clicks for a donor audience

Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.

experiments.title: How impact callouts on a high urgency sticky bar impacts click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including impact callouts on the April Eagle Lake pushdown for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in click rate and engagement with the pushdown because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make. because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2025 - 05/05/2025
For The Navigators’ April Eagle Lake campaign, we tested adding impact callouts to each gift array amount on the pushdown. When a donor selected a gift amount, that amount was automatically pre-selected on the donation page, where the corresponding impact message was reinforced in the copy—creating a more seamless and compelling giving experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including impact callouts on the April Eagle Lake pushdown for homepage visitors resulted in an 18.4% increase in Clicked CTA button for All Traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 53.1%, which is below the generally accepted level of 95%. These results indicate that the impact callouts did in fact get more people to engage with the pushdown.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different ways of including impact callouts or other strategies to improve donor conversion metrics.

experiments.title: How redesigning content blocks on the homepage impacted clicks.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning content blocks for homepage visitors will achieve more clicks on said blocks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2024 - 07/24/2024
This test explored the impact of redesigning the content blocks on the Homepage. The goal was to increase clicks (engagement) and conversion by creating a more enticing and easier to navigate look to the content. We changed copy to be in white box overlaying image, with CTAs presented as links and highlight teal when hovering. CTA links always teal on mobile.
Our hypothesis was that redesigning the content blocks on the homepage will make them more engaging for the visitor and will lead to higher clicks and ultimately higher donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the redesigned content blocks on the homepage did not lead to more clicks from visitors. In fact, the treatment version saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control version. Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 11.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.

This experiment also did not impact donor conversion rates:

Control: 5%

Treatment: 4.9%, which was a 1.3% decrease with a 53% level of confidence.

 

The differences between desktop and mobile were also relatively small:

Desktop

Control: 2.4% click through rate; 5.2% donor conversion rate

Treatment: 2.4% click through rate (1.1% increase with a 21% level of confidence); 5.2% donor conversion rate (0.38% decrease, 11% level of confidence).

 

Mobile

Control: 2.6% click through rate; 4.9% conversion rate.

Treatment: 2.5% click through rate (1.6% decrease with a 34% level of confidence); 4.7% conversion rate (2.3% decrease with a 62% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How the placement of resources on a ‘listen now’ page impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the most recent broadcasts for those looking to catch up will achieve a change in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2025 - 04/07/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
This page is where people go to listen past sermons and TV broadcasts.
Building upon the initial test of a grid layout, in this version we wanted to test whether moving the positioning of the resource of the month, as well as the station locator, impacted engagement with the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that moving the positioning of the resource of the month and station locator on the Pathway to Victory website did not have a significant impact on engagement with the page. Although the treatment group saw a 7.3% decrease in clicks for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.3%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.
One of the goals of this experiment was to see if moving the featured resource in-line with the top grid, rather than a big call-out, led to more gifts. Overall, the treatment version had 3 more gifts in total, which is a 32% increase in conversion rates, but only at a 50% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How rotating a “Gift Card” widget with a Tribute Thank-You message impacts clicks and tributes
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that rotating a Gift Card widget in with the tribute Thank You card for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in Gift Card clicks/traffic without decreasing tribute giving because the additional CTA is only shown to those who’ve already donated, providing them with an additional next step rather than detracting from another important CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2025 - 03/10/2025
This experiment was a development upon a previous test to promote Gift Cards/Ways to Help as an additional CTA on user profiles. In the original test, the Gift Card widget had been swapped in place of the Tribute Widget on a rotating logic. Although this did dramatically increase clicks and traffic to the gift card page, it also resulted in a valid 30% decrease in tribute giving.
In this iteration, we hypothesized that only rotating the Gift Card in with the Tribute thank-you message would ensure that the additional CTA could be communicated, without removing another important ask, but instead, prioritizing the next step for people who’ve already donated. The logic for this setup was 50/50, half the time users would see the thank-you card for their tribute gift and the other half of the time they’d receive the additional Gift Card widget. Versus in the control, they were only shown the thank you message.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Rotating a Gift Card widget in with the tribute Thank You card for users on a site profile resulted in a 17.5% increase in Send a Gift Card clicked for All Traffic. This indicates that the additional CTA was effective in generating more clicks and traffic to the gift card page.

2. The 78.1% statistical level of confidence is below the 95% threshold, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid. This suggests that the increase in Gift Card clicks may not be entirely reliable.

3. However, another key aspect of this test was to incorporate the Gift Card widget without negatively impacting tribute giving. In this case, the treatment was a success. Reversing the original decrease seen previously, while also indicating that this treatment can be safely shown and likely will result in great education and clicks to Gift Cards.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the importance of careful consideration and testing when introducing new elements to a site profile. By analyzing the results and refining future experiments based on these findings, we can improve the user experience and drive desired actions effectively.

experiments.title: Does a more personal subject line increase engagement

experiments.title: How removing a CTA link at the beginning of an email increase clicks and donations on a donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by removing a a link at the beginning of an email for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in clicks, emails, and donations because it shifts the focus toward the content and value of the eBook before presenting the CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. Hoover recirculates their content offers by sending out an email to their Donors and Nondonors every Thursday offering a free eBook or course. On this Thursday, Hoover sent out the offer, “The Ten Causes of the Reagan Boom”. This specific email was to their donor audience.
Our hypothesis was that if we remove one of the links in the email it would increase clicks and email acquisition and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that by removing a link at the beginning of an email for Hoover's donor audience, there was a 7.4% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that shifting the focus towards the content and value of the eBook before presenting the call-to-action may have a positive impact on engagement.
Another interesting thing to note is that the control version received 16 gifts, while the treatment received 12 gifts.
Here are some potential reasonings behind the increase in clicks:
1. Reduced Link Fatigue & Increased Focus
In the control email, the reader sees two instances of the eBook link. When people see the same link twice, they might mentally skip over it, assuming they've already processed the message.In the treatment, there’s only one link, making it stand out more and increasing the likelihood that the reader clicks it.
2. Building Anticipation
The control email presents the link early on, potentially causing some readers to click right away and miss the rest of the message. Those who don't click immediately might not feel the need to continue reading.The treatment removes the early link, keeping readers engaged until the end, where they see the CTA more prominently and with more context.
3. More Natural Flow & Call-to-Action Strength
The treatment version subtly shifts the focus toward the content and value of the eBook before presenting the CTA. This can make the final CTA feel more compelling, rather than transactional.The single call to action at the end could create a sense of exclusivity or urgency, reinforcing the importance of clicking.
4. Less Distraction, More Commitment
The control version interrupts the email with a link early on, which might cause some readers to skim or disengage.The treatment keeps the reader focused on the narrative before directing them toward a single action.
5. Perceived Value & Scarcity Effect
By not giving immediate access to the link, the treatment might make the reader feel like they need to read carefully to find out where to get the eBook. This small increase in effort can create a greater sense of investment, making them more likely to follow through.
6. Psychological Consistency (Zeigarnik Effect)
The Zeigarnik Effect suggests people remember unfinished tasks better than completed ones. The treatment email builds curiosity without immediately satisfying it, keeping the reader engaged until the final CTA.
