experiments.title: How shortening email copy for a content offer impacts donor signups and donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening copy for active donors will achieve a change in content offer KPIs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2024 - 09/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation, we wanted to test whether shorter, more-to-the-point email copy impacted clicks and email signups, as well as donation conversions.
This experiment result is for active donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that shortening the email copy for active donors resulted in a 6.3% decrease in clicks for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Moreover, when looking at downstream metrics:
The treatment version had a 13% decrease in email sign ups for the content offer, with an 80% level of confidence.
 	There was no difference at all when looking at donation conversions after the sign-up compared to total sent, but of those that signed up and THEN donated, the treatment had a 16% increase in donation conversions with only a 36% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How changing ad copy impacted CTR on Facebook ads
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the copy to be shorter with called out value for Avila’s Facebook audience will achieve an increase in CTR because the engager understand the value better.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/13/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of their most popular courses is a video series from Dan Burke, Into the Deep. For this Facebook ad test, we wanted to change the copy to be shorter and focused on their relationship with the Lord. Describing more of the positive impact that the engaged would have with the course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that updating the copy to be shorter with a focus on the value Avila Foundation provides for its Facebook audience resulted in a 7.2% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. This indicates that the audience may have responded positively to the more concise message highlighting the relationship with the Lord that the course offers. However, since the statistical confidence level is below 95%, these results should not be considered valid for future decision making.
The cost per registration/email acquisition had a 8.8% increase with a 46% loc.
Control:
sample size: 7271
emails: 99
Treatment:
sample size: 6951
emails: 103
In future experimentation, it is important to continue exploring the impact of messaging that emphasizes the value of the courses in drawing Christians into deeper union with Christ. It may be beneficial to test variations of the copy further to see if a more significant increase in engagement can be achieved. Additionally, increasing the sample size or running the experiment for a longer period of time may help to gather more reliable data for making informed decisions about messaging strategies.

experiments.title: How presenting a daily donor goal in a recurring campaign affects email click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it creates a sense of urgency and allows donors to feel they are part of a tangible, short-term impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/05/2024
The Navigators ran their bi-annual sustainers campaign in October with the goal of acquiring new recurring donors. Their campaign goal was 90 new recurring donors, but we wanted to see how their file would respond to presenting a daily donor goal instead of an overall campaign goal. To do this, we replaced any instance of the campaign goal with a daily donor goal of 4 new recurring donors. This change was made in emails 3, 7, 8, and 12.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the presentation of a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal did not lead to an increase in conversion rate as hypothesized. The treatment group saw a 1.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group, with a statistical level of confidence of 15.5%.

Since the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for making decisions. Therefore, it is important not to draw any definitive conclusions from this experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies for creating urgency and a sense of immediate impact for donors. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is adequate and that the statistical analysis can provide reliable and conclusive results. Experimentation should continue to be a core part of The Navigators' strategy for optimizing their fundraising efforts, but conclusions should only be drawn from experiments with valid and statistically significant results.

experiments.title: How giving a monthly gift to a person instead of a fund impacts overall click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving the option to give a monthly gift to a person rather than a fund will increase conversion rates because it creates a personal connection and fosters a sense of ongoing partnership.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/06/2024
The Navigators ran their bi-annual sustainers campaign in October with the goal of acquiring new recurring donors. Their campaign goal was 90 new recurring donors. In the last email of the campaign, they wanted to test how their file would respond to having the option to give to a person rather than a fund of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing the option to give a monthly gift to a person rather than a fund may have a positive impact on click-through rates for acquiring new recurring donors. This suggests that creating a personal connection and fostering a sense of ongoing partnership could potentially increase donor engagement.

However, it is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant enough to be considered valid for future decision making. The 16.6% level of confidence falls below the 95% threshold typically used to determine the reliability of A/B test results.

In the future, it would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to determine if offering the option to give to a person indeed has a significant impact on conversion rates. It is also important to consider other variables and factors that could influence donor behavior, such as messaging, design, and timing of the campaign.

Overall, while the initial findings are promising, further testing and analysis are necessary to make informed decisions about implementing this strategy in future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a popularity call out impacted email clicks and donations for a Donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that refreshing the email copy for Hoover’s Donor audience will achieve an increase in clicks and conversions because they see that people wanted this offer with the “30,000 people” callout.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2024 - 11/13/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Victory and the Savior Generals. This experiment was for their Donor audience.
We wanted to update the copy to have these updates:
Shorter
Mentions well-known and respected Hoover fellow, Victor Davis Hanson, at the beginning of email
And have the callout “Already downloaded by more than 30,000 people in the last 6 months, you don’t want to miss your chance to learn from history’s most influential leaders from one of our country’s most esteemed historians.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the email copy for Hoover's Donor audience with a shorter, more engaging message mentioning Hoover fellow Victor Davis Hanson and including a callout about the popularity of the offer can lead to an increase in clicks. The treatment saw a 5.4% increase in clicks for all traffic, indicating that the updated copy resonated better with the audience.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 40.3%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, while the results suggest a positive impact, they should not be used for future decision-making.

 

However, with the donations Version B showed a 11% increase in number of donations with a 34% level of confidence.

With total revenue being:

Version A: $1859 with 33 gifts

Version B: $2595 with 36 gifts

 

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of the email copy to further optimize for engagement and conversions among Hoover's Donor audience. It's important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results, allowing for more reliable insights to guide decision-making.

experiments.title: How a more conversational tone on an email offering an ebook impacts click through rates for an existing donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a more conversational approach for donors will achieve a change in clicks and email sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/03/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Income Inequality, we wanted to see if a more conversational approach to email copy made an impact on click throughs and email sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved using a more conversational approach in the email copy for donors, resulted in a 2.0% increase in clicks for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 17.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

3. When we look at full funnel statistics, we see the following:

The treatment had a 14% increase in email sign-ups, with an 80% level of confidence.

The treatment had also had a 28% increase in instant donations after people signed up to download the offer, with a 57% level of confidence.

 

Overall, having a more conversational approach to copy did result in increases across the board for an existing donor audience.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts active donors during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional reinforcement for active donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment, which included additional reinforcement about WUNC and its purpose, resulted in a 16.0% decrease in click-through rates compared to the control group.

2. The statistical confidence level of 44.6% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid for decision-making.

3. Moreover, the treatment version had a 50% decrease in donations.

This experiment shows us, based on a small sample size, that active donors to WUNC do not need to be reminded about what WUNC is and does to be motivated to donate.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts prospects from a political newsletter list during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional value prop for political newsletter prospects will achieve a change in email clicks and conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates. This specific experiment targeted prospects that had signed up for a political newsletter before the elections.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional call-out reinforcing the value proposition of WUNC did not have the intended effect on click-through rates and conversion rates during the Fall Fund Drive. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group.

Moreover, the donation conversion rate was also lower for the treatment option, with a 33% decrease in conversions and a 35% level of confidence. However, the sample size was extremely small (only 5 total donations).

This test bears refinement and repeating with bigger audience sizes.

experiments.title: How using more focused copy on a non-donor email offering up a content offer impacted offer performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing copy for a non donor audience will achieve a change in content offer performance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Personal Freedom and the Moral Case for Capitalism, we wanted to see whether copy that focused more on the offer, and not use pop culture references, impact email and content offer performance. This experiment was for their non-donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that focusing the copy for a non-donor audience resulted in a 2.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. While this shows a potential positive impact on performance, the statistical level of confidence was only 47.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before making any definitive conclusions or decisions based on the results. This may involve running the experiment for a longer duration, increasing the sample size, or making adjustments to the variables being tested.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to continue testing different variations of copy focused on the offer and compare the performance results to further understand the impact on audience engagement and conversion rates. By consistently experimenting and analyzing the results with a critical eye on statistical relevance, Hoover Institution can make more informed decisions to optimize their email content and offers for better performance in the future.

experiments.title: Do Meta Suggested Image Optimizations really increase engagement?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing Meta image optimizations for Facebook traffic will achieve higher engagement because Facebook said it would.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/22/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV runs regular content offers and direct asks on Facebook (Meta). Meta has been urging marketers to use its image optimization suggestions, which it promises will significantly increase engagement and, therefore, donations.
As experimenters, we are skeptical.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing Meta image optimizations for Facebook traffic did not lead to higher engagement for Pathway to Victory's content offers and direct asks. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.5% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 60.8%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 90% for validity.
Purchases saw a 8% increase but only 62% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is important for Pathway to Victory to not solely rely on platform suggestions or promises when conducting experiments. It is essential to rigorously test any changes or optimizations before making decisions based on them. Additionally, future experimentation should aim to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence to ensure the reliability of the results. It may be beneficial to continue testing different strategies and optimizations to find what works best for engaging with the audience and driving donations.

experiments.title: How adding copy that emphasizes how many people downloaded the offer on an email to donors impacts click throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding claims of popularity for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in email sign up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2024 - 09/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Trickle Down, we wanted to test whether adding copy that emphasizes how many downloads of the offer have happened in the past impacts sign-up rates. This email was aimed towards a donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding claims of popularity for content offer downloaders can potentially impact email sign-up rates positively. The treatment group, which included this additional copy, saw a 15.2% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.8%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.
Moreover, when digging into downstream results, we see the following:
The treatment version had a 4% decrease with a 21% level of confidence. In terms of instant donor conversions, the treatment had a 55% DECREASE in donations, with a 95% level of confidence.
Future variations of this experiment could look at the congruency between email and landing page and donation form to make sure content offer downloaders see the same messaging across all points of the funnel.

experiments.title: How refreshing copy impacted click through rate on a recirculation offer for Donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy for Hoover’s donors will achieve an increase in click-through-rate because people will be more likely to read the copy if they’ve never seen it before.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2024 - 10/04/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. This specific test was on their Donor audience. They have enough offers to do different offers for roughly 3-5 months before the same offer is sent out. We suppress anyone who has previously downloaded this offer. So for this test, we decided to try new copy in the email to “refresh” the offer and hopefully increase the click through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy for Hoover's donors did not result in an increase in click-through-rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is only 56.3%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to test different variables such as formatting, visuals, or timing of the email to see what truly resonates with the audience. It is also important to continue monitoring and testing different variables to optimize performance and achieve the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How adding copy that emphasizes how many people downloaded the offer on an email to non-donors impacts click throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding claims of popularity for a non-donor audience will achieve a change in email sign up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2024 - 09/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Tricke Down, we wanted to test whether adding copy that emphasizes how many downloads of the offer have happened in the past impacts sign-up rates. This email was aimed towards a donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding claims of popularity to the email sent to a non-donor audience did not have a significant impact on email sign-up rates. The treatment group, which included claims of popularity, actually saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 74.5%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.
Moreover, when looking at downstream results, we see the following:
The treatment version had an 8% increase in email signups, with a 74% level of confidence.The treatment version had a 35% increase in donations, with a 60% level of confidence.
For non-donors specifically, adding in references to the popularity of the ebook increased all key metrics. Future testing would look at testing congruency on the messaging--does adding references to the popularity on the landing page or donation page impact performance metrics?

experiments.title: How a dynamic homepage donate button on mobile impacts clicks and downstream donor metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the dynamic text on a donate sticky footer for IMB homepage visitors will achieve an increase in button clicks and downstream revenue metrics because the dynamic, typing text is more eye catching to the user .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/10/2024
In an effort to push more of IMB’s homepage traffic to their main donation page, we decided to run a test of adding an additional “give now” button to the homepage.
Control: No changes
Treatment 1: Additional Give Now button sticky footer (“Give Now”)
Treatment 2: Additional Give Now button sticky footer (“Give Now”) + Dynamic Text (Types out “Send the Gospel”)
Our hypothesis was that the dynamic text would increase the number of clicks to the donation page because it was more eye catching than Treatment 1.
This test only ran on mobile devices.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donate sticky footer on the IMB homepage resulted in a 42.3% increase in donations for all traffic. Whereas, the dynamic donate sticky footer actually resulted in a 13.5% decrease in donations at 34% LOC.

While neither of these metrics validated, it does suggest that the static donate sticky footer was helpful in pushing more of IMB's homepage traffic to the donation page.

We can also look in clicks based on each treatment.

Sticky Footer: 104 Button Clicks

Sticky Footer + Dynamic Text: 88 Button Clicks

This data suggests that the dynamic, typing text was not as beneficial as the static text in directing visitors to the donation page.

Due to these results, we have chosen to roll out the static button to all mobile visitors.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the call to action below a button impacts clicks to activate a free membership trial
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding copy below each button on the landing page for site visitors will achieve an increase in clicks through to the membership activation page because it reinforces the call to action that they will get a free 30 days of membership.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/27/2024
We noticed a decline in clicks through to activate a free NextAfter Institute membership trial, so we looked back at earlier, better-performing versions of our landing page and noticed there used to be copy reinforcing the CTA below each button on the page. So, we tested adding the copy back to see how that would impact clicks through to the activation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no distinct difference in the results between the control and the treatment. The test did not validate. We will test this again during a time where we are pushing more traffic to this page to see if we can validate a result.

experiments.title: How pulling the home donate button out of the hamburger menu on mobile impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pulling the mobile donate button out of the hamburger menu for mobile homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks on the donate button because the button will be more visible and accessible therefore reducing friction for mobile users. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2024 - 08/01/2024
We have seen a positive impact with other clients pulling their home donate button on mobile out of the hamburger menu to make it more visible to the visitor, so we wanted to test this on IMB’s homepage.
This test ran only on mobile with traffic equally split between the control and the treatment.
The treatment had a “Give Now” button pulled out of the hamburger menu, but we also left it within the hamburger menu to view clicks on both.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that pulling the mobile donate button out of the hamburger menu for the IMB homepage led to a 32.2% increase in clicks just on the donate button within the hamburger menu.

As well as, the new mobile donate button resulted in a total of 78 clicks. While the control button within the hamburger menu only had 22.

While this test is difficult to validate, it does not suggest that having the donate button more visible to the user on mobile devices is helpful in getting more users to the main donation page.

**Note: the 'Give Now - Mobile Header' button is tracking clicks on the NEW donate button that appeared outside of the hamburger menu. The 'Give Now - Mobile Menu' button is tracking clicks for both the control and treatment of the original donate button within in the hamburger menu.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post as a paid Facebook ad with a brighter color background affects click-through rate and other downstream conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a brighter color background for the Facebook ad instead of a darker color for Facebook users will achieve an increase in click through rate and decrease in cost per click because the brighter color background will create a sense of vibrancy and attention-grabbing effect, capturing users’ focus and prompting them to take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2023
We have recently tested Facebook ads for this ministry client, which was an experiment between organic vs. normal graphic ads. We found that the organic post led to a higher click-through rate and donor conversion. Knowing that, we then wanted to conduct an A/B test between two different organic Facebook ads. The control Facebook organic ad was the original organic ad, with simple white text on a dark-colored navy background. Our treatment for this experiment was the same simple white text but placed on a brighter color teal background. We predict that the brighter color background can create a sense of urgency and stand out to the Facebook user, leading to an increase in click-through rate. The copy on both ads is the same.
This was an A/B test with 50/50 split in traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a brighter color background for the Facebook ad did not perform better in terms of Click-Through Rate (CTR) and Cost Per Click (CPC). The treatment group, which used the brighter color background, actually saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this decrease in clicks was only 10.1%, which did not validate since it was not above a 95% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test other variables or factors that could impact CTR and CPC, such as ad copy - this can include ad copy length, font, size, and even emojis/symbols.

experiments.title: How using a personal image on a sticky bar impacts clicks to a webinar page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar that puts a face to the name of the presenter for our website traffic will achieve a higher click-through rate to the registration page because it adds authority to the ask and makes it more personal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2024 - 06/27/2024
Something we’ve tested into a lot with clients is a “humanized sticky bar.” In an effort to humanize our messaging more and improve our user engagement, I wanted to apply that concept to registration for a LinkedIn Livestream.
The design of the sticky bar stayed the same with the only difference being the addition of Nathan’s (the presenter’s) headshot.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate. We ran this during a very short window of time, so I would like to test this again during a longer period of time to see if we can get a larger sample size and get it to validate.

experiments.title: How Adding an Additional Link in an Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional link for the fye appeal emails will achieve a higher click rate because there is an additional opportunity that wasn’t there before.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Mood runs a FYE campaign each year that relies a lot on email. For this test we wanted to see if adding an additional link in the copy would increase clicks to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While adding an additional link does increase the click rate by 4%, it is only at a 75% LoC. Furthermore, looking down the adding an additional link does not negatively or positively impact donor acquisition or revenue.

experiments.title: How Adding an Additional Link in an Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional link for the fye appeal emails will achieve a higher click rate because there is an additional opportunity that wasn’t there before.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Mood runs a FYE campaign each year that relies a lot on email. For this test we wanted to see if adding an additional link in the copy would increase clicks to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While adding an additional link does increase the click rate by 4%, it is only at a 75% LoC. Furthermore, looking down the adding an additional link does not negatively or positively impact donor acquisition or revenue.

experiments.title: How Adding Sharing Where Donations Are Being Allocated in an Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being realistic about the support that runs Moody Radio for the FYE campaign will achieve a higher click rate because it shows the user the impact that they will make.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
During Moody’s FYE campaign we wanted to see if we added copy that said exactly what their donations are supporting could increase motivation to get to the donation page
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding specific information about what donations are supporting can potentially increase motivation and click rates for the FYE campaign on Moody Radio. Although the treatment group did see a significant increase in clicks compared to the control group, the statistical level of confidence was below the desired threshold of 95%.

Results on the donation page show little to no impact on the amount of transactions or the average gift.

This indicates that while the results are promising, they are not conclusive enough to make a definitive decision based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing this hypothesis with larger sample sizes or different variations in the message to determine if this strategy consistently leads to increased engagement with the donation page. It is important to remember that valid results with a high level of statistical confidence are crucial for making informed decisions based on experimental outcomes.

experiments.title: How a higher contrast donation button impacts clicks and overall giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and overall giving because the higher contrast button would attract the visitors attention quicker therefore reducing friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2024 - 05/29/2024
In an effort to push more traffic to IMB’s Home Donation Page, we tested using a higher contrast button to attract visitor’s attention.
This type of test has resulted in a lift in donor conversion metrics for other clients, so we were excited to see how it performed.
Our goal was that by using a higher contrast button color, more visitors would click the button to be directed to the donation page, therefore, increasing giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors resulted in a 4.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 27.3%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and potentially increase the validity of the results.

Overall, while the initial results show promise, it is essential to rely on statistically significant data to make informed decisions about implementing changes to the website.

experiments.title: How a higher contrast donation button impacts clicks and overall giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and overall giving because the higher contrast button would attract the visitors attention quicker therefore reducing friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2024 - 05/29/2024
In an effort to push more traffic to IMB’s Home Donation Page, we tested using a higher contrast button to attract visitor’s attention.
This type of test has resulted in a lift in donor conversion metrics for other clients, so we were excited to see how it performed.
Our goal was that by using a higher contrast button color, more visitors would click the button to be directed to the donation page, therefore, increasing giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the higher contrast button did not have a positive impact on clicks or overall giving. In fact, all tracked metrics resulted in invalid decreases.

"Give Now" Button Clicks:
Control - 95 clicks
 	Treatment - 93 clicks
 	3.2% decrease in clicks, 17.6% LOC
Control - 362
 	Treatment - 335
 	8.5% decrease in clicks, 76.4% LOC

experiments.title: How using a lighter background on a video series landing page impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a light background for the video series landing page will achieve an increase in clicks to the checkout page because the contrast from the white background will improve readability for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2024 - 04/25/2024
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They created a landing page for a new paid video series and wanted to know if the current, black background of the page was harming the user experience.
We decided to test a light-background version of the page against the current dark background. Our hypothesis was that the white background would lead to more clicks to the checkout page because white backgrounds in web design are shown to enhance readability and reduce visual strain.
We ran this as a split URL test in VWO, with a random 50% of the traffic going to the dark background page. The other 50% were instead shown a light background version of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment was that changing the background color of the video series landing page from black to white did not result in the desired increase in clicks to the checkout page. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 2.4% decrease in visits to the Focus Store Checkout Page compared to the control group.

Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 15.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of this experiment were essentially flat and shouldn't be considered a valid test result.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that changing the background color alone may not be enough to drive increased engagement with the checkout page. Further research and experimentation may be necessary to identify the most effective strategies for improving user experience and encouraging clicks to the checkout page.

experiments.title: How using a testimonial in a Facebook ad affects CTR
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a testimonial for facebook users will achieve a higher click-through-rate because it adds credibility to the advertisement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2024 - 05/31/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. The Property Rights content offer has a low click-through rate, and the team wants to improve its performance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which included a testimonial for Facebook users, resulted in a 12.7% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic with a 54.0% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

2. The Cost per Click experienced a significant 470% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that the treatment may not be cost-effective compared to the control.

3. The 25-34 age group saw a substantial 1000% decrease in clicks with a 98% level of confidence when exposed to the treatment. This suggests that the addition of a testimonial may not resonate well with this demographic.

Based on these findings, it is clear that the addition of a testimonial for Facebook users did not yield positive results in terms of Clicks for All Traffic, Cost per Click, and engagement from the 25-34 age group.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider refining the messaging of the testimonial, targeting specific audience segments, or testing different types of social proof to see if it positively impacts the performance of the Property Rights content offer. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient and that the statistical confidence levels are high enough to draw valid conclusions for decision-making.

experiments.title: How a Humanized Pop-Up Impacts Clicks and Downstream Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a pop-up from the perspective of a real person and including their picture for users on the Human Coalition homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it humanizes the pop-up and creates a more compelling experience having it come from a real person “in the field” .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2024
For Human Coalition’s Mother’s Day campaign, we hypothesized that a humanized pop-up written from the perspective of a Practice Manager at a center and the inclusion of her picture would generate greater click-thru and downstream donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved a pop-up written from the perspective of a real Practice Manager at a center and included her picture, resulted in little to no difference in downstream conversion. Flat results were also observed on click-thru rate as well:

Control: 1,538 | 15 Clicks | Click Rate: 1.0%

Treatment 1,552 | 13 Clicks | Click Rate .8% | -14.1% Difference | LOC: 31.4%

Although a greater sample size and further testing is required to achieve a valid learning, what this trend does indicate is that the humanized experienced made little difference for users on the homepage.

experiments.title: Will a plain text email achieve a higher click rate vs a HTML style email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending a plain text email instead of a heavily templatized email for HUC sends will achieve a higher level of engagement because the email will feel like it came from a person instead of a mass marketing email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
For our first set of experiments for Moody Radio we wanted to test into a more personalized email instead of a heavily templated email.
For this we came up with several tests that we wanted to do.
HTML vs. Plain Text
Hyperlink vs. Button
1 CTA vs. 2 CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that sending a plain text email instead of a heavily templatized email for house and HUC sends resulted in a decrease in clicks for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further investigate the preferences of the target audience to understand what type of email format resonates best with them. This can help in creating more personalized and engaging communication strategies in the future.

experiments.title: How replacing a button in an email for a hyperlink achieved a higher click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a button with a raw hyperlink in an email for a huc campaign will achieve a higher click rate because it looks more personal and give the illusion of more trust because it feels like a 1-1 email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
For our first set of experiments for Moody Radio we wanted to test into a more personalized email instead of a heavily templated email.
For this we came up with several tests that we wanted to do.
HTML vs. Plain Text
Hyperlink vs. Button
1 CTA vs. 2 CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

- Replacing a button with a raw hyperlink in an email for a HUC campaign resulted in a 4.5% increase in Clicks for All Traffic.

- The treatment group showed a higher click rate, which could indicate that using raw hyperlinks in emails may appear more personal and trustworthy to recipients.

- However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.8%, which means the results are not considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to:

- Conduct further tests with a larger sample size to increase the statistical confidence level.

- Consider testing other variables such as email copy, subject lines, or sender name along with the hyperlink vs. button format to understand the impact of each element on click rates.

- Continue experimenting with personalized elements in emails to see if this leads to higher engagement from recipients.

- Keep track of statistical significance to ensure that results are reliable and can be used for decision making.

experiments.title: How including a gift array impacted clicks on a direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift array options for nondonor email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because there is greater clarity around the giving amounts and easier entry point to the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
During a fall recurring campaign, we wondered if introducing the gift array options for monthly donors would drive additional clicks on an appeal by introducing the decision at an earlier stage in the funnel. We split the non-donor file 50/50 and included a clickable gift array for the treatment group.
experiments.keyLearnings: The inclusion of gift array options for non-donor email recipients did not achieve the expected increase in clicks to the donation page. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 44.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. For future testing, we will consider testing different variations of the gift array options, such as the layout, wording, or placement within the email, to see if any of these factors may impact the click-through rates.

experiments.title: Is there a difference in conversion when asking for $10 or $25 gift from nondonors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for donations for $25 or more for nondonor subscribers will achieve a higher average gift because the based level giving has been raised.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2024 - 02/23/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV primarily raises funds through donations made for books written by Dr. Robert Jeffress. For Easter this year, PTV decided to create a premium offer specific to Easter and to support the mission of PTV, spread the Good News of Jesus Christ to a lost and hurting people, confront an ungodly culture with God’s truth, and equip the saints to apply scripture to their everyday lives.
Last year, PTV tested into asking nondonors for a gift of $10 or more and saw great results.
With this offer, we decided to test a higher gift ask of $25
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the donation ask from $10 to $25 for nondonor subscribers resulted in a 0.7% increase in clicks for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 4.8%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for validity. Therefore, these results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

Donations and average gift
Control: 15 gifts, $21.33 average gift
 	Treatment: 14 gifts, $21.43 average gift

experiments.title: How letting members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, are appreciated affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Letting members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they will know their gift is helping support the mission
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if more members would click if they knew they would receive a Member’s Card no matter how much they gave. The treatment included the phrase, “Any amount you give is greatly appreciated” as part of the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version, which included the phrase "Any amount you give is greatly appreciated", did not perform as well as the control version in terms of click-through rates. There was a 9.3% decrease in clicks for all traffic with a 44.1% confidence level, signaling that the control version was more effective at encouraging action. The conversion rate was also lower for the treatment email.

2. The statistical confidence level of 44.1% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that the results are not considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Consider the messaging carefully and ensure that it aligns with the organization's goals and mission. While emphasizing that any amount is appreciated may seem like a positive way to encourage donations, it's crucial to test different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with the audience.

2. Continue to experiment with different messaging, calls to action, and incentives to understand what drives donor behavior. By testing various approaches, we can gather valuable insights to inform future campaigns and optimize results.

3. Increase the sample size or test over a longer period to improve the statistical confidence level of the results. This will help ensure that the findings are reliable and can be used to make informed decisions.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different strategies and messaging to determine the most effective way to engage supporters and drive action. By learning from these results, we can continue to refine our approach and improve the effectiveness of our campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: How letting non-members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, are appreciated affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Letting non-members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they will know their gift is helping support the mission
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked non-members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if more non-members would click if they knew they would receive a Member’s Card no matter how much they gave. The treatment included the phrase, “Any amount you give is greatly appreciated” as part of the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment version, which included the phrase "Any amount you give is greatly appreciated," did not perform as well as the control version in terms of click-through rates. There was a 4.2% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic and a slight increase in conversions with a 58.4% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

2. The hypothesis that letting non-members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate was not supported by the results of this experiment.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the impact of wording and messaging on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variations of language to determine the most effective way to communicate the value of donations to non-members. Additionally, it is important to ensure that experiments are designed to achieve a statistically significant level of confidence to obtain reliable results that can inform decision-making.

experiments.title: How explaining the value of the membership card to non-members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the open rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value non-members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked non-members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if non-members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including reminder text about the value of a membership card can potentially increase the click-through rates for donation emails, as evidenced by the 0.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical significance level of 11.0% is below the standard threshold of 85%, indicating that these results may not be reliable for future decision-making.

Note: While the click-through rate increased in the treatment email, the conversion rate had a slightly lower conversion rate.

Moving forward, it is important to continue exploring the impact of including value proposition reminders in donation appeals, perhaps refining the messaging or placement within the email to maximize effectiveness. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes to validate the findings and make more informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How reminding members about the value of the membership card in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the click-through rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including reminder text about the value of a membership card did not have the intended impact on the click rate for the Members Card campaign. While the treatment group saw a 3.5% decrease in clicks compared to the control group, the statistical confidence level was only 22.3%, which is not sufficient to draw a valid conclusion.

Note: The treatment email also had a lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the wording and placement of value proposition texts in emails. It is possible that the added text in the treatment group did not effectively communicate the value of the Members Card or may have distracted recipients from the main call to action.

To improve the effectiveness of future experiments, it may be helpful to test different variations of value propositions, incorporate feedback from focus groups or surveys, and ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, considering other factors such as the timing of the email, the design elements, and the target audience can also impact the success of the campaign.

experiments.title: How mentioning a match offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the match gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members will increase the click-through rate because the incentive to give is given greater emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if lapsed members would click if the matching gift offer was placed earlier in the body of the email. The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action that mentioned the matching gift opportunity after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the matching gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members did not increase the click-through rate. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Note: The conversation rate of the treatment email also experienced a slight decrease.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing the placement of the matching gift offer within the email to determine the most effective position for maximizing click-through rates. Additionally, exploring other factors such as the messaging around the matching gift offer or the design of the email could also be valuable in optimizing fundraising efforts for lapsed members.

experiments.title: How mentioning a match offer sooner in a fundraising email to members affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the match gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to members will increase the click-through rate because the incentive to give is given greater emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click if the matching gift offer was placed earlier in the body of the email. The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action that mentioned the matching gift opportunity after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the matching gift offer earlier in a fundraising email did not increase the click-through rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment saw a slight decrease in clicks compared to the control. However, the statistical confidence level of 23.9% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Note: While the click-through rate decreased in the treatment email, the conversion rate increased slightly.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the placement of incentives like matching gift offers in fundraising emails carefully. While the hypothesis in this experiment did not hold true, it does not mean that emphasizing incentives early in emails is ineffective across the board. It may be worth testing different messaging strategies, placements, or types of incentives to see what resonates most with members and drives higher engagement. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence in results before concluding is crucial for making informed decisions based on experimentation.

experiments.title: How addressing one specific problem in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for members
experiments.researchQuestion: Addressing a specific problem/issue will increase the click-through rate because members have a clearer understanding of the problem they will help solve.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if describing one issue America faces instead of three issues would encourage members to click. The control mentioned three issues while the treatment focused on one. The issues were mentioned in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that addressing a specific problem/issue in an email campaign can potentially increase the click-through rate. In this case, the treatment focusing on one issue saw a 13.5% increase in clicks compared to the control that mentioned three issues. This indicates that providing a clear and specific understanding of the problem members will help solve can be more impactful in encouraging action.

Note: The donation conversion rate for the treatment email also experienced a slight increase.

However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is 57.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for statistical significance. Therefore, these results should not be considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of addressing specific issues versus multiple issues in email campaigns. By conducting further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations, we can gather more conclusive data on whether this approach effectively increases engagement and donations. Additionally, it may be helpful to explore other factors such as the framing of the issue, the language used, or the placement within the email to further optimize campaign effectiveness.

experiments.title: How adding match-giving language to the call to action affects the click-through rate for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe adding match-giving language to the calls to action will increase the click-through rate because it provides urgency and a feeling of greater impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click more if the matching gift offer was mentioned again in the call to action text. The control email described the matching gift opportunity once in the body of the email. The treatment mentioned the match in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding match-giving language to the calls to action can potentially increase the click-through rate. The treatment group, which included the matching gift offer in both the body of the email and the call to action, experienced an 11.3% increase in clicks compared to the control group.
Note: The treatment email also had a higher conversion rate.
However, it's important to note that the confidence level of the results was only 70.1%, falling below the 85% threshold typically required for statistical significance. As a result, these results should not be considered valid for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of emphasizing matching gift opportunities in calls to action. This can help to determine if this strategy consistently drives higher engagement from recipients. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure results have a high level of statistical significance before drawing any definitive conclusions or making changes based on the findings.

experiments.title: How Autonomy Language in a website appeal popup impacts clicks to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language in a donor appeal popup reinforcing a donor’s autonomy will increase clicks by empowering them to make an informed choice
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2024 - 03/25/2024
Buckner International had a popup on the website for visitors promoting an opportunity to give $15 to give shoes to a waiting child. We hypothesized that using language that granted the reader greater autonomy to make a choice would increase clicks on the popup by empowering them to make an informed choice.
For the Control popup, we led with a statement that the reader should know something urgent. In the treatment, we asked if we could tell them about something urgent. The button language reinforced their autonomy by saying, “Yes, I want to give shoes to a waiting child.”
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, there wasn't enough data in the experiment to validate the results. The popup was setup to only fire every two visits. Due to the traffic volume of Buckner's blog, that didn't provide enough data to validate the treatment.

experiments.title: How belonging to a like-minded community in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for lapsed members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe creating a sense of unity will increase the click-through rates because supporters want to belong and connect with other people who have the same values and passions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked lapsed members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if including text about being in a community of like-minded individuals would encourage lapsed members to click. Text was added to the body of the email and in the call to action.
The idea of unity is based on a principle from Robert Cialdini and the psychology of persuasion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the inclusion of text about being in a community of like-minded individuals did not have a significant impact on click-through rates for The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control, with a statistical confidence level below the threshold for validity.
Note: The donation conversion rate experienced a slight increase in the treatment email.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider alternative hypotheses and strategies for increasing engagement among lapsed members. This could involve testing different messaging approaches, incentives, or targeting tactics to better understand what resonates with supporters and drives them to take action. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are designed with a sufficient sample size and statistical power to draw meaningful conclusions.

experiments.title: How belonging to a like-minded community in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe creating a sense of unity will increase the click-through rates because supporters want to belong and connect with other people who have the same values and passions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if including text about being in a community of like-minded individuals would encourage members to click. Text was added to the body of the email and in the call to action.
The idea of unity is based on a principle from Robert Cialdini and the psychology of persuasion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the inclusion of text promoting a sense of unity among supporters did not lead to an increase in click-through rates for The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment group experienced a 12.1% decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a statistical level of confidence of 70.6%.
Note: The donation conversion rate also decreased in the treatment email.
These results should not be used for future decision-making as the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%. However, it is important to note that the hypothesis about creating a sense of unity was not supported by this experiment.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different messaging strategies to understand what resonates best with supporters. It may be helpful to explore other psychological principles of persuasion and to consider the specific values and motivations of The Heritage Foundation's audience when crafting messaging for campaigns. Additionally, conducting further experiments with larger sample sizes and incorporating feedback from supporters could provide more reliable insights for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How belonging to a like-minded community in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe creating a sense of unity will increase the click-through rates because supporters want to belong and connect with other people who have the same values and passions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked non-members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if including text about being in a community of like-minded individuals would encourage non-members to click. Text was added to the body of the email and in the call to action.
The idea of unity is based on a principle from Robert Cialdini and the psychology of persuasion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that incorporating messaging about unity and being part of a community of like-minded individuals can have a positive impact on engagement, as evidenced by the 2.7% increase in click-through rates for the treatment group. While the results were not statistically significant at the 85% confidence level, there is still an indication that this approach resonated with supporters.
Note: The donation conversion rate decreased by 5.9% with a 31% level of confidence in the treatment email.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the concept of unity and community in messaging to see if it consistently leads to increased engagement. Additionally, conducting larger sample-size experiments could help increase the statistical significance of the results and provide more confidence in the findings. Overall, the principle of creating a sense of unity and belonging among supporters appears to be a promising strategy to continue testing and incorporating into future campaigns for The Heritage Foundation.

experiments.title: How 1 call-to-action vs 2 calls-to-action in an email impacts click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the number of CTA for an email will achieve a higher click rate because the opportunity to click has grown.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
For our first set of experiments for Moody Radio we wanted to test into a more personalized email instead of a heavily templated email.
For this we came up with several tests that we wanted to do.
HTML vs. Plain Text
Hyperlink vs. Button
1 CTA vs 2 CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the number of calls-to-action (CTA) in an email can potentially lead to a higher click rate. In this case, the treatment with two CTAs resulted in an 18.5% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group with only one CTA. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 48.5%, which means that these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size to increase the statistical significance of the results. It may also be beneficial to explore the impact of different types of CTAs (such as buttons vs. hyperlinks) and the effect of using HTML vs. plain text emails. By continuing to experiment and test different variables, we can gather more reliable data to make informed decisions about optimizing email performance for Moody Radio.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired actin is clearer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. The email was a personal communication informing members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask members to “activate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the call to action from a question to a statement did not lead to a higher click-through rate among members. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks and the number of conversions, although the results were not statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it is important to note that small changes in wording or formatting may not always have the desired impact on member engagement. It is essential to conduct thorough testing with a significant sample size and statistical confidence to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence member behavior, such as the timing of the email, the content of the message, or the overall design of the communication.

Overall, the findings from this experiment should not be used to make decisions about future email campaigns, as the results do not provide a clear direction. Instead, it would be advisable to continue testing different strategies and elements to determine the most effective approach for encouraging members to take action.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with non-members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for non-members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired action is cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. The email was a personal communication informing non-members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask non-members to “activate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage non-members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the use of a statement as a call to action for non-members resulted in a slight increase in clicks compared to using a question. While the results showed a 0.3% increase in Clicks for All Traffic, the statistical confidence level was only 6.1%, which is not significant enough to make a valid conclusion.

The number of conversions for both emails was the same.

In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size to determine if using a statement call to action consistently leads to higher engagement from non-members. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other variations in the call to action to see what resonates best with the target audience. Overall, it is crucial to rely on statistically significant results before implementing any changes based on an experiment.

experiments.title: How does receiving a premium compared to receiving personal recognition for their gift affect clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personal recognition for monthly partners will achieve an increase in clicks and donor conversion because donors would feel more valued by the organization and the personal touch of their name being added to the group.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test whether a premium or personal recognition for their recurring gift would be more effective.
The control focused on the same premium that had been used throughout the campaign, and the treatment focused on a more personal recognition for their monthly gift. The treatment also promised to send the monthly partner a picture of their name on the window with all of the other monthly partners.
We hypothesized that the treatment would greatly outperform the control due to the personal touch and recognition of becoming a monthly partner because recognition is a motivating factor for the majority of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which focused on personal recognition for monthly partners, did not outperform the control, which offered a premium for recurring gifts. The treatment actually saw a drop of 3.7% in clicks at a 21.6% level of confidence. These results are inconclusive; however, we also wanted to look into if this test impact donations rather than clicks.

The control had a total of 13 donations, and the treatment had 8 donations. The results in terms of transactions show a drop of 36.1% in transactions at a 71.3% level of confidence. While transactions also did not validate, it indicates that The Navigators audience did not respond well to recognition for their gift.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of personal recognition versus premiums for recurring donors, ensuring that the sample size is sufficient to draw valid conclusions.
