Additionally, considering other variables or elements that could influence donor behavior, such as messaging or incentives, could provide a more comprehensive understanding of what motivates donors to contribute.

experiments.title: How including an image of a premium in an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including an image of the premium for email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there is greater clarity about the children’s book and its intended audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
When offering to send a book as a premium for gifts of a certain amount, we wondered if having an image of the book being offered would have any impact on email engagement by generating interest and curiosity about the contents of the book as well as creating congruency between the email and the donation page, which also had the image of the book displayed at the point of transaction. Since the author also writes books for adults, we wanted to provide clarity at the point of decision-making that this book would be best suited for young readers or any children in the visitors’ lives.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including an image of the premium in email communications might have a positive impact on engagement and click-through rates. Although the treatment group did see a 7.1% increase in clicks, the statistical confidence level of 64.8% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of visuals, such as images of premiums, in email communications to gauge their impact on recipient engagement. However, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results. Additionally, incorporating other elements such as A/B testing different visuals or messaging strategies could provide further insights into what resonates best with the audience and drives the desired actions.

experiments.title: How Urgency Language impacts clicks on a website popup during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency to a website popup during the year-end campaign will increase clicks by making the appeal more timely.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For Buckner International’s Year-End campaign, we wanted to test the impact of urgency language on a popup we were running to drive website visitors to the year-end donation page. Both popups would feature an image of a mother and child in need and invite users to make a year-end gift. The difference would be in the urgency of the appeal. The control would ask if the reader could “support a family in need this season?” The treatment stated, “Time’s running out to support a family in crisis.”
We hypothesized that the added urgency of the treatment would increase engagement by making the appeal more timely. To ensure that people didn’t see the popup every visit, we set it up to show once every two visits.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, website traffic was far too low during the campaign to validate this experiment. We had only 174 sessions that saw the popups and a total of 4 clicks. While both had a healthy CTR, there wasn't enough traffic. Future tests will need to either fire every visit and/or target higher-traffic pages on the site to be able to validate.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits associated with becoming a recurring donor impact click-through rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the benefits of becoming a monthly partner  for the donor will achieve an increase in click through rate because  of the perceived exclusivity and incentives associated with becoming a monthly partner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For The Navigator’s February Sustainers Appeal, we hypothesized that calling out the benefits of becoming a monthly partner would increase our click-through rate as well as our donor conversion rate. The control email emphasized the importance and need for monthly partners, focusing heavily on the difference it would make for the organization. Whereas, the treatment focused on the benefits for the donor themselves.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted for The Navigator's February Sustainers Appeal tested the impact of emphasizing the benefits of becoming a monthly partner for the donor, as opposed to focusing on the organization's need for monthly partners.
The treatment email, which highlighted the benefits for the donor, showed a 7.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control email. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 52.7%, meaning the results cannot be considered statistically valid.
However, the treatment greatly outperformed the control in terms of transactions. The treatment saw a 161.3% lift in transactions at a 99.6% level of confidence, meaning that the results are valid!
Therefore, the key learnings from this experiment are that The Navigators housefile is much more motivated to give when exclusivity and incentives are coupled with an appeal. While the results showed that readers were slightly more inclined to click the link when benefits to the donor were included, the individuals who did take the next step within the treatment, were much more motivated to give.

experiments.title: How having multiple calls-to-action impacts the click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the number of ctas in an email for the mass communication emails will achieve a higher percentage of engagement because there will be more opportunities throughout the email to engage with the content.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/23/2024
This is the last test in our design of experiments to make a mass marketing email feel like a personal 1-on-1 email.
We started testing with HTML vs. Plain text and then pivoted to how we present CTA’s within the email (button or raw URL). This final test will be seeing how many cta’s we should put in the email itself. Will the Audience respond better to only having one or is it more ideal to have two?
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the number of CTAs in an email can lead to a higher percentage of engagement, as evidenced by a 6.9% increase in clicks for all traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 20.4%, which is below the typically accepted threshold of 85%. This experiment will need to be retested with a plain text email.

experiments.title: How an actionable and enticing subject line impacts clicks

rows.name: Connection with God, Neighbors, and Community
three steps to deepen your relationships…

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a more actionable subject line in a cultivation housefile email will achieve more clicks because it create more curiosity .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2024 - 02/21/2024
Luther Seminary sent an article in a cultivation email about three steps to deepen your connection with God, neighbors, and community. We tested using a more actionable subject like impacted clicks to see if it encouraged more people to click to read the article.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which used a more actionable subject line, did not perform better than the control in terms of Clicks for All Traffic. In fact, it experienced a 2.5% decrease in clicks.

3. The statistical level of confidence for the Clicks for All Traffic results was only 22.9%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experiments, it is important to consider the following:

1. While creating more actionable subject lines may increase open rates, it may not necessarily lead to more clicks. It is important to strike a balance between creating curiosity and providing clear information in the subject line.

2. It is crucial to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence in the results before making any conclusions or decisions based on the outcome of the experiment.

3. Further experimentation and testing may be needed to fully understand the impact of different subject line strategies on email performance.

experiments.title: How calling out security reinforcement impacts clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out the security of a gift for potential donors will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate because it will reduce the anxiety of making a gift by emphasizing that their personal information will remain confidential.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2024
For The Navigator’s February Sustainers Appeal, we hypothesized that calling out the security of a gift within the email would increase the click-through rate as well as the donor conversion rate. This ran as a 50/50 split test to their housefile to test the effectiveness of security language for their audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email resulted in a 9.2% drop in clicks at a 60.2% level of confidence as well as a 72.6% drop in donor conversion rate with an 80.2% level of confidence indicating that the control email was more effective. Since both of these metrics fall below the validation threshold of 95%, we can not conclude that The Navigators audience does not respond well to calling out the security of a gift, and it may be worth testing again. It is possible that calling out the security of the gift before the potential donor landed on the donation page caused additional confusion and friction. Furthermore, this campaign sent people to a recurring only donation form which could have played a role in the lack of validation of the experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be interesting to test the impact of a security callout on the donation page as it may convert more donors as well as test this concept on an appeal where one-time donations are also accepted.

experiments.title: How a simplified message that focuses only on impact in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects conversions for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the message and only focusing on the impact in the final email of the campaign will produce a higher conversion rate because the treatment email presents a more straightforward and concise message, which can be easier for recipients to understand quickly.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for members to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how a concise message that only focuses on their impact in the final email of the campaign would affect conversion rates. We believe a clear and simple call-to-action may lead to a better response as it reduces cognitive load for the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment email, which focused solely on the impact of The Heritage Foundation, did not have a significant impact on conversion rates compared to the control email.

The treatment email saw a .35% decrease in clicks for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence is only 3.4%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Although there was no difference in clicks, we did see an 8.9% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 77.4% level of confidence. This tells us that using concise copy for the members-only segment does not motivate them as much to renew their membership when compared to other segments like lapsed donors and non-members.

experiments.title: How adding clarity around the nature of an offer using an image impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity with a digital image for housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because there will be enhanced clarity around the nature of the offer they are signing up for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2023 - 12/09/2023
When sending a Christmas devotional offer, DTS wanted to add clarity to potential readers that this was a digital offer and not a physical book since some of their other premiums are a physical copy of a particular resource. We set up a test to determine if additional clarity in the email would impact click rates from the housefile.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we saw after running the experiment was the treatment that added a digital image for housefile email recipients resulted in a measured increase of only 0.1%, and it only reached a 1.3% level of confidence so the experiment results are not valid and the results are flat.

This will have to be tested on future content offers to get a better understanding of how housefile email recipients are motivated.

experiments.title: How removing a signer’s image in an email footer signature impacted clicks

rows.name: Control - With Image in Footer Signature
Treatment - No Image in Footer Signature

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a photo from the email signature for housefile recipients will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because the email will appear more human and personalized.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2023 - 12/14/2023
When sending a course registration email, we wondered if the signature image was causing the email template to appear more like marketing and less like a personalized email coming from a real human. We split the file 50/50 and tested each version to measure engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing a photo from the email signature for housefile recipients resulted in a 7.5% increase in clickthrough rate. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.8%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.
We will want to re-test this concept in future experiments to fully measure the impact of removing the photo from the email signature.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array in the body of an email appeal impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a sample gift array for a direct email appeal will achieve an increase in engagement because there will be additional cognitive momentum to continue the process to the next step.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/28/2023
In a high-urgency campaign, we typically keep the decisions isolated per each step. In the email step, we help the reader decide “Do I want to click?” and then at the donation page step we help the reader decide “How much do I want to give?” In one email, we wanted to test if adding gift array options at the email level would help more people begin thinking about both questions at once. The treatment version added a gift array of buttons, letting the reader know they could click one of the buttons to choose their donation amount and begin the process of making a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: After splitting the email between the control and the treatment, we observed a 1.2% lift in clicks at 9.4% level of confidence for the treatment version which is not statistically significant so the experiment results are not valid. The engagement remained flat, even when we added buttons with gift array amounts in the body of the email. We will want to look downstream and see if there is any significant difference in completed donations from the treatment group that would lead to more meaningful results to see if moving the gift array into the body copy helped produce more motivated visits from email traffic who had already decided what amount they would be interested in giving and continue the cognitive momentum in the process when they arrive on the donation page.

experiments.title: How adding a second link in an email impacted conversion for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a second link for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate because there are more opportunities to click . because there are more opportunities to click .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2023 - 11/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
This is an experiment we ran for PTV a couple of years ago, but we wanted to test again to see if the results still held.
We tested a second link in the email to see if it would increase engagement and down funnel donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a second link in the email did not result in a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks for all traffic, implying that subscribers did not engage more with the email when presented with an additional link.

2. The control group, which did not have the second link, outperformed the treatment group in terms of clicks. Although this result is not statistically significant, it suggests that the original email format may be more effective in driving engagement.

3 Of those who received the treatment only 37% clicked the first link and 63% clicked the second link.

4 Donations saw a 0.9% increase with a level of confidence 4.5%.

 

Based on these results, the presence of a second link did not impact engagement or donations.

experiments.title: How language specific to existing contacts impacted Facebook clicks

rows.name: FBD 350 - Join Us Reach - May 14 member control
FBD 350 - Join Us Reach - May 14 member treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by making ad copy specific to FBD contacts for existing contacts on Facebook will achieve a higher conversion in clicks because they are more personalized to their relationship with First Dallas. because  they are more personalized to their relationship with First Dallas.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2023 - 05/14/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Leading up to Sunday services, First Dallas utilizes Facebook to share what the sermon will be and invites people to join in person.
This campaign also targets existing First Dallas contacts through the HubSpot/Facebook integration. We decided to test and see if copy that is specific to this audience would increase clicks on the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test if making ad copy specific to First Baptist Dallas's existing contacts on Facebook would result in a higher conversion in clicks. The treatment resulted in a 2.4% increase in clicks for all traffic, but with only a 12.2% statistical level of confidence, the results cannot be considered valid.
During the experiment, the connection between HubSpot and Facebook was broken. This led to the experiment not spending its full budget or reaching its highest potential. We will rerun this experiment in the coming weeks to seek valid results.

experiments.title: How a simplified Facebook image impacts click converstions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by simplifying the image and removing friction  for Facebook ad viewers will achieve higher click conversion because the ad will highlight the important aspects of the sermon.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/20/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each week First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share the upcoming Sunday’s message and sermon series.
Over the last few weeks, the Facebook ad performance has been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to test the traditional ad with an image with sermon details vs an ad with a simplified image that has Dr. Jeffress’s image removed and the sermon title removed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment tested the effectiveness of using an ad with a simplified image and message versus an ad with more details and an image featuring Dr. Jeffress. The hypothesis was that the simplified ad would achieve higher click conversions by reducing friction. However, the results showed that the control ad with more details and an image featuring Dr. Jeffress performed better, with a 4.3% increase in clicks for all traffic at a 61.1% level of confidence.
Since the level of confidence is below the 85% threshold, the results are not considered valid, and future decisions should not be based on them. There are some potential insights that can be gleaned from the experiment, such as the effectiveness of including details about the sermon and featuring Dr. Jeffress in the ad. Future experiments could build upon these insights to test different messaging and images to see if they can improve ad performance.

experiments.title: How a simplified image impacted Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reducing the content on the image for Facebook viewers will achieve an increase in clicks because it is less friction and simplified information.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each week First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share the upcoming Sunday’s message and sermon series.
Over the last few weeks the Facebook ad performance as been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to test the traditional ad with an image with sermon details vs an ad with a simplified image of only the sermon series title.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment indicates that simplifying language and images on Facebook ads may not be the best way to achieve increased clicks. Our hypothesis was that by reducing information on the image and content, Facebook viewer engagement would increase. However, as demonstrated in this experiment, clicks were actually 6.1% lower when a simplified image of only the sermon series title was used.
It is important to remember that although the control won in this experiment, the statistical confidence was too low to be considered valid. This means the experiment has not completely invalidated our hypothesis and the test of simpler ad copy and imagery may still be worth experimenting with again in the future.
As a result, the key learning from this experiment is that more experimentation should be done in regards to messaging, images, and ad copy in order to find out what performs best. It is important to conduct experiments often and on a consistent basis in order to stay updated on changes in Facebook ad performance and understand what works best at any given time.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by including the word “now” to a donate button affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve greater conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2022 - 04/19/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, there will be an increase in conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Click rate on the donate button was higher for the variant (11%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (77%).
• Overall donor conversion was higher for the variant (24%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (95%).
• RPV was slightly higher for the variant (1%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (53%).
• One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (15%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (79%).
• One-time RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (58%).
• Recurring RPV was higher for the variant (40%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (85%).
• Recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (69%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (96%).
• As the hero already uses "Donate Now", we recommend not changing the main donate button despite the significant life in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How changing donate button copy to convey greater clarity and urgency affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve an increase in click rate because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2022 - 02/23/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, clicks will increase because donors need to act immediately. We examined how different CTA’s affected the click rate on the main donation button. There were four variants with differing language; testing the verbiage of donate v. give v. help in addition to temporal urgency now v. today. This test exclusively measured click rate and did not delve into conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped for lack of significant results after 2 1/2 weeks.
• Lowest performing treatment was "give now" (-1.6%) with a confidence of (9%).
• Best performing treatment was "donate today" (12%) at a non-statistically valid confidence of (64%).
• Further tests will examine the impact on donor conversion and click rate.

experiments.title: How adding value with more descriptive impact language affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that improving the value proposition with more descriptive language will achieve a higher click-through rate by clarifying the impact and outcome of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/31/2022 - 09/26/2022
We wanted to know if replacing non-descript, program-focused language with outcome-focused language affected would improve the value proposition in the minds of web traffic and give them more motivation to click through and learn more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated that using more descriptive, outcome-focused language to promote a program has the potential to increase click-through rates (CTR) by communicating more value to prospects and thus giving them a better reason to learn more by clicking through.
Treatment one asks "Do you believe children deserve a better future? Join the team" This not only implies the outcome of joining but also aligns the offer with the prospect's values, further compelling them to act and producing a 36.4% increase in click-through rates with a 76% level of confidence.
Treatment two was less specific than the control, simply stating that a monthly donation can make a difference without communicating what that difference is or why it matters and producing an 18% decrease in clicks.
And while the 36.4% increase in clicks created by treatment one was not statistically valid, we know that there is little risk in rolling out a treatment that has shown a lift. We therefore recommend rolling this treatment out to 100% of traffic.

experiments.title: How promoting a specific gift amount impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by asking existing donors for a specific gift amount for email audience will achieve higher conversion rate because the decision anxiety of how much to donate has been removed.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2023 - 02/08/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas that shares the truth of God’s Word through radio, television, digital resources, and books.
Each month PTV offers a different book resource to their email file that provides the audience with an opportunity to make a donation and receive the resource as an appreciation gift.
Existing donors on the PTV email file historically receive a nonspecific gift amount asked. In an effort to increase donor conversion, we decided to test a specific ask amount for this audience.
We have conducted similar testing for the nondonor email segment and have seen positive results in providing nondonors with a specific gift amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
On the email level, we did not see any significant or valid directional lifts. However, for donations and revenue, we see an interesting story...
For donations, the control saw 15 donations and 16 donations for the treatment. That calculates to a 5.2% increase with an 11.1% level of confidence.
For revenue, the control brought in $975 with an average gift of $65, and the treatment brought in $789 with an average gift of $49.31.
As a reminder, the treatment asked for a donation of $25 or more while the control was nonspecific.
The transaction break down for the two variants were
Control - nonspecific amount ask
1 - $10 gift3 - $25 gifts1 - $30 gift1 - $35 gift3 - $50 gifts1 - $75 gift3 - $100 gifts2 - $150 gifts
Treatment - $25 ask
2 - $7 gifts7 - $25 gifts4 - $50 gifts2 - $75 gifts1 - $250 gift
For revenue we saw a 13.3% decrease in overall revenue with a 61.6% level of confidence
Surprisingly, we saw that the control donors were more likely to make a gift of $25 or more without being prompted with 11 gifts. While the treatment audience was more likely to stay at the $25 level.
As this audience did not respond as well as to the specific ask as the nondonor segment has, we will begin testing into generic language around value prop and mission of PTV to see if a change will increase response.

experiments.title: How a email subject line drives clicks

rows.name: 10 Core Beliefs for Standing Strong in a Shifting World
Brand-new book "What Every Christian Should Know"

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding clarity to the subject line for the housefile will achieve an increase in click conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2023 - 01/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry with a mission to pierce the darkness of the world’s culture with the light of God’s truth. In that effort, each month PTV shares a book or resource to equip Christians to share the truth of God.
The premium for January 2023 is a brand-new book by Dr. Robert Jeffress titled “What Every Christian Should Know”.
In an effort to drive premium purchases, we decided to test the subject lines in the initial kick-off email to see which resonated more with the individuals on the email list. For our treatment, we added clarity by announcing the release of the brand-new book, while the control stayed in alignment with the traditional approach.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this test, we saw 2 different results.
For the "10 core beliefs" subject, we saw a 5.7% increase with a 100% level of confidence in opens. However, we know that this is not a conversion rate that should be totally trusted due to various platforms "pre-opening" emails.
For the "Brand-new book" subject line, we saw a 10% increase in clicks with a level of confidence at 65.4%.
What we found interesting is that more people open the email with the subject line "10 core beliefs" however the higher click count occurred on the "Brand-new book" subject line. This leads us to believe that people are looking for information around the core beliefs but were not as interested in purchasing a book. Whereas the "brand-new book" subject line saw more clicks meaning that people knew what they were opening and were more interested in learning more about the book.
For this new book, we are going to continue testing copy and seeing which angle of this book resonates best in an effort to drive purchases.

experiments.title: How beginning an email with a declaration vs a personalized sentence affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting an email with a more personalized sentence rather than a declaration for email openers will achieve a higher click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/08/2022
Luther Seminary was running their year-end high-urgency campaign, and for the first two appeal emails of the campaign, they wanted to test if starting the email with a more personal sentence instead of a declaration would keep users reading longer which would ultimately increase the click-through rate.
The control email started off with an explicit statement that could be viewed as not personal or more “organizational”. The treatment email started with a personal statement that sounds more conversational or like you’re speaking with someone, not just an organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment email saw a 10.1% decrease in click-through rate with only a 57.7% level of confidence. Because of this, we can assume that starting an email with a declaration or personalized statement does not affect the click-through rate of the email. We believe this does not have as much of an impact because it's changing something very small in the email when compared to the overall value proposition the rest of the email provides.

experiments.title: How adding an image of an incentivized item to an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing an image of the special CATIS coin in the welcome series email for potential CATIS members will achieve an increase in clicks because they’ll be impressed with the coin and gain a true feeling of possessing the coin.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2022 - 12/02/2022
As part of TPPF’s welcome series, new names are sent an invitation email to join the Come and Take It Society (CATIS) by making a monthly gift. When joining, members receive exclusive perks. One perk is receiving a special CATIS coin. This specific perk is mentioned in the email, however, the email does not include an image of the coin to give potential new members an accurate, tangible impression of the coin.
We hypothesized that adding an image of the coin would further pique the interest of email recipients, giving them a preview of the special coin they would receive, and therefore encouraging them to click through the email to the CATIS donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in an 89.9% lift in clicks - a 6.3% click rate compared to 3.3% for the control. Our hypothesis proved correct: Those who saw the coin were more likely to click within the email. The coin image gave these people a preview of the coin and provoked visions of what it would be like to possess the coin. The mere mention of it can only go so far. By just mentioning it, email recipients don't know what the coin looks like and if it'd be worth essentially paying for as part of a monthly donation commitment. They could envision the coin as appearing tacky and poorly made. Showing an image of the coin promotes something tangible and takes some of the guesswork out of the person's mind.

experiments.title: How campaign first messaging impacts clicks from donors in Giving Tuesday emails
experiments.researchQuestion: What messaging will get a more positive response for Giving Tuesday in an email campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/02/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that does its primary fundraising through monthly premiums. For Giving Tuesday, they offered an exclusive bundle only available on that day.
As Giving Tuesday has grown exponentially around the world, we wanted to test how we could stand out during a small timeframe when all organizations are making an ask.
We tested this by using Giving Tuesday based messaging at the beginning of the email and then later talked about the mission of PTV for the control and developed a treatment using PTV mission-focused messaging at the beginning of the email and then later used Giving Tuesday messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not receive a sufficient change in clicks, only 2.5% increase with a 16.8% level of confidence. We did see a 9.2% increase in opens with a 99% level of confidence.
While email opens is no longer a reliable metric, due to Apple and other platform privacy rules. We did observe a high enough increase that warranted a deep look.
When the control email hit inboxes, the viewer saw the subject line and the first few words of the email which were "Hello [firstname], On #Giving Tuesday..." For the treatment, the viewer saw the same subject line but a different first few words. They saw "Hello [firstname], As you know Pathway to Victory..."
Our hypothesis is that the increase of opens is due to the initial response of the email not being Giving Tuesday focused like ever other email in the inbox.
As we did not see a valid response to this test, we will continue testing to see if the order of messaging can impact conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar impacts clicks to the donation page from website visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar will increase clicks to the donation page for website visitors
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/07/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They wanted to test ways of improving visitor-to-donor conversion on their website. They hypothesized that having a sticky bar with a message from a child asking for a donation with the face of the child accompanying the message would increase donor conversion by “humanizing” the appeal.
People give to people and this would make it more of a conversational, person-to-person experience. They ran the test for the entire July matching donor campaign against their control, non “humanized’ bar.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month, we were unable to validate any impact on clicks or downstream donor conversion. While we couldn't validate a lift, the client did enjoy the interactivity of the treatment design and will continue to use it and test it on other offers and portions of their donor funnel. However, in doing so, the client is not expecting a lift in conversion from the new design.

experiments.title: How content preview impacted the click-through rate on a digest email

rows.name: Some cool new things for you, Vilavanh
Some cool new things for you, Danielle

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing preview content from a digest email for our general subscribers will achieve more click-throughs than a digest email with preview content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/31/2022 - 09/02/2022
We previously tested adding preview links to a digest email as a strategy to increase click-through rate on a digest email (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4515). In that test, there was a 30% increase in click-throughs on the treatment email.
To further test this, we wanted to go one step further and test using the preview links with our normal content previews beneath vs. just the preview links. We were curious to know if the extra content added value to digest recipients, or if it had no impact on their email engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings: Results did not validate. It seems removing preview content had no real impact on the click-through rate of the digest email, but results were trending in favor of the control version with preview content.

experiments.title: How offering a survey about cards changed response rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for themed responses for survey responders will achieve change the response rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2021 - 10/29/2021
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. They offer physical cards during Eastern and Mother’s Day; for this experiment, we wanted to survey their email audience to see whether people would respond more for an Easter ask versus a Mother’s Day ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both the Mother's Day control and the Easter treatment had essentially the same click-through rates to the survey, with only a 1.3% decline with the Easter treatment version, for a 33.6% level of confidence.

Since the results of this experiment were so close, this experiment will be tweaked and re-launched for next year.

experiments.title: How using a video in a Facebook ad affects click through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a video in an ad for Facebook users will achieve higher click rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2022 - 02/27/2022
In an effort to increase click through rates on a FB ad promoting a survey, we wanted to know if presenting the issue through video format would provide a lift in click through rates. We hypothesized that by offering a different format that presented the narrative in video form, rather than text, more viewers would stop to interact with the ad and hopefully click through
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we were able to see a 49% lift in click through rates. This indicates that the video format stood out in people's feeds and presented the issue in a way that inspired viewers to click through.

However, due to the low number of impressions received by the two ads, we were unable to validate this experiment. The treatment received only $19.37. We also noted that the video format was not the only ad element that was changed. The body text was also shortened and revised to use more urgent language that compelled viewers to act.

In future experiments, we may want to distribute the spend between control and treatment more evenly and ensure enough spend is designated to provide sufficient traffic. If we can validate the lift provided by the video format, we can then test the copy variations against one another.

experiments.title: Does adding a donor’s last gift into a supporter card renewal appeal increase renewals?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reminding a donor of their last gift date for members of AFP will achieve higher click throughs and donor conversion rates..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/19/2022 - 03/05/2022
Americans for Prosperity has a long-standing membership card, which is promoted through mail and digital channels. For this year’s renewal campaign, we wanted to test whether including the image of the card, with a donors’ information overlaid on top of that image, coupled with a shorter copy, would lead to a higher click rate and higher donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: Our treatment version with image and donor information performed better than the control version, with a 10% increase in click throughs. While not a valid result, it also resulted in 800% more donations than the control version.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a donor countdown impacted donation page visits
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a micro-ask in a sticky bar for website visitors at calendar year-end will achieve an increase in donation page visits.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2021 - 01/01/2022
In order to drive traffic from the website during a high urgency campaign, we launched two versions of a sticky bar to show site visitors. The control used donor alignment and a deadline to indicate urgency. The treatment version used a micro-ask: will you be one of the 10 donors we need today? We ran this as an A/B split test during the month of December to see if we could learn which message would most resonate with donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not achieve a significant level of confidence, the experiment ended after 4 weeks with a 5% increase in traffic at a 32% level of confidence for version B. We will need to further test this concept for other campaigns to better understand which message and format will help website visitors take interest in clicking through to the donation page. We will also want to introduce a more specific call to action language on future iterations of this test such as "Click here" to make certain visitors are aware of the next step we would like them to take and how after reading the sticky bar copy.

experiments.title: How removing images from emails affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing graphics for the digital audience will achieve higher click through rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2022 - 01/28/2022
With a file that is accustomed to receiving emails that are very graphic-heavy and premium focused, Pathway to Victory’s file was primed for a plain-text test. For this experiment, we removed all images and added in the full URLs (instead of hyperlinking text). While we had previously tested this during a high-urgency campaign, with the plain-text version winning, we wanted to see whether this trend would continue for a ‘regular’ campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The control version received more clicks than the plain-text test version, but the number of clicks was so close that there was no statistically valid result from this experiment.

experiments.title: How changing a headline in an email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does changing the headline of an email appeal affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/18/2017 - 10/18/2017
National Breast Cancer Foundation was running a high-urgency campaign for Breast Cancer Awareness Month. In email 5, they talked about their free mammography services. They wanted to test whether a more vague headline vs. a headline directly about that content would increase clickthrough rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate, but the topical headline produced a 13% decrease in clicks.

experiments.title: IAS – General Email (September 2021)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a personalized sender for our general file will achieve higher engagement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2021 - 10/05/2021
In this experiment, we wanted to see how the sender name would affect engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were not able to validate the experiment due to the small sample size. Next time, we will do a 50/50 split test

experiments.title: How a redesign of a newsletter offer impacted clicks from the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for homepage visitors will achieve more clicks to sign up for a newsletter.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2021 - 08/02/2021
As we evaluated the Adventures in Odyssey homepage, we wanted to find a way to increase the number of people who were clicking on the link to sign up for the newsletter. We wanted to develop a treatment that increased clarity about what the visitor would receive through the newsletter, emphasized the appeal by highlighting the benefit and value of getting access to the content rather than not signing up at all, and indicated the offer was unique, exclusive, and why it is distinct from signing up for any other newsletter. We took the control headline “Sign up for our Newsletter” and replaced it with “Get access to the latest resources for your family”. We also revised the supporting body copy from “Sign up to receive the monthly Adventures in Odyssey newsletter directly to your inbox” to “We’d love to share our most recent and helpful resources with you and your family! Be inspired to grow in your faith through these exclusive updates you won’t find anywhere else!”. And the final element we changed was the button text which read “Subscribe Now” while the treatment version said “Get Access Now”. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and let the experiment run.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were not able to reach statistical significance on the experiment, but the control saw a 20.8% increase with a 72.5% level of confidence when compared to the results of the treatment. What's particularly interesting is when reviewing the segments, we had a significant portion of samples that were visiting from mobile and who were labeled as "new visitors" but both of these segments show lower conversion rates, so future tests for this page could be more strategically designed to address these types of visitors since they make up the majority of the traffic volume. It also appears that returning visitors are more likely to click to sign up for the newsletter so we could potentially develop additional prompts such as a pop-up on someone's second or third visit that highlights the offer more prominently once we know they have shown a continued interest in the content. Phase two of this experiment involves testing the landing page copy where people end up once someone has clicked the button to subscribe so we can find out if people are actually converting and entering their email address to get the newsletter on the second step of the process after they have clicked through from the homepage.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 95% level of confidence.New visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 131.9% increase in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 97% level of confidence.Direct visitors had an 83.4% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition in a monthly donation ask email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that expanding the value proposition for a monthly donor ask will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/11/2021
In this monthly donor ask email, Live Action tested an expanded value proposition to become a monthly donor over a shortened version that left out a lot of the benefits of becoming a Life Defender.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a slight lift in clicks from the expanded value proposition treatment, but ultimately this experiment proved invalid.

experiments.title: Will a more personal email inviting people to a webinar increase engagement and signups?

rows.name: How can you improve your online fundraising, Steph
How can you improve your online fundraising, Gianc

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a more human, personal email for email subscribers will achieve more clicks and therefore webinar registrants.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2021 - 02/05/2021
NextAfter does at least one webinar a month and sends an email to big part of their email file to invite them to it. Previous testing has shown that a personal style email with some design elements works best. These ‘personal’ emails are often from someone, in this case Brady, but written by someone else, in this case Riley. For this test, we wanted to see if an even more personal email from and written by Brady with more personal anecdotes and tone would increase engagement (clicks) and therefore webinar signups.
experiments.keyLearnings: In the end the added personalization didn't help or hurt. When we looked at conversions there was a 3.2% increase in signups (not significant) and a slight click to conversion rate increase (82% vs. 81%, also not significant) but neither were valid. When we looked at replies back there was also a slight increase in replies to the more personal email but also not valid. So the more personal email with Brady's tone had no significant difference in driving engagement, clicks, and signups.

experiments.title: How adding a blog description above an article can lead to an increase in clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the article descriptions above the article image  for the highlight section in the newsletter will achieve an increase in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2021
At the bottom of RTB’s weekly newsletter email, they would have a “highlights” section where they would link to other articles/blogs. Below the articles, they would have a paragraph that would go into detail about what each blog/article was. The hypothesis was if we move the detailed description of what each article was directly above the article it would then lead to more clicks.
This was set up as a 50/50 split and was sent to their entire newsletter subscription file.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall this experiment was a wash. We saw a slight increase in clicks but this experiment was nowhere close to validating. While this experiment didn't validate we did see that adding the description above the articles didn't lead to a decrease in clicks so we are looking to test this again.

experiments.title: How using communal language in a sticky bar impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding communal language for website visitors will achieve increased traffic to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2021
To highlight a Challenge Grant with an upcoming deadline, we added a sticky bar across Buckner’s website to draw additional clicks over to the donation page. For the control we used time-focused language to build urgency but we wanted to test incorporating communal language and remind visitors of their friendship with the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: We ran the test for two weeks ahead of the Challenge Grant deadline but we did not receive enough traffic to validate the experiment and only reached a 55% level of confidence so we can't say for sure if this had any impact on donation page traffic. We'll look for additional opportunities to test into using communal language on housefile appeals where we have an established connection with the audience.

experiments.title: How using communal language in an email appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: How will communal language impact conversion in an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2021 - 03/08/2021
Care Net found very positive results in a previous experiment with using communal language. This experiment was run on the homepage of their website. They wanted to test this similar approach in an email appeal to see if they would see a similar response through this donor theory. To test it, communal language was added to the call-to-action of the email appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings: The communal language variation had no impact on click rate or donor conversion. This is due to the low engagement rate of the email. We will test this approach again in an email appeal and incorporate more communal language throughout the email to see if we can increase engagement and conversion.

experiments.title: How adding benefits of monthly giving affected clicks in an appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding benefits of monthly giving for a housefile appeal will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2021 - 03/11/2021
For a high-urgency campaign, we wanted to test if including the benefits of becoming a monthly donor would increase clicks to the donation page. This was the last email in the campaign and it was set up a personal note forwarding the previous email from earlier in the day with one final request to give before the deadline. The treatment version listed the benefits of becoming a monthly donor and included an image of the premium for monthly donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Since this email was sent to an audience limited in size, we were unable to validate the results of the experiment. We saw a 24.1% increase in traffic on the treatment but with only a 43.8% level of confidence so we cannot claim statistical significance. Moving forward, we'll plan to retest this same concept in future emails—as well as launch the test in other channels that may reach a wider audience —to see if there are any learnings to be applied.

experiments.title: Will sending a voicemail increase the liklihood of attending a webinar live?
experiments.researchQuestion: Are people more likely to attend a webinar live if they get a voicemail?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/30/2020
For a webinar in March, we wondered if a ‘thank you’ voicemail from Brady that reminded them of the date and time of the webinar would increase the likelihood of them attending live (as opposed to watching later or not watching at all). Using Zapier and Slybroadcast to automate the process, new webinar registrants would get a thank-you voicemail soon after they had signedup with the message of thanks and when/where to join.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no difference in performance

experiments.title: How including a video thumbnail affects clicks in a cultivation email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a video thumbnail outperform a raw link in number of clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/04/2020
TPPF wanted to test if including a video thumbnail would increase the number of clicks in a cultivation email.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, this experiment did not get enough traffic to reach the level of confidence needed to validate.

experiments.title: Will more text on a Facebook video ad lead to more clicks to the donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more copy lead to more clicks to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2020
KUOW Public Radio is Seattle’s NPR news station. Their independent, nonprofit newsroom produces award-winning stories, podcasts and events. They were running a campaign and wanted to focus on securing more monthly gifts and hoped a Facebook ad could drive more people to click and go on to make a donation. They’ve seen other Facebook advertising experiments where longer copy has led to increased donations and decided to test it for themselves. In each design, there is a video that was clickable but the main call to action and hope for the ad was to get people to click Donate Now and then visit the donation page where they could continue on to complete a recurring donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Neither the control or the treatment led to any donations and while the treatment had a slight increase in clicks, it was not significant. When looking at total unique clicks, the shorter ad (control) showed a statistically significant increase but the bulk of those were video views so, if the goal was to get more people to watch a video, it appears shorter copy is more effective. It should also be noted that the difference between the control and treatment was perhaps not great enough to prove the hypothesis. Also, taking a donor from Facebook ad to a recurring gift can be challenging.
So moving forward some ideas that require more testing and exploration are:
Shorter ads with videos where the goal is video views or just brand awarenessClearly longer ads where the goal is donations or clicks to a donation pageA short vs. long test with just an image and not a video that can be clickedDonation focused ads asking for a smaller one-time donation as opposed to a recurring gift

experiments.title: Will bolding in an email increase click rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding some bold treatment throughout the email copy increase click rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/28/2020
For event invitation emails we take a more long form approach as we’ve seen that be most successful in the past but are always interested in seeing how we can improve and not alienate scanners who aren’t looking to read. So for this experiment we bolded some of the key points of the email like what the event was and when, what it was based on, where to learn more, and evidentials like past event attendee survey responses.
experiments.keyLearnings: We sent to a targeted and smaller list so the result was difficult to validate but there was a directional decrease for the bold/treatment. There was no difference in unsubscribe rate so it doesn’t appear the bolding really upset people but could just be that it felt a bit more ‘marketing-y’ and didn’t get the same engagement. Possibly worth re-testing with a larger email list size but could be risky.

experiments.title: How a shortened digest email using bullets affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying the design and content of the digest email lead to more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2020
This experiment was inspired by a recent experiment from DTS. They took their designed newsletter email and simplified it down into a short intro blurb and a few bullet points. While we had previously tested into this more designed digest email format, it was only in the context of a long-form digest email with 6-8 different pieces of content to share.
For this experiment, we wondered if simplifying the design and content of the digest email down to a few bullet points could increase clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there was a slight uptick in clicks, it wasn’t anywhere close to reaching a statistically significant result. As a result, it appears that this change in design made no real difference. It’s possible we might see a more significant result with a larger sample size.
This experiment shows us that both design styles may be acceptable and equally effective. This gives us more flexibility in how we write and craft these types of emails moving forward.

experiments.title: How removing the banner of Evangelize emails improves clicks
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We wanted to increase clicks with our Evangelize emails.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/27/2019
We tested this with our monthly Evangelize emails. We believed that people may see our monthly newsletter banner and immediately lose interest and not scroll down to the links.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that this experiment had no impact on people clicking on the links. We will continue to for ways to improve our monthly newsletter emails.

experiments.title: How adding an image of an offer in an email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding an image of an offer in an email affect click rate to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2019
Hillsdale College recently launched a new online course titled “The Second World Wars.” They are now offering this course as a DVD boxed set and are offering it as a premium offer to their housefile. They wanted to know whether or not adding an image of the DVD boxed set to the email would have any effect on click rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 4% lift in the treatment version with the image of the DVD. The confidence level of this experiment was not at or above the 98th percentile, so this experiment shows no change in results. Even with a larger sample size, this test is not likely to validate. This is because there is such a small difference in click rate that a larger sample size will not determine any valid conclusion. This image test can be run instead on the landing page where people actually convert. We hypothesize that people like to see visuals with text as it helps them better understand what is being asked of them/offered to them.

experiments.title: How more mystery in a subject line affects click-rate

rows.name: The webinar recording is ready for you

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a subject line with more mystery lead to more opens and clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2019 - 07/22/2019
In this webinar follow-up email, we had been using the same kind of subject line for a while. While the sample size is low and hard to validate on, we wanted to put a hypothesis to the test that a subject line with more mystery would lead to more opens and clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: As we assumed, our sample size was not large enough to validate the result, despite seeing an 11% increase in clicks from the treatment version. A more radical test will be required in order to see a statistically confidence result.

experiments.title: How linking one line of text instead of two affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will having only one linked line of text in email copy affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/26/2019 - 07/10/2019
Hillsdale College ran a fiscal year end campaign. In this campaign, they versioned emails to lifecycle audiences: lapsed donors, active donors, and nondonors. In their email to active donors, they wanted to know if linking one line of text to a donation page instead of two or more lines would affect click rate. They decided to test this on their third email appeal in the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 5.8% increase in clickthrough rate in the treatment with only one linked line of text to a donation page. However, this is not a large difference and the level of confidence is insufficient to conclude this experiment.

experiments.title: How prompting for a reply impacts email engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will prompting the reader for a reply increase the email click through rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2019 - 05/21/2019
Alliance Defending Freedom was sending out their weekly email cultivation email to a select group of subscribers. The primary goal of these emails is to increase the overall engagement of this group, as a result we wanted to test out tactics that could improve this metric.
Every email sends them an article to read on the ADF blog. In order to give them a reason to read the blog post, we wanted to try prompting the reader to respond to the email. The belief was that if we gave them a reason to read the article (in this case, to tell us their opinion), they would be more likely to consume the content.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was no statistically significant measurable difference between the control and treatment version of this experiment. However, one of the more challenging metrics to measure is the actual replies to this email. This would be a higher form of engagement than a click through rate but it is not easily captured.
This will need to be updated once we receive anecdotal evidence from the ADF team on the number of responses.

experiments.title: How offering more content options in an update email affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending a newsletter style email with more content options lead to create click-throughs?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2018 - 12/10/2018
In this experiment, we were testing a first iteration of a more newsletter-like email. We have more content than we are able to share through these long-winded personal emails, so we wanted to see if sending more content options in more of newsletter or digest format would affect our click-rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no significant difference in click between the two versions, indicating that this change in formate does not have a measurable impact the likelihood of someone visiting our website. That being said, option B allows us to share more types of content which could theoretically lead to greater engagement.

experiments.title: How a content focused homepage affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a content-driven homepage increase the number of clicks to additional content on nextafter.com?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/13/2017 - 08/15/2017
The NextAfter homepage had been focused on the work of our consultancy since the web site was first created. Over time, we have started to produce more and more content based on our online fundraising research. We’ve realized that this content is most often the first point of contact that someone has with NextAfter. The perceived problem is that our content is not being featured on the homepage.
Elevating our top content to our homepage has the potential to improve SEO, improve a user’s ability to navigate to the content they’re looking for, and gain a stronger understanding of our research. A homepage structured around our content will also give us more flexibility in the future to produce, feature, and market new content types and offers.
We decided to test this content driven homepage to see 1) if it would improve or maintain bounce rates, and 2) improve or maintain conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The number of clicks to view other content on nextafter.com saw a 5% increase, although it only reached a 77% level of confidence. Additionally, we did not see a statically valid difference in conversion rates on the homepage.
We also evaluated the average session duration of the control and treatment, finding that the content driven homepage had an 18% increase in session duration with a 91% level of confidence.
With 2 of 3 key metrics trending towards an increase, and conversion rates having no statistically valid change, we will begin to roll out this new homepage. In this case, even having no change is a win for us because this new design and layout will help us craft and organize our future content more effectively.

experiments.title: How the inclusion of graphical data in an email affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a statistical image that reinforces the message increase the number clicks generated for the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2015 - 04/20/2015
As part of their monthly April appeal, Jews for Jesus wanted to validate the work being put into different aspects of the monthly fundraising campaigns.  One of these elements was a visual that was placed in the email to help drive interest in the topic and statistically reinforce the topic of the campaign. The goal of the experiment was to determine if the content needed the external reinforcement provided by the graph.
experiments.keyLearnings: With no statistically significant lift to the number of visitors to the page, it becomes evident that the image of the video and content of the email is enough to pique the interest of the email file.  The additional image of the graph did not have any influence on the visitors’ choice with regards to the call to action.

experiments.title: How the addition of a button at the bottom of an email appeal affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a button improve click-through-rate in a monthly email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2016 - 03/12/2016
Jews for Jesus wanted to see if they could improve their click-through-rate on their monthly eAppeal by adding a button to the bottom of the email as the CTA. They traditionally used text links only in their eAppeals, but wanted to test that against the addition of a button.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of the button increased the click-through-rate by 13%. This would suggest that the addition of a button in an email appeal brings clarity to the CTA. It visually drew a person’s attention and showed them where to click. Because this test didn’t validate, additional testing is recommended.

experiments.title: How length of copy and a soft ask affects clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email appeal would improve conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2015 - 08/14/2015
Each month Jews for Jesus sends an email appeal. They wanted to test the length of the copy and the call-to-action of their emails in an effort to increase the click-through-rate. Would a soft ask inviting people to “click here and find out more” motivate people to click-through to the landing page, or would a hard ask that was more direct improve click-through? They put it to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The email that was shorter in length with the soft call-to-action had a 11.9% increase in conversion rate over the longer email with the hard ask. The short copy teased a story while the long copy told the entire story in the email with an ask for a gift.
People were more motivated to read more of the story than to give a gift. Though the longer copy had value proposition language supporting the ask, people weren’t ready to give a gift yet. This test didn’t validate and additional testing will be done.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition affects clickthrough

rows.name: It's a challenge to be a christ-follower

experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would increase clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2016 - 05/11/2016
Each month, Jews for Jesus sends out an eAppeal. The messaging for these eAppeals comes directly from their mail appeal for the month. When they converted the direct mail letter into an eAppeal this month, there seemed to be two different value propositions in the letter. One value proposition focused on how it is becoming more challenging to be a Christian in America today, and the other focused on a specific outreach event happening in Israel.  With having two different value propositions and versions of emails, they tested them to see which would get a higher click-through.
They split-tested their email file, dividing it in half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We weren’t able to validate these results, but what the initial data told us is that more people clicked through from the Israel outreach CTA than the control. This could be attributed to people being more motivated to give to a tangible need than a general value proposition and how the value proposition for the treatment was reinforced in the CTA.  Example: “Your gift will go immediately to direct, effective and unapologetic evangelism that is designed to present the Gospel in a way that Jewish people can relate to.”
In another test we did for this email, we found that the Israel outreach event increased donations by 47.3%. This audience is more motivated to give to a tangible event than a relevant value proposition for the organization. You can see the full details of that test here.

experiments.title: Shop vs Store on Main Navigation
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shop prompt more clicks than store?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2016 - 03/25/2016
We are looking at changing the navigation on our site to be more action oriented to give people multiple calls to action in the navigation. We decided to start this with the shop vs store test.
experiments.keyLearnings: Shop gave us a small lift, but after 5000 page views did not receive confidence. We will go back and add “now” to the shop button and see if that increases the lift to the point of confidence.

experiments.title: TV Show (sidebar vs under)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will dropping the CTA button below the video negatively affect CTR?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2016 - 07/08/2016
We are redesigning the TV show page with Denver New Media and we wanted to see the affect of their design.
experiments.keyLearnings: It did affect it negatively, but only about 6% decrease in clicks. This was not confident, either.

experiments.title: Blue Links vs Red Links (Color of CTA)
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the color of a link play a part in the number of click throughs?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2016 - 09/23/2016
Different colors elicit different emotional responses. We are looking to find which colors increase click throughs for our different email audiences/topics.

experiments.title: MEU Stylized vs Plain (CTR)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a plain, letter-style email get more clicks than a stylized email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2016 - 10/14/2016
Looking to see if MEU audience responds better to the regular email or to a plain, letter-style email that looks like it came from a real person.
experiments.keyLearnings: More testing needed.

experiments.title: E Appeal Stylized vs Plain (CTR)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will plain text get more clicks than stylized text.
experiments.keyLearnings: More testing needed.

experiments.title: How using an image of the free resource offer in an email can decrease clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the image of the book help increase clicks to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2017 - 05/23/2017
Live Action created a new content offer to acquire new names and emails. They used this same content offer as a cultivation offer to their housefile. In an effort to increase click-through from the housefile email for the offer to the landing page, they put an image of the eBook in the email. They tested this against the same email without the image of the book.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not validate, but the initial data showed that the treatment with the image of the book slightly decreased clicks in the email. In a previous test we learned that their housefile responds best when the email from the sender is highly personal, casual and conversational. What the initial data in this new test suggests is that by having the image of the book in the email, it may not have felt as genuine or authentic that the sender personally sent them this email and eBook. This could have caused friction and resulted in less people clicking through to the landing page.

experiments.title: How mentioning a specific giving amount in the call-to-action of an email impacts click-rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email would increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2017 - 06/05/2017
Live Action sends monthly email appeals to their email file. In an effort to get more people clicking through to their donation page, they wanted to test if putting a specific donation amount in the call-to-action would help increase conversion. The control didn’t mention a specific giving amount and the treatment mention a specific giving amount of $50. All of the other copy in the email remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically valid, the treatment email decreased click rate by 10%. We will need to do more testing around this idea to know exactly the impact a specific giving amount has on click-through, but we could hypothesize from this initial data that mentioning a specific giving amount to people presents a point of friction in the conversion process.

experiments.title: How using a button instead of a raw link impacts conversion in a cultivation email
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a button instead of a raw link increase clicks in a cultivation email?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2019
The Missionary Oblates had tested into using raw links for the hyperlinks in their email communication. This finding was over a year ago and they have continued to use raw links in all of their emails since then. As they were sending a cultivation email to their email file they hypothesized that maybe their file had grown used to the raw link and it wouldn’t have the same impact as it had when they had originally tested it. They developed a treatment that used a button instead of a raw link and split their file in half to see the impact on clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a directional lift of 5% from the treatment that had the button instead of the raw link. Because of the volume of emails that the Missionary Oblates send, we’d recommend testing this approach again in an effort to validate the approach and track conversions all the way through. We ultimately want to see the impact this kind of experiment has not only on clicks, but donations as well since the cultivation email offered people a free content offer that had a donation ask on the end of it.

experiments.title: How a condensed version of a direct mail letter performs against a long version
