rows.name: Fight "Fake News" and Stop Campus Indoctrination (short)
Fight "Fake News" and Stop Campus Indoctrination (long)

experiments.researchQuestion: Will long or short copy result in a greater amount of clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2018 - 05/01/2018
The Leadership Institute sends out a 7-12 page direct mail letter each month to its housefile list. They wanted to see if sending that same letter, digitally, would have an impact on clicks. They wrote a condensed version of the long letter to test against the original. Their hypothesis was that the condensed version would generate more clicks than the long version. They did not remove Word formatting for the long version.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was only a .04% difference in click rate between the two versions. There was insufficient data to determine if longer or shorter copy was more effective.

experiments.title: How extra formatting in the email affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will extra formatting in the email affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2018 - 04/25/2018
This experiment was tested in the email copy. Hillsdale College wanted to see if adding bold text and two extra links in the copy would increase click rates. We hyperlinked two phrases in the original copy and added some boldness to the original text to see what impact would be made.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment had a .47% click rate in comparison to a .44% click rate. We can conclude that there is no difference in the bolded version and the original version with no bold lettering. After a side by side comparison, the additional formatting was not remarkably different. As a result, it provided minimal impact on the readers likelihood to take action.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky bar to a website affects email acquisition conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a sticky bar to our website with a content offer convert names from our web traffic?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2018 - 02/19/2018
Buckner was wanting to diversify the ways they acquired emails through content offers, and to take advantage of their website traffic to convert names. They wanted to test whether offering the 7 Scriptures offer on their website as a sticky bar popup would convert people. The initial test measured clicks to the landing page and the tested variable was control copy vs. supporter copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 10 days of running the experiment, we only acquired 1 new name from the landing page. Because of this learning, we have concluded that other forms of acquisition are more effective than a sticky bar on the webpage.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition about a matching gift affects click rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Does additional value prop about a matching gift increase clickthrough?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2018
Buckner International was running their first June solicitation email regarding a matching gift and the program it affected. They wanted to see if adding additional value prop about the matching gift would motivate more people to click the donation link.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test was not statistically significant and did not produce a significant directional lift. If this test were to be repeated, more elements would need to be changed, or copy would need to be changed more drastically in order to see a true difference.

experiments.title: How value proposition copy affects clicks on a donate button in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the additional value proposition copy affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/19/2018
Leadership Institute sent a daily email called CampusWire, updating their audience about developments through their campus network. At the bottom of the email, they had a simple button that asked the reader to support CampusReform. While this did produce donors, they wondered if adding value proposition to the button would increase clickthrough rate and thus increase donation page traffic. They crafted a treatment and sent it to half of the file for several weeks to see if it would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After several weeks, there was no statistical difference between the two treatments. Clickthrough rate overall was low, since this is at the bottom of an email designed to get people to click through to the site, but it showed that the additional value proposition was not significantly increasing motivation—which suggests that readers were already motivated to give just through the content.
This experiment deserves further review to see if donation page conversion improved. It also suggests that fundraising might increase if this value proposition was broken out into its own email, away from the context.

experiments.title: Does Adding the Word “Free” to a Landing Page Subhead Increase Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding “free” language to the offer page subhead increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2018 - 05/17/2018
Our landing pages for The Hoover Institution’s ebook offers typically focused on simply explaining the benefits of the offer content rather than emphasizing that they are getting it the content for free. Since the typical Hoover donor is usually an older, intellectual, middle-to-upper class demographic, our assumption was that they would likely view a “free” offer as having less value than otherwise. However, we decided to test this hypothesis just in case our assumptions were incorrect.
experiments.keyLearnings: As there was virtually no statistical difference, we concluded that adding "free" language to the subhead was unnecessary to the offer's success. It was not necessarily hurting the value of the offer, but it was also not compelling enough to increase our conversion rate. In this case, we would rather stick with simply relying on the value proposition of the offer to sell itself.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an in-site ask affected clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign of an end-of-article call-to-action affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2018 - 05/15/2018
Leadership Institute had seen a large jump in donation traffic through the implementation of an end-of-article call-to-action on their CampusReform website. They wanted to test to see if improved copy and layout could increase clickthrough rate from the site and hopefully increase overall donation revenue. They created a treatment that added increased persuasive copy, a third-party quote, and statistics to bolster the value proposition. They also made a softer ask in the button, asking the reader to “learn more” rather than “support Campus Reform now”
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, there was no valid difference in clickthrough rate. After receiving several hundred impressions per CTA, the treatment showed a slight decrease in clickthroughs, but nowhere near validity. The team went back to the drawing board to try to find something more radical that would positively increase clicks.

experiments.title: How link placement in a course offer email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a link placed higher in a course registration email increase clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/11/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if having a link to the course registration page near the top of the email would increase clicks to the registration page. The control looked like our normal, longer form personal emails. The treatment naturally flowed into a link to the page early on, and the rest of the copy was the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment didn’t significantly affect clicks to the landing page, indicating that link placement in a personal email like this does not affect the likelihood of getting a recipient to click. The value proposition is the primary influencer of the click.

experiments.title: How the value proposition of the call-to-action impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing on the impact of the gift instead of the challenge aspect increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/26/2018
For their March appeal, Alliance Defending Freedom was sending a follow up email explaining the outcome of their recent Supreme Court case. As part of the appeal, they were also announcing a matching grant and using that as one of the primary reasons to give a gift. Since it was a “challenge” gift, much of the language around the call-to-action was about “accepting the challenge.” We had the hypothesis that by focusing on the value of the gift (ie. to protect lives and freedom) instead of the challenge, we could get more visitors to the website and more gifts to the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no statistically significant impact on overall gifts or clicks between the two treatments. It would appear that the value proposition of the various calls-to-action were not significantly different enough to see a measurable impact.

experiments.title: How adding more text links affects clickthrough rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding more text links increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2018 - 02/07/2018
In this experiment, we were getting ready to send a research update email featuring recent content and learnings. Our typical email includes only raw url links, and focuses in a select few pieces of content. We wondered if adding additional text links, and more in line opportunities to view content could increase total clicks from the email to the website.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although it didn’t reach the level of confidence we would like, the treatment using more text links was leaning towards the winner with a 14.2% increase in clicks. This indicates that we can drive more traffic to our website from email marketing when we included additional links and more varied content.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacts call volume
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign increase calls?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2017 - 01/25/2018
One of the top webpages for the Pregnancy Decision Line is the “Types of Abortion” page. We hypothesized that a radical redesign of this page – removing the header image, adding value proposition to the headline and body copy and connecting personally with the end-reader through the tone of the copy – would motivate more people to call, which is the primary objective of the page. They split the traffic coming to this page and tested the two against each other
experiments.keyLearnings: After six weeks of testing this page, we weren’t able to validate the experiment. There was a slight decrease in calls from the treatment, but because of the test’s low level of confidence we aren’t able to pull any learnings from the experiment or know with certainty that the radical would decrease call volume. We will propose a different angle in testing to better understand the motivation of the end-user.

experiments.title: How putting a countdown clock in the body copy of an email appeal impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a countdown clock increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2017 - 01/31/2018
During Care Net’s year-end campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase motivation of their email file to click through to their donation page towards the end of the campaign by increasing urgency in the email appeal. To do this, they inserted an animated countdown clock in the body of their email copy and tested this against the control version that didn’t have the clock. The clock counted down to the end of the year and informed the end reader that their time was running out to make their year-end donation. All other copy remained the same. They split their file in half and tested this approach
experiments.keyLearnings: White we weren’t able to validate the experiment, there was a definite directional lift with the treatment of 54%. By adding a countdown clock to the copy, we were able to increase urgency to the message and motivate more people to click-through to the donation page. This tactic will be implemented moving forward for high-urgency campaigns in an effort to not only get significantly more people to click through to the donation page, but potentially give to their organization.

experiments.title: How hyperlink text color affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: In this experiment, we asked how the color of a hyperlink text in an email affects click-through rate? Our hypothesis was that changing the color from the traditional blue to red would grab the reader’s attention and increase incentive with a more urgent feeling.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2017 - 08/09/2017
A study we saw indicated that changing the hyperlink text color from the traditional blue to red increased the click-through rate of the email by 52%. So we decided to test this on one of our small contact list chase emails.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not validate due to a minimal lift and the small sample size of the test. This does not give us confidence to change all hyperlink text colors going forward.

experiments.title: How a video screenshot versus a raw link affects click rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a video screenshot or a raw link increase click rate in an email?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/05/2018
National Breast Cancer Foundation was running their year-end campaign, and wanted to test whether more people would click a screenshot of a video or the raw Youtube link of the same video. The rest of the email was the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The raw link decreased clicks by 7%, but the test was not statistically valid. However, it’s likely that the image of the video supported the value proposition of the email more than the raw link with a line of copy before it. The image reinforced the emotional connection that people have with women in their lives.

experiments.title: How instrumentation error affects a donation button test
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation button will drive the most traffic to the donate page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2015 - 11/18/2015
The Hillsdale online courses donation page currently averages a conversion rate of ~40%. While it is exciting to have this high of a conversion rate, what this implied to us is that we are only sending the most highly motivated visitors to the page.  As a result, we wanted to move our testing to earlier in the donor funnel to see if we could drive an increased volume of traffic to the donation page.
We decided to run a test on the donation button that is displayed across the online courses website. The current button uses “Donate” as the call to action and the color blends into the background a bit. We had the hypothesis that we could improve the click rate by making the button stand out more and changing the CTA to be a bit softer of language.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test are invalid due to instrumentation error. It would appear that the VWO snippet was removed from many of the pages and blocked from several others.

experiments.title: How a countdown clock added to an email appeal impacts click through
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the addition of a countdown clock in an email help get more people to click through to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2016 - 12/31/2016
In the final days of a year-end campaign, urgency plays a key role in people making gifts to an organization. On the second to last day of NBCF’s year-end campaign, they hypothesized that they could increase the urgency of the ask by adding a countdown clock to their emails. They developed a treatment that had a countdown clock added to the top of the email copy and test this against their control that didn’t have the countdown clock on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Because NBCF’s email file is still in it’s infancy, there was low engagement and we weren’t able to validate the results. From the data that we were able to collect, the addition of the countdown clock received less clicks than the control that didn’t have the countdown clock.
We recommend testing this approach again once the email file has been cultivated more and there is a large enough sample size.

experiments.title: How evidentials of success impacted subscriber click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shorter copy increase the subscriber click through rate to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2016 - 01/12/2017
For the first email in their year end campaign, the Illinois Policy Institute wanted to determine what depth of explanation yielded the highest level of engagement from their subscribers. They set up two different emails, a short and a long version. The long version was nearly identical to the short with the exception of including bullet points in the copy with evidentials of their accomplishments in 2016.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the shorter version did see a 23% increase in the number of clicked, it was not a statistically significant lift. With such a low level of confidence (77%) it would be dangerous to try to make too many assumptions about the impact and results of this experiment.
This test does go a long way to proving that we need to create a more holistic reporting loop so that we can tie donation results back to an individual email. It would be a pretty safe assumption that a shorter email will increase the number of people clicking through to the web page but the behavior on that page is what is important to monitor. If the longer email sends less subscribers with a higher motivation, that may have a better impact on donation results.
For future appeals, it is recommended to utilize UTM tracking to allow attribution back to the individual email.

experiments.title: How simplifying the email design affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: A simplified email will have a more personal feel to it which will prompt a greater number of click thoughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2014 - 12/22/2014
The standard format for DTS emails is a designed layout with images and a color scheme that matches the website. In an effort to increase the number of visitors to the calendar year end campaign, we created an experiment to test the standard design against a simplified email.
experiments.keyLearnings: The design of the email did not have any significant impact on the supporter’s likelihood of clicking on the email. It also did not have any significant impact on the donors’ propensity to give. Moving forward we will utilize the existing email templates to save both cost and time.

experiments.title: Understanding the perceived value of video to email recipients
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using text instead of a video will allow donors to focus on the call-to-action which will increase the response rate of the campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2014 - 12/30/2014
Video is often used as a way to increase visitor engagement on donation landing pages. However, it was our hypothesis that the videos actual distracted from the ultimate goal, giving. As a result, we created an experiment to test a video against a text version of the same message.
experiments.keyLearnings: With no significant difference between click rates and nearly identical response rates (both are 0.1%), the effort put into the video may not be necessary this late in a year end campaign.

experiments.title: How placement of a call-to-action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the placement of the call-to-action button increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2016 - 03/30/2016
CaringBridge developed an email appeal for their March 2016 fundraising campaign. They had traditionally put the call-to-action buttons in the body of the email, giving the donor a large clickable area to click through and make their donation. However, since their email had a postscript, they hypothesized that putting the call-to-action at the end of the email might make more sense, as it allowed the user to digest all the copy before taking action.
They split their email file 50/50 to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment did not produce a significant lift. This shows us that the placement of the button does not have a significant effect on the clickthrough rate. Subsequent tests showed that removing the button actually improved results, so this element does not appear in current emails.

experiments.title: How different value propositions on an in-site donation ask affect clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will different approaches to the prospect-level value proposition increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 08/03/2017
CaringBridge’s user interface offered several different opportunities to give. One of these was internally called the “donate box”, and contained a personalized message that was placed in the updates section on an individual user’s journal page.
The control message had a simple straightforward ask: “Honor [FirstName] with a donation to CaringBridge. You make [FirstName]’s website possible”.  They wanted to test different messages to see if they could radically increase clickthrough rate, and with that increase in traffic, increase conversion rate and revenue.
They developed three treatments:
The first treatment reversed the order of the messages, focusing on the site itself: “[FirstName]’s CaringBridge site is supported by generous donors like you. Make a donation to CaringBridge in honor of [FirstName].”
The second treatment showed the impact of the site: Help [FirstName] stay connected to family and friends. Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
The third treatment made an emotional appeal that offered the chance for the user to provide more than just funds for the author: “Show your love and support for [FirstName].Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
Since CaringBridge has large amounts of traffic, they launched a 4-way test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: After several weeks of testing, none of the copy variants produced any significant lift over the control. While this was disappointing, a further review of the data found that one of the variants produced a significant increase in revenue after the click. This showed that the value propositions might not produce a result immediately, but might inspire a higher motivation to give.

experiments.title: How using clarity in the call-to-action can impact traffic to a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which giving button copy would increase traffic to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2017 - 10/11/2017
Care Net wanted to increase the volume of traffic going to their primary donation page. One of the ways to try and do this is to test the kind of copy that was being used on the “donate” button of their homepage. Their current donate button currently used value proposition focused language, “Save Babies Now”. They hypothesized that a treatment version with language that more directly reflected where the button would take them once they clicked on it might increase traffic to the donation page and ultimately have a positive impact on donations and revenue. They treatment button copy said “Support Care Net”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment copy had a slight increase in conversion, but the results weren’t statistically valid. The experiment ran for over a month and would need a more radical treatment with a larger sample size to validate.
Even though the test didn’t validate, we can hypothesize that with the slight increase in conversion from the button that said “Support Care Net”, the copy presented more clarity with the end-users action. They knew exactly where they would be taken if they clicked on the button. By saying “Save Babies Now” in the control, the copy may have been too vague and created a higher level of friction in the mind of the end-user – where would they be taken if they clicked on that button? What if they weren’t ready to “save a baby now”?
When we test copy in a call-to-action, it is good to keep in mind that clarity trumps persuasion.

experiments.title: How adding region-specific wording to an email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does making copy more personal to the reader increase click rate in an email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2017 - 11/28/2017
Buckner International was giving a free offer to people in their email file as a gift for Giving Tuesday. They wanted to test whether adding region-specific copy in the email would increase clickthrough rate. The control had generic language, whereas the treatment would use contact-specific wording and apply their most local Buckner branch’s name in that copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The region-specific copy increased clicks by 3%, however, this experiment was not valid. A more radical test should be performed to increase learnings about conditional copy to the reader.

experiments.title: How a shift in copy tone affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: An email talking about how Heritage impacts the constituents’ family life will drive more clicks than one talking about how they stand for the constituents’ principles.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2014 - 12/24/2014
In the fourth email in the Heritage year end campaign, we tested what messaging point (principles or family) would drive the most traffic to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the two treatments spoke to different prospect level value propositions, the copy did not seem to be different enough to see any significant impact on click throughs or donations.

experiments.title: How the “Next Article” button impacted engagement – V2
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “Next Article” button to the blog increase the percent of people that explore the ADF site?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2016 - 01/03/2017
We had previously validated an experiment that proved a “Next Article” button at the end of a blog actually decreased the amount of people that read the next article. We had doubts about the validity of this experiment due to a technical issue so we decided to run the experiment again to see if the same results would be achieved.
experiments.keyLearnings: This proved what we expected about the previous test; there was a technical issue that had invalidated the results. This experiment proved the simple act of adding the button did not impact the likelihood of the visitor to read the next article.

experiments.title: How a banner header image impacts article engagement for desktop users
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the header image on blog pages increase reader engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/26/2017 - 11/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom was interested in knowing how blog page design impacted reader engagement on their site. They wondered: Will removing the header image on blog pages decrease friction and increase reader engagement? They created a trackable goal that measured clicks from one blog to a second article. They then created a treatment version that removed the header image and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Alliance Defending Freedom found that the removal of the blog header image did not significantly impact reader engagement on their blog. The treatment page, in other words, did not produce a statistically significant increase or decrease in reader engagement for desktop users.
This test does, however, seek to address an important question–one that further testing will continue to try and answer. That question is: How does design impact page functionality and usability? This is an important, ongoing question that every nonprofit marketer should be motivated to answer for their organization because both form and function must exist in a healthy balance.

experiments.title: How adding a video screenshot in an email affections visitor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email treatment will produce the highest click rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2015 - 05/30/2015
With the landing page containing a video of Dr. Arnn, we wanted to see if that video was a motivation to visit the page. We created two versions of the email, one with a screen grab of the video and the other without.
experiments.keyLearnings: Having a video in the email did not prove to increase the engagement with the Hillsdale audience. Typically having an video will increase the click rate, but in this instance the video did not add enough perceived value to cause an increase in the number of recipients to click the link.

experiments.title: Understanding the perceived value of video to email recipients
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using text instead of a video will allow donors to focus on the call-to-action which will increase the response rate of the campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2014 - 12/30/2014
With no significant difference between click rates and nearly identical response rates (both are 0.1%), the effort put into the video may not be necessary this late in a year end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: Video is often used as a way to increase visitor engagement on donation landing pages. However, it was our hypothesis that the videos actual distracted from the ultimate goal, giving. As a result, we created an experiment to test a video against a text version of the same message.

experiments.title: How a shift in copy tone affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: An email talking about how Heritage impacts the constituents’ family life will drive more clicks than one talking about how they stand for the constituents’ principles.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2014 - 12/24/2014
In the fourth email in the Heritage year end campaign, we tested what messaging point (principles or family) would drive the most traffic to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the two treatments spoke to different prospect level value propositions, the copy did not seem to be different enough to see any significant impact on click throughs or donations.

experiments.title: How simplifying the email design affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: A simplified email will have a more personal feel to it which will prompt a greater number of click throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2014 - 12/22/2014
The design of the email did not have any significant impact on the supporter’s likelihood of clicking on the email. It also did not have any significant impact on the donors’ propensity to give. Moving forward we will utilize the existing email templates to save both cost and time.
experiments.keyLearnings: The standard format for DTS emails is a designed layout with images and a color scheme that matches the website. In an effort to increase the number of visitors to the calendar year end campaign, we created an experiment to test the standard design against a simplified email.
